  • I took some screenshots https://twitter.com/javascripl/status/1709706593896

comments.author: Antti Haavikko
comments.text: Yeah this was like my bonus entry cause I hit the 13k limit on my “main game” and still wanted to do some characters and more animations.

api.name: To Health With You!
api.description:
You play a 13th-century apothecary stranded on an island after a house call. A boat is coming in the morning; you just have to survive the night. However, this night is swarming with the undead. Using the logic that what’s healthy for the living must harm the undead, you fight them off with your medicines.

At your disposal, you have:

  • Ginger Shot as your main attack. Ranged, single-target with a 0.5-second cooldown. Your basic immune booster. Harms the undead.
  • Vitamin Bomb. Affects all targets in the area of the explosion. Loaded with all the goodness for a healthy life. Lethal to the undead.
  • Turmeric Cloud. A cloud that slows down the undead within the area. Normally used to cure bad joints, increasing mobility.

Movement is done with W, S, A, D. Cooldowns are visible in the upper part of the screen, to the right of the health bar.

  • Shoot Ginger Shot with your left mouse button.
  • Target Vitamin-C Bomb with space. Left-click at the desired location.
  • Target Turmeric Cloud with ‘e’ or ‘q’. Left-click at the desired location.

You get a score multiplier for killing several zombies in one shot, and if you survive to the end, you get a bonus as well.

comments.author: Alpine Games
comments.text: Fun to play but I feel like sometimes the enemies spawn too close to you.

comments.author: Armaan Mohammed
comments.text: The game is nice overall and the animations are smooth but the enemies are a little overpowered and spawn way too close to you for you to be able to react. They also spawn a little bit too often.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
I would use completely different sprites to define what’s walkable and what isn’t - grey and green aren’t different enough and I found myself trapped because I thought I could walk to a place where I didn’t.

I have the feeling the enemies cheat - they move faster on diagonals and some of them sprint to you when super close. There are really no ways to defend yourself or avoid them - if you get stuck into one of the grey blocking walls, you are dead.

The audio is nice but becomes super repetitive, would be nice to have some variation.

comments.author: Crystal Parker
comments.text: The graphics are very nice, I felt like the controls were a bit confusing.
games: Journey to the East; Silk Road Adventure

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • Graphics were nice
  • Different abilities worked out nicely and the combo of slow / area attack was fun

Feedback:

  • Controls weren’t obvious at first, the wall of text on the game page hindered scanning it for the controls.
  • Q is a difficult button to click when you’re moving left with WASD as the ringer finger that would press Q is currently occupied. Same with E and moving right
  • I wish there was a way to avoid the super fast enemies when your slow cloud is not on the screen and on cooldown. I couldn’t ever make it to the end because I would get surrounded and the fast enemies would overtake me and I couldn’t shake them or defend myself
  • Movement felt flat. By that I mean that my only movement choice was move around at a constant speed, which makes the action feel stiff and unengaging. In contrast if the player (as an example) could dash the movement would feel a lot more dynamic and engaging. The slow pace of the movement didn’t help with this either.

comments.author: John Swana
comments.text: Good game nice level design and plot

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Well, not a bad game, but also not super interesting and balanced. Just backtracking from tons of zombies without any result. There is several abilities with a cooldown Enemies are the same, with some procedural params

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I liked to play. It was quite scary to be encircled by so many enemies :) The graphics and animations are very well done, maybe you could add some music to make the game even more fun.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Decent entry, you have enemies, a nice map and sounds. All works well, you could improve by adding a more arcade feel and some particle FX.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: I dig the tree sprite! And the terrain in general looks nice.

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text:
Highly stylized little pixel art survival game! Nice visuals, very playable, and simple inputs. My main feedback is that I wish the monsters spawned in little puffs of smoke (like in Zelda games, to give you a warning) or something similar.

Nice game tho. Good job!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Instead of the declared WASD, I could control the arrows. The E and Q buttons did not work at all. If you play Space very quickly with the Left mouse button, the zombies will not die, but you will use up your supply.

Although it doesn’t really resonate with the theme of the contest in my opinion, it’s an interesting game in general.

Thanks for game! Congratulations!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I enjoyed playing the game. The graphics were good and I liked the levels.

The idea of the game is good, and I enjoyed exploring, but the enemies come thick and fast and just appear from nowhere.

The enemies are a bit quick, felt quicker than me. Plus once they are on top of me, it’s hard to hit them as you need some distance to have an angle to attack at.

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text:
Really cool idea to turn medieval healing practices into weapons against the undead! That’s a really unique concept and it makes for some very nice mechanics.

The game increases in difficulty nicely as the timer goes down, which keeps the game interesting as you get further into it, which is great. Visually, the game could’ve been a bit more dark and spooky. That might’ve added a bit more to the undead/survive-the-night feel of the game.

But all in all, very well done!

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Not bad! I loved the look of it, and I like the idea. Bonus points for puns and silly logic :)

The actual gameplay though, felt a little flat. Basically, if every enemy is a bullet sponge, then you feel like you’re endlessly waiting for enemies to die. To get the start of a nice rhythm, You just needed one more trash mob that popped in a single hit, to fill in the gaps between the bullet sponges. There are also balance issues where you’re forced to use slow and bomb to deal with the sprinters, but they’re on cool downs, so if 3 sprinters spawn in a row, it’s game over. Sprinters are also a good candidate for lower hits: they’d be about quick reactions, rather than forcing the use of slow and bomb. Then slow could be more about setting up traps to bomb.

I really wanted to see if there was something at the end, but after 2 tries it was a no!

api.name: 13 Samurai
api.description:
Can you get the Yoshindo sword, heavily guarded by 13 of the most skilled samurai of the empire. Will you succeed, or get slain by the masters? Try the game to find out!

  • WASD: movement
  • Space: Attack

How I made it

Hi, I’m Andrei, I’m 12 years old and I made this! The project was kind of ambitious, for the size limit and given that I will not be at home for most of the jam’s duration, but I’m happy I made it! The first thing I bumped into was the WebGL renderer. My knowledge in 3d graphics is theoretical and rare, but with the help of my amazing father, I managed to pull it off in the first few days.

I thought I could make a generative island with perlin noise, but it looked like a 3 year old made it. So I made the island shape in blender, and generated some trees. Simple as pie! Only then I realised that I had to convert those .blend files into something my renderer would understand. So I spent a couple of days making a obj to vertices and indices, only then to realise it was too big. I wanted to make it in binary encoding, but nah, JSON!

And then I made a samurai. It took two remakes to make it not look like a potato. And I had to animate it. Man, I really miss Three.js. But I managed to do that.

And then I found out in the last couple of days that I needed music. I thought I could make my own synth, like last year, but my brain was empty. After one day of struggling, I gave up and used zzFxM. Cringe, but at least it works.

Oh, but wait. I don’t have a start screen. I always forget to do the start screen in game jams. I just made it quick and dirty at the end. Nice.

So finally, I can upload and boom! Done and dusted. It was a hard challenge juggling between code golfing, simplicity, webGL, and hardcore mathematics, while doing a lot of schoolwork. I really hope you like it💖

I used VSCode, Blender for modeling, zzFx for sound and music, and a small part of sanity

comments.author: Konrad Linkowski
comments.text: Cool idea and the game work smoothly but I couldn’t kill any samurai sadly

comments.author: Andrei Iacob
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback! I think I made the game for try-hard keyboard smashers…

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
The image is a bit fuzzy when moving, but otherwise this game looks pretty good!

Short game but well done!

comments.author: Andrei Iacob
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback! I think I can lower the motion blur to fix that.

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
Great game, especially for a 12 year old! I wish I could do something like this when I was 12!

Unfortunately the first time I played the game, it seemed completely impossible. I died right way while barely doing any damage. Sometimes when something like this happens, I’ll break out my laptop to try the game…sometimes I won’t, but I’m glad I did this time.

See, my main computer has a 120 hz monitor. Even my phone is 90 hz. It seems like you’ve programmed the games logic to work at 60fps, and are using requestAnimationFrame. On any monitor with a higher refresh rate than 60, your game becomes unplayable.

Switching to my old laptop, the game wasn’t hard at all, but suffered from a weird motion blurring. I’m not sure why. I killed all the samurai but couldn’t find the sword.

Honestly if you fix the issues with higher refresh rate monitors, and can fix the motion blurring issue, I think this would be one of the best games this year!

Still, a great achievement for anyone to build a game like this in under 13kb. Even more so at your age. Congratulations and I look forward to seeing what other games you make in the future!

comments.author: Andrei Iacob
comments.text: Thanks for all of the great feedback! Yes, this game I totally forgot about this in my game. Thanks for the tips! I do have a 160hz screen but I didn’t bother playtesting… Now I know!

comments.author: Nik Lever

games: Sir Coadalot and the Holy Grail

comments.author: Andrei Iacob

comments.author: Andrei Iacob

comments.author: Justin Sullard

comments.author: Andrei Iacob

comments.author: Alpine Games

comments.author: Andrei Iacob

comments.author: John Edvard

Good job on this entry. The models and the world fit well together.

I like that I have to sometimes lure an enemy in so I can fight 1 on 1, and not 3 on 1. I need to be tactical which I like.

comments.author: Andrei Iacob

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

Feedback:

comments.author: Andrei Iacob

comments.author: John Swana
comments.text: Nice game by the way good concept and sounds

comments.author: Andrei Iacob

comments.author: Alexander Petrov

comments.author: Andrei Iacob

comments.author: Andrei Iacob

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu

comments.author: Andrei Iacob

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:

I noticed that you can pass through the textures of trees and stones :), got the sword, went on :).

If you add the ability to control the camera, expand the territory a little (make all kinds of bridges between the islands), add a few elements - it will be even more fun in my opinion!

comments.author: Andrei Iacob

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Great graphics, simple but nice music, cool attack sounds and action, the enemies are easy to fight but it all works.

Great you put a full game together with intro and ending.

Improvements:

Impressive game, enjoyed playing.

comments.author: Andrei Iacob

comments.author: Tom Harris

comments.author: Andrei Iacob

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text: Super impressive amount of 3D and with sounds and some little animations for the samurai, it’s a really nice entry! Super impressive that you are only 12 and making games like this, congrats! It could have been nice to use the new sword against some more enemies as a sandbox or something after defeating everyone to see how much stronger the sword is but the story is less important here! Really cool what you did here, some small things to iron out but generally it’s a fun game! Making the combat a little more intense could be nice going forward !

comments.author: Isaac Benitez

Nice! You made me want to try 3D! I mainly do pixel art. Adding music would have given the game a good boost! Could find the swore :’(

Congrats!

api.name: Parry
api.description:
The 13th Century was a chunk of time during which, according to Wikipedia, pretty much only war happened. In this game, you play a pacifist who’s just trying to survive. While a bunch of enemies attack you, your goal is to simply parry their attacks.

Game Instructions

  • Move your sword with the mouse.
  • Rotate with Q or E.
  • Parry enemy attacks by intersecting the enemy sword for around half a second.
  • Try to create perpendicular angles between both swords (i.e. make a ‘cross’ or ‘x’ shape). This will give you a higher score.
  • If you don’t parry the enemy sword in time, it will attack you, lowering your score.

Enemy Descriptions

  1. Peasant: Slow, but unpredictable.
  2. Barbarian: Quick, but predictable.
  3. Paladin: Like the barbarian, but smarter.
  4. Archer: Long-ranged sniper.
  5. Duelist: Skilled and precise.
  6. Dual Wielder: High-speed assassin.

comments.author: Konrad Linkowski
comments.text: I know it’s meant to be hard but I’ve got -2000 points and I don’t know if I parried correctly even once

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Parretty good game :D

I felt like the controls felt a little clunky to the game though

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
On my laptop, the game had a scrollbar because it wouldn’t fit on the screen, and this seemed to cause the sword to be about 2 inches above my cursor.

On my desktop, the game ran at double speed (120hz monitor), and was impossible. So it ended up mostly unplayable for me.

comments.author: Elliot Nelson
comments.text:
Definitely theme-inspired, and an interesting gameplay concept.

I found all but the first few levels to be pretty much impossible, as you only have a split second to move the mouse. Maybe upcoming (next) attacks could have a fading in outline, so the player can plan their next steps if they are paying attention; or, a successful parry should slow down the next attack, so there’s a reward for managing to parry.

comments.author: Justin Sullard
comments.text:
This is a very interesting idea, and I could see many more levels of this keeping me entertained through the gambit. I loved the dual wielder, and was almost expecting to come across some that weren’t parallel.

I even see potential for a multiplayer game with a sort of “Attack”/“Parry” multi-step round where you setup attacks for your opponent to Parry while they do the same for you, then you get to Parry each other’s sequence. Just an idea I had while playing, how to play with others.

I did want a bit more precision in my ability to set the direction, like perhaps click and drag to set hilt and tip, or maybe a big/little swing like Q/E for big swing and A/D for small adjustments or something, but I’m also not very good at arcade style games.

comments.author: Alpine Games
comments.text: Tough but fun game. Simple and easy to learn, not to master.

comments.author: Emre Guneyler
comments.text: not bad, osu vibes, could be more innovation.

comments.author: John Swana
comments.text: Simplistic and good game anyway like the sfx

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • Unique concept of trying to parry only instead of attack
  • Game description made me chuckle
  • The visuals on the swords measuring attack time and parry time was clever
  • Getting extra points for correct rotations was a nice touch

Feedback:

  • It’s a very difficult game. I could never stay in the positive because the swords spawn so fast and there isn’t enough time to hover over them for half a second each before they attack
  • A lot of levels feel overwhelming based on how many swords spawn at a time and how fast they spawn in

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Looks like rythm or reaction game, but maybe I’m too old for all this sh*t I can’t hit any enemies at all, maybe 5%) In general it’s not bad, interesting gameplay, but too complicated as for me.

comments.author: ch!k0
comments.text: i laughed throughout the game . it was very amusing and i found the score going beyond -100 very funny lol. though i didn’t expect the rotation to have a 180 deg limit. it was very interesting!

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: I couldn’t read any texts in the fantasy font, as the default font for me was something really unreadable, so I probably missed something from your game.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: Graphics are basic but the game concept is original and the game play is really fun! I liked the diversity of the enemies but sometimes it seems too difficult to beat them.

comments.author: Sebastian Dorn
comments.text:
I had to set the monitor refresh rate down to 60 Hz and use Chromium instead of Firefox for it to be winnable. Otherwise the game was running too fast.

It would be nice if the sword could be rotated all the way.

I liked having different enemy styles, it adds variety to it.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: simple and fun, a few more aracde FX and juice would really bring the game to life!

comments.author: Chris Glover
comments.text: I did not have the reactions to get through even the first level with a positive score. Would have been nice to have some wind up animations or something so I could get in position. I feel like this could benefit from having really simple patterns early on, then start combining them to form more complex ones later, like DDR with swords

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A fun yet simple game.

I liked the sound effects. The game concept was good.

You need to be pretty quick with the mouse to get a reasonable score.

It was good to have some variety between the enemies.

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Hey there!

What an incredible concept!

I found it super interesting and got quite excited to beat my score every time. I like the fact that there’s no “losing” just a bad score, so you keep going and don’t feel like you have to stop.

Try thinking of those who don’t have a qwerty keyboard (yes there are!) maybe adding support for arrows would have been nice.

I would have maybe added a level 0 - tutorial, where you can actually take the time to place your sword and understand how to rotate it.

Otherwise, sick job!

comments.author: Cameron Early
comments.text: I thought the idea of rotating the sword to parry was an interesting concept and enjoyed playing through the battles! Some music to accompany it would’ve been a nice touch (maybe in time with the parrying somehow)

comments.author: Frahaan Hussain
comments.text: Nice twist, liked that you could choose sound on or off. Needs more levels and variety.

comments.author: Ania Kubów
comments.text:
I am a big fan of minimal looking games that are anything but minimal! Parry is a visually appealing game that is relatively quick to grasp after playing a few times. Whilst quick to grasp, it is not easy - making it very addictive.

It would be great to see a skippable game play tutorial incorporated into the game play as the first round.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: Cool concept, it reminds me of that VR game, Until You Fall — the parry works pretty much the same there. This game mechanic has a lot of potential

api.name: REVENGE
api.description:
Controls

  • W for Jump
  • A D for Move
  • Press left button to use left hand to attack
  • Press right button to use right hand to attack
  • Release left button to take back left hand
  • Release right button to take back right hand

Tips

  • You can earn money in the TATAKAI section by defeating the enemy
  • You can use the money in SMITHY to forge weapons or armors
  • The more money you spent in SMITHY, you can get more powerful outfits
  • You can change your outfits in OUTFIT
  • You only can take the outfits totally weight to 1000
  • Weapons have properties such as weight and length. They decide the attack rate and the amount of the damage. More heavier weapon can knock back the ligther weapon. Shield can knock back weapon
  • Armors can protect you from being damage
  • Each part have a separate health, they decide the performance of your sprite.

If you win the game in REVENGE, you finish the game. GL!HF!

comments.author: xem

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

  • My first impression and understanding (before playing the game, only reading the description, screenshot and title):
    • Mysterious cover art! I think I’m in for a side scroller with fights and cool movements? The description fed into my idea, but I might be in for some more deeper fighter?
  • Great work on:
    • Wow! I’m impressed on the amount of things you put into the game and the systems that are in place.
  • I liked that you:
    • Thought about all the systems and tried to put something together that is quite different.
  • I think some small improvements could make much more justice to the effort you put in the game (again, I’m aware you might already know all the below):
    • Dying and getting sent back to the initial screen is quite though. I went through it three times. I didn’t have any learning changes from the deaths with the duel and the amount of time I had to give to try again really made it unlikely I was going to win the duel.
    • The UI needs a bit of organisation. At least for me it had scattered information that took me some time to scan and understand what it was about.
  • I made some screenshots while I played it https://twitter.com/javascripl/status/1708970143659810912

api.name: Of All Trades
api.description: Of All Trades is a game about information sharing in the 13th century. Today we don’t think about how easy and ubiquitous skill sharing is due books, internet, etc. The game’s goal is to show how difficult it would have been to find and apprentice under the masters of various trades at the time. Instructions: Click on the isometric image to move around. When your character is aligned directly with that of the next tradesperson in your apprenticeship list, you automatically learn that skill. Some skills let you traverse different terrains. When you complete the entire list of trades and meet the lord of the lands, you win the game. There are 25 fiefdoms in a grid and each fiefdom is 200 x 200 blocks. Tradespeople will be out wandering the countryside but generally you will find them in settlements (homesteads, farms, villages, towns, and the main city).

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Very interesting game in terms of the concept, the gameplay and graphics (I liked the style).

It was a bit hard to understand how to move around at first but once you understand it, it’s less of a problem.

The game has a very board-game feel to it (which I mark as a positive!)

I got a bug with one of the trademen - I couldn’t train with them :(

EDIT: I came back to this game and figured it out, there was no bug - it just wasn’t obvious that you had to learn the trades in the correct order! I was luck in the first few that ended up being the right order.

I wish the game has a some feedback sounds or some tunes.

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: Sorry, your game just sat at “loading” and my browser kept reporting that it was slowing everything down. Nothing happened and I couldn’t even open a dev tools window to see what was happening.

comments.author: Brian Hambley
comments.text: I’d like to put more time into this and complete. Quite adictive once you get going.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Ok, this was actually fascinating! I really like the concept, and the way it ties into the theme is unique and really thoughtful. I made it up to miner in the list before I bowed out.

I like the way it looked in general, but I think you could have had a larger display. I was squinting at the map, and the apprenticeship list was still mostly scrolled off screen at my resolution. A larger map, and the list to the side would have helped!

The inputs are also hard to learn initially, the transparent overlays don’t immediately make sense, and the graphics don’t make it clear that you’ve got 8 way movement on a grid. At first I thought they might be hexes! I’d suggest the direction buttons should be always visible, should be the same size in a circle around the map, and I’d have greatly appreciated keyboard input too.

comments.author: xem
comments.text: seems cool but doesn’t start for me too

comments.author: Vivian Easley
comments.text: I would be extremely curious to know what system you were on and browser. I haven’t been able to recreate this across any browser. Thanks for trying and sorry it didn’t work for you!

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • Very unique concept and game play
  • I liked that some of the trades gave you abilities, like the Shipwright letting you cross water

Feedback:

  • I didn’t find the minimap in the corner very helpful. To me it just showed a brown square with the red square in the center the entire time. I wish it would let me see the location of towns or something
  • There wasn’t a good way to know who I was looking for when I approached people. I would just have to click on everyone to find the one person I wanted
  • It took awhile before I realized that the list had to be done in order
  • Controls weren’t obvious at first. I also didn’t know which character I was at first so was even more confused as I tried to move around

comments.author: John Swana
comments.text: Good nice game i think the interface needs some clean up, simplicity

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Well, a little complicated game, UX is not so great Looks like you need a pen and paper for checking all info and wondering. Also you need more clear information about position and directions I think. But the idea is bold

comments.author: Vivian Easley
comments.text: I ran out of time but the original goal had been to include a second map of the actual current fiefdom instead of just coordinates. That might have helped what you were snagging on in regards to directions. Thanks for the feedback!

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I spent time to understand how to play this game, it was not so obvious. I love the concept, the graphics and all the details of this word! It is not a casual game: one probably needs lot of time to complete it. I would have loved a cool/slow music background.

comments.author: Grav
comments.text: What a great game. It contains a lot of interesting features actually: biomes, 3D topography, detailed descriptions of each trade person. The use of emojis here is very clever and not distracting. The game loop is essentially navigating around the terrain to reach the next village, which might have become boring if it wasn’t broken up by all the interesting items/npc type things that gave you a bit of flavor text. Some music and maybe keyboard controls would really elevate it. I finished the game, but the text at the end left me wondering if I missed something. Is there an Easter egg hidden somewhere?

comments.author: Vivian Easley
comments.text: There is an easter egg/alternate ending! Great deduction! Yeah, essentially ran out of time for music and controls but really glad you enjoyed it :-)

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
The graphics are neat, the map is huge! Took me some exploring to start finding people and to get the the waters edge.

A little tricky to line yourself up with the mobs.

took me a little while to work out the controls but once I had was easy to move about. Some help finding the first person would be useful so players know what they have to do next.

Feel I need to come back and try this again to give it a lot more time.

comments.author: Vivian Easley
comments.text: Thank you for playing and the feedback! The game is always supposed to start with a person very close to you that gives you your first directions. That may have been a bug if they weren’t there. Sorry about that.

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text:
Really cool concept and a unique implementation of the theme! Executed in a really neat way as well. Makes for a very interesting experience!

Exploring the world and looking around for craftspeople to apprentice under is really cool. However, lining up with a person to talk to them when you’ve found them can be quite difficult. There were times where I found someone but they always seemed one block away from me, which made progressing quite difficult. The list of people to meet also takes up quite a large part of the screen that could’ve been used to make the map larger and easier to interact with.

However, all in all, a really unique idea made into a really unique game! Great job!

comments.author: Taufik Sani
comments.text:
I would’ve loved to have audio in the game as it would have immersed the player in the game, especially when you’re travelling around the blocks.

In terms of game innovation, its pretty special as you don’t have a lot of free roaming games on JS, not to mention within a 13kb build. It is a huge map, and there is potential to expand this further.

Might be also interesting to implement a save game, as gamers would be able to revisit from previous progress. Seems challenging to finish the game in one go.

Control wise, if we were able to control the player by using keyboard and see the character walk from one block to another.

Graphics - some animation of the player roaming around would’ve been a nice touch.

comments.author: Jedidiah Weller
comments.text:
Hi Vivian!

First off - I REALLY enjoyed the concept of this game. You make a good point about the ease of knowledge in which we have in today’s era, and I found this game concept to be super thought provoking. Bonus points for originality.

Here’s my feedback!

  1. Good work on both the isometric design and fog of war. Isometric design is more difficult than regular 2d, so bonus points here for taking that leap successfully!
  2. Good job putting a mini-map in; however, I felt that the mini-map wasn’t very useful. I’m not sure if it was a bug or just not finished implementation; however I didn’t find it changing in any intuitive manner. A mini-map showing places that were discovered would be helpful; although you’d want to redesign I’d suggest
  3. I’d suggest improving the documentation and layout of the tradesmen list - it wasn’t apparent that you have to discover them in order at first, and I saw other comments say the same thing
  4. Music would have been a nice addition to the application
  5. Really enjoyed the small details of the characters and their ‘personality’, such as the woodcutter missing fingers and the birds chirping. These were all nice touches, well done!
  6. I’d suggest making this a responsive game where the list is on the left-hand side and the map and gameplay is on the right hand side.
  7. You can enlarge the display of the map & sprites without showing more of the map; and this would likely be a lot easier for many folks to read and enjoy.
  8. I’d suggest moving the text and dropdowns outside of the apprentice list, and instead make it a separate ‘interaction window’. 9. That way the list is their goals, and the interactions have their own section of the UI. 10.Great work on folder structure for your application!
  9. Include eslint, prettier, and stylelint into your repo so there’s standardization.
  10. Bonus points for adding a picture and todo to the README 🙂

api.name: Knights of Acre
api.description:
You must stop the caravan from reaching Acre. You must kill all knights and the Caravan, but you only have 20 arrows, and a bunch of knights.

Use WSAD to move your knight. Aim by holding right mouse button, shoot with left mouse. You can switch knights using Space.

I runned out of space too soon so I had to cut content to fit the limit and the gameplay suffered (I even had to chop one arm of the knights!). I hope you enjoy it.

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
The graphics are of course brilliant, all the animations are especially impressive. As you say yourself in the description, the gameplay suffers a bit.

While the slowdown while aiming is cool, it actually make the game harder, at least if you’re moving while shooting and/or shooting at a moving target. Since the arrow takes time to travel, you have to be able to gauge your motion and the enemies so you can lead them or whatever. With time slowed down, this is basically impossible.

I think just much faster moving arrow, and/or slower moving enemies and no slowdown on aim would solve it. Or, just adjust the arrows momentum based on the fact that time is slowed down? Not sure.

But really just a couple tweaks and you’d still have a really nice game. Even as it stands it’s very impressive graphically and could be a foundation for something really great!

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: Thanks for your comments. I tryed faster arrows but then you couldnt dodge them, but yes, there is a problem with the arrows, mostly that they get the speed of the horse when shoot, which leads to strange movement hard to predict. I tryed without it but then you could shoot an arrow and it just stayed behind. Anyway, lots of space for improvement but thanks for your time.

comments.author: Chris Glover
comments.text: Bit tough to play with a track pad, but very impressive graphically

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text:
totally missed that some people use trackpad, in the postcompo I will add a key to replace RMB.

Thanks :)

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Graphics look amazing and worked really well. The animations were great too. The 3D was impressive.

Couldn’t find the caravan? Rode out in the wastelands for what seemed like ages.

I wasn’t able to get the arrows to shoot very well, and when holding down the keyboard to move, the mouse didn’t work.

comments.author: Thiago Romão Barcala
comments.text:
The game mechanics are very entertaining! It is hard to get good at aiming, but it is still fun to try.

At first I didn’t see the enemy knights at the horizon, so I thought my friends were my enemies, and I had to prevent them from reaching the place where they were going to (in the next attempt I realized they were just following me). After killing all my friends I was at some beach without anything else around. In the second attempt I saw the enemies, and later on the castle.

I wish there was more content, but I had fun!

comments.author: Brian Hambley
comments.text: Hard to tell which way to go and couldnt fire 2nd arrow, presumably the first one has to finish but took an age at times. I killed all the enemies and conquered acre but the caravan kept shooting and killed me and got game over? Looks great. love to see an unrestricted more completed version.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Absolutely fantastic graphics, this one grabbed me from the get go. Heck, the graphics are so nice, I thought the thumbnail was an 80s style marketing exaggeration, imagine my surprise!

Unfortunately the game itself was unplayable for me. I couldn’t for the life of me work out how to hit anything, my arrows seemed to fly off in random directions, and while the HUD seemed to indicate I had a quiver of arrow, every mouse click appeared to empty it out, so I got to fire once every few seconds. Sadly gave up after a few minutes.

Such a great first step though, I hope you build on top of these techniques next year!

comments.author: John Swana
comments.text: Works just as advertised good game

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Well, this 3d style is impressive, castle and knights as well Gameplay is a little weird, but in general it’s really about the caravan, guard and raiders. Bow physics is strange, so I got my hits only then I fired on straigh line. Also didn’t find how to stop caravan after all enemies death. But great job, really impressive. It’s sad about arm)

comments.author: Steven Lambert

Well done:

Feedback:

comments.author: Crystal Parker

games: Journey to the East; Silk Road Adventure

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: yeah, it is too hard. But remember that presseing SPACE you can switch to other riders!

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Unfortunately, I wasn’t able to move properly: I have “search as you type” enabled in my browser, and you don’t call .preventDefault() on the key events. So when I try to move, the search opens for me.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: I will take note!! wasnt aware of that problem as it didnt occur to me.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Looks really good, animations and graphics are quite impressive.

The controls are quite difficult and shooting was not easy.

Always impressive to see 3D in JS13k!

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: thanks a lot, yeah, making 3D is 13KBs was hard but fun too!

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text: First, sorry that you have to cut one arm of the knights, but it also makes the game unique! The gameplay and theme are impressive, especially considering the 3D graphics, camera angles, and animations, all within a compact 13 kb size! The controls for shooting are a bit challenging, particularly with the slowing down effect. It makes it harder to move and shoot precisely, and there’s a slight delay in seeing the results of a shot, which can feel a little bit disruptive to the gaming experience. Overall, really cool game!

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: thanks for your comments!! Yeah, there are some problems with the arrows path that make the game hard, I will work on fixing it in the post-compo version.

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text:
Technically this is pretty impressive. Really cool that you were able to put this in 13 KB. As you wrote the gameplay needs some improvements. Here are the things I would improve first:

  • The mouse sensitivity felt a bit high for me. Maybe it is because of my settings. Maybe a setting for the sensitivity would be nice.
  • The time it takes to charge up the arrow is really long, I would shorten it.
  • Not sure if the aiming mechanic is really needed… Maybe it works also without it, so that you simply always aim from the main view (without going in the “aming mode” when pressing right mouse button).
  • Some sound effects and music would have been great.

Anyway, congrats to this very cool entry!

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text:
Thanks for your comments. Yeah, mouse sensitivity is a problem I tryed tweaking the value many times but something is odd, not sure why. I though about reducing the arrow time but then you will get out of arrows too soon, and without a way to replenish them it was a problem. I couldnt add auto-aim as it will require some extra maths that take too much space. Really sad that I didnt have space for audio/music, I had many ideas thought.

Anyway, thanks a lot!

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

  • My first impression and understanding (before playing the game, only reading the description, screenshot and title):
    • At first I thought Acre was a Brazilian state :)
    • I don’t know what to expect from this game other than the cover art seems to me an adventure style game. The description gave me some points that I should expect action.
  • Great work on:
    • Having a complete working game!
    • 3D! It’s always a great feat to have a 3d game into 13kb!
    • Have a core loop in place. Aim, shoot, ride.
  • I liked that you:
    • Added the slow-mo to the aiming! With it it was already quite hard to hit anything, I couldn’t imagine without it.
    • Added the option to switch between knights, it gave some feeling of overseer of the battle, not an individual.
    • Split between movement and aiming. Aiming and shooting to the side was really cool, it felt I was doing a really hard thing.
  • You could improve (again, I’m aware you might already know all the below):
    • It was pretty much impossible to hit things without getting dangerously close. It was almost kamikaze attacks to take down the enemies. I think what was happening was that the physics of the arrow were not matching the slow-motion velocities, but the real-time ones. Because of that, arrows that I thought would go straighter than they were actually going were missing all my targets. Compensating for that was really tricky and I failed at doing so.
    • I liked the movement of the horses, but they felt a bit more like cars than actual beings. I’d expect horses to be able to take some sharper turns.
  • I took some screenshots https://twitter.com/javascripl/status/1709674007191052732

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: wow, thanks for taking so much time to write to me. You are right that in the cover art it looks more adventure, I wanted to make it more about exploring places but… size. I had to stick to a very simple core-loop or risk reaching the date without a proper game, Im not very fond of the way it ended but anyway. I feel all your points are valid. Thanks again Victor for your review and all the screenshots in twitter! super fun reading them.

api.name: Cathedral builder
api.description: 13th century, golden age of cathedrals… Will you be able to build a cathedral in less than 100 years? Dig up rocks… cut stones… and build… to see your cathedral growing progressively.

comments.author: Ariel Wexler
comments.text: REALLY well made clicker-style game. The graphics and interactions were spot-on and the music had me vibing. Only critique is that the bouncing-around bonuses were unpleasant to click / not-always-responsive.

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Nice clicker game, definitely one that got me glued until I finished.

I felt like the auto-clicking should’ve been permanent, and you’d just upgrade it though.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: To me, incremental games / clickers are these chill experiences where you do things at your own pace, so playing the decisively un-chill clicker sure was interesting for a change.

comments.author: burntcustard
comments.text: An interesting clicky game. The music is good, but as far as I could tell there were no other sound effects? I attempted it 3 times but didn’t quite manage to build the cathedral within the 100 years. The floaty bonus buttons that moved around were a little tricky to click - I managed it, but I think slower or more wiggly movement would have been less awkward feeling.

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text: I guess this game falls into the cookie clicker genre. The game is well made: I liked the graphics and animation, audio is nice. Mobile version requires a bit more polish work: would be nice to get rid of scroll bars and to increase “upgrade” button size.

comments.author: Alexey Kalmakov
comments.text: Finally, won! I really need to know how you made this nice cathedral building animation. Amazing animation work!

comments.author: John Swana
comments.text: Nice sounds and concept i would like to play a post compo version of this

comments.author: Crystal Parker
comments.text: I enjoyed it, it had replayability because you didn’t automatically beat it the first time.
games: Journey to the East; Silk Road Adventure

comments.author: Jack Le Hamster
comments.text: Doesn’t seem too different from the average cookie clicker, yet I got addicted to seeing the bricks flying into the cathedral and kept trying to finish it in 100 years. Still haven’t managed to do it! This thing keeps going forever! The voxel cathedral looks really nice, seems like it would have taken a lot of time to design it.

comments.author: Christian Staudte
comments.text: A great clicker game, I really love the graphics and how the cathedral gets bigger and bigger over time. It is just such a cool idea!

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Interesting clicker variation, I tried several times but didn’t finish this cathedral. Graphics is minimalistic, but really interesting, especially cathedral Maybe it’s more logical to build it with horizontal and not vertical layers)

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • I loved that the cathedral actually gets built as you play. Amazing job with the visuals
  • Music track was well done

Feedback:

  • Bouncing bonuses were hard to click on sometimes
  • I was surprised that the auto-clicker was not permanent and that I had to continue to reenable it. This goes against most styles of auto-clicking upgrades so it’s an interesting twist to the idea but also unconventional one that makes it a bit jarring
  • I would have liked a different visual for the auto-clicker button between “unable to afford” vs “cannot buy because auto-clicker is already on.” Many times the auto clicker would stop working but then the button looked like it did when it was turned on. The first few times it happened I thought something broke and didn’t realize that I just couldn’t afford it. It also hurts scanning the buttons to see when the auto clicker runs out.

comments.author: Ben Fox
comments.text: Harder than I expected, but it was a good challenge. Really loved the incremental block-building effect on the Cathedral.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: A classic very nice fited in the theme. I loved the representation of the cathedral.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Loved the build animations and design! A bit too much clicking but built most of the cathedral.

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: A simple game, but I really like it!

comments.author: Francesco Bedussi
comments.text: My two kids really loved this. I like the graphics and the soundtrack, but I don’t like the clicker games

comments.author: ch!k0
comments.text: hands down, this was so well made and the detail to stones piling up forming a building/structure was amazing! i love how you can autoclick the features and upgrade the increments. the music was calming as well. but, the timer would have been better if it was counting down instead of up, by then the player would know how much time is left. great game!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
I liked it - congratulations!

The only thing that was confusing was that when the game ended, regardless of whether you won or lost, you could not see your construction result under a pop-up window, it would be good to process it somehow to allow you to enjoy your project :).

comments.author: Ania Kubów
comments.text: Alot of great aspects to this game! The color palette, visuals and music greatly complimented each other. I also enjoyed the fact that a tutorial on how to play was built into the game play. This really minimised the need for reading any big chunk of text before getting started. Very impressed with how the buildStones stacked over each other to create the final Cathedral. Nice work!

comments.author: Aurélio A. Heckert
comments.text:
Nice, but I only win after running the lines below on the browser console:

game.variables.year = 1000
game.variables.oc = 99999
         :-)

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

  • My first impression and understanding (before playing the game):
    • The screenshot is very interesting taking together with the name. It implies it will be a cool one.
    • The description sells it as being really open ended
  • Great work on:
    • Having a complete working game! Also with some additional polishing!
    • Core loop! I got actively engaged in making my cathedral under 100 years!
    • Pacing! The game has a clear start, mid game and end game strategies. I really liked the “abandon all the activities and focus on getting the last stones out” ending. It took me three runs to shape that out correctly.
  • I liked that you:
    • Had the (3d?) model of the cathedral actually building through the game, it was a great way to show progress!
    • Had this small twist with the expiring auto clicker, it went from mindless clicking to actual strategy! On the first and second run I was just clicking through all the available updates and auto clickers until it became too expensive for me to move on, eventually failing. Only when I realised that I needed to time correctly what would be on auto clicker and what I’d need to sweat my clicking to keep going. Really good job in adding this layer of learning here!
  • You could improve:
    • I get it might be intentional, but I was not sure the floating bonuses during the game after levelling up were positive net for me to take. I’m already busy focused on keeping everything working on the left, I hardly have attention left to pick a small floating button flying away randomly. Maybe it could just be automatically added? It could give the “uff, a small help after levelling up” moment.
  • I took some screenshots here https://twitter.com/javascripl/status/1709316359102898495

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: interesting game graphics and animations but the flow of clicking itself is not very attracting, cool game keep it up.

api.name: Marco...?
api.description:
Marco Polo has stopped by for lunch. Unfortunately he is lost inside your massive castle. Your job is to find him.

Select the X on the floor to move around. Select * in the UI for audio clues. Select the green doors to reveal where Marco Polo is.

Turn up the volume and listen carefully. And don’t enter the dungeon…

VR headset with stereo sound required. VR controller required or optional hand-tracking. Standing pose or sitting, with 360 degree rotation required. Not suited for mobile devices.

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text: Simple game, nice castle. Nice idea with the positional audio, although I’m wondering if it was correctly implemented (distance does not seem to give a lower volume).

comments.author: John Swana
comments.text: :/ VR gear not found anyway you would make it work regardless. good game

comments.author: Teddy Chen
comments.text: The concept of the ‘Marco Polo’ game in VR is fantastic. Would love to see a 3D model of Marco Polo pop out of the door when you find him. I think having a more complex environment to explore would be cool, such as multiple stories.

comments.author: Sébastien Vandenberghe
comments.text:
I love it of different this game is from most of the others !!! It is a really awesome idea to be audio driven.

I think the gameplay could be greatly improved by a richer environment to walk in and some additional trap doors :-)

Spatial audio is really the key differentiator here so again congrats.

comments.author: Fernando Serrano Garcia
comments.text: Very original to use spatial sound for gameplay! I would have appreciated a maximum number of attempts to win or perhaps having the sound change position if we don’t succeed within a certain time frame

api.name: Fort Knight
api.description:
• WASD / Arrows to move
  • Attack with Spacebar or Left Mouse Button (Hold to charge up)
  • Change weapon with number keys 1 to 4 or click the icons
  • Block with Shift or Right Mouse Button

Break tree stumps, skulls, graves and rocks for HP and upgrades.

Break all of the resources to stop mobs spawning, defeat the mobs and then cross the bridge to the next stage! Defeat 10 stages to rid the land of Skellys and Goblins!

comments.author: Justin Sullard
comments.text: This game LOOKS amazing, it sounds awesome, but I just can’t get past the goblins no matter what I do. Blocking only gets me so far, and they just freaking keep coming, getting me stuck in the water, or other things. I never got past Stage 4 after a dozen play throughs, and I feel like there’s got to be something I’m missing, because this is an otherwise awesome game. Maybe I’m just too slow.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
The goblins are there to make breaking items a pain, if you have your sword (1) or hands (4) equiped then hold shift and the shield blocks all the spears.

The key tactic is keep moving while you break the trees and rocks, dont let skelly’s touch you and keep the sheild out.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
I love the look and the sound, reminds me of the good old Amiga-days. Gamplay is nice too, the switching of weapon/tools by the number-keys is a bit awkward. At least when playing with the mouse (would be better using the mousewheel, as the icons did not work for me.)

One time, i think i clicked outside of thze game-area, the knight just began running to the left, and did not stop again… hm,

Nevertheless a really good game, thx for writing it!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: There are a few bugs, a lot of time went into features and animations and I then lost time on testing.

comments.author: Thiago Romão Barcala
comments.text:
I like the fighting mechanics, the graphics and the music are good, and the in game tutorial helps getting used with the game.

The levels felt a bit repetitive though. In the first attempt of finishing the game I didn’t get very far. Then in the second attempt I realized that it was probably a better idea to keep killing enemies to improve my skill instead of directly clearing the resources. Then I realized that if block constantly the goblins’ spears can never hit me, so it became a bit too easy. I’d go to the next level, clear the stones, clear the trees, then switch to the sword, keep block constantly, kill the skeletons, and then the goblins are easy prey.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: With more time and bytes Id like to have added a cool down or some sort of mana for the shield so it was not over powered. You kind of found the best tactic, i agree if I had cut back a little in some areas I could have added more gameplay and variation.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Loved the look of this, really dig the iso perspective. Together with the audio, this gave me 80s Amiga vibes.

The design was engaging, I like the dilemma between wanting to clear the resources and keeping the enemies down. The actual combat felt mushy though, I was never sure if I’d hit someone, and after a while something appeared to go out of sync with the animations and my guy stopped really swinging his sword at all, even though I appeared to be damaging enemies.

Great stuff in here though, with some more polish to tighten it up, this would be a top contender.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback, the combat needs improving if this is to be further developed.

comments.author: Alexey Kalmakov
comments.text: Very nice isometric graphics and animations, love how level got loaded. I was very hard already from third level… don’t know why. It would be very nice if reflected arrow (spear? nice effect tho) could hit skeletons.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: The arrows can hit the skeletons but the hit boxes and comabt need improving, thanks for the feedback.

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Very impressive work, congrats man

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Thank you, I am happy with what I achieved and also a little dissapointed with how I submitted, lots of gameplay aspects feel unfinished.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • Looks amazing!
  • Music track is fantastic. Goes really well with the game
  • Putting on a skull was a fun find

Feedback:

  • When introducing each item it wasn’t clear that the text was the hotkey to use it and not the number of times I needed to use it. Having the number hotkey under the item’s icon would have help
  • I forgot shield key by the time I needed it, could use a reminder in the text what it was
  • It wasn’t exactly clear what was needed to clear each stage. I was stuck on stage 3 killing enemies and bridge never came. Took me awhile to think about destroying the tree to move on
  • I kept dying in stage 4 to constant knockback and getting pushed off the edge of the map
  • I couldn’t attack enemies from above / below, which lead to the previous problem of getting constant knockback from the skeletons when they were below me
  • Trying to hold the shield button while simultaneously trying to attack was very tiring. Would have liked a toggle for the shield or something

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Thank you for such detailed feedback, I had to stop dev 3 days before the end and should have planned for this and asked for some play testing; some of these issues I was aware of and had to accept others I just overlooked!

Some replies to your comnents: Tutorials were quickly added and I accept your point 100%, it is not all clear, when I realised time was short I should have scaled back and simplified things.

A known issue with the knockback but I had to accept it.

Attack directions: the whole combat needs some thought, it works but could be so much better!

The sheild should have had a cool down and a toggle sounds sensible, you can use the mouse buttons to attack and shield up.

Again I appreacite the comments!

comments.author: Brian Hambley
comments.text: Graphics, Music perfect. The tune particularly I loved as are the graphics and also very clever implementation. Got stuck on about level 4, with seemingly infinite baddies spawning. Be nice to see this with a less combat approach? Would be a a great adventure/exploration game.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Thank you, that is an interesting comment, the combat needs improving or as you say removing. Will have a think about this as I would like to rewrite the game.

comments.author: Umz
comments.text: Loved the animation of the water especially, and the transitions from start and end of levels. This is well made, alot of fun to play. Really enjoyed it.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Thank you, lots to work on if I come back to this post jam but there is something there worth exploring I think.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
Very nice map, and the music is stylistically just perfect, such old RPG vibes.

Goblins are shamelessly OP! They really wouldn’t let me steal their trees and rocks.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Thank you, there are some features and ideas in here which could work if I fixed all the short comings.

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Great Job Interesting concept, good graphics. Gameplay is a little not obvious, but in some way unique combination. Worst thinkg is direction of attack and attack hit zones. Sometimes really hard to undertsand, when you hit enemy, when he can hit you etc.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Thank you, I agree, the combat needs the most work to make this more enjoyable to play.

comments.author: John Swana
comments.text: Good, soothing music and characters.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: Very good game, I really enjoyed playing. The graphics are amazing, the iso perspective is perfect, the waves are so fun and the music is awesome! Great job!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Thank you, glad you enjoyed it :D

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: such amazing graphics! love how it starts a new level by tweening the tiles. I wish you could click on weapon icons to switch, to avoid having to press 1,2,3. I also wish I could attack up and down as well as side-to-side. I noticed faint black lines at the bottom of many tiles do to a rounding error / floating point imprecision. to remove that glitch could you snap level tile sprites to integer positions when drawing them? All in all, an incredibly impressive js13k entry and fabulous work!!!!!!!!!!!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Thank you for the feedback, you can click on the weapons but the hitboxes may not be great, I think mouse wheel would have really helped for players to change.

The combat as I have noted in a few comments is where the most improvement could be made.

The black lines are interesting, I see them on Windows but not on the Mac I developed on, could have been a simple fix too!

Thanks for the positvies and helpful advice :D

comments.author: Miguel Ángel Pérez Martínez
comments.text: Looks very very very cool! The enemies can be too difficult at a times but everything else is great!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Thank you, lots of positives to take from this game but also lots of issues which need to be considered next time around!

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: Awesome game, the difficulty ramped really fast with two skeletons in one stage and 2 skeletons and 4 archers at once in the next - only one archer needs to be introduced at first - maybe I was quick in the previous stages so archers did not had time to spawn? When they were hiding in the castle it was almost impossible for me to kill them. I didn’t like the bullet-arrows that much. If the arrows had a range there would have been some safe ground in the rear. I think the castle itself should be an obstacle that you cannot walk through and if the archers could mount in to shoot from the towers, then leave to chase when you’re away it could provide some more strategy.. I know that’s too much for a 13kb game but is something to consider - in my opinion the axe and the hammer are unnecessary and the resource could be just treasure chests.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Thank you, the mob movement and the castle position is a problem with the design for sure, I should have allowed the castle to be destoryed, moved it to the side and had larger maps maybe, but yes needs some work!

Thanks for playing and taking the time to feed back :D

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Really enjoyed playing this, feels like a well polished game.

I enjoyed the pixel graphics, and the music, the different enemy types were good to add variety. The way the levels drew was pretty neat, and the waves worked well, I thought the isometric worked well, and I know how hard it is to get right so you did a good job on that.

I liked the concept of having little “islands” which you need to complete before moving on to the next one.

The water on the land slowed down your travel a lot which made it hard during battles sometimes as it didn’t seem to affect the enemy’s movements. Also I was able to walk off the map into darkness…

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Thanks, there are lots of gameplay bugs in here, as a concept its a good start maybe but I lacked time to fix all the things that affected the fun. Apprecite you taking the time to feed back :D

comments.author: Luis Gurmendez
comments.text:
I really like this entry, I saw all your iterations through slack and I have to say that it’s amazing great job!!

I love the art :)), and the water effect!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Thank you appreciate the feedback :D

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text:
Fun visuals, simple gameplay, there’s even sound & music. Very impressive scope for such a tiny amount of data.

(You might be aware of the seams between the tiles.)

The water waves are mesmerizing! Overall nice polish and very playable. Great work.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Thanks Matt, some float issues on Edge mostly it seems (“seams”), something to watch out for next time!

I may have aimed for a little too much scope but was a lot of fun to make.

comments.author: Gauthier Billot
comments.text: I really like the tiles animations. The title is also one of the best for this theme ;)

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Nice game, played to the end - congratulations!

I didn’t immediately understand that those zombies were generated until I knocked down all the stones and trees, I already sinned on the balance, but it turned out to be an interesting mechanic! It happened that I somehow missed a stone and wondered what it was… until I finally saw it, :). Quite a few laps were run… :D.

Despite the fact that it does not resonate with the theme of the contest - I like everything else, thanks for game!

comments.author: Grav
comments.text: Great one. I also got to finish it but it took me a few tries. The music is so good, sound effects, graphics. Awesome job.

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text:
Amazing game. Pretty impressed of what you were capable of putting in 13 KB. I really like the graphics, the music and the first few levels which are used to learn the mechanics. There is not much I can say where you could improve. Just some minor things:

  • I got once stuck between two stones and an enemy and could not free myself.
  • It seems that you walk faster left and right compared to up and down. Maybe it is because of the perspective, but it felt a bit strange to me.
  • For a game jam game, which is usually just played once for a few minutes, maybe the difficulty was a bit high after stage 4.

Very well done. Amazing entry!

comments.author: Jedidiah Weller
comments.text:
Hi Ryan,

Great work on this submission! I truly enjoyed the interface and interaction, here’s my comments and feedback:

  1. The music and sound effects were SPOT ON - good work on this! I really felt engaged and I even danced a little in my chair 🙂
  2. The movement was great as well, and I enjoyed the feel and interaction when chopping trees and other interactions
  3. In terms of UI, the selection is missing the hotkeys for 1,2, 3,4 etc to let the user know. I guessed it though
  4. Really good work on the water fluctuation - bravo there
  5. I would have liked to see the full source files and a more in depth README and license file to know how to run it. This would also allow me to check for best practices such as eslint, prettier, etc.
  6. I wasn’t sure what the point of hitting the tombstones / skeletal remains was, so might want to make that more clear or even make it a bonus?
  7. It appears like there’s a different font at the bottom and top hud - if it’s not, it might be a UI bug because it appears differently for me
  8. Also - bonus points for how responsive you are to everyone’s comments!

Overall it was a great submission, and I look forward to seeing what you do with it next. Hit me up on twitter @jedihacks for any discussion!

api.name: The Knighting of Sr Isaac
api.description:
The Knighting of Sr Isaac, is a simple random room generated roguelike game where you combine different weapons with unique attack patterns against enemies.

Prove yourself worthy of being called a knight and see how far you can go!!!

Game instructions

  • w/a/s/d to walk and v/b to attack on desktop.
  • Gamepad and atk buttons a/b for mobile.
  • Pick your starting weapon with v/b in the keyboard or press the buttons in mobile.
  • v/b or a/b allows you to pick a weapons to that respective hand, it also allows you to open doors.
  • Treasure room can only be opened if you have the key.
  • Clicking in the top right speaker or pressing m can mute the game sounds.
  • Enemies may drop their weapons after dead.

Weapons in game

Close melee

  • Fist: down atk
  • Shield: down-side atk, pushes enemies down
  • Axe: down-side atk
  • Morning Star: side atk
  • Hammer: top-side atk
  • Sword: top-side-down atk

Long range melee

  • Spear: down atk
  • Greatsword: 360 atk
  • Halberd: top atk

Range

  • Trowing Knives: down-side atk
  • Trowing Axe: side atk
  • Crossbow: top-side atk

Enemy Behaviours

  • Afraid: keeps himself at a distance from the player, if player gets close they run away.
  • Agressive: follows the player and attacks.
  • Defensive: gets close to player but keeps a distance to perform attacks.

comments.author: Alpine Games
comments.text: 13th century version of The Binding of Isaac! This game is super fun to play and the art and music are great. Enemies behaviors add a lot to the game and the the rooms are just different enough.

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text: Pretty cool game! Love how the player runs, and liked the graphics as well!

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
13th Century Binding of Isaac! :D

These characters look the cutest - I love the art style and the animation! It’s got the right amount of challenge.

I love how the different weapons change the gameplay style. The ones I prefer are defnitely not the ones my wife prefers!

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: The pathfinding is really bad and the bosses can be cheesed and killed with little to no effort. Kind if sad because this is otherwise a neat wee game.

comments.author: Justin Sullard
comments.text: This is a phenomenal entry. I will have to circle back to see if I can get good enough to take on a boss. I loved the sound especially, and the cadence of the music and the animation synced well. Excellent work.

comments.author: Brian Hambley
comments.text: Lovely. Really like the graphics style, love the bosses. Audio is great. Reminds me of Adventure on the Atari VCS for some reason. Managed a few levels. The enemies aint that bright and can be trapped, especially the bosses which I like and presume this was deliberate. Will continue.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: A nice and chill roguelike. Two gameplay features that stood out to me were the substantially different weapons, and the fact that the enemies would take damage from spikes. This has a lot of potential.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
That was fun! I’m impressed by how much is in here, down to a lovely minimap. Game looked great, sprites were lively and very clear.

I have to admit though, after 3 tries I still couldn’t beat the first boss! Even with a projectile weapon I still eventually just got clobbered :)

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Great entry, nice controls, graphics, gameplay and music.

The spikes are a little over powered, I used these to kill the huge looking bosses which felt like cheating!

Not sure I really killed 510 enemies, that stat may be counting the wrong stat.

Good Job!

comments.author: Thiago Romão Barcala
comments.text:
Cool animations, music and sound effects. I also liked the option to collect the different weapons. At first I thought shooting my axe in only one direction was too limiting, soon enough I realized that it makes the game more strategic.

I found that the range weapons work better for me, so even though I tried some of the melee weapons I always went back for my axe or knives.

The AI could receive some improvement, so that they avoid obstacles, especially the bosses, because I was able to kill 3 of them by just hiding behind a wall and then shooting my axe until they die.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • Graphics and animations look great
  • Love the Binding of Isaac reference
  • Movement felt solid
  • Minimap was helpful and useful

Feedback:

  • I couldn’t figure out how to aim. For example the throwing knife (I think) only ever was able to throw to the left-down direction instead of the way my character was moving. This made it extremely difficult to fight enemies as I would have to get to the right-up direction of them just to attack them
  • I couldn’t figure out the range of the melee weapons. Probably due to the previous point, the melee weapon only attacked in one direction so it was hard to figure out where I could hit an enemy from and how far away

comments.author: burntcustard
comments.text: My best was 2440! The ranged weapons definitely seemed better than the close melee, so my usual tactic was just to go to the top left of each area and spam throwing cigarettes er I mean knives at the bad guys. The angled nature of the different weapons felt a bit clunky, but the music and graphics and little wiggly animations were really nice!

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Great Job Really interesting game. Looks like usual roguelike/zeldalike but this one-side weapon idea is really awesome. Looks like there are some balance problems with several weapons - they looks useless, but in general it’s really fun game. Also would be interesting to adopt somehow to your enemies, maybe it’s possible by adding themes, for example “hell level” with bigger amount of devils attacking left/right, so you can adopt with spear. One of few games I played several times.

comments.author: Rémi Vansteelandt
comments.text:
What a cute dungeon crawler! I enjoyed it, I would only leave a few suggestions:

  • I found it very confusing that I couldn’t aim my weapon. Even enemies seemed to always aim at the same spot. Couldn’t figure out if it was a bug or intentional mechanic
  • game is a bit difficult, I felt like I could use a bit more initial health, especially since it’s so easy to run into spikes
  • speaking of spikes, I found that the most efficient strategy was to lure enemies into spikes. This didn’t feel like a legit mechanic, and more like an exploit, which made the game a bit less fun. Would have been nice if I could have pushed the enemies into the spikes by attacking them instead

Graphics, animation, music are top notch!

comments.author: John Swana
comments.text: I love the crispy pixel graphics and game mechanics

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: Very nice game, I especially liked the pixel art! I loved killing the enemies by attracking them to the spikes. Sometime the music get slower, I don’t know if it is a bug or a special effect.

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Unfortunately, I couldn’t properly move: I have “search as you type” enabled in my browser, and you don’t call .preventDefault() on key events, so when I try to move, the search bar opens for me.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Loved the pixel graphics and the two handed attack option. I liked the rooms linked by doors that only opened with enemies in that room were defeated - very Zelda like.

The music and sound effects worked well.

Goodness the boss is a bit hardcore.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
The music is very bright, a little calmer tones would be better for me. The controls almost did not work for me in terms of the description, the movement worked only on the arrows, attacks and sound could only be controlled with the mouse.

Interesting game, thanks and congratulations!

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text:
As the name implies, this is a 13kb version of the Binding of Isaac, and it has been done really well! You control the knight who can move around the different rooms, dispatching the baddies, hunting for a key to exit to the next level, or perhaps a new weapon to collect. You can carry two items, one per hand, so it’s up to you to decide which ones to juggle: perhaps a spear and a shield or two throwing axes? I really liked the fact that you had to be considerate about which weapon to take. For example, if you get the throwing knives in your left hand, they can only fire to the left! This means you need to carefully plan your attacks.

It’s graphically great, the controls are smooth and fluid, the animation cute, the effects fit perfectly, and there is plenty of fun to be had. This feels like a complete, well-rounded, and balanced game, and for that, it should be applauded.

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: really amazing work! the art was perfect, the action was so super smooth and I found exploring the world interesting and challenging! lol but why do you keep hitting yourself in the head? =) the attack animation looks more like you are beating up your own silly self. One of the very best js13k games I played this year. Excellent work!

comments.author: Jeremy Burns
comments.text: Great entry! The art style is really good, though it took me many injuries to stop parsing the spikes as flowers for some reason. Gameplay and animation are really smooth, and the difficulty curve is well designed. I like the variation in the color palette as you progress through the levels. It’s a bit tough to read when you’ll take damage from an enemy at close range with a melee weapon, and that makes the ranged weapons seem a bit OP in comparison. Overall really well done though, awesome work!

api.name: Samurai Sam
api.description:
Drag finger (or mouse) across the screen and slice objects in the same color as indicated in the top of the screen. The game is over if you cut the wrong color, or don’t cut the blazing arrows. Remember to slice all arrows.

Decentralized

I added a Web 3 menu where the player can opt-in for the decentralized challenge. When a player selects this option, the game will download the NEAR SDK and fetch metadata for 2 different swords, which are actual NFTs on the NEAR blockchain. The swords can be purchased and used in the game itself. The NFTs are part of a Paras.id collection. If you buy one, you own one and will see it in your NEAR wallet and on Paras.id. You can resell them or transfer them if you wish.

Known bugs

  • If sound doesn’t play, try to refresh the game, and click once (not drag) on the screen
  • If web3 doesn’t work, try to reload the page. If it doesn’t work at all on js13kgames’s site, try the exact same version of the game here

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
I always love a fruit-ninja style game.

I feel like the items should pop in bit more more frequently in the beginning but plays pretty well!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback. Yes, I also think it takes too long before the actual fun starts.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: I appreciate the clever SVG and Bezier utilities!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thank you very much, and especially for noticing the Bezier utilities.

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text: The rendering in the mobile becomes laggy, still the game play is good!!!

comments.author: John Edvard
