comments.text: Thanks for the feedback. The game’s performance isn’t great when there are too many items on screen. It’s an improvement point for later. Thanks for letting me know.

comments.author: Viljami Peltola
comments.text: Very playable! Could have gotten harder a bit faster. I loved the graphics and animation!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thank you very much. Yes I agree, the difficulty ramp is a bit slow.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text: This is a great take on Fruit Ninja. Really appreciate the clever cutting tech. It looked ok, and the audio matched nicely. It probably needed a little more of a speed/pattern ramp though: I played until ~2000 in score, but it didn’t seem to be changing much anymore so I called it a day.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
Thanks for the feedback. I had some trouble balancing the game properly. The color switching speed and the number of items being spawned will increase over time, but that’s about all I was able to do to increase difficulty.

Adding some more elements or add an extra color to the mix could help with making the game more interesting after 2000 points.

comments.author: Thiago Romão Barcala
comments.text:
Audio effects were spot on, and the music also. I liked the slicing effect. I bought one of the swords on NEAR and it made a cool light saber sound effect, and it appeared when I was slicing something, but I didn’t notice any other difference.

I think the start of the game could have been made as a tutorial that only shows for the first time, and that can be played again from the menu, because for the next attempts you want to get direct to the action, but you have to play the tutorial all over again.

Also, I thought it would be cool to be able to slice the long objects two times. I noticed that you can slice the arrowheads too, but I thought about slicing the same object in two movements would feel very samurai-ish.

One small thing I noticed with the sword I bought, not sure if it was intentional. When I started slicing, the sword blinked in the position I ended the previous movement before jumping to the place I started the new movement.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
Thank you for actually trying the web3 feature. I wasn’t sure if anyone would try it or not. I wanted to leave some burn marks or scratch marks on the surface after slicing with the NFT swords. And I also wanted to add some extra particle effects, like smoke, or sparks of fire.

The thing you noticed with the sword is a bug I wasn’t able to fix in time. It’s definitely not intentional.

Thanks for the comment about slicing the same objects several times. I was thinking about it at one point, but decided to leave it out of the implementation. I think it could be a cool feature to add, and now I wish I added it.

The idea to only show the tutorial once is also a good suggestion. It can get boring fast when the player has to start over with almost zero slice and dice action.

Thanks for the valuable feedback.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Really clever implementation of the slicing affect! I think you gamified it really well.

Nice background music and the arrows add some extra skill requirement to what is quite a chilled (satisfying) game.

Works well on PC and mobile!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thank you very much for the wonderful feedback.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • Fruit-ninja style games are always fun
  • The music and audio worked really well. It was nice that the music and color changes were synced
  • Arrows added a nice extra challenge

Feedback:

  • It would be nice if there was some sort of an indication in the play area (bottom half of the game) of when the color was about to change. A lot of times I would be focused on the game area and not able to look at the color indicator at the top of the screen and be about to slice or mid slice when the color would switch

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback. I’m glad you mention some improvements I could have made on the color switching. I tried changing the background as well as the indicator on the top, but I see that it’s still not sufficient enough to let the player know the color is about to switch.

comments.author: Alexander Sumarokov
comments.text: Wonderful game, the mobile adaptation is well done. The concept and gameplay are interesting.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thank you very much for the kind words!

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Good game, not a bad mechanics in general. Get some problems with chinese air lanterns - sometimes they move outside the screen. Also get some problems with color switch - sometimes you can get color change at the end of flying phase, so you now need to kill red parts, but they almost dissapear, so you don’t have time. In the same time you can’t hit them earlier - it’s still not their color. Good Job!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
Thanks for the feedback. Yes, there are some bugs in the game I wasn’t able to fix before the deadline.

I appreciate the comment about color change. I wasn’t sure if it worked well or not, but I see that timing the color switch with the waves would be better.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
This is a fun game, and looking into how you did it, the game is all the more impressive.

Reminiscent of fruit ninja type games, it plays pretty well on mobile, not so easy on laptop touchpad.

The graphics and swipe mechanic and sounds worked pretty well.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: I’m so happy for the kind words. I’ve never tried the game with touchpad, but I can imagine it’s not easy.

comments.author: Brian Douglas
comments.text:
Really well executed.

The slice animation is super satisfying and the music matches the samurai theme.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thank you very much, I appreciate the feedback

comments.author: John Swana
comments.text: This game appears to appeal more to mobile players, nice creation still.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Yes, that’s absolutely correct. Mobile first, desktop second. I think it plays best on an actual mobile device. Thanks for the feedback.

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: Could use difficulty/level select, so not to have to replay the boring parts.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback. That’s a great idea. Playing the tutorial over and over becomes a bit annoying, I agree.

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: I like this variant of fruit Ninja! The graphics are very good and the game feels very smooth and polished. Well done! I think there should be an option to skip the tutorial, so that you do not have to do it anytime you restart. Congrats to this cool entry!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback, appreciate it. Yes, I agrre, the tutorial makes the pace too slow in the beginning.

comments.author: Tim Sulmone
comments.text: Enjoyed the slicing mechanics and physics. Good for mobile devices. Also well done on the Near integration using NFT upgrades.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Thank you very much for the positive feedback!

api.name: Gothic Tower
api.description:
There has been a disruption in the space-time continuum and you have to fix it. Go back to the 13th century Gothic Tower and collect all the green time stones.

Note: Before you exit you need to collect ALL the green coins. In the time warp process some unfriendly creatures might have escape into our dimension. Eliminate them with your ray gun (x). You have also been equipped with a jet pack (z). Good luck!

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
I spent aaaages dying to the first monster… and then I realised I had a jet pack xD

Quite a simple sidescroller, the collision between the shapes is a too big for the shapes themselves though.

I like the time-traveller theme.

comments.author: Daniel Cohen
comments.text:
I thought the first level was impossible until I figured out the jetpack. Then the game was very compelling.

I died so, so many times, but I kept wanting to try again. The level design was very good for that. It felt like success was just one “press R” away. Thanks for making me restart at the same level when I died.

Some power-ups like temporary invincibility on the early levels would have been nice to give the feeling of easy success and progress / ramping difficulty.

Well done.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I enjoyed playing this game.

The sound effects were good. I liked all the little characters.

The enemies seemed to be much better shots than you, as they could direct their shots rather than only being able to shoot horizontally.

Perhaps the jetpack shouldn’t have infinite fuel?

I found having to restart completely when you were hit a bit difficult, perhaps having a health bar would be good?

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: In order for the player to get that there is a jetpack right away, maybe have the character fly-in the stage initially? I’m not very good at this games, so I gave up on stage 4, but if there was a checkpoint, it would have helped me. Very nice sounds. Would love better character sprite/animation (check Apple ][ Hero game). Not convinced about the theme though..

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: Flying is satisfying, both the sound and the particles are lovely. Everything moves kind of fast, I get killed in an instant.

comments.author: Thiago Romão Barcala
comments.text:
The game is insteresting but extremely hard for my abilities.

Even after deciding on using the jetpack for everything I struggled to kill the 3 enemies from the first level.

Just a small remark: I was playing on German keyboard, and the Z letter is swapped with the Y, so it became a bit uncomfortable to play. I would also often get confused and press the letter R when I wanted to press Z (they are just one key apart in the German keyboard). Maybe using enter or some more distinct key for restart could be an option.

comments.author: Grav
comments.text: Nice execution, very difficult but very satisfying when you win.

comments.author: Sebastian Dorn
comments.text:
It is running at different speeds depending on the monitor refresh rate. A bit too fast at 144 Hz and very slow at 60 Hz.

The light/darkness radius and particle effects look nice.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • Quick restart makes dying not so bad

Feedback:

  • Extremely difficult game. The enemies shoot at you with pinpoint precision the moment they appear on screen and the bullets move so fast that it doesn’t give you any time to react or dodge them. I died sooo many times on the first two levels that I gave up
  • The enemies being able to fire in any direction while you are able to only fire directly in front of you makes the enemies even more difficult. The difficulty could be reduced if the enemies could only fire in one direction (for example, the stars shoot directly in font of them while they move up and down while the pink things can only shoot straight up)
  • Enemy bullet speed could be reduced in order to give the player time to react and dodge with skill

comments.author: John Swana
comments.text: I couldn’t win first level, this game has the potential to be something awesome!

comments.author: Luke Stapley
comments.text:
Wayyyyy too hard. This game has too many bullets and not enough time for the gamer to enjoy. The other items are all fine.

Also, how did they get ray guns in the 13th century????

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: For me game is SUPER fast No option to react to bullets. Maybe you are using some fixed FPS without time correction, but on your PC it’s too low Really impossible to play

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
The jetpack physic are really cool. Unfortunately though the game runs way to fast for me (I have a 120hz monitor). You need to either use the timestamp from requestAnimationFrame to adjust your physics, or lock your frame rate. It would also be nice to have a larger play window.

Due to the issues I didn’t get very far, but I think there are some nice base physics here and if you tweak a few issues it could be really fun!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
The pop-up about the level information disappears very quickly, I don’t have time to read the text. It would be good to have information about the number of all green crystals in the level, so that you know how many more you need to collect and do not miss anything. I can pass through the ground from below, that is, there is a collision under the feet, but not under the head in the territories that are in the air.

The last level is hard…. Maybe if the jet pack had a recharge time, it would be harder and more interesting and also increase the game to full screen, and also support of the WASD movement additionaly :).

Good game! Thank you and congratulations!

comments.author: Frahaan Hussain
comments.text:
Cool concept, but definitely not an easy game. I liked the sound effects.

I would have preferred a bigger game view.

comments.author: Tom Harris
comments.text: The enemies are impossible to avoid which might be due to my monitor having a high refresh rate, so I couldn’t get very far! I like the cool retro style and sounds though.

comments.author: Tero Jäntti
comments.text:
Nice work with this action game. Going around with a jetpack is always fun!

The graphics and sound work well to create atmosphere. There could be character animation and music of course, but I think the elements here fit well together. The lighting and the dim patterns in the background make it look really interesting. And the particle effects are cool! While I don’t mind that the game runs in a small frame, it would be more pleasing to the eye if the background color of the website was darker and the frame horizontally centered.

The controls work quite well. A couple of things could improve the gameplay, however. First: I often noticed the enemies when they were too close to avoid, so the camera could be aimed better, perhaps taking player’s movement direction in account. Second: collisions of the bullets with the player could be calculated with a smaller box than what the sprites are, in order to give the player a good “close call” feeling when avoiding the bullets.

I finished the game after about an hour and a half of playing (well, I’m quite clumsy with action games :)) It was often difficult, but it did’t feel unfair though, with enough practice it was doable. There is good variety in the levels.

api.name: Mongol March
api.description:
In the 13th century, the Mongol Empire marched to conquer the world. As a Mongol commander, your duty is to lead your army to conquer European castles.

This is a tower attack game based on Tetris-like blocks. Strategically arrange the blocks to summon Mongol soldiers and lead them to victory.

Controls

  • Z: Rotate Blocks
  • Left Mouse Button: Place Blocks, UI Interaction

Gameplay

The goal of the game is to pursue the highest score. Players need to strategically place blocks on the strategy board. To place a block, hover the mouse over the board, and the block will be displayed based on the anchor point. Blocks can be rotated by pressing “Z” key or by clicking on the anchor point. Fill the entire strategy board with blocks as much as possible, and there will be unexpected effects.

Each block represents a type of soldier. If you encounter an inappropriate block, you can click the “waive” button to give up. When there are no more blocks, click the button to start attacking the tower. The game will generate soldiers according to the order of blocks (left to right) in the strategy board. When there is a blank grid in the strategy board, the grid will be locked and cannot be arranged into squares in the next round.

Score

The scoring is calculated based on factors such as the attack power, attack range, attack speed, and attack points of the defeated soldiers and the castle. In addition, the remaining Mongol soldiers will also become bonus.

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: Really nice effort! I love the graphics and how you integrated a tetris type thing in there, well done.

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
The only thing missing from this game is sound. Everything else is 5/5.

The tetris strategy board as a way to set up what units you have access to is brilliant! And just when I thought I was done being amazed, the “gaps” carry over as blocked out areas! So it does feel like you have one warband and that’s the one warband that you continue with.

Brilliant game! Definitely in my top 5.

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: Very nice idea. Got a good strategy as early as my second run. Overall the pastel colors work ok on the battlefield, but down on the strategy board they were too bland, especially the damaged pieces needed more prominent colors, so I could see them easily. The placement of pieces on the board was a bit hard on the edges, especially if I couldn’t see the piece - it just needed to be semi-transparent when outside.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Very interesting mechanic using the tetris board, and the graphics and color palette are awesome!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Concept is interesting with tetris as a way to lay out the attack strategy. Did not realise at first way the time line would run and put the infantry in the wrong place.

The graphics and colours are very nice, plus the design of the characters is good.

Critical Feedback:

  • Entities overlap
  • No sound
  • Visual feedback on fights would be good
  • First level a little tough

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
This is an EXCELLENT idea. Love the concept and you’ve executed it fantastically for a jam. I also love the graphics, the palette is absolutely charming, and everything is so big and illustratively bold. One note: you probably want to make the “dead” cells on the strategy bold a very dark color, so that they stand out more when you’re placing.

The only thing I’m missing (aside from the obvious audio) is a better indicator when you can’t place a piece. Right now it just disappears, which I think will confuse some players. Maybe you could turn it into an outline and leave it stuck to the mouse, unaligned with the grid when you drag it? That way you’d get a “snap” into solid color when it works, and a “snap” into outlines when there’s no valid position.

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
Wow, I love this idea so much! What a great concept, and the graphics are really good.

However, the game left me a bit frustrated. Making me have to place my troops in reverse order is frustrating. Let the troops I place on the right side come out first, and the troops I place on the left side come out last, as that is what lines up with where they are on the screen. The disappearing pieces also made it hard, because if my cursor left the area by one pixel, piece gone. Piece turned the wrong way? Can’t see it to turn it back because it’s invisible.

Finally the difficulty is too high too soon and seems almost random, as sometimes lesser troops do better than more. I couldn’t really work that out.

It’s really a shame because I think if those things had been better sorted this would be my number one game of the year. I really really love the idea and would love to see it developed further.

comments.author: Chris Glover
comments.text: Took me a while to work out what does what, but overall I enjoyed the fusion of concepts way more than I was expecting. I do love an auto-battler. I have to admit, I didn’t get that I was selecting a powerup after each wave and selected skip each time for a while.

comments.author: lmgt475
comments.text: Nice concept! I always like tower games. The gameplay also reminds me of one of my favorite game during childhood: Ocean Express.

comments.author: Christian Staudte
comments.text: Such a great idea, it was really fun to play! One thing: the fights were too slow. Waiting around for 2 minutes just to see the tower defeat my units was a bit frustrating

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • I really liked the unique way that the Tetris pieces are used as the units you’ll send in the fight, and that the order from left/right dictates how the units are deployed
  • I especially liked the way that empty spots carry over to the next wave and become blockers for placing the pieces
  • Really good visuals on the units and very nice UI in general

Feedback:

  • I didn’t like that the piece would vanish if the rotation prevented it from being placed or would end up off the board. I would often be rotating a piece to fit and it would disappear after rotating it and I’d have to move my mouse around trying to get it to show up again. This is especially annoying when you’re trying to fill the top row and it only fits one way
  • I would have liked to see a total army composition (every available piece) and not just a next piece in order to figure out what spaces I could leave. For example I left two T spaces open in order to come back to fill them but then I was only ever given one T piece. Had I known there was only 1 available I wouldn’t have left a spot for two
  • I would also have liked to see the enemy army’s “board” in order to better strategize the order to send my own army

comments.author: John Swana
comments.text: I love the graphics and color palette used in this game.

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
This is cool! I like the design and motion of the units. Feels like little toy soldiers marching around.

Gameplay is fun! I agree with another reviewer that the contrast should be higher on the grid for “unusable” squares (grayish purple) and “usable squares” (gray).

Overall, really cool idea. I would totally play an expanded version!

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Unfortunately, I could not rotate blocks: I have “search as you type” enabled in my browser, and you don’t call .preventDefault() on the key event, so when I try to rotate a block, instead the page search opens for me.

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Great game, really unique combination of tetris and strategy planning. Great Job!

comments.author: Rémi Vansteelandt
comments.text:
Neat idea! Some feedback:

  • Once I placed all my pieces, it took me a while to understand how to start the battle. It wasn’t obvious to me I was supposed to waive pieces
  • When placing pieces, if I placed my mouse over an invalid spot, the piece would disappear. This felt very unintuitive. I would have preferred to have an indicator that the spot is invalid, maybe with a red piece, so I could more easily readjust. Alternatively, adjust the piece for the player automatically before placing it so clicking feels more natural
  • Music would have been a great addition. The game is fairly slow paced and it would have been nice to have a song while preparing for battle, and a different one for the battle.

Great job overall!

comments.author: yinghua
comments.text: Creative gaming experience! I never thought you could play Teris like this.

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text:
What I liked:

  • Interesting concept
  • Some strategy elements
  • Cute graphics

What I liked less:

  • I probably needed a few more rounds to understand what the good strategies are
  • Not sure what seeing the next piece brings to the game as it is not really like Tetris where I need to make quick decision

Overall, it is a good strategy game that probably requires time to fully explore.

comments.author: Fabiennelin
comments.text: Except the audio, everything is perfect! Creativity unique theme, developed through the similar Tetris game, with non-monotonous game design, encouraging player to continue challenging themselves.

comments.author: Jeremy Burns
comments.text: Fun combination of tetris and tower defense mechanics! The graphics and animations suit the game well. It would be nice if there were a count on-screen of the current number of soliders, or if they were prevented from overlapping, since sometimes it’s hard to tell how many you have left. The bonus mechanic is a nice addition to chain the rounds together!

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: The idea of filling the board with tetrominoes which then become a resource is grand!

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

  • My first impression and understanding (before playing the game, only reading the description, screenshot and title):
    • The cover art sold me on some kind of platformer where I’d battle side to side. The description is telling me it will be a tetris game :o
  • Great work on:
    • Have a working complete game!
    • The graphics and polish level. The colors choice made it really feel well worked, and the animations are great for the size and time available.
  • I liked that you:
    • Created a nice puzzle with some dimensions, I need to think about the timing of the army, the type of unit, the space I’m filling in the board, and what possible pieces will be fit in the future.
    • Have a widescreen game. The dimensions made it a bit more unique. It creates some expectations because you, the game designer, made a conscious choice of stretching the space, and now I, the player, will be expecting something about this choice.
  • You could improve (again, I’m aware you might already know all the below):
    • Pick your game! Is this going to be a tangram or a tetris? In tangram you can rearrange the pieces when you make a mistake, and in tetris you get progression by filling up rows. Neither of these two advantages are really in your game. It was really trick to really have any plan for my attacks. A lot of the information was hidden and I had to commit to choices (place in the board) I had no control over if it would pay off or not, it was all left to randomness. It’s ok to have randomness, but taking out control from the player makes for a frustrating play.

api.name: The Monk's Window
api.description:
We bless you and your efforts. – the monk

Help the monastery finish their window. Spin to find the vassals you want.

You gain three panes each time you complete a round of Fox and Geese, a medieval board game. Three rounds completes the whole window and wins the game!

Controls: Click / Tap

My favorite part is the monk when you finish all three rounds 😅. Refresh to play again.

Developer stuff: While making this, I wrote several things as code pens - here is that that collection: https://codepen.io/collection/gYBYRe

Thanks for playing!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: It’s probably the best usage of emojis I have ever seen (and the 3D rendering effects are great!).

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text: 5 stars for the ending. The voice-over and random names are also hilarious. Very well done!

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Loved the ending xD

Pretty cool use of 3D effects and emojis

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: first, I wish i knew about emojis earlier, using them would have reduced alot of kbs and enhanced the graphics so well done. gameplay is not much of gameplay but the end is epic using narrator was nice move

comments.author: Justin Sullard
comments.text:
This is awesome, and the audio especially has me curious as to how you pulled it off. A solid entry, and great use of primarily HTML, CSS, and SVG.

I ran into a weird bug during my second round where I was able to jump nothing, and that’s what I got for my last pane, which was a little confusing as there was no icon visible but the pane selection was still present.

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: Love the Vitrage, it’s so colorful! And I like the button for admiration at the end. But is this really called “stained-glass window” in English? No word for this type of art? Anyway, amazing rendering techniques. Only the red ribbons didn’t looked like buttons for me. Also my monk had different face though - he had glasses! Yet it turned out the invention of glasses (first pair of corrective spectacles) was created in Italy sometime between 1268 and 1300! So you are spot on the theme even if the Emojis had tried to fail you :D

comments.author: John Swana
comments.text: Classic, and relatable game i like the graphics

comments.author: Christian Staudte
comments.text: That was such a surreal experience. Loved the party at the end :D

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Well, it’s really weird stuff) But puzzle is intetresting and playable in general. Also many fx is really impressive. As far as I understood it’s unicode symbols. Good Job, it’s quite creative

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Graphically and technically a very nice game. I also liked a lot the Fox and Geese game and of course the final monk screen :). The choosing of the vassals and windows I did not feel that much, because it had no influence on the gameplay (at least I did not realize anything). Anyway, still a nice game and I had fun playing it.

comments.author: Jack Le Hamster
comments.text: Lol, nice trippy ending! I hope others get to reach that far.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
The game didn’t work for me I’m afraid, it was too small to see what was going on and bunched up on the left hand side of the browser window.

This was on latest FireFox

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
My lady, my lady-lady, what? ahahha :D

This is a masterpiece! Although the topic does not really resonate with the topic of the competition, everything else is just… ahahaha, I laughed from the heart :D.

Also, I did not immediately understand how to choose pictures for the windows, but this is probably because I did not read the text), when the cell on the grid lit up - everything went, went).

A monk as a loader - why not? :D

When I finished the game by choosing the pictures for the windows, I was surprised that there was no “play again” button, but when this disco started I fell out laughing ahahaha…

The graphics are also at a height! I love SVG! Well, the music already emphasized all that :). The way the monk moved his head back and forth with his body was cool and a lot of fun! If you could high five to the monk - it would be icing on the cake!

Oh, I really enjoyed it and had fun, thanks and congratulations!

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
The dancing monk though!

Lovely rendering, too, — I generally quite dislike emoji, but here the 3D effect looks glorious, even if some of the vassals would render as tofu on my windows10 box.

And thanks for sharing the Codepens! Love the 3dmojis one the most.

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text:
Very unique idea for a game! Having a name and coat-of-arms for each vassal is a nice touch, that makes the choice of which vassal to take a lot more fun! Great idea to make the game based on an actual medieval board game, that fits the theme really well.

Gameplay-wise, it can be quite difficult to win from the AI, since they almost always make the choice to outwit you. This can make it so you spend a lot of time chasing pieces around the board, which slows down process a bit. But the party at the end definitely makes it all worth it!

comments.author: Frederic Kyung-jin Rezeau
comments.text: Great game, beautiful art and animations, especially the end scene! Very well done.

api.name: Death Sea XIII
api.description:
Death Sea XIII takes players on a perilous journey through treacherous waters as they assume the role of Captain Cedric Leviathanhunter and his intrepid crew in the year 1293 fighting with unknown monsters.

Game for desktop Browsers.

Controls

Keyboard arrows ← ↑ → ↓ and ↵ Enter.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • Graphics are really well done. The main menu screen looks great
  • The radiating out effect when an enemy dies helps sell the impact of the hit

Feedback:

  • Would have liked some more feedback on hitting enemies with more than one life. Since they wouldn’t die in one hit the bullet would just stop on them but it wasn’t easy to tell at first if that’s because I wasn’t damaging them or because they took more than 1 hit to kill

comments.author: Misael Braga
comments.text: Thanks for your feedback. I am will consider all of it on the next project.

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Hard to understand how many hits some of the enemies take. I would’ve liked to have some control over when to fire the cannon ball too.

Overall it’s quite enjoyable, it reminds me of those old vertical scrolling shoot’em ups but with a ship! :D

The ship not being able to stand still adds an extra bit of difficulty, which is nice.

comments.author: Misael Braga
comments.text:
Thanks for your feedback.

I am really glad you apreciated it.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
The gameplay, sounds and graphics are good, all runs well. Missing some gameplay / features.

These types of games work well with some progression rather than endless play but this is a good start for a vertically scrolling shooter.

comments.author: Misael Braga
comments.text: Thanks for your feedback. I am really glad you appreciate it and i will consider your tips for future projects.

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: I appreciate the Hi-score emphasis and the music fits really well. Didn’t like the single instance of the sound effect though. Others have stated the problem with the lack of feedback when hitting enemies. The other issue for me was that the powerups were too similar, yet the bullet hell one should definitely stand out, because the enemies were getting ridiculously hard with that type of chasing. Also firing the cannon with Space would’ve helped a lot. Overall good shoot’em up!

comments.author: Misael Braga
comments.text: Thanks for your review. Really, i should had create a hit feedback for stronger enemies. I will consider it on future projects.

comments.author: Luke Stapley
comments.text:
The game has a great opening screen, and the idea of a boat for a shooter is great. Also, the sound effects were great.

The only big issue is that the enemy sprites didn’t really feel nautical, so they need to be improved. Also, no special bosses after so many points would add variety. The powerups come at two at a time, making the bullet one weird as I collect both and have the timer reset.

The best thing is that there is a high score list and a reset function. Makes me feel I’m back in the arcades.

comments.author: Misael Braga
comments.text:
Thank you for the feedback. I am really appreciated it!

About the enemies, i have thought to create some squids but my poor drawing skills in a low resolution screen doesnt works well, hahaha.

I am happy about your comment about retro feeling. It was my main goal on the project. :D

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Simple scrill shooter, playable and with several enemies, but not super various

comments.author: Misael Braga
comments.text:
Thanks for your time and feedback about the project.

**sorry for my poor english

comments.author: Johan Åhlgren
comments.text: A nice scroller with a real retro feel. Good job!

comments.author: Misael Braga
comments.text: It was my greatest objective with this project, this retro feel. I am happy to hear it, thanks a lot!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
I liked the opening letter and its content - it sets a good context. However, it is too far removed from the topic of the competition, although I think the game is good, my record is 504 :).

When I chose to play without sound, I couldn’t start the game, the buttons didn’t seem to work to close the start screen… Also, I’m more used to controlling WASD, and someone is used to arrows, it would be good to always offer both options as for me.

I noticed fish in the ocean - I was happy :). The last type of the enemy’s troops had already clung to me too much, and what he liked about me… Maybe it would be a good idea to add some kind of heavy cannon blast with half the map radius that would have a lot of cooldown time but give you a chance to catch your breath

Thanks and congratulations!

comments.author: Misael Braga
comments.text:
Thanks for your review. It is very important. About the game story, i confess, it was not a medieval story after all.

The WASD is a great idea, it would be a good addition for the project. The cannon blast could be great too! i have imaginated it exploding half of the enemies on the screen! :D

Its very nice that you noticed the fish, i have drawed it to kill some time and it have became nice. :D

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text:
What I liked:

  • It is a classic shooter
  • The controls are fine
  • A couple of items
  • Highscores

What I liked less:

  • No music in-game, and only one sound effect
  • Not much variety

Overall, it is a fine little shooter.

comments.author: Misael Braga
comments.text: Thanks for playing and for the feedback. It would be really great if that last points was implemented.

comments.author: Ania Kubów
comments.text: A great shooter game! I really enjoyed the sound effects and the smooth movements of the ship. What was interesting from this is that it was not like any shooter game, where the main player scrolls from left to right. The main player was able to move all around the game board, which I thought was a good implementation due to the main player being a ship. It would be great to see some obstacles thrown into the game play as well as enemies. Something like land that the ship cannot go over - for example.

comments.author: Misael Braga
comments.text:
Nice! I am really glad you noticed that. I have spend some time working on that.

Thanks!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
I really like when games try to be faithful to the NES aesthetics: the splashscreen, the intro music, the simple but slowly progressing gameplay and the sound effects, and the solid pixel art. It’s unfortunately missing music during gameplay! I would also top it off with a pixel font. The controls were really smooth and the progression felt adequate.

It wasn’t clear to me when the cannon was shooting. Was it always automatic? Maybe there should be a button to use it. The monsters that follow me were very hard to kill, but maybe that’s just me,

comments.author: Misael Braga
comments.text:
I like the NES asthetics too. Games like Bloodstained: Curse of the Moon have tried to revive this and it is pretty good.

About the cannon, it is automatic. My son gave me this button recomendation too when he was testing.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Nice! Now I’m thinking about why we don’t see more straight up scrolling shooters. Maybe we’ve forgotten this genre exists!

Great work on getting some pleasant graphics in, by and large the game worked as I imagine you intended. The sounds were basic, but they did their job. Solid effort.

Next time, I’d think about the screen composition a little more: your screen is pretty empty, so the game feels empty, even though you have lots of things flying around! You also want to be more deliberate about your enemy spawning. The random you have now is fine to test and get started, but the real story telling artistry of great shooters comes from their spawn and bullet patterns. If you’re into the genre, you’ll want to focus on leveling up that next!

comments.author: Misael Braga
comments.text:
Thanks for playing and for the feedback.

The enemy spawning patterns like rounding and queue would be really a good fit for the game.

api.name: TimeRush
api.description:
TimeRush is an exciting web-based game where you can collect various items, control time, and maximize your earnings.

Unlocking the Power of Blockchain

In TimeRush Game, players can collect various items, manipulate time, and maximize their profits. What sets this game apart is the ability for players to create and develop tokens within a blockchain ecosystem. Dive into the world of TimeRush and experience the thrill of both gaming and blockchain innovation.

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: I didn’t find being forced to collect the tiles with minus time very fun. I would think those would not have to be collected to proceed. This mechanic makes the game basically pointless.

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
The instructions mention that we must avoid the blocks that decrease the time but it feels like we’re forced to pick them up.

Often, no other blocks spawn until I click the Minus time blocks.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
At some point you have to pick the negative tiles before spawning new tiles - this kills you pretty quickly. The “powerups” on the side felt useless and almost impossible to read what they do (the text is super dark grey on black background).

I know it was already difficult to reconciliate blockchain and the medieval era, but I’m sure peasants weren’t trying to “maximize their profits” ;)

comments.author: Viljami Peltola
comments.text: Good underlaying idea! A tad hard to avoid decreasing time when there are no other options and Iäm too ADHD to wait for more blocks. Clean full game from start to end.

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: I didn’t found that much intrigue as it currently stands. If the newly spawned Blues were able to cover or destroy the yellows in some clever gameplay mechanic it would’ve been awesome. I just craved a way to be able to outsmart the RND. The things on the right were so small I couldn’t read them. Also with the name Time Rush, an ability to manipulate time to maximize profit and the theme of 13th Century, entirely different associations were coming along prior to starting the game.

comments.author: Snj
comments.text: Sometimes one must subtract by force, to unveil the path to fresh opportunities. This teaches us not to shy away from even unappealing options, when there is a prospect.

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Mmm, I didn’t understand anything. Weird clicker.

comments.author: Ben Fox
comments.text: The concept was cool, but it didn’t feel like I could do enough to feel like I had any control over my success. Even after upgrading/purchasing the first Time and Coin upgrades I never saw those things spawn. At one point I got into a situation where I clicked the last tile and no new tiles spawned at all, so just forced into a game over.

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: I think the game has quite a big flaw: At some point it might happen that you end up with only T-1 items, and no new items (especially +1 ) appear as long as you do not press the T-1 item. In these cases you will loose the game and you can do not much about it. I would also change the color of the font on the updates a bit. They are very hard to read. I think I understand what kind of game you wanted to create and it might be fun if you tweak it.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
Things stopped spawning for me after buying the first 2 upgrades.

I also don’t get the “blockchain” part of the description. Maybe it’s a mechanic further in the game?

comments.author: Tim Sulmone
comments.text: Pretty fun clicker game, a mix of aim training and quick decision making.

api.name: Go Forth and Conquer
api.description:
The true story of a brave Knight who went forth and conquered kingdoms to become the rightful King.

  • WSD / Arrows to move
  • Spacebar to throw

Collect small rocks to throw at baddies, rulers of Kingdoms and general terrain to aid you progress. Rank up your nobility to become King and ultimate ruler, if you can be bothered.

Gamemodes

  • Go Forth - An infinite runner hybrid, get as far as you can conquering land.
  • Conquer - Conquer kingdoms to become the ultimate ruler, King of all you survey.

comments.author: Sebastian Dorn
comments.text:
Great start screen.

The character feels a little slow and hard to control, but the game is pretty forgiving – even if you barely miss the jump, you can still save yourself on the edge. Feels chaotic, but in a good way. Also a nice detail that enemies can lose their helmet.

A little dangerous: I hit ESC to pause the game. But ESC quits to the title screen.

comments.author: Brian Hambley
comments.text: I never thought about that, I thought ESC was default for Quit :) but your right, pause with an option to continue or quit would be way better.

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
I think it may be accidental but when the knight starts to rotate due to getting caught on the edge of a collision box, it makes for very funny situations.

I liked that you had to jump on the rocks to break them into smaller ones - though that wasn’t very obvious.

The way the knight moved felt a bit clunky at times but once you get used to it, it’s not so bad.

Pretty enjoyable overall.

comments.author: Chris Glover
comments.text: Impressive physics engine, and challenging gameplay, the player is a little tough to control however

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
I loved the physics and the whole concept. I wish the controls were a bit more responsive, though. The physics are awesome!

I managed to kill the boss giant but failed to get the crown because I fell through a gap in the map… :’)

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
Cool physics based platformer!

It got a little too hard too fast imo, with the single width platforms starting in level 2, especially considering you can’t control your character in mid air. You should let the player adjust the characters speed in mid air. It’s not realistic of course, but it feels more fair, especially for landing on tiny platforms.

But I did still enjoy it. Anything physics based is always cool to interact with, and I had fun playing through!

comments.author: John Swana
comments.text: I like the physics in this game. Got my vote

comments.author: xem
comments.text: very fun to have 2D physics in a platformer

comments.author: Brian Hambley
comments.text: Thanks to your code :) pretty pleased with what it was capable of and how easy it was to implement in my project. So thank you again for making it available.

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: I enjoyed knocking the fat king’s crown and all the other nice gameplay touches. Cool character design. When I saw the helm of one of the enemies fell in the pit I almost jumped there to try and get it haha. Missed a jump sound. Super curious how would that game feel with different speed / gravity / heaviness on the physics..?

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Nice physics and fun game play, would have been good if the character rotation continued rather than resetting after falling jumping.

Quite chaotic but fun, nice pixel art.

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Well, interesting variation, but not a really obvious game. There are some problems with controls. But it’s playable in general.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: awesome chaos! i like the amount of particles and effects and the physics behind

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A fun game. Cute graphics and good soundtrack.

The platformer physics worked well, and I liked the rotating boulders bouncing off stuff.

The boss (king) was pretty big and hard to defeat, but did manage to steal his crown.

I seemed to run out of rocks to throw quickly, and wasn’t sure how to get them back again.

comments.author: Brian Hambley
comments.text: Yes, not enough rocks. I realised a bit too late but I’ll definitely look at in v1.1.

comments.author: Frahaan Hussain
comments.text: Interesting concept, really enjoyed the procedural nature of the game, this is a great starting point for a mobile game.

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: the ability to move mid air would make it easier to play, and why is it rotating like this out of random reasons, nice graphics and gameplay.

comments.author: Brian Hambley
comments.text: I’m going to try out controlling the player mid jump in the post release. Its built using physics, things rotate with inertia, momentum and interaction with the environemt, a bit like in real life really :D

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text:
Execution of the theme: I love the storyline in combination with the art, game play and audio. 

Gameplay: It’s quite a solid game given the limitations, some suggestions on how to make the game even better:

  • To smoothen the player journey an auto refresh / restart could help them stay engaged with the game
  • The direction of the rock throwing feels a little wonky / off 

Overall, a super fun game that I would love to play more!

api.name: Dynasty
api.description:
The 13th century has come… Try to establish a dynasty that will stand the test of time. Arrange marriages to give your kingdom the best chances to develop. Make Your kingdom as powerful as possible and score as many points as you can for 7 generations of your dynasty.

Controls

Controls: use mouse and click on ‘Dismiss’ or ‘Accept’ buttons to choose marriage candidates for Your dynasty.

Good luck!

comments.author: Elliot Nelson
comments.text: Interesting concept - although the gameplay options are simple, I really found myself getting into trying to min/max the different attributes while still having enough passion or luck. Nice work.

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Tinder in the 13th century :D

I really enjoyed the base mechanics - I don’t think it’s explored fully, but the rough idea is already quite fun.

You can ignore some of the events by maximising your luck which will allow you to have more points at the end.

Graphics on the pictures are pretty good and the interface is quite simple and easy to understand.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
I think the game introduces too much information at once at the beginning while failing to make clear the consequences (I resolved all scandals through luck but this was always displayed on a tiny text after my decision).

However the idea and the portraits are amazing, good job :)

comments.author: John Swana
comments.text: I like the graphics so many ideas and details for a 13kb demo

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: The portraits are amazing, but the Black Death belongs to the 14th century, not to the 13th.

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Great game, really interesting puzzle concept. Also it’s great that where is a lot of events. Good job!

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Hey there!

In my opinion, the graphics are what make this game stand out. Really well done on designing the portraits and the menus and buttons. It really gives a nice atmosphere.

It’s too bad there is no audio, it’s definitely the type of game you’d like a nice medieval background music to chill to. Otherwise, you take the risk of your players getting bored.

I felt that the game was missing a bit of tutorial at the beginning. The gameplay and idea is great, but I felt thrown into it without guidance, and had to rely on tests and failures to understand what works and what does not. A bit sad!

Great submission overall, really well made!

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: The graphics are truly a highlight, really cool visual style. I semi-expected to see some very complex graphics code, but I guess good ol’ GIF89 format works wonders!

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text: Short but sweet, interesting premise for a game. I’m kinda shocked that the little base64 portrait gifs ended up looking so good!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
Interesting and unique gameplay. I wasn’t able to last long, my children just don’t seem to want to shag that much no matter how high their passion!

I really like the UI and the pixelated portraits. I liked that all the information was immediately visible without having to open menus and tabs.

If I would suggest something, would to to somehow apply some randomness to how the pictures look, like changing the color of their clothes or hairs. At the very least, avoid repeated consecutive portraits.

It wasn’t clear to me what the outcome of the events was, whether I lost or won.

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text:
Execution of the theme: very original execution of the theme, both in terms of art, in-game topics and overall storyline and narrative, well done!

Gameplay: it’s a little shallow, it could use a tutorial to make the connection between the marriages, the health of your empire and the goal of the game clear.

Overall a very original cool game!

comments.author: Marcin Walczak
comments.text:
Interesting idea, definitely in theme. Managing marriages and balancing attributes can be challenging.

Good looking graphics. I really like these pixelated portraits.

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text:
What I liked:

  • It is a medieval dating app game!
  • Some strategy elements
  • Easy to understand
  • Good UI/graphics

What I liked less:

  • A background music would have been cool
  • Not much replayability value after a couple of rounds

Overall, I really enjoyed this game. Good job!

api.name: Plunder Survivors
api.description:
Plunder all the gold ye can, me hearty, but beware the ghosts! Upgrade yer ship to give yourself the best chance to survive for 5 minutes against a new wave of ghosts every 30 seconds.

  • Desktop: Arrow keys
  • Mobile: Virtual joystick by putting finger on the screen

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Enjoyable. The upgrades are pretty nice but they ramp up in cost quite quickly.

When a wave starts, it’s impossible to dodge the ghosts since they appear from all sides. They should pick one or two sides and come from there instead.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
i like the upgrade-options, that makes the game much more interesting. Maybe some upgradeable interactive weapons would be nice? An expensive “nuke” for example, to clear at least a part of the screen..? The graphics are solid. The pixel-effect is a good aesthetic choice. But i wonder how it would look using non pixelated vector-graphics?

The sound-effect is ok, maybe a piece of music (haunting, or sea-shanty) would add to the atmosphere of the gameplay?

The initial waves of ghost are a bit too much to handle.

But i played the game a couple of times, and was entertained by it every time. Good entry!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: I wish it was easier to dodge attacks - sadly the map doesn’t allow for this in many places. Loved the questionable rum upgrade :D

comments.author: John Swana
comments.text: Nice and unique game unsure about the objective though

comments.author: Ben Fox
comments.text:
Style is fun, controls were easy! I like the gameplay, but it felt a bit too hard to me. After the first wave there were immediately so many ghosts it was hard to avoid, couldn’t outrun them, and hard to maneuver away with the narrow channels.

Upgrades were great, lots of options, but I also wanted to be able to spent all my available gold rather than just choosing one.

comments.author: Rémi Vansteelandt
comments.text:
My kind of game, this is one of the games I played the longest :)

I love the idea, but I’d love to make a few suggestions for improvement:

  • waves get out of control very quickly. At wave 3 I couldn’t handle much anymore. Even if I focus on the objectives, I couldn’t complete the game
  • the map sometimes forces you into a dead end to deposit your gold (towards the edges of the map), causing you to take more damage
  • sound cuts off a bit, which isn’t easy on the ear
  • maybe explosive cannonballs for crowd control? Would also add a bit of strategy

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Well, interesting concept and also percs, but reallly not balanced. At least too difficult for me. But good graphics and process Good Job!

comments.author: Grav
comments.text: I think I am a bit biased because I really like this style of game, but I thought this was so much fun. I do understand where commenters are coming from in terms of the difficulty, but I think this game is about picking the best of two bad options. You’re going to get hit, it’s about mitigating that by choosing the correct path and selecting the best upgrades. Great to see this as a full game too in future

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text: Cool & stylized aesthetic. Dodge the monsters and get the loot! Simple and enjoyable. Fits the theme well. Good job!

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Hey there!

This is so well done! Nice sound effects, simple yet they really do the job well. The first wave is simple enough to get you started in the game and understand directly + wanna keep going.

Really neat overall, the upgrade add a bit of replayability too.

comments.author: Jeremy Burns
comments.text: Straightforward and fun game! Seems like the arrow keys didn’t work for me on desktop, but it wasn’t too bad using the mobile controls with a mouse. The waves seem to come a bit faster than I can get upgrades for, but that could be solved with a bit of tweaking and player testing.

api.name: Guess the letter
api.description: Type in the letter as soon as you can recognize it

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text: The game play is good. The graphics are simple and good looking!!

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Simple premise and a different take on letter guessing games.

The audio was a bit too strident for my taste. Graphics were quite simple but good.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
What a great little idea for a game! The script font made it just ambiguous enough what the letter would be, and throwing in the number red herrings really worked. Love the illustrative style.

Oddly enough, I really liked the audio. It reminded me of 80s toys like the Speak n’ Spell.

comments.author: Justin Sullard
comments.text: This was a very original take on things.

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
Clever idea, and definitely enjoyable at first. Needs a much faster difficulty ramp up though, as it never got challenging. I also don’t think making people focus closely on a blurry thing and then constantly shaking the screen around is the best idea… I quit playing at like 880 points from a combination of queasiness and it never getting challenging.

Remove the screen shake and make the difficulty ramp up a lot faster (make the time you have shorter and the sharpening slower), and it would be a simple fun little game!

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: Funny idea, it could have been explored further I feel.

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Well, unusual visual puzzle. Interesting concept, but too fast for me. Letters are customized, so it’s really hard to guess before you will se everytihng.

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text: Can you guess which letter is appearing in the eyeglass? It starts out very blurry and then gets most crisp. The faster you guess, the more points you get. The font is, of course, well suited to the theme of this challenge - even if the sound effects sound like something from a 1980s Speak and Spell :) Good original fun, and more on theme than you may first think.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A fun little recognition against the clock. I liked the concept of the game.

I played several rounds but it always said level 1.

The graphics were good and it was nice to have sounds, but albeit a little coarse.

Perhaps altering the font which was being guessed would add another level of difficulty.

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text:
Very playable with clear immediate goals. It’s fun to guess the letter as it comes into focus. I had some trouble with the font but I get that it’s supposed to give the game a little more challenge.

My one bit of feedback is I’d like some short term goals, and/or perhaps some breaks. Maybe dividing the content up into levels with a reward screen between? Something like that.

Overall easy to play, enjoyable, and great work!

comments.author: Jeremy Burns
comments.text: Very unique take on the theme! It’s well executed and the game play is obvious. I feels like it goes on for a while without giving any kind of feedback though, especially with the “Level 1” at the top hinting at progress coming at some point. I ended up just pausing and clicking the stats button to see how I was doing. It would be nice to have a progress bar of how far you are through a level, and possibly shorter levels. Definitely a cool concept and fun to play though!

api.name: Turrets and Trebuchets
api.description: Mouse only. Click on a trebuchet to load it (increase its strength), or outside it to move it. Click on a village to attack it.

comments.author: Christian Staudte
comments.text:
Extremely clever idea and I had quite some fun playing it.

Small things to improve:

  • accidental clicks on the trebuchet which load it can be extremely fatal. maybe this could be reversed?
  • The game is extremely difficult. Since each tower can attack each of my trebuchet I died even though the chances were pretty low.

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Unusuall artillery game, but looks more like a puzzle with big part of random and luck.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
It took me a while to understand that you select a catapult by hovering it with the mouse. That’s not clear from the UI. So I instinctively was clicking to select, but that also make them stronger. It would be better if you click to select, and then click again to make them stronger. It was also not clear at all that the yellow chestnut was an item to fix the catapult.

There’s some level of strategy to the game, but ultimately whether you win or loose is strongly down to luck which makes the game less fun and that made me stop playing. But it has potential and I would play an improved version of this idea for sure.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • Interesting little puzzle / strategy concept
  • I liked that you could move and still shoot. Helped make it less deadly to move and get into a better position

Feedback:

  • I didn’t know what the orange vegetable did at first. Took a few levels to figure out that I needed to move on top of it, but then I didn’t know what it did (was full health already). Took another level to figure out it healed
  • I was constantly clicking on the trebuchet to move it, which would then load it instead. Hover then move is an unintuitive control
  • It got really hard around level 5. I would try to move my trebuchets out of range of the towers, but since each tower would attack multiple times the odds that they would hit me were high while the odds that I would hit them were low. Keeping trebuchets close enough to hit would also lead to them being destroyed in one turn
  • It would be nice if there was a display to show hit chance so I could gauge how far away I could be but still hit reasonably well

comments.author: Tim West
comments.text:
Fun little game! Easy to learn & play.

A few things I would change:

  • Allow deselecting a trebuchet for attacking (sometimes I accidentally clicked the trebuchet when I wanted to move it first).
  • Provide a visual indicator when I can move the trebuchet. We have the text that appears/disappears, but it would be more immediate feedback on the map.

I have mixed feelings about how the game ends. We have numbered levels which suggests that there might be a pre-defined ending, but the game keeps scaling the difficulty up until the player reaches a level that is likely mathematically impossible to win. I would’ve preferred a defined ending, such as a final boss on level 5 where the enemy can move around the map.

Awesome stuff!

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Hey there!

The mechanics work really well, it’s a really fun concept and didn’t expect to enjoy it that much. Some better design or environment decors would have made it even better! A little music too would have made the game feel more complete.

I do agree with others that a little tutorial explaining the actions you can do could help, but I did get it during the first level.

Nice one

comments.author: Taufik Sani
comments.text:
Gameplay was a little confusing as the instructions per round wasn’t clear. Could have been a little clearer if there was an indication whether it was still the player’s turn or AI’s.

Having to figure out the strategy to outmaneuver the opponent made the experience interesting and less predictable. I like how you can also reposition the trebuchets, adds an additional layer of strategy to the game.

As for controls, what might have made it better when you highlight a trebuchet and it shows the percentage of success hovering over each tower.

For Level 2, I don’t understand why the towers are able to go multiple rounds shooting down my trebuchets. Not clear as to why this is the case. Maybe

comments.author: Jeremy Burns
comments.text: Fun strategy game. The UI took a little getting used to with the hovering mechanic being a bit surprising and the movement range of the trebuchet not being marked, but beyond that I had fun playing through a few rounds. Nice work!

api.name: WitchCup1276
api.description:
Welcome spectators! The renowned Witch World Cup of the year 1276 is about to start! The contestants will race each other on four different courses, one harder than the other! Beware of low flying brooms and jinxes!

How to play

You play the witch dressed in classic black.

Use [Arrow]-Keys or [WASD] to steer your witch (up = accelerate, down = break, left and right to turn the broom)

Use [Space] to cast a jinx that will stun your opponent (if you can hit her)

Use [Escape] during the race to pause and display some options (restart, abort, continue)

The Jinx-Cooldowntime and the brooms speed/turnrate may be updated using your earned witchcraftpoints.

The music during the race is a cover version of “Furatum Alci Provinciam” by CORVUS CORAX (Die Könige der Spielleute! Go check them out!)

Special thanks to Castus (and Norri, Hatz, Micha, Victor, Xandru and Alex) from Corvus Corax and Doro Peters from Absolut Promotion for the granted permission to use the composition! The song was created using Soundbox from sb.bitsnbites.eu

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
Offroad but with witches! Super cool! Love the particle effects, love the graphics, and the racing is quite fun.

This is a game that would greatly benefit from controller support. I found it quite hard to turn, brake and accelerate all with arrow keys. Using triggers for acceleration and braking and an analog for steering would really add a lot!. I would have also liked to see some different backgrounds.

Still, really fun game, I really like the little dirt trail details. I played quite a bit. I even stuck a breakpoint in just for fun and gave myself 999999 witch points. I was interested to learn that there is no limit on speed or turning ability ha! I made them so high the game was unplayable, but it was still cool just to play around with. Nice game!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
Love the idea and sound design!

I have the feeling that the controls have a little “life of their own”, it was never fully clear when I would be able to take a turn and when I would just crash into a tree. Sometimes I would over-steer trying to avoid an obstacle only to find my witch turning exactly in the direction I didn’t want to (didn’t end well). Would be awesome if colliding with another witch would end up in a small fist-fight pushing both witches sideways instead of just penalizing you and making you lose speed. The crashing particle effect and jinx look very similar to me, which was confusing at first.

Despite this, when you manage to speed up, the feeling is very cool :)

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Hi, the trees have a collider-circle around the trunk (moved a tiny bit upwards) So it’s possible to to fly behind the treetop (the witch will shine through, a bit), but not near its trunk.

I like the fist-fight-idea :-)

The intention behind only punishing the player is, that the cpu-witches have a tiny advantage, as a skilled player is always better than them. Especially when throttle is handled well: let go of the forward-key before sharp corners or even brake a bit ;-)

The collisions are even designed to deflect the player (just turn him a few degrees to the direction where no collision would occur), when the angle of the crash is flat enough. That often works with the trees.

comments.author: Emre Guneyler
comments.text: very well, though i can’t possibly keep up with other witches.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
Witch rally is a neat idea. No handbrake though, I crashed into each and every hairpin turn ._.

The vegetation looks decent! Nice touch, prerendering a lot of different trees and rocks.

Sadly the music didn’t play for me, but I searched on YouTube and it totally smashes, great song.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
** Ive read some of the other comments, for anyone reading, let go of accelerate when you turn corners, push back to break a little and you can win. Once you upgrade it becomes easier **

Really cool entry! Managed to win all of the races, got my speed up to about 8, turning 7 and reload to about 6. So had spent quite a lot of points I think. At one before upgrades I thought backwards felt faster and tried to race in reverse and almost won :D

Graphics are nice, interested to see how you implemented the trees and dirt.

Music is simple but affective, sound fx a little quiet.

Really enjoyed playing this, great gameplay. I found one bug, I opened the dev tooks which messed up the track size and the computer all got stuck in a narrow track :D

Overall a great entry!

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • Really nice little racing game
  • Liked the ability upgrades to help fix your steering and speed
  • Nice variety of levels that use different track styles

Feedback:

  • I had no idea which witch I was at first. Lead to me wiping out at the get go for a bit
  • It took awhile to understand that to initially make the hairpin turn (without upgrades) you have to completely let go of the acceleration and just turn
  • I wish there was a catch up mechanic when you are first starting out as once you get behind there is no way to ever catch up (and you get behind a lot by hitting every tree on every turn)

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
thx for the kind review and feedback.

And THX for kontra.js!

I’m not using any engine for js13k, but I’ve learned so much from using the framework in the past!

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Great Job! Interesting and fascinating game, very unusual type of race. Theme is also interesting

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: so smooth and awesome! I love the movement; once I figured out that I needed to slow down to make tight corners it was easy to win the races. overall a really great game, nice job.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
What a great take on a racing game, which fits the theme perfectly. I love how the witch and broomstick rotate when we turn, and the added bullet/spell makes the race more forgiving since I’m able to overtake someone in front of me by hitting them with my magic. The particle effects are subtle, yet effective. The music also captures the essence of the theme.

The upgrades are a nice addition and helps with replay-ability. A few things I wish the game had:

  • Placement indicator, am I first, second or third?
  • Sound effect when crashing, and maybe a “broom engine” sound

I think this game could have worked well with local multiplayer as well. I would love to play it together with my son.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: Thx for your kind review! Local multiplayer was on my list of features as fallback, in case the cpu-controlled witches would not turn out well. I also thought of adding it as last feature, but did run out of time :-)

comments.author: Grav
comments.text: Great music, visuals, controls, great game. really curious about how you did the visuals Reminds me of playing slicks ‘n’ slide as a kid. To those struggling with the turns, it gets way easier the first time you upgrade turn rate, which you can do straight after your first race even if you come last.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: Thanks! I wrote a small postmortem for the game, where i tried to describe the creation of the visuals. Have a look here: https://github.com/DerBenniBanni/js13k2023_witch_racing/tree/main/postmortem

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text:
Wasn’t sure what I would want to upgrade before playing the game. The AI is extremely strong and there doesn’t seem to be a way to catch up once you fall behind.

I appreciate the music, the game runs nice ’n smooth, and it fits the theme perfectly. Good job!

comments.author: Taufik Sani
comments.text:
I love it, but man I keep getting last in the first few rounds!

But I see you might have intended the progression where player earns points to upgrade the broomstick!

Never played a racing game on broomsticks, but this was a fun take of racing might’ve been in 13th century. The cast-a-jinx is cheeky, and I like how you are able to upgrade your broom via the points you earn.

I like the smoke effect when you accelerate. Nice touch. The tracks are also interesting - navigating the corners and turns require a bit of skill from the player, so that’s great as well.

Audio is great, captures the vibe of possibly 13th century racing!

I think this game has the potential to be something more - i can see it as a race game people would be fond of playing because of how interesting the tracks are!

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Great game and very polished game. The gameplay and the flying of the broom feels very nice. I also like the art style and graphics a lot. Maybe the music was a bit repretitive after a while, but I know that it is hard to incorporate longer tracks into 13 KB. Amazing what you were able to create with just 13 KB!

api.name: Overforged
api.description:
Welcome to “Overforged” where you step into the shoes of a skilled XIII century village blacksmith. Your mission is to keep the villagers and the Lord well-pleased with your craftsmanship. You’ll manage your forge, craft weapons and tools, and make crucial decisions to keep your forge running and the people happy. But beware, a displeased Lord can bring an end to your time at the forge. So, craft wisely and manage your resources!

Controls and Gameplay Elements

Character Movement

Use the arrow keys or A and D to move your character around the forge. Interact with forge tools by pressing the Spacebar.

Furnace

The furnace is the heart of your forge. It heats the steel for crafting. Pay attention to the tooltip above the furnace; it turns green when the item is heated.

Box of Infinite Steel

A box that provides you with an endless supply of steel.

Pile of Coal

Your source of fuel for the furnace.

Anvil

This is where the magic happens! Use hot steel to forge it into weapons or tools. Use 1, 2, 3 keys to select what you want to craft.

Grind Wheel

Some crafted weapons will need to be sharpened here before selling.

Counter

Place ready items here so your customers can purchase them.

Pro-tip: It’s better to switch a heated item in the furnace with a non-heated one to save time.

Good luck, blacksmith!

comments.author: Ethan Smith
comments.text: Love the pixel art! Wish players learned to play solely via the tutorial game play instead of reading three pages of instructions.

comments.author: Oliver Smith
comments.text: I like the concept and the art style and gameplay here are great. It is well balanced and most importantly fun to play. Some sounds would have been a nice extra but it works well as it is. Really good work.

comments.author: Samir Hodzic
comments.text: Lovely pixel art and engaging one, well done.

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Love the art style and the animations.

Nice to have a tutorial ingame but I’d preferred to have some feedback sounds and read the instructions in the entry description.

Love the smith overcooked vibes to this, it plays really well.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
Very cool game. I think with the tutorial in place you could skip the first pages of text. Sound effects would improve the experience greatly.

I am not sure if it’s a bug or I don’t know how to do something, but I would like to be asked every time I pick a metal rod what I want to do with it - the idea of being stuck making swords and more swords was frustrating. I also felt swords were much more difficult to make than everything else - would love to get more variety of things to do but with less steps.

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Great game Simple, but unusual and really interesting. Also interesting craft tree. Good job.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
My record: 8 days, 112 villagers - never bought a bench :D

Good game, I liked it! I played a little! I liked the study mode. Regarding the pro-tip with installing the next object in the furnace when taking the finished one - it only confused me, so I played one iron at a time :).

I would like to get an assistant for so many days of service who would at least work at some point :D.

Although I didn’t find enough resonance between the game and the theme of the contest, quite innovative though! I had fun playing!

Thanks and congratulations!

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text:
Very nice pixel art! Love the different characters and all the items you added to the game.

The tutorial is good. I personally think the tutorials work better when they explain by doing, rather than by having the user read instructions.

Having music would have added a great touch to the game.

Congratulations.

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Hey there!

Incredible to be honest! The pixel art is super clean and really gives a nice atmosphere to the game.

Also, it’s not something we see a lot so I’ll emphasize on this. Thank you for the tutorial!! It is SO IMPORTANT to get players to enjoy the game and want to play again. You nailed it.

Too bad it’s missing some music, it would have been even better.

Well made!

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Very good, polished and especially good looking game. The art is amazing and I also liked the gameplay. For my taste it was a bit too difficult, but maybe I am just not a very talented smith. I really liked that the different items had a different workflow. I was also impressed that you were able to incorporate a tutorial and a simple level! The main thing I was missing is audio (music and effects). I also experienced a bug, where I automatically craft item 1 any time I move by the anvil… I could not craft any other items anymore. Congratulations to this really cool game. I wished I could do such nice pixel art myself :)! Very well done!

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: The graphics are top notch! The tutorial is really professional-looking. Amazing, that.

comments.author: Tom Harris
