comments.text: The gameplay was engaging and well balanced. It was challenging but not impossible. Sounds and music would have been a really nice addition to this game to set the mood. Overall it was fun to play and would be great to see a more full version of a game like this.

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text: The life of a blacksmith is pretty stressful! It felt like I was constantly behind on orders and the customers were getting unhappy. I do not think the pace of the game should be reduced though. Being able to work under pressure and deliver the goods that is needed is what makes this game fun.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
This was fun, it’s inspired to do a blacksmith Overcooked, I can see how this would grow to be a whole game.

I think the pace was a little mean though. I thought I was going at full tilt, with the optimal pattern, and I still only managed 8 days, and never got a bench :(

Graphics are superb though, full stars. Fantastic sprite work, all animated well, under a coherent GUI with a nice font. Well done!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Like playing overcooked but as a blacksmith.

The game was fun and easy to pick up what to do quickly.

I liked the “powerups” which helped fulfill as many orders as you can before the customers left.

api.name: Cathedral Master Builder
api.description:
Welcome to the 13th century, a time when magnificent cathedrals adorn the skyline of every major city, except yours.

You’ve been appointed as the CATHEDRAL MASTER BUILDER by the bishop to build a cathedral before the century’s end.

The bishop✝️ provides money💵 for your project. Visit the TOWN🏘️ to recruit skilled workers for your BAKERY🥖, SMITHY⚒️, and MASONRY🧱. Procure iron🧲 and stone🧱 from the MARKET🧺.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Finally completed in 1305, a bit late but we got it done!

The Smith and Bakery were not needed after a while as I had so much resources from those, came down to hiring and firing the right Masons. Did not seem I had much to control other than getting the best workers and making sure to buy bricks and magnets.

All worked well but needed some more options.

The books and stats were a nice touch and can see a lot of effort went into that detail.

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Thanks a lot for playing and the feedback. Yes, agree if you are playing it casually at some point you do not need to do that much anymore and just wait. I already expected that balancing it is a difficult task and planned some more time for playtesting. However, I think you know how time flies in a game jam :).

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: Very complete presentation. I didnt last enough to see it completed because I didnt feel engaged to do it. Maybe drawing the cathedral with lots of emojis could had made it more fun.

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Thanks for playing and the constructive feedback. Yes, agree. Some more animations would have helped.

comments.author: Grav
comments.text: A nice, complete game about timing the market. Presentation is great and not so tough so as to become frustrating. Knows what it is and executes that well. Good work. 🔥

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Thanks a lot for the nice feedback! Glad to hear you enjoyed it.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
G’day!

Completed construction in 1290 :).

Using emojis as graphics is a good idea and looks good, I liked it! I also liked the animation of the amount of resource extraction that appears above each building.

I can say that I immediately understood the idea of the game, but I did not immediately understand all its mechanics. For example, I did not immediately understand that there will be no progress if there is no money, and only after a while I understood that the gameplay goes sequentially from left to right, passing each building, where a complete passage represents a completed round. Once I figured this out, managing the construction became much easier because you already know at what stage you need to save money and when you can purchase resources.

During a long game, the sound becomes a bit boring and tiring… I had to turn it off :). In my opinion, a sound that would draw less attention to itself would be better, something simpler, calm tones. I also think that maybe some construction sounds would improve the gaming experience.

Although the topic is somewhat general, the idea of the game and its implementation is good!

It was simple and fun to play, thanks!

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Thanks a lot for playing and the feedback. Finishing the cathedral in 1290 is very good! Fully agree to all of your feedback, especially the music. KBs and time was running out :). I had some things planned to make it more obvious how the game runs, but again: No KBs and time left :D.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • I liked that you could manage the locations, including firing people and seeing their output
  • I liked that the people got old and died so you couldn’t just camp with a good set
  • I liked the different stats and abilities of each person so you could hunt down really good ones

Feedback:

  • I wish there was a way to sell excess food. I had like 4k at one point and just removed all workers from bakery to save money instead of selling it
  • Once you’ve got enough money, iron, stone, and workers there’s not much else to do. I think I just sat idle for the last 40% of the cathedral building
  • I’m not exactly sure what clicking on the Bishop location is suppose to do? Didn’t seem like I could manage the location or do anything with it really
  • I’d be nice if there was a way to “save” a worker in the Town. There’d be times where I’d look at the market, see a good worker, then need to fire a worker from the location to free a spot up, then go back to the Market and the worker would be gone due to year cycle

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Cool, thanks a lot for playing my game and the comprehensive feedback. I really liked working on it using Kontra. Yes, the balancing of the game might need some tweaking. In the bishops location you could only check the stats of the Bishop (how much money he provided) and nothing more. Fully agree to “saving” workers. I was also thinking of a way that you could much faster fire your workers when you see a better one, but had no time to add this feature.

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text: Nice Game, Good Work!!! I enjoyed playing it, it would have been good if there was a mute button for the background music.

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Thanks for playing and the nice feedback. Yes, the music is a bit repetitive :).

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Interesting time management game with simple enough rules. Some functions aren’t very simple and obvious, but in general game has not a bad design. After first time it became boring, not so many options. But anyway, good job.

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Thanks a lot for playing and the feedback. It was very difficult to balance the game and make it still exciting in the limited time I had to playtest it. So I am not so surprised that it might be boring the second time you play it. Tells me again that I should plan more time for playtesting :).

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text:
timing is issential to get a good deal, fluctuations being random just make it simply wait for a relative low value for a product and just spam buy. maybe next version make it related to how much you buy or add other markets competing with better prices 2 or 3 offers maybe. the personnel cards is cool i liked it though its clear that almost always a couple no-go due to negative personalities lol. graphics use emoji smart way, layout is simple, nothing much is needed to grasp the idea and click event ease the control having just one option of controls make things very simple for players not much to do really go focus on a plan and just click here and here. good overall gameplay, would be nice to have a mute button in game but music is soft and not disturbing.

you can consider adding a speed modifier to make things not go boring, but I think its more as a tycoon game, you can try to add more things to make players last more playing it again and again.

good luck

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Thanks a lot for playing and the comprehensive feedback. Agree, that there are still many things to tweak. I also felt that the market prices do not really come through the whole game. I like your idea to make this less random. Yes, I think there is still a lot of potential to add more stuff to make it more engaging, especially in the late game.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Hey, neat idea! The presentation was nice, good use of the emojis, but really nice thinking on all the ledgers. Felt really in theme.

The flute song was nicely done, but after a while it was a little much, and the game did last quite a while. Maybe next time leave a gap in between repetitions? If you had a few UI bleeps and bloops for opening the various windows, that would have covered in between.

The actual game was perhaps a little passive. I zoned out a few times and still completed somewhere around 1290. I think it works as a sort of idle game, but it still felt like it needed more pacing. Something to throw you off and delightfully surprise you every now and again? Maybe the deaths and new clergy weren’t celebrated enough to act in that capacity.

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Sorry, I am a little late with my feedback, but I just realized that I forgot to answer. I think everybody who played my game and commented deserves an answer, even though it is a bit late. Thanks a lot for playing my game and this feedback. Fully agree to your comments. The sound was put together pretty quickly and I was happy to have at least something :). I planned to have some more unexpected events, such as disasters where you would need to react to, but had not enough time (and space) to implement it. Fully agree that the death of the bishop should be “celebrated” a bit more and maybe have much more impact than it currently has.

comments.author: Jedidiah Weller
comments.text:
Hi there,

Good work on completing this in time! It was a good concept, so let me give some feedback and suggestions:

  1. Controls - the clicking piece is easy; however, it felt jarring to instantly go into each screen (especially with no tutorial or gradual introduction of elements). I’d suggest adding a fade-in / fade-out, as well as a button-pressed state for each button so we can see what is being clicked
  2. UI - The UI needs some work on this one. First suggestion would be to use a tool like this https://uxplanet.org/best-three-color-scheme-for-music-player-5da996d7f460?gi=403ce9d9bec0#:~:text=There%20are%20some%20patterns%20what,very%20edges%20of%20
  3. UI - After a better color selection, the game would benefit from some sort of ‘focal’ separation. Right now, it’s not clear where I should focus my attention. Best way is to either introduce elements one at a time, or divide groupings up so they’re not all present visually at the same time
  4. A lot of the other commenters added good comments about gameplay mechanics, so I’d suggest taking a look at those (my feelings are similar). I think you have a strong mechanic base; just need to find different way of presenting it.
  5. Bonus points for documented README
  6. Bonus points for good code structure and file naming!

Overall it was a good submission, and I look forward to seeing what you do with it next. Hit me up on twitter @jedihacks for any discussion!

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text:
Sorry for this late reply. I really appreciate that you took your time in providing me such a detailed feedback! Some answers to your feedback:

  1. Fully agree that the controls do not feel really responsive and you do not get a feedback from it.
  2. Yes, colors and UI design are not my strongest points and I also do not like to work on it :). When I am under pressure I tend then to do all the other stuff which is more fun to me :). Thanks a lot for this suggestion, I think with such articles I can easily make it more appealing to my audience.
  3. Agree the screen is a bit messy and it is hard to find your way through it.
  4. Agree. I could also feel a lot of weak points in it, but just ran out of time.
  5. Cool thanks, happy to see that some people appreciate that :).
  6. Thanks. I think I did a terrible job in optimizing the code for size, but at least it is readable :). Not sure what will happen with it as I usually do not revisit my jam entries. However, I really like the general concept. So let’s see. Thanks!

api.name: The 13th King's Odyssey
api.description:
In a shadowy realm of the 13th century, a once-mighty king finds himself trapped in his own castle. Now, he must use his wit and uncover ancient secrets to escape the castle’s ancient curse. Join him on his journey to break free in this enchanting tale of courage and magic.

The 13th King’s Odyssey is a hard precision platformer game. Where you play as an unnamed King. You need to help him to escape the castle.

Controls: Keyboard

  • Left (A or left arrow)
  • Right (D or right arrow)
  • Jump (Space)
  • Enter to next room (Jump or W)
  • Single Wall Bounce by jump to the wall and jump again.
  • Press Enter to Start or Pause the Game.

Tools used

  • js13k-typescript-starter
  • ZzFz

comments.author: Elliot Nelson
comments.text: Theme and graphics are on point. Gameplay and controls are both quite good, although maybe a little bit TOO hard – I tried for quite a while but I could not manage to get past that single spike patch on the 3rd level.

comments.author: Justin Sullard
comments.text: This game looks awesome, and I feel like with some work on the controls, and removing the screen-shake when you bounce your head (or fewer puzzles that require bouncing your head to traverse) this could be a really solid platformer. As it stands it was super frustrating, and I don’t think I ever made it beyond the 3rd level (the one with 3 tall skinny pillars) as the controls felt like it was as much luck as anything, and it eventually crashed twice during game play.

comments.author: Himanshu Bisht
comments.text: Thanks for playing my game and for your honest feedback. This jam was a great learning for me, as it was my first-ever game and game jam. I will try better next year. :)

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text: The graphics are fabulous, love the look. Really didn’t enjoy the gameplay though. Spent a few minutes trying to get past level 3 and it was just too frustrating to continue, sorry!

comments.author: CodePA77
comments.text: Pixel graphics are beautiful. Love the overall look of the game. As it’s mentioned in the description it is a Hard platformer so I take it like that. Sometimes it feels too hard, but also when the level is completed it gives a sense of achievement. It’s so hardcore that I managed to reach level 5 only. In my play, when I jump to a pillar it doesn’t hold and slips and I had to control that. I think with some tweaks it can be a very good platformer.

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
I love the graphics, great looking game. I also think all the right elements are there for a challenging platformer.

However, I do think the game was too hard. I think more emphasis on timing rather than pixel perfect jumps, and lighter gravity would help too. It requires a bit too much perfection to be fun, at least to me. Also a game like this benefits greatly from controller support. Looking at tools used, it looks like you used my starter kit, which has controller support built right in, but it was removed I guess? I can understand needing to make space, but I do think it would have helped a lot here.

Still I think fitting these graphics into 13k is a nice achievement, and with just a small amount of tweaking it would be a really fun game! If you tweak the gravity and put in controller support after the comp I’d love to play it again!

comments.author: xem
comments.text: jumps are too hard but great design

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: I felt the game was punishing on purpose rather than difficult (or fun). The graphics are super cool though!

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Hard precision indeed!

The sound and the graphics are well done and they achieve a good look and feel.

However, I can’t quite if it’s a camera shake or a performance problem (or maybe both?) but whatever that is, makes the game even harder. It distracts from the precision aspect of the game. In addition the gravity feels just a little too strong. Those two points make the controls feel a little clunky.

Take the above feedback with a pinch of salt - Of the “hard platformers” I enjoyed Super Meat Boy and that’s probably it.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
pretty hard platformer, with very neat graphics.

I like it!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
G’day!

Jumping from the wall is cool!

Very sensitive controls, very difficult to keep balance, could not play to the end…

In general, you can play if you tweak the controls, make them less sensitive… thanks!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Nice graphics, animations and sounds.

The screen shake is a little jarring and sadly I could not make it past the spikes on maybe 3rd level, I always fell into the spikes below.

I dont mind hard platformers but felt it wasnt my fault when I kept dying, improved controls / gravity would have really helped which I think will be quite easy to improve.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: Too hard for me, but very well presented.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
The graphics are just perfect.

Precision platformers are cool, such an underrepresented genre.

I feel like this game would benefit the most from gamepad controls, on the keyboard I just can’t.

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Well, control and physics are cursed) Maybe it’s the target, but it was really hard with this movement physics and jump inertia. Overwise in the game a quite good, it’s pretty and well designed. Also it’s possible that game is too fast on my PC (or it’s too fast for me)

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text:
Very nice! Really like the art style chosen for the game. It gives off a fittingly dark, medieval tone.

Gameplay-wise, the mechanics are simple and easy to pick up. The direction and goal of the levels is clear from one glance, which makes it easy to get hooked into playing the game. The platforming is a little bit floaty however. Pressing one direction only slightly can cause the character to move forward further than expected, which can make precision platform quite difficult. Since precision is necessary to move through the levels, this can make the game quite hard.

Besides this however, very nice game with a great mood. Well done!

comments.author: Himanshu Bisht
comments.text: Thanks for playing my game and for your nice feedback :). I do agree friction can be a bit low.

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Hey there!

Amazing game, the graphics, the atmosphere, the menu. Really the type of game you want to dive into. Plus, the levels are quite short, so you want to keep going, nice motivation for the players.

Too bad there’s no little music to play along! I’ll admit, I love hard games, but this one really got me, I got stuck on level 2 not understanding how to pass the first jump lol. Maybe a little tutorial on what mechanics are and how to use them on a step by step basis would avoid players rage quit quickly :)

Really neat job overall!

comments.author: Himanshu Bisht
comments.text: Thank you for your valuable feedback and for playing my game. :) This being my first jam, I learned a lot and will apply all the feedback in my next jam.

comments.author: Ania Kubów
comments.text:
What a delight to play!

I was immediately impressed with the smooth movements of the main character. It is also quite a feat to make you feel like you are ‘feeling’ the players jump moves, and I think this game does that well with the accompanying sound and reverberating visuals.

It was a bit hard at points but overall very impressive!

comments.author: Himanshu Bisht
comments.text: I’m glad to hear that you had a good time playing my game. Thank you for your kind words and feedback. It’s much appreciated! :)

api.name: Castle Attack
api.description:
Welcome to “Castle Attack,” an exciting medieval-themed game. In this game, your mission is to destroy chests strategically placed within various castle forts using a ballista. Here’s how it works:

Controls

  • Use the arrow keys to adjust the angle of your ballista.
  • Press the spacebar to launch projectiles from the ballista.

Objective

In each level, you’ll find three chests hidden within the castle walls. Your goal is to obliterate these chests. These chests contain the treasures of rival kings! By destroying them, you’ll weaken your competitors and enhance your own wealth and power.

To advance to the next level, you must destroy at least one chest in the current level. Challenge yourself by aiming precisely and strategizing your shots, but remember, you have a limited supply of projectiles for each level. The number of projectiles and their types vary from level to level:

  • Boulder: A standard, solid projectile.
  • Time bomb: A projectile that detonates three seconds after being launched.
  • Impact bomb: A projectile that detonates upon impact.

Choose your projectiles wisely and conquer each level to become the ultimate castle attacker! Best of luck in your medieval adventure!

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text: Great execution of familiar gameplay! Making the last level’s first boulder hit the chest just right through a small opening, at max ballista elevation and speed, is a great little detail, and was a hit, no pun intended, with the junior playtesters in my household.

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
Wow! Fitting these physics in 13k is very impressive! Really fun game. I got all chests except one in level 5 and one in level 7. I love how the pieces show damage with a texture update before they fully break.

Often times my first shot in every level immediately went to 100% power, which was pretty frustrating. Maybe an issue with a timer not getting reset or something? Not sure.

Otherwise it was a really fun game! Nice job!

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Love the physics here :D Impressive!

This game held me for quite a while.

The sounds are a bit harsh but otherwise well done. The controls are really easy to grasp.

I only realised we had different types of projectiles on the 4th level, which only made the game better.

Well done.

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: Nice level design and level progression. Cool variety of weapons - the latest stages were challenging and fun - I managed to clear the 6th and 7th stage perfectly on my first run, but the fifth stage was hard! The only thing I wished was the traction string to be white and more visible.

comments.author: Johan Åhlgren
comments.text: Impressive in 13k! Lovely “Angry Birds”-inspired game. Good job!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I enjoyed playing this game. The graphics were good, I liked all the background elements as much as the foreground elements.

Reminded me a lot of angry birds.

The physics worked well with all the crashes and explosions.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: Great game, it brought me back to my childhood playing Gorillas in my first PC. Impressive technical work to do a physics engine.

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Interesting, though not very realistic physics, and technologically far beyond the 13th century. Anyway, I couldn’t stop until I had played all levels.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • Was really fun to destroy the castle. Physics were great
  • Great job on making the shots require more skill as the levels progressed
  • The different projectiles and their ordering was a nice touch
  • Castle design and background were really nice

Feedback:

  • Would have liked a power bar so I knew how much power I was giving the shot as I held down the spacebar. That way I could repeat with consistency shots, especially when doing the same level multiple times
  • The castle walls falling sound was a bit loud, especially as it stacked on itself

comments.author: Frederic Kyung-jin Rezeau
comments.text: Fluid physics and well balanced levels. “Crush the Castle” in 13k! Impressive to see a well executed physics game fitting within the competition constraints. I played all levels and wished for more.

comments.author: Tom Harris
comments.text: I loved this game! Had me hooked. Great physics and fun, challenging gameplay. I’d play more of this game if there were more levels ;)

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: very much room for improvement into making a very lasting interesting game, like 500 levels or so and will still be nice, keep up.

api.name: Mini Merchant
api.description:
You are a merchant who has to sell goods to the people (and crabs) of the village.

Use your mouse to interact with the world. Drag and Drop items near the store to sell them.

Read more about possible interactions by clicking on signs.

You have a short life, so you have to sell as much as possible in 7 minutes.

Tip: Prepare more goods in your yard in advance. Hire cyclops for help, they are not smart, but an extra pair of hands is never a bad thing.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Kenney pixel art graphics worked well and look good in the game. Enjoyed playing this.

Sound effects were good. I liked the dragging stuff around mechanic of the game, even with the getting stuck on obstacles part.

Found it hard for people queuing up to actually hand them the stuff they wanted, wasn’t sure exactly where to drop it.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Loved this. It looked great, it sounded OK, but most of all it was a fascinatingly new experience for me. Love that you didn’t tutorialize the whole thing up front, I had a few AHA! moments while playing that were super rewarding.

A+ game!

comments.author: Elliot Nelson
comments.text: Great little game, The 7 minute time limit really forces you to evaluate how long each action takes and prioritize. My high score so far is 159!

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
This was a fun and very unique game. I like the idea of having the weight of the objects come into play!

My biggest frustration was that the mouse cursor is only captured over the canvas rather than the whole window. If my cursor ever went one pixel out of the canvas I lost control. This made the game quite hard when dealing with items near the sides. I also felt the bouncing sometimes was too much and made it hard to fit things through the small gate opening, but thats also just part of the challenge of the game!

Overall, still really enjoyed this one, and very unique! I have not played a game like this before. I really like time management style games, and this is a really cool idea for one!

comments.author: Alexey Kalmakov
comments.text:
Thanks for the detailed feedback, Rob!

The cursor management is something that I definitely need to fix in the post-jam version.

PS: I used your TS starter pack, thanks for it!

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text:
Despite the simple gameplay at glance, the game surprises with the content amount and challenges you read all the signs :) Also drag mechanic was unusual and had to get used to it (but was a bit annoying especially after the gym upgrade).

Love the art and sound. Managed to score 111 :p

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
Really liked the idea! Moving things around to place them is a cool twist for a shop keeping game.

I wish the houses had big icons on them so it would be easier to know what could be produced on each one (instead of having to read messages). A timer would be cool (to know how more time you have to finish each order on time). I also miss the possibility of customizing the layout somewhat - moving super heavy objects out of the way is cool, but some things cannot be moved and the space between is so tight you cannot avoid getting stuck on things (like fences).

All in all, a very nice game :)

comments.author: CaptainAligna
comments.text:
I really enjoyed this game!

Gameplay is simple and clear! It requires a test playthrough first to understand the game, which is fine!

Because it’s a small game for a gamejam, the time limit was a very smart feature to add since it makes us want to play again and get new highscores~

The hand strength mechanic really caught my interest and the only thing I feel lacking is some sort of clarity on how long the client will wait and want to leave.

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
I felt dragging the objects was needlessly cumbersome. I think it’d have worked better with an overcooked-style gameplay.

That said, everything else was really really good. The pixel art was beautiful and synergised well with the game. The audio was very responsive too.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
I like the concept, I probably need a few more goes to get more efficient at the preparing of good in advance, I took advantage of existing stock too much early on.

The physics feels really good, nice drag feature to move stuff about.

Graphics are really great, love the pixel art look.

Great game, 7 minutes flew by.

comments.author: Luis Gurmendez
comments.text:
Loved it! Really nice art style <3. I loved this game although I have to admit it was pretty stressful! I couldn’t satisfy my customers. At first I didn’t understood the houses around my farm(?). The second time I played it it was easier since I tried to be have every material in stock.

Great game loved it!

Just one comment the first screen was confusing with all the things moving around!

Impresive how you could build this in 13kb! Love the draggin aorund functionality

comments.author: Alexey Kalmakov
comments.text:
Thanks, Luis!

I couldn’t satisfy my customers. It’s ok, there is no way you can satisfy them all =)

Just one comment the first screen was confusing with all the things moving around! Sorry to hear it, probably I was so happy to make menu more live and dynamic, so I forgot to adjust amount and velocity =)

comments.author: Allen Schmidt
comments.text: Really cool concept! The art worked really well (even if it is just Kenney assets) for the theme, and I liked the shoehorning in of some elements (like cyclops and crabs haha). I think the collisions would have worked better if everything was implemented as circles, since that would have caused less getting stuck on things. It was a little tough trying to queue up items because the cyclops I hired kept just putting things right in the doorway, and grabbing items I was trying to use to make beer/food crates before I managed to move them to the right spots. Overall though well done!

comments.author: Alexey Kalmakov
comments.text:
Thanks for the feedback!

Circles for collisions is a nice idea, which I totally missed during the development and it shouldn’t be hard to implement. Maybe I can do that as an improvement for the post-jam version.

Cyclops are… bastards. It wasn’t planned like this, but there is no point in having more than one cyclop hired in the current version of the game. Is it a metagame? =)

Elliot Nelson (his feedback is above) managed to get a high score of 159 points without Cyclops at all. He also ignored clients for the first 3 minutes to focus on creates/beer production. After analyzing his play style I made 169 points in one run with one Cyclop.

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text:
What I liked:

  • The graphics are cute
  • The gameplay mechanic is quite fun
  • The controls drove me crazy at times, but it is also part of the fun
  • Sound effects, thank you!
  • The signs help understanding what to do

What I liked less:

  • The cyclops are half useful half painful (on my way sometimes)
  • The controls… even though it is part of the gameplay, bumping everywhere drove me crazy!

Overall, it is a super cute and well-made game. The controls are both fun and annoying :-D

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: I love it! This has some features that I initially thought I could fit in my own game actually. I love that you actually have to source the products from the surroundings, and that there is also second-level manufacturing. The gym idea is really funny. The graphics are damn cute as well as the sound effects, just missing the cute music! The dragging is intuitive and fun. I found my self a bit overwhelmed with customers, but I guess I just need to get better.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: Such a cool trading game! I love the management aspect of having a lot of stuff, but also having a path wide enough to be able to bring anything to the storefront. Things having different weights is a neat touch.

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

  • My first impression and understanding (before playing the game, only reading the description, screenshot and title):
    • I must say right before I checked on this one, I was out of another game that used the same tileset and was about selling stuff, so I’m biased towards that.
    • The cover art looks to me tiled based and I am going to expect I will be tossing things around. Those two white squares on the cover are intriguing as some sort of boundaries? 
    • Love the sense of humor of the description, and I have ONLY 7 minutes of life?? Oh no…
    • After I played and went back to the previous game, I saw that they mentioned you as teaching them ECS :) So now I know how your games are connected.
  • Great work on:
    • Having a working complete game!
    • The tight interactions with the world, it felt really polished.
  • I liked that you:
    • Made the interaction of the mouse an actual interface of selling an item. It felt really immersive.
    • Added the cyclops helpers. It was fun to try to beat them when I was trying to take something out of the shop or make some recipe. I was not 100% they were net positive to my games :D
  • You could improve (again, I’m aware you might already know all the below):
    • I had one problem during the gameplays where my items were getting stuck in dumb ways, even more when I hired cyclops to lift stuff into the shop. They were just throwing them at the door and making it impossible to push anything inside.
    • I really liked what you are going for here, but I think there are too many jobs for a single hand. I didn’t manage to keep the peace or make any kind of strategy to improve at the game. The amount of work was just unmanageable.

api.name: Dragon Simulator
api.description:
Live the life of a dragon on a small island

  • fly around
  • hatch eggs
  • terrorise villagers
  • catch and feed wildlife to your hatchlings
  • set fire to hungry leopards

Controls

  • Mouse: look/aim
  • Left Mouse Button: expel contents of stomach
  • Space: jump/flap
  • Hold Space: glide
  • WASD (on ground): Move
  • W (while gliding): Turn to match camera
  • Release W (while gliding and holding something): Drop

Flapping and gliding take the angle of the wing/body into account. Facing upward will brake, downward will accelerate, left or right will turn in that direction. Gliding is much easier to control than flying by flapping alone. Use of a mouse (not a track pad and definitely not touch/mobile) is recommended.

You can catch live prey by flying low overhead. If you do it right the dragon will automatically pick it up. Best achieved by swooping down on something on a ridgeline before it’s aware you’re there.

comments.author: Ariel Wexler
comments.text: Very cool game. The controls felt a bit clunky sometimes, but the concept here was really really cool.

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
I love the idea, and the dragon animations are really impressive. Unfortunately for me the controls were way too hard. I tried gliding, but I always just fell down. It felt like I could never get enough speed, or that gravity was too strong, or both.

Still, being a dragon and burning things was satisfying, I just wish the controls were easier. I think changing what W does is part of the problem. Imo, it should keep you moving forward even while in the air. I know that logically this doesn’t make sense ( you have nothing propelling you), but fun factor wise then I could just glide around and kill stuff, which would be cool.

Still, graphically impressive and the fire breathing is awesome!

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: The running animation is very fun! I love the grass and pebbles, look amazing while walking around.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Impressive animations and scenery, nice use of emojis to help with the limit on space.

Controls a little difficult, could be smoother.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: I had performance issues with it (looked like around 15-20 fps on a GTX3080). I like the tech but I wish there was more to do or some sense behind it. I am also not sure if the controls were bad due to poor performance.

comments.author: Crystal Parker
comments.text: I really enjoyed this, it’s impressive! The controls were good. My daughter says your flames looked like he’s barfing cheese, just thought you’d find that thought amusing too.
games: Journey to the East; Silk Road Adventure

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text: A bit clunky but very impressive. The 3D graphics and animation looks amazing for the size!

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • Trogdor!
  • The 3d graphics looked nice and the dragon was impressive
  • Enjoyed setting things on fire

Feedback:

  • I died right away to a Jaguar next to my nest. I didn’t understand it was attacking me. Couldn’t figure out what to do after I died so had to restart the game by closing it and reopening it
  • Controls were pretty hard. Couldn’t figure out how to control the dragon while gliding/flying
  • I never could make it back up to the mountain. I would try to flap my wings to get up the mountain but it would just turn me around and make me fly back down towards the sea
  • I also couldn’t figure out how to catch animals while gliding, just ended up burning the larger ones

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
Woah!

The main controls (running around, flying, and especially gliding) feel really nice. I liked the run animation, too, haha

As some others mentioned, swooping and grabbing something was a little too hard. Once I’m close enough to an animal to grab it, I’m already hitting the ground. Maybe if you could tweak it to “latch on” to an animal at a greater distance, that would be cool.

But yeah, just running and flying around the island was fun :)

comments.author: Leo Kuo
comments.text: That’s awesome to make a 3D game in 13kb! I think if there is a goal in the game to let player to achieve will be better. Also, the dragon cannot turn the direction while flying. A little bit hard to control the dragon especially when I want to go up to the hill.

comments.author: Francesco Bedussi
comments.text: Reallh cool 3D graphics, a little bit too difficult to control.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: The game is visually very impressive! Unfortunately it’s incredibly hard to control the direction of the dragon. It’s only loosely related to the theme.

comments.author: Jeremy Burns
comments.text: Great graphics and interesting idea! It was running at a pretty low framerate on both Chrome and FF on linux for me, and that might have affected the controls, since I had a real hard time controlling the dragon. It was fun to shoot fire though, and I can imagine it being really good with just a few fixes to the performance and controls.

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: After a very short time, the screen just turns white (no errors are shown, but it happens consistently for me).

comments.author: Chris Glover
comments.text: Almost certainly WebGL context loss. If there’s something else using your GPU in the background it can happen, although I’ve had it happen to me without that for certain particularly inefficient versions of the code, so I suspect it’s hardware dependent.

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Well, it’s not a game, more like prototype, but really impressive. 3d, animations, island, a lot of entities. Nice Job!

comments.author: Ania Kubów
comments.text: For some reason I was not able to get the game to load - the description sounds great though!

api.name: Upyri
api.description:
The first record of Vampires occurred in the 13th century. The anti-pagan treatise “Word of Saint Grigoriy” reported pagans worshipping Upyri, creatures that we now refer to as Vampires.

While the details of 13th century Upyri have been lost to history, you are unfortunately all too familiar with them. You are the latest to be sent to the castle as a sacrifice to the Upyri. Can you defeat the creature within and escape the castle?

Controls

  • WASD or Arrow keys to move
  • Mouse to look
  • Any mouse button or the E key for Action

Gameplay

Upyri is a horror themed adventure game where you need to solve puzzles to escape the castle. Explore and look for hints in the environment. Make your way into the Keep in the center of the castle, then the tower, and find the lever to open the front gate.

Bonus info

The symbol used as the games icon and featured in the game is based on the real pagan symbol for the devil. The pagan symbol for the devil is a circle with a crescent on top facing upwards, representing the horns on the devils head. Here the symbol is flipped upside down to instead represent the fangs of the Upyri.

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text: I love the ambiance this game evokes, it’s really sinister right from the start. The night sky is also incredibly well done! The game is challenging (in that you need to wonder around a bit to figure out the next steps) but not too much, so it keeps you engaged right to the end. The sound effects and music are the only parts that aren’t excellent, but given that this game includes a complete realistic 3D castle, including textures, it makes sense that certain things had to be heavily optimised in terms of byte impact.

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
Thank you so much! Ya there were a couple more things I would have liked to do music and sound wise, but ya as you rightly say there’s only so much space, and also only so much time.

Appreciate you playing the game, the kind words, and feedback!

-Rob

comments.author: Ariel Wexler
comments.text: Impressive piece given the size constraints! Really like the graphics and genre and ambiance of the game. I wish the sfx had some variation in playback rate to give some life to the footsteps (the #1 sfx in the game).

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
Thank you so much! I worked really hard to fit a game like this into the size and time limit.

The variable sfx rate is a good idea, and one I actually had in my game last year (Charon Jr.). The playback rate of the engine increased as your speed increased to make the sound of the engine revving up.

But ya, in the context of fitting the whole 3d rendered castle, hi res textures, shadows, etc etc, that just wasn’t one of the features I could keep. Such is the nature of the comp :)

Thank you for playing the game, the kind words, and feedback! -Rob

comments.author: 0ctothorp
comments.text: Impressive for a 13kB game. I got scared by the Upyri :D I wish the game let me walk faster 😅

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
Thank you! I really appreciate it! I did try to tune the movement speed to where it didn’t feel slow and monotonous, but that it also wasn’t a fast action game. The game is modeled after a “puzzle horror” type game like Amnesia, where it’s a little slower paced. I did also try to put in “completion skips” so that when you reached your goal, you could drop down for a faster route to the next goal and didn’t have to retrace your steps as much.

That said, when you are exploring I totally get wanting to get around faster, especially in the longer corridors or when hitting a dead end.

Thank you for playing the game, the kind words, and the feedback!

-Rob

comments.author: Chris Glover
comments.text: Wow, that castle is an amazing achievement! Suggestion: A little bit of fog in your shader would add a lot of atmosphere and would only be a few bytes

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
Thank you so much! The fog idea is a great idea, and it had crossed my mind but I just never got to it.

Thank you again for the kind words and feedback! -Rob

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: Wow, nice looking game but was basically a slideshow on my laptop right from the get-go. It’s a shame because I was looking forward to playing this one.

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
This is really unfortunate. Every year I test on my 2015 Macbook as sort of a performance test. The game ran alright on my 8 year old macbook and I considered that good enough given the constraints of the comp. If it’s a gpu bottleneck, there’s not much I can do, but likely it’s a cpu bottleneck, which I could improve with a little extra space and time by building out a larger grid for the broad phase of the collision detection. But since I was very tight on space and time and it worked on my laptop, I didn’t get to it.

I hope you’ll be able to give it a try in the future!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
I like the 3d graphics, and that the sounds are synced to some special events. (Seeing the blood…) Also the castle is very well designed for the gameplay, for instance, when a trigger is pulled there is a shortcut to get back down by jumping instead of walking back the whole way.

Talking of “walking”: I’d really love to be able to jump (even if its not needed to escape, but this could be used in leveldesign aswell. Also the ability to run. (In combination with jump)

It’s a bit short, but really entertaining up to the end. Very well done!

Edit/Update: I was wondering, what shock-moment the other people were referring to, and so i started it once again. This time leaving the stake behind. What a twist! Nicely done, i even got goosebumps although i was prepared for “something” :-)

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
Thank you so much! I also really appreciate you noticing and calling out the level design. Integrating actual level design is quite hard in a 13kb game (and in 30 days), and I put a lot of thought into making it as nice as I could given the constraints.

The game originally had jumping, but it introduce a few problems like being able to jump out a window to escape, being able to jump over the wall to escape, and needing to add ceiling collision detection to the game.

It does make sense though, could have some obstacles or gaps on the castle walls as an extra small challenge, and would give the player more agency. So, I agree it’s a good idea, just not one that I could fit into the game along with the whole castle, textures, shadows, upyri model, etc :)

I really love the edit/update where you went back and found upyri! My hope was that most people, even if they picked up the stake, wouldn’t think immediately to stab the cofffin and would try pulling the lever first.

Thank you for the awesome review!

-Rob

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
I think the only thing I would add is a sprinting mechanic - if you run too much, you are at risk of awaking the Upyri.

I loved the fact that if you fail killing it, you are respawned close (so you don’t have to replay the whole game from the start).

As others pointed, fog would be a great addition for its atmosphere - or a single point light at the player’s position, to simulate a torch. Other than that the modelling, texturing (those clouds!) and the sparse sound (played always at the right moment) are awesome :)

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
Thank you so much! I really like the idea of having sprint but limiting it by having it alert the upyri. Makes for quite a nice jump scare opportunity while have a balance of movement speed. I considered having a sprint that would only last like 2 seconds and then you’d “get tired” and stop, but that felt lame.

The upyri scare idea solves the same goal but doesn’t feel lame. Could also be extended to where you can sprint all you want outside on the grass, but inside it was a risk. An idea that would be reinforced by the different footstep sounds. Then you can sprint to the edges of the map outside and then just slow down once you go inside.

I also was going to have the player hold a torch in the original design, and ideally have it be the light source for the shadows. But point light shadows are a bit tougher than directional, and I just ran out of time and space.

Thank you again though for playing the game, the kind words, and great feedback! -Rob

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Well that was a surprise! That was a lot of 3D to pack into such a small package, and great work with the procedural texturing. Next time, focus on the palette to give it a little more life. Get more contrast out of your hues, e.g. put some purple in your ambient, then light it with teal moonlight.

The gameplay was maybe a little too simple, but it kept me going to the end. I got a kick out of the different footstep sounds for different flooring materials :)

comments.author: Viljami Peltola
comments.text: Fantastic looking game!! Gorgeous! I wish I could walk a bit faster. It was a bit dark in the beginning but I turned the display brighter. I didn’t finish the story. Probably the best looking game so far.

comments.author: Alexey Kalmakov
comments.text:
Gosh, I guess 13kb is enough to scare me. This упирь was so spooky that I asked my wife to play this game after me to check her reaction. It’s the first js13k horror I played and it’s amazing! Nothing to add, really.

Looking forward to seeing postmortem on this one!

Oh! And was surprised that in the end of game (when you walk form castle) there is no upir to follow the player =( Please add one.

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
Wow, thank you so much! I love that you had your wife check it out to see her reaction. The fact that the game made enough impact for you to share it means a lot, really appreciate it!

I do have a post mortem, it’s on js13k resources page under the 2023 post mortems, but you can also just click the link to my website right here by my comment and the upyri post mortem will be right there at the top (as of right now it’s the latest thing I’ve posted).

I did think about having upyri float up from under the bridge as you cross it. I think it’s a really nice idea, but I also wanted the player to be able to escape. Still I do think there’s a hybrid approach where they do come after you but then you run faster and the screen fades out. Maybe you make it maybe you don’t, the player doesn’t know for sure.

But ya thank you again for the kind words, and for playing and sharing the game!

-Rob

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Goddamnit man.

“It’s 13Kb how scary can this game be?”

“Oh, look some blood… ooooh spooky. Hah.”

“Looks like it’s just a moody puzzle game”

“Victory is at hand… one more lev-OH NONONONONONONO CRAP CRAP CRAP”

This is my review, I typed it with my heart still racing and I’m not changing it.

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
Wow! Thank you so much. I think this is my favorite review so far as It captures exactly what I wanted to go through peoples heads when they played the game!

Seeing someone have the exact experience I wanted the game to provide makes all the hard work worth it. I really appreciate it!

-Rob

comments.author: Elliot Nelson
comments.text: Wow, compelling! Great atmosphere, nice 3D textures, decent audio (I like the different footsteps for different areas). The puzzles are simple but remind me a lot of classic 90s DOOM – “I see a lever over there, how do I get there” type of problem-solving. Great job.

comments.author: Armaan Mohammed
comments.text: I Never Thought I would see A 3D Horror Game IN 13Kb 🙀 The game is so well done and Even little things like the loud footsteps against the quiet background Makes The Game TRULY Terrifying. 👻. Overall one of the best games I have seen so far! 😃

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
I was looking forward to playing this one after seeing your progress updates. Very nice!!

I agree with others that the graphics and atmosphere are top-notch. The sky looked great!

One small thing– I kind of wish I couldn’t fall? I kept falling off of stuff and having to walk back up, haha

Very impressive!

EDIT: I was wondering about the symbol used in the game. Thanks for providing some info about that. Cool!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Wow, the 3D graphics were amazing.

I liked the castle layout and level. Even looking into the distance from the battlements to the hills and sky.

I found spiral staircases with blocked off doors, keys and such.

But found it quite hard to move around because I could either move with the keyboard or look around with the mouse, but not do both at the same which slowed my progress.

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text: Wow thank you for the compliments on the graphics! That’s very strange about the issue with the mouse. I haven’t seen that or heard it from anyone else. Do you have the same issue with any other game that uses mouse and keyboard like this?

comments.author: Marcin Walczak
comments.text:
I love the atmosphere. Visually impressive. From castle textures, to beautiful night sky. Generating svg textures was a clever idea.

Castle is well designed. Subtly placed shortcuts save you from unnecessary backtracking.

Sound of footsteps depending on surface is a nice touch.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: This is such a great game! The 3D is really impressive with awesome textures, lighting and shadows. The player can run up and down to several floors; I have no idea how you do it :) The sky is beautiful, and the game itself is super interesting and fun to play! Just to propose a small improvement, to me the sound of the player walking was unpleasant. Great job, congratulations!

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: The graphics look really great, though the strong shadows don’t really fit with the dark sky. Unfortunately, the game was really slow on my computer, I probably spent more time staring at the loading screen than playing it.

comments.author: Markus Fisch
comments.text:
Absolutely great!! Couldn’t stop until I made it! :D

And the graphics are so insanely good for just 13k! Wow!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
My first game on the computer (not including the default ones like spider solitaire, etc.) was “Prince of Persia: The Sands of Time (2003)”. Well, playing your game immediately reminded me of my first game, although the theme is different, but the game world itself… I really enjoyed it, thanks!

Also, I had a desire to try to make such a game for a long time, but I started my journey on the web, due to strange circumstances :D, well, and C++, where would you go without it)), but only console development, so you can say that I developed desktop hardly practiced.

And I thought for a while to try to switch to desktop development, with the help of all kinds of engines such as Unreal Engine or Unity 3D… but still I wanted to be able to not just work on learning new things again - which is undoubtedly a worthy cause, but I would like to immediately go to the most fun thing for me - creating a game :D.

And today I came across your game, at this contest, which I knew about even earlier, but I decided to try it only today. And how lucky I am!!!! I met your game! The game - which shows that a game that would have a game world like in the game “Prince of Persia” or even better - you can try to implement it even as a web game! And your game is a living confirmation of this!

Ohhh, I’m happy! I am happy that I decided to participate in this competition!

Well, now a little about your game :D.

I noticed that the sky is static. If the stars and the moon moved a bit, very little, maybe even different cosmic rays - I could admire it for a long time :).

I liked how I moved, but for some reason I always wanted to be able to jump into windows, that is, to have a jump and jump on small objects, I think I would have even more fun then!

When I first opened the sarcophagus of a vampire - I was scared :), but he paid for it with his life :D.

As I played the game, I noticed an interesting selection of text - hahaha, even this is fun aaahh))).

Thanks and congratulations!

comments.author: Jeremy Burns
comments.text: Fantastic work! The graphics are amazing, the ambiance is really well executed, the controls are smooth, and the collision is solid. A little bit of extra juice might go a long way with this one, like a slight head bob and footstep sounds, but even so it’s great as-is. I really enjoyed reading the post-mortem, and I’m looking forward to seeing where this engine goes next year!

comments.author: Tim Sulmone
comments.text: Game plays incredible smooth, and audio was great. Was really blown away by the graphics.

api.name: The Saga of Temujin
api.description:
A short demo of a future game which follows along the story of a famous Mongolian warlord, by the name of Temujin.

Controls: Click to select, hover to find out more information.

The game may appear difficult at first, but there is a path to victory. Apologies for the lack of completion. I am but a newbie at programming and unfortunately was not able to work on this project as much as I had hoped.

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
I wasn’t quite sure if the game was working at all, but then I figured out that your units are at the bottom right.

Sadly you can’t build anything or train new units (it seems?), but you can move the horse and the little guy around and kill the enemies.

Looks like it’d have a nice potential to be a tactical/strategy game.

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text:
Congratulations to this nice starting point of a game. You write that you are a newbie, but I think the game has already a lot of things in it. I liked the turn-based mechanics, but was missing a bit more instructions on how to play and what the goal is. I think some more information on what the goal of the game is would have helped. To improve the game I would work on the following things first:

  • Clear description on what the game is all about
  • Small animations, e.g. when attacking and getting / dealing damage, to better understand what is going on
  • Improving the controls and clarity on what you select… I did not like the two status bars on the right (hover, selected). Maybe, a dedicated screen where you can quickly look at the stats when you select them would help. The obvious things, e.g. health you could even show directly in the game.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Congrats on getting a game done in time for the competition! It looks interesting and I hope to see it completed in the future.

Feedback:

  • It took me awhile to figure out what to do. I could move the horse but I couldn’t figure out how to attack with it. Took me a good while to realize I was attacking things but they took 2 hits to kill them. Some type of feedback to show I attacked would be helpful, like showing a health bar and it going down when hit

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Congratulations on the game!

Recommendations: more intuitiveness (I didn’t immediately understand that I’m starting from the bottom), more context (it seems I have to beat those at the top, I rode up to them on a horse, but I didn’t quite understand how to win) - I think these two points could make the game complete ( that is, one where you can not only lose, but also win) - which would add fun.

I also think that pressing the spacebar or enter to complete the move, in addition to the obvious button that needs to be pressed with the mouse - would make management easier in my opinion.

Well, in general - continue to create if you are interested in it! Good luck!

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Well, simple turn based strategy prototype. Potentially strategy game can be built, but for now it’s a little boring puzzle-like game. But, not a bad try

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text: I don’t know what to do in this game. I have tried it a couple of times but since I have no idea what the goal is, I am just randomly moving on the map. I died a couple of times, sometimes I am not even sure why. Overall, it seems like a super early version. I was able to move on the map and fight with a bunch of other characters, so the basic mechanics are working. A message explaining what I am supposed to do would have helped a lot. Anyways, good job making something that works, especially as a newbie programmer!

comments.author: Ania Kubów
comments.text:
Well done making enough of a game to submit!

I can see how you plan on developing the game and therefore I am excited about seeing how this unfolds further.

With the game so far I enjoyed the retro feel and innovation!

api.name: Tavernity
api.description: Experience what it’s like to be a 13th century tavern owner. Serve patrons their drinks in a timely manor, lest they get angry! Oh, and watch out for moles.

comments.author: Daniel Cohen
comments.text: This is a nice complete game. Some omnipresent, persistent drinking music would have completed the ambiance. I was a little confused originally where the beer was being placed because the size of the “avatar holding the beer” looked like it was going to go to the tile to the left. But was so surprised when you could hold two glasses at once! Also, the increase in difficulty was really well done, and speaking as a former bartender - I feel like not much has changed in the centuries since :) Well done!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Nice idea, a lot of frantic keyboard bashing! Well made, all worked fine, very challenging once both rooms were open.

My first go I ended up killing everyone and having the place burned down ooops!

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
I wish the camera wouldn’t follow the character - I got a bit of sickness.

Other than that, this game is fun! I wish I could murder some of my patrons with that sword though :P

comments.author: Johan Åhlgren
comments.text: A nice game! Frantic, and I got more hooked than I initially expected. I like how the area opens up, which makes it more difficult but how can also put out the fires and repair the tables. Well done!

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: I must admit: When I first played the game, I wasn’t really convinced. (Perhaps I had already played to many other games that day.) But now, that I have played all the games, and recall them, this is one of the games I remember best. Even though it’s not “impressive”, it actually has everything a good game must have, and by now I think that if I’d create a list of my favourite games of this year, this game would be on it.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
I really enjoyed this one right off the bat. It’s like the missing follow up to Tapper!

Looked ok, sounded ok, played great. I did get overwhelmed pretty quickly, I think one level after the fires started, but I had fun doing it.

comments.author: newman s lanier
comments.text: A solid effort. And, the gameplay is actually pretty good. The style is a real throwback. Once I got the hang of it, it was pretty fun to play. It’s simple and good. Well done!

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Wow, really cool game. I liked it a lot! Very polished and complete game which feels really good. There is not much which could be changed to improve it. The main thing I would improve is to add some music. I think some fast and nerve wrecking medival music would really fit here and add a lot to the atmoshpere. MAybe also some tavern background noise would be cool. I also like the art a lot with one exception: It would be really cool if the guests would have been in the same art style and not just stick figures. These are just a few minor things I noticed. Besides that, this is a very cool entry! Congrats.

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Oh, crazy timing game Good balance, you need to take the orders faster and faster. Funny mechanics with angry visitors. Good job.

comments.author: Jedidiah Weller
comments.text:
Hi there Benjamin!

Great job on this submission, it was a very intuitive game and well done! Here’s my feedback:

  1. I really enjoyed the simple UI and I was pleasantly surprised when the character started turning angry and burning the table. I lost the first attempt because of this, but I wasn’t mad!
  2. I didn’t realize at first that a mug had to be filled - It would be nice to have a conditional statement on the first time they pick up a mug that says “fill me here” so the player understands that mechanic. I figured it out through trial and error but having the player be taken through a mini tutorial is always a good thing
  3. Really enjoyed the overflow concept - a good way to expand the map!
  4. The graphic asset for the sword rack is a little hard to see, might be worth expanding that to take up 2 tiles so it’s easier to visually see it.
  5. Good keyboard bindings
  6. The music and sound effects could be enhanced - I didn’t get a tavern ‘feel’ without any music.
  7. GREAT work on using eslint and prettier! You get bonus points for your code structure and documentation.
  8. Also great work including a test directory for pathfinding - extra bonus points there!

Overall, really great submission and I look forward to seeing what’s next! Feel free to hit me up on Twitter/X @jedihacks to have a discussion!

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
https://dev.js13kgames.com/2023/games/tavernity

Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

  • My first impression and understanding (before playing the game):
    • The devil’s burning table is a big setup for the cover art and tells me I might be in for some real dynamics in this (rogue-like?) tavern ownership.
    • Haha, I had in mind the devil would show up to burn my tavern, but they were just mad customers :)
  • Great work on:
    • Have a complete working game! The core loop is there!
    • Got a whole level progression going on there. The challenges ramp-up significantly with the moles and more rooms to take care of.
    • Nailing the layout of the tavern and how the guests feel like customers. Even though there was like 10 people on the initial levels, I had the impression of a busy pub evening.
  • I liked that you:
    • Made sure the main mechanic of the game – collecting, filling and delivering glasses – was really well done. It behaved exactly as I expected, with a really nice surprise when I learned that I could carry two glasses at the same time. I was even more happy when it did the correct thing of collecting the empty glass from a table and delivering the filled one from the other hand. That made the game so much fun and it made me really in control of the situation.
  • You could improve:
    • This might be really particular, but I think the game was trying to make me fail too much? When I was handling the mugs and trying to be strategic on when I took them and when I filled them, I was having fun. It was tight but I had some space to improve. The moment a mole came in or a customer burned out a table, I was just too busy to take care of them, I had no way to strategise, plan ahead and keep the knife or the water bucket next to me. My best score was 65 after three attempts.

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text: The game becomes quite hectic after a while. Pretty fun though. Got me thinking of Tapper on the C64, even though they are not really that similar. Since the movement was grid based I found it a bit dizzying when moving back and forth between the barrels and the nearby tables.

api.name: 13th century 2048
api.description:
In 13th century,lords were expanding. Lets play as a lord in this 3*3 2048 game to make your beautiful castle. Oops! Someone ill-intentioned.

Drag with your mouse to merge tiles, go to top 2048 level castle. However, someone don’t like you, the purple army will let your castle level one divided by two. Careful!

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: An interesting twist, though the smaller board and the deleting tiles make the game much harder (or even impossible?, if I calculated correctly, even a 1024 would require extraordinary luck), I only got to 256. I would have liked keyboard support, and a moving animation instead of having the tiles to appear every time.

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: twist of reducing level is a good addition but was appearing more than the upgrade tiles

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text: Ok game.Not sure why sometimes the board would not move to a direction. Graphics very simple. No sound.

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Could do with some feedback sound and animations for moving the squares.

I felt like the -2 blocks were a nice addition.

comments.author: Rémi Vansteelandt
comments.text:
Keyboard controls would have been great. Given you have mouse controls, mobile support could probably also be added fairly easily.

I would have also loved to see animations when the tiles are moving, otherwise it’s sometimes hard to figure out what happened (even though I played 2048 a lot in the past)

comments.author: Jeremy Burns
comments.text: Took me a little while to figure out what the controls were, but after I figured them out it was a fun take on 2048. The “-2” tiles require some extra planning, which is nice. An explanation of the controls and some basic sounds with something like zzfx would go a long way towards polishing this up into something great.

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Not the most innovative game, but I liked that you added a twist to an existing game. I would defenitely work on the animations. It feels really strange that the tiles always pop up. I would make sure the animations also fit the movement of the tiles (left, right, up, down). It was a bit difficult to follow the movements and understand what really happened. Some sound effects and music would have also been cool. Anyway, congratulations to your entry!

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

  • My first impression and understanding (before playing the game, only reading the description, screenshot and title):
    • It seems to be the 2048 game, where I merge tiles, but with a spin-off of negative tiles. I’m wondering if those tiles are multiplication or not.
  • Great work on:
    • Having a complete finished game!
    • Customising each card as the growth of your reign!
  • I liked that you:
    • I really felt the pain of having a 256 castle being torn by a -2 sword in a whim! This twist added some flavour to it.
  • You could improve (again, I’m aware you might already know all the below):
    • Games are very much judged “by the cover”, so at first when I saw your screenshot I was not sure about how the game would go. Now that I know it works fine, I think you could have a much better shot (the github readme has a much better one) and sold the game better.
    • The interaction with mouse works, but it’s not ideal. I think the swipe action is ideal for the mobile only.
      • I don’t think you can actually get to 2048 with a 3x3 board.
      • My game was over in a valid position. All the tiles were set, but I could still merge a 2 and get going.

api.name: Dynasty United
api.description:
Your army overwhelmed by the attack of the alliance of the 3 kingdoms.

You will act as the Mongol Empire ruler Genghis Khan.

Your army discovered a secret path.

Follow the path and execute the alliance leader Cao Cao.

Enemy Bases

  • left wing = Wu Dynasty
  • right wing = Shu Generals
  • center back = Shu Dynasty
  • main base = Wei Dynasty

Instructions

Click areas with red color in the left side to move army. There’s no going back. After that another display will popup.

Pick an army between cavalry, archer, and infantry to match up enemy armies. It similar to game rock paper scissor.

Restraints

  • cavalry << archer << infantry
  • cavalries beats archers
  • archer beat infantries
  • infantries beat cavalries

Hint: You can hover through enemy box to scout if your luck is bad :(

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text: Could do with a bit of sound. I think I got a bug in the game where I couldn’t move to the next red area :(

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text: It’s basically rock, paper, scissors, but with the Mongol army instead. Click one of the three red boxes at the top of the ‘map’ to start, and then pick which type of troop to fight with (cavalry, archer, or infantry). Depending on what the baddie picked depends if you win or not. If you do, you fight a few more times, then move to the next section and try to take down one of the generals. I couldn’t seem to progress beyond the pink section of the map, and it didn’t seem to matter how often you lost the battle.

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Hey there!

Great concept overall. With little design, you managed to create a world around kingdoms fighting. Too bad there’s no music! It would add so much to the game and the ambiance.

Also I’m not sure I managed to progress in the game, the fight only happened when I clicked in the same place and nothing really happened after winning it. Maybe a little bug.

I also feel that the hovering over the enemy pick is not a good idea, as I ended up doing it without realizing if it was normal or a cheat. (I’d personally remove it!)

Overall, cool concept!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: I don’t understand the game mechanics or the controls. I can always see the enemy’s pick so there’s really no challenge. I was only able to click on one red area. Clicking anywhere else didn’t do anything. I must have missed something, but I wasn’t able to play.

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Congratulations to your game. To be honest I did not get how the moving on the path works… The rock-paper scissor style game is a good idea, unfortunately you can only see the enemys pick when you hover over it. Not sure if this was by intention. I think the restraints should be written differently, right?: cavalry > archer > infantry > cavalry.

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: I think game doesn’t work properly for me, I can’t select any zone except cao cao Good try, but it’s really hard to understand what happens.

comments.author: Tom Harris
comments.text: Not very intuitive to play, nice rock paper scissors idea and theme.

comments.author: Jupiter Hadley
comments.text: I do like how simplistic this concept is - sort of like Rock Paper scissors - I do think there should be a cool down on checking the enemy’s pick, as it makes it too easy. Neat idea though!

api.name: Guardian Village
api.description: Click on the character to build a collection, barracks, arrow tower hospitals, houses, etc., and strengthen your village

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
The controls are a bit clunky. The game looks like it’d be fun with a little more time spent on it - I know you found the competition a bit too late :(

Good effort though!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: Unfortunately, there’s an issue with the game being contained in a very small container, so it’s difficult to play it. I understand you only had a few days to create it, but it looks like it has potential.

comments.author: Thiago Romão Barcala
comments.text: It feels like this would be a great game, but I couldn’t find more to do besides building things. The Job assignment doesn’t seem to be working. It took me quite some time to realize I could collect the trees and stones with the “remove” button. Maybe it would have been cool to just start without any resources in the bag, and then collect the resources to build something.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Alright, this looks really cute and has lots of potential. The controls are a bit confusing, as usually in this kind of games, you don’t need to click “move here”, you just click, and that’s where they go. There’s many confusing things in the UI, like it’s not clear why I’m earning money (the buildings?) and I can’t seem to assign jobs to the villagers. And I guess you planned on having battles in the future, but that doesn’t seem to happen yet. Great job on all the pixel art. As you said yourself, the game seems pretty incomplete, but I encourage you to continue and share it with us on slack once it’s a little more advanced.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Hope you come back next year and allow yourself more time, you have what could be a nice game.

Graphics are fun and I was able to move about and build.

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text:
A bit hard to give feedback, given the game is not finished. You were on to something though, all the ingredients to create a fun game seem to be here. With idle games like these, balancing it usually the most important thing to keep players engaged and challenged.

I hope you stil finish the title at some point in time.

Good lukc!

comments.author: Ania Kubów
comments.text: For the limited time you were able to use to create this game I am very impressed! You can see a lot of thought went into what this game will be - so you can definitely see the potential. I also enjoyed the retro feel to the game. Can’t wait to see how you develop this game further!

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Well, interesting prototype, but of course there is no proper target here. Just a strategy/city building game proto

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I like the concept of the game, and the graphics look good

Unfortunately the game didn’t work for me

I was able to click on several things and even drag trees and rocks around, but couldn’t get it to build anything or progress the game

comments.author: Frederic Kyung-jin Rezeau
comments.text:
This game would need more work to be completed but this is a promising start for an ambitious project.
