comments.text:
Very cool graphics, and fluid controls. Great execution!

Would love to see levels progression, rooms and a few more items/boosters to collect/steal.

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Hey there!

Incredible concept, loved it.

I would have enjoyed a bit more explanation, inside the game. Like controls being visible all the time, or an actual goal (can you lose this game?)

Having different levels would make it more replayable in my opinion, otherwise after 2 or 3 plays, you feel you’ve done it all!

The music is sick, really well done!

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text:
Interesting Robin the Hood implementatioin) It looks like some of the rich people are with tie. Not really diversed game, but playable.

Good job.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I enjoyed playing the game, the pixel graphics were great

It was fun to jump around and get get coins

I always ended up losing my life on the first stage, so I wasn’t sure what game progression there was, or what I was going to do with all the money I was collecting

comments.author: Magnus Lindh
comments.text: The pixel art and the music are fantastic! I don’t know which is the best way to steal from the rich but staying at the bottom seems to be the must lucurative and safe option.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: Love the graphics and the animations!

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
This looks fantastic! Awesome sprite work. Sounds nice too.

It plays… well, the moment to moment stuff, movement, collision, the juice when coins are dropped, it’s all great, but in its current state it doesn’t add up to much yet because there’s no arc, no goals. You need something to pace out the gameplay, highs, lows, etc. There’s a start and an end, but no middle.

This is an awesome jam game though, and you’ve nailed so many basics. Looking forward to what you do next!

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text:
Fun game! I felt like a winning strategy was to stay at the bottom of the level and blast anything that came down there.

One thought: If there were enemy archers or crossbow men this kind of strategy could be prevented since it’s not possible to jump enemy arrows when you have a ceiling straight above you.

api.name: Meandering Medieval Merchant
api.description:
Explore 13th century England as a merchant earning as much coin as you can. Hire guards to help protect you on your travels.

In combat, click and drag to move and aim respectively. If you are not aiming at an enemy you will guard instead.

comments.author: newman s lanier
comments.text: I liked the theme alot. 13th century merchant and, I think, using real places in England. The controls in the combat was cool, but I couldn’t figure out how to attack and aim. I liked the sound effects in the combat and made me nostalgic. Good effort. Thank you!

comments.author: Emre Guneyler
comments.text: sophisticated game, too many buttons and data to cover at one go.

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
I thought this was a really clever game. The mechanic where you could learn about neighboring cities was great, and the little icons telling you where which item was good to buy vs sell was nice. It was really satisfying to buy low and sell high! It was also cool when you could set up a nice trade route between places.

I think what holds the game back a little is the audio, which is a bit jarring, and the combat. It was definitely not intuitive to figure out how to attack. You should just let players click on the person they want to attack, rather than the convoluted hold down to point triangle within circle at the feet of the enemy until a yellow circle appears… Also the enemies and good guys looking exactly the same made it even more confusing.

I still enjoyed it once I mostly figured things out though. Going to give it another go to see if I can get a better rating!

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Sure, the game throws a lot of info at you in the first few screens but if you’re used to trading/hauling games it’s nothing too daunting.

The little icons near the city in the map view are very handy but this is the kind of game that I play with a spreadsheet beside me :D

The sounds are simple but they serve a good purpose.

I wish there was a “Buy all” and a “Sell all” for each item type.

Good effort!

PS: bonus points for the aliteration in the title :D

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • I liked the concept of going around to different cities trying to find things to buy/sell
  • The map with all the high/low price info was super helpful so I didn’t have to memorize which city to buy/sell things at
  • I liked that cities had a limited supply of items so you couldn’t just bounce between two cities to abuse high/low prices

Feedback:

  • Need a way to just enter the number of things I want to buy/sell, or a more efficient way to buy lots of items at once. Having to click the + button 89 times to buy all the items was very tedious
  • I didn’t understand combat at first. I started by clicking where I was standing and the direction towards the enemy, but that only moved me a little. I tried then clicking further away and dragging, which moved me a lot, but then I couldn’t figure out how to attack. Clicking and dragging towards the enemy didn’t seem to do anything. Took me awhile (almost died) to figure out eventually how I was suppose to attack the enemy
  • Wish there was a way to move to a city further than 1 away. I would be 3 cities away from where I wanted to sell an item but would need to click on every city along the way instead of just being able to go to the city I wanted in 1 click

comments.author: Nathan Long
comments.text: Clever, wound up playing it longer than I meant to. I did figure out that I could pretty much just sell everything in London for a profit…

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
Interesting concept. I enjoyed traveling around finding the best prices to buy and sell.

However, the world seems very static. The demands don’t change, the products I bought in the past remain out of stock forever. Traveling gets tiring after a bit, as it requires too many clicks. I’m pressing “return” so often. And PLEASE, give me a way to just “sell/buy all stock”. As it’s too tedious to wait 30 seconds to buy everything. If you improve the UX a bit and make the world more dynamic, it could be a really fun game.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Love the idea of the game. The map and travel worked well.

Got a bit confused when entering places as to what to do for the best outcome.

I’ll have to come back and play.

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text: The game concept is clear and well-aligned with the theme! I really enjoyed the trading system and the freedom to come up with your strategy! It would be convenient to have a “max/all” button for buying/selling items. Also, traveling among cities could be smoother, as the current process requires multiple clicks. The battle mode lacks a bit of clarity, and it can benefit a lot from having some instructions or more direct gameplay. The game shows great potential! You could also introduce more features like fluctuating prices/needs, buildings, active combat, population management, and others!

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text:
Great Job.

Really good interpretation of classical caravan like game (trader game) with all main parts, like trading with different costs and battles. Simple but interesting game with a lot of options.

I think it would be better with some luxury goods (more expencive) and limited trader capacity. For now it looks like little unbalanced. Also In one place I can’t move to neigbhor city, because it’s too far to the top (maybe I have to wide screen or didn’t find zoom in/out function)

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Oh man, maybe it was just the sparse blips, but despite looking nothing like one, this put me in mind of old Speccy & C64 games. What a treat! I had a blast travelling up the isles making a few bob along the way and showing bandits the pointy end of my sword. Thanks for laying out the trading opportunities too, it’s great not to have to guess at everything and take notes; made for a very approachable start.

Nice one!

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text:
What I liked:

  • The trading simulation is good
  • Fights could be more exciting but the gameplay mechanic is good enough for this type of game
  • Quite a big map!
  • Sound effects
  • The UI took me a few minutes to understand, but passed that it got easy to play.

What I liked less:

  • With a bodyguard it is fairly easy to win any fight ( I might not have time to go far enough in the game maybe?)
  • Minimalist graphics

Overall, it is a fine trading simulation, I had a fun time playing it.

api.name: CastleRun Maze
api.description:
A King in a medieval castle collects gold coins that are spread across the labyrinth. Collect as many coins as possible and don’t end up at the bottom of the game field.

Use arrow keys on your keyboard to move the player.

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text:
I didn’t think I’d enjoy this game but it was pretty cool, well done.

Unfortunately the algorithm didn’t work correctly after about 4 minutes and I got stuck on a part where there was no forward path :(

Also if you move far off the top of the screen… then you never collide with anything and the map scrolls forever, but eventually the hero appears again but inside a walled off room, leading to his eventual death.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
Interesting take on a labyrinth game. I haven’t seen the labyrinth moving before.

The sound effects are a bit screechy, and caught me by surprise.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I enjoyed playing this game, the jeopardy of not knowing if going after treasure would lead you to a dead-end that you would need to navigate out of to not get stuck was fun.

A well thought out and implemented game.

The sound of collecting the treasure was a bit loud and coarse.

Also the movements were a bit low resolution, perhaps moving freely would be better rather than a whole block at a time.

comments.author: Jeremy Burns
comments.text: This game was fun once the pace picked up! It would be nice if the game allowed you to hold down the keys to move, without taking into account the system’s pause-before-repeat-key delay, my fingers were getting tired of tapping when I finally died. Might also be good to not spawn gold in places that are sealed off from the player. Smooth animations, consistent held-key handling, and some more polished sounds would take this game a long way.

comments.author: Johan Åhlgren
comments.text: I liked this simple game, more than I initially expected. Simple graphics, that did the job. Well done!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: This gets quite hectic :) Nice game. Maybe it would be cool, if the king would run smoothly instead of jumping in steps?

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: Some games are tons of fun even when they are very simple and this is a perfect example. Nicely done! I enjoyed speedily moving around the maze as it descended. I was able to move ultra fast because it was locked to a grid, which I appreciated! I would have liked it less with tweened, slippery, analog style movement. The movement was perfect as-is, in my opinion, perhaps because I can type mega fast but have poor platformer timing. It made me feel good at the game, moving a zillion tiles per minute. =)

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
Nice challenge. The difficulty progression is slow but it quickly gets hard. I got 74 points.

The sound when you catch a coin is way too loud and sharp, it was painful.

The theme fit is a bit farfetched.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Good game, congratulations!

Score: 59 :).

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Interesting infinite maze game. Not sure, is it possible, but looks like there are options to get the trap path before you will see it. Sounds a little annoying. also would be nice to have controls with key pressing. Good job!

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

  • My first impression and understanding (before playing the game, only reading the description, screenshot and title):
    • It’s a 2d top that will be a pacman.
  • Great work on:
    • Have a finished working game!
  • I liked that you:
    • Had a randomly generated level but thought about the player experience and the need to have a clear path, good experience!
  • You could improve (again, I’m aware you might already know all the below):
    • While the speeding definitely starts to test my ability to path through the level, it’s hardly a learnable skill – there’s nothing indicating to me ahead of time that the path I’m going down is going to be the correct one. This means that at some point I would just unluckily take the wrong turn and be punished by randomness. That doesn’t make for a good player experience. Taking for example that Helicopter game or Flappy bird – games that are about collision and making you stuck – both make for a compelling experience because all the skill is on the player and the challenges that show up give the player enough time to decide. Failure needs to be designed to be on the behalf of player actions.
  • I took some screenshots here https://twitter.com/javascripl/status/1709294081199726709

api.name: Khan - Westward Conquest
api.description:
Khan - Westward Conquest is a deck-building dungeon crawler.

Can you, the Great Khan, defeat all the enemies standing in your way??

Every stage you win, add a new card or a new ability to your deck. Build your strategy, defeat your foes, but make sure to pay attention to what your foes are doing next!

Cards have different effects, ranging from dealing damage to strengthening yourself to weakening your enemies. These effects include:

  • Defend: Stops X attack damage, lasts for a single turn.
  • Enrage: Do 50% more attack damage, lasts for X turns.
  • Weaken: Do 25% less attack damage, gain 50% less defend, lasts for X turns.

If you forget, there is a “Help/Info” button, or hover over any of the D/E/W letters for a reminder.

Controls: Mouse/Touch
Platform: Desktop/Mobile (Best in Chrome/Firefox)

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: Nice wee game. It was confusing that I selected a card, clicked “end turn” and nothing happened to the enemy. Near the end of my game I discovered you actually have to click on the enemy to attack them, and that “end turn” actually skips a turn.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
I’m a sucker for card battlers, and this was not an exception. Great execution of the theme and gameplay. The game is balanced, and I had to play it 3 times before I beat it.

The music is subtle, and does not get in the way of gameplay. The sound effects make the game pop even more. The game plays well on mobile too. Great work.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: Classic gameplay but well presented.

comments.author: Grav
comments.text: Really great game, easy to understand but gameplay is challenging. I got so close, will try again to see the end. Nice one.

comments.author: Luke Stapley
comments.text:
Very fun card game. Felt like a real strategy game. I think the only issue I have is that when I tried to change the difficulty, the game wouldn’t scroll up. More of an issue with the site, I guess.

The only big issue is maybe having an option for reading the instructions at any time. Other than that, not bad.

comments.author: Chris Glover
comments.text:
Coming from someone who tried making a coherent card game for a jam (and mostly failed): congratulations on making something easy to learn, fun, and playable! Good variety of options too, which is hard to do in a competition like this.

If I had some constructive feedback, it would be that it could do with a bit more tactical depth somehow.

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Hey there!

Really well made overall. I’m impressed by :

  • the music, simple yet efficient
  • the small tutorial, good enough to get started
  • the design, pixel-art and yet gives a good atmosphere

It’s been really well thought and executed, well done! Especially the cards zooming in when hovering them, it feels really natural.

As improvements, I think that the information on the screen are a bit scattered everywhere, I wasn’t sure where to look to know when I can continue attacking or when I should stop etc… Similar to hearthstone, I think that the Stamina bar should probably a real bar, not just a number, so you actually see “I’m gonna run out of options, let’s wait a turn”

Really good game overall!

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Wow, really great game. There is not much I did not like. I really liked the gameplay and I think the game is also pretty good balanced. Maybe the first few levels are a bit too easy, but it gets gradually harder afterwards. The graphics are also great. Just the vignette effect, I would remove. I do not think it is needed. Congratualations to a really good entry!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A neat idea for a turn based card style game.

Graphics were good, and game mechanics worked well.

Once clicking on a card at the bottom, I wasn’t sure how I could use it what I could do other than click on end turn to continue.

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Card game in 13 k! Really a lot of options and several strategies in the battle. Nice Job!

api.name: Swords & Quills
api.description:
Swords & Quills is a grand (tiny?) strategy game set in 13th Century British Isles.

Make your nation the most powerful in the British Isles before the 13th century is over. Use your armies and ambassadors to ally or conquer other lands.

Controls

Mouse

  • Mouse wheel to zoom in/out
  • Middle click & drag to pan / Shift+Left Click & drag to pan
  • Left click regions to select them
  • Left click the Build icons to add buildings to the region’s empty plots
  • Left click the Unit icons to add units
  • Right click dismisses your current in-progress action (like placing an ambassador, or selecting where an army should be going).

Getting started

Fill out your title, name and nation name. Then pick one of the existing nations. Pick a large one if you want to have it easier - larger nations have more territories to build Castles, Mines and Farms.

Strategies

You can play aggressively, by investing in Farms and Armies and conquering your neighbours. Or you can play diplomatically, by placing ambassadors in foreign castles and purchasing their territories, or allying with them. Or a mix of both!

Time controls

You can Pause/Play/Play faster with the controls on the top right. Pause the game if it’s becoming overwhelming, but make sure to click Play as some actions only happen when the game’s time is ticking.

Armies

When you create an army it appears in the region sidebar as a square with the number of individuals in that army. Clicks it to move the army to another territory.

To the left side of the army list, you can merge and split your armies further.

Armies cost gold to train and consume food.

Ambassadors

Ambassadors increase reputation with the nation where they’re deployed. They do this by gifting your gold to that nation.

Ambassadors cost gold and they consume food from both your and the foreign nation resource pool.

Castles

Increase the region’s defense, provide seats for foreign ambassadors and tax the land to give you gold.

Farms

Produce food to support your (and foreign) ambassadors and armies.

Mines

Produce gold. You need this to train units and build buildings.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I liked to play this game! The map is great and I loved the way we can stop and accelerate the time. The controls and the logic are easy to understand, which is not always the case for this type of game! I have played several times but I have always been defeated quite quickly :( So, to me, the game is too hard and I would have appreciated the possibility to choose the difficulty.

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Hi Homer, thanks for playing!

The difficulty depends mostly on the size of your starting nation. There’s a random factor in how much the other nations love/hate you too.

I’m so glad you thought the controls were easy to understand. That was the bit I was most unsure of.

comments.author: CaptainAligna
comments.text:
Very solid gameplay, everything is very clear and easy to understand. I had a blast playing it and couldn’t stop until I won!

I found the game quite difficult if you start with a one region area, but after I started with a bigger region (green at bottom) the game became quite easy… so it serves well as a “choose your difficulty” feature.

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Thanks Aligna!

The size of your starting nation was meant to act as a difficulty choice indeed.

I think I should’ve made that a bit more explicit somehow.

Glad you enjoyed it!

comments.author: Chris Glover
comments.text: I managed to get a victory after a few goes. I was looking for a bit more strategy in the armies (e.g. trying to get a boost from defending or having a castle for example), but it seems like a straight numbers game. In the end I found just putting all your units into one giga-army was the best strategy for me.

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Thanks for playing, Chris!

The castles do give you some advantage when defending but in hindsight, I think I should’ve boosted the amount.

Originally I had 3 different kinds of war units and they became stronger as they won battles but I had to remove that because I ran out of space 😞

comments.author: Leo Kuo
comments.text:
Great job! I always love strategy game and this game reminds me of the Civilization series. It give me a sense of achievement of conquering lands. Also, the game provides pause and speed up, it levels up the experience!

In the game, I’ve been adopting a conquest strategy to achieve victory, and I find it a bit challenging to understand the purpose of the ambassador. Perhaps adjusting the game balance to make the ambassador’s importance more significant could be helpful!

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Thanks Leo, I’m glad you enjoyed it!

The ambassador used be way overpowered so I took the easy way out and had to bring it down a bit.

There’s definitely more balancing to do!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Good game! I actually played a bit!

The speed up time button came in very handy as normal time is a bit slow for me. In the end, I managed to win only in the largest nation, I tried in others, but there it is worth a lot more effort and time :).

When the result is shown and I resize - everything disappears.

Congratulations!

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Thanks for playing! Larger nations have a much easier time playing.

There’s a degree of randomness in the level of relationships between the nations so you may end up being unlucky and everybody hates you.

I never tried resizing the game after the score screen appears but now that you mention it, I know exactly what’s happening! 😅

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
What a joy to play this game! I like the zoom- and dragable map, and that you can choose the diplomatic way or the warriors path.

Had fun playing

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Thanks for playing Christoph!

I was afraid the zoom felt a bit weird!

Hope you didn’t find it too hard!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
The UI and map all look really good, I like that you added the time speed up and slow down and even pause.

I tried playing as a small and large area but had some difficulty making any progress.

Once I bought a farm and a mine my resources would go up very slowly, I had a small army which just about kept my land, it kept getting defeated.

I struggled to get the ambasadors to do much for me so need some help understanding how to use them.

Would be good if the first few minutes had some tips so i knew what to do, I kept restarting; placing a farm but never really gaining any food?

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Farms give you food which works like a population cap.

My best strategy is to buy 2 Farms for the capital territory and get an army going as fast as possible, then a mine if I have another spare region. If not, another farm.

Ambassadors will increase reputation with the nation you palce them in, reputation can be used to establish alliances and/or buy regions from other nations.

comments.author: Nathan Long
comments.text: Very thematic and fun strategy game.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
This game looks fun but I couldn’t figure out how to play. I tried 4 times and always die super fast. I wasn’t able to have an army fast enough before they enemies started attacking.

The UI was constantly blinking. It was hard to read the hover tooltips because they kept blinking so intermittently.

I strongly recommend you improve the balance of the game and the UI and release a new version because I think this game has a lot of potential.

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
If you’re having trouble staying alive, I recommend starting with a larger nation and regularly pausing the game so that you have time to take your actions.

My go-to strategy with a large/medium nation is opening with a mine, then a farm and then sending an ambassador to a medium/large nation to increase reputation with them so that we can become allies (they’ll help defend you). Then it’s getting another farm and building an army as soon as possible.

The blinking UI is a bug I noticed a bit too late (particularly prominent when you speed up the time) and I didn’t have the time to sort out :( Sorry about that.

Thanks for the encouraging comment!

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text:
I quickly got stuck with no idea what to do. I cannot really judge this game based on my experience with it. A tutorial would definitely be great.

Note: I have read the description of the game with the explanations on the strategies to use, but yet I was confused and things were a bit too slow for me.

Overall, I see potential for this game, but I sadly couldn’t explore it enough as it is not really beginner friendly. I am sure other experts will have a much better experience with it.

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Many things aren’t immediatelly obvious and I sacrificed the tutorial for some features. I relied on people having played similar gamescom in the genre.

I’m quite surprised with how the majority of players have figured it out - I knew it’d look a bit daunting for many others though.

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Well, small, but proud strategy game. It’s not so simple to play, but there are all main mechanics - gold, food, diplomacy and war. Almost true 4х (2.5 x) Good Job!

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text: Cheers, thanks! Definitely needs to be a bit mire forgiving though

comments.author: stxtus
comments.text: Good maps and i like the style of the graphics themself

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text: Thanks! Wish I could’ve had a larger map though!

comments.author: Ania Kubów
comments.text:
You can tell a lot of thought went into the game play of this submission and I am happy to see that the theme was stuck to!

It took me a while to figure out how to play this game and I am wondering if the game play instructions could be worked into the game play somehow? Either through pop up’s or a run through performed by the computer?

Overall great work!

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Thanks for taking the time to play my game!

Initially I had a tutorial like in last year’s entry but I had to remove it due to lack of space 😭

comments.author: Frahaan Hussain
comments.text: More instructions in game would help a lot, but an interesting concept.

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Agreed on the instructions!

I had to remove my step-by-step tutorial (like I did last year).

Thanks for playing my game!

api.name: Knight
api.description:
In the long span of 100 years, living in the 12th century, a turtle witnesses the contemporary knights braving thorns and obstacles. Playing the role of a turtle, we must guide the knights to a safe path, avoiding monster attacks, and ensure a peaceful journey throughout these 100 years.

Unlike RPGs, this is a rhythm game. Your path is determined by the score you achieve.

Press the crosshair in sync with the musical notes’ beats to earn corresponding scores.

The musical notes have left and right distinctions.

Controls

Key in:

  • left: a/z/left window
  • right: s/x/right window
  • pause: p/click out of windows
  • mute: click out of windows
  • return: r

comments.author: Daniel Cohen
comments.text:
I’ve always wanted to make a rhythm based game! This is beautiful and well done. I’d recommend - a consistent drum beat or high-hat metronome would be helpful to keep the rhythm and allow the “hit area” for hitting the correct key to be a little smaller.

Really fun. It made me want to play a few times. Well done.

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: Unfortunately I received 2 console errors as soon as I started… Uncaught TypeError: q is null Uncaught DOMException: CanvasRenderingContext2D.drawImage: Passed-in image is “broken”

comments.author: Marvin
comments.text: I’m sorry that I discovered this BUG after I sent it.( XDD The bug is in Rotate Image function …

comments.author: Ben Fox
comments.text:
I also got console errors on load, although refreshing the page seemed to resolve it and I was able to play.

Because the score was so integral to the path it would have been really nice to have it much closer to the crosshair/rhythm path so I didn’t have to take my eyes off it to know how I was doing.

It also felt like there wasn’t a whole lot of sync between the music and hitting the notes, but visually it was pretty clear.

comments.author: Jani Nykänen
comments.text:
Pretty soon after starting the game, I get the following error: Uncaught DOMException: Failed to execute ‘drawImage’ on ‘CanvasRenderingContext2D’: The HTMLImageElement provided is in the ‘broken’ state.

This happens on every browser that I have tried (Firefox & Edge on Linux). However, refreshing the window (twice) seems to fix the issue.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Worked for me, made it through several paths before failing. I think the socring to select route was a bit over complicated.

Nice graphics and good to have a music track, with this genre I think the music is key though, didnt feel like I was playing to the beat.

Good effort at a difficult genre!

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text: Try as I might, I just couldn’t get this game to work :( The menu starts, the graphics look great and then the first level crashes with a canvas drawImage error. I tried different browsers, refreshed the page, cleared cache etc but no joy sadly. Which is a real shame, as graphically it looked awesome from what I could see!

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
The music plays, but the game doesn’t seem to respond to controls.

It’s cool that you’ve made a custom pixel font!

comments.author: Armaan Mohammed
comments.text: Does not work for me 😕. Maybe only specific devices work ?

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Hey there!

It feels like a really good idea at the start, rhythm games are really one of my favourites. The design and the music gives a good atmosphere. However, the tutorial lasts only 2 seconds and you really don’t have time to read what to do. So I restarted a few times to try and understand what to do but half of the time, the game hangs at the beginning and the gameplay does not start.

A bit disappointing as it’s a great idea, you should probably have had it tested by some friends to get their feedback!

comments.author: Antti Haavikko
comments.text:
Rhythm games really live or die by the music. There really wasn’t a good beat to follow and noted seemed almost randomly placed and to have nothing to do with the music. It was really awkward to judge the arrows too, especially the “special all the directions marked or whatever” ones.

But on the other hand it was boringly easy. Just two different buttons on a slowish pace isn’t really demanding when the “nice” window is so huge that you can basically play it to any song really.

Nice pixel art. The font made it kinda tough to judge the numbers at some points. And the route score stopped working for me at some point too and after going few more levels blind and it not picking up again, I decided to quit.

After couple of levels, you stop seeing all the route options which isn’t fun. And I’m pretty sure quite a few from the last ones are even completely impossible to achieve. And it’s not really great design when the best strategy is to quickly scan the routes for first heart and just go for that score and then watch the paint dry for a minute not doing anything.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: Music need to be more sync with the actions. It felt a little bit repetitive.

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text:
A really innovative idea combining a RPG with a rythm game. I also really liked the graphics! I still think the idea needs some tweaking to make the game really good, but it is a good start. Here is some feedback which hopefully helps:

  • I had the feeling that the notes to play are not fully in sync with the music. Maybe it is in sync with the beat, but it would be better if it also fits the meoldy.
  • As an old WASD gamer it would have felt more naturally to me to use A/D instead of A/S, but this might be personal taste and habit :).
  • I would have liked to get a bit more audio feedback on the fact if I have missed or not.
  • Maybe a pause in between the steps would be cool, so that you have time to appriciate the path and the things which are going on on the path much more. I really had to focus on the notes and did not see much of the rest.

Anyway a really cool entry and a good starting point for a innovative and fun game! Congratulations.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
For some reason, I couldn’t start driving unfortunately… and therefore play the game…

Maybe if you tell me how to start the game so that it works - I could try again.

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

  • My first impression and understanding (before playing the game, only reading the description, screenshot and title):
    • Well, it’s a rhythm game :) Until that was written I imagined a sort of a tower defense style game. I can’t picture out how a turtle will be helping out a knight that lives 100+ years (!?) on the 12th century (!1?!?).
  • Great work on:
    • Having a complete working game!
    • Figuring out a core loop for the game. The minigame->challenge->minigame gave it a really nice pacing and I think that with minor adjustments, could really make a cut for a serious mobile game. All the elements for making it entertaining are there. Check out “sprintRPG” as a somewhat close example of what your game could be!
  • I liked that you:
    • Had a really cool world-building introduction of your game on the description. I personally believe that getting this solid foundation shapes a lot of what’s going on the game and automatically makes it more interesting. I mean, what’s not to like from a turtle guiding a knight from the 12th century?? Wow!
  • You could improve (again, I’m aware you might already know all the below):
    • I know it’s a limited time and size, so making the notes coming to hit at a really specific time (matching the music) would be really outstanding, but not having them following not even the tempo of the song was frustrating :( The core of the game was to be about rhythm, but I couldn’t really enjoy that part given the notes were coming (probably) at random.
  • I took some screenshots while playing https://twitter.com/javascripl/status/1709302301045698797

api.name: Castle 🏰 Escape (2023)
api.description:
It’s the 13th century.

You wake up in a cell, on top of a tower… Embark on a point & click adventure to escape a castle filled with puzzles, encounters, bad jokes and… surnatural things!

Credits

  • Code, art, gameplay: Maxime Euzière
  • Music: Ryan Malm
  • Sound effects technology: Anders Kaare
  • RoadRoller (without it my zip would be 16.7kb): Kang Seonghoon
  • Beta-testers: Adrien, Sam, Damien, Julien & Anouk
  • Deceptive clickbait thumbnail: deepAI.org + Paint

Controls

Click / touch to interact with everything. (on mobile the hover texts don’t show up but it’s still playable)

Decentralized bonuses

(Accessible after starting a new game)

  • Game hosted on IPFS
  • Decentralized leaderboards (submit your high scores!)
  • Making of: https://xem.github.io/articles/js13k23.html

comments.author: Alpine Games
comments.text: This is my favorite entry this year! The escape genre is fun and unique and the puzzles are all fun and not too easy/hard. Sometimes items got duplicated but I just modified it in localStorage. Overall very enjoyable to play through.

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Thanks a lot! I fixed all the dup glitches in the post-jam version :)

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
Love the game, really nice puzzles with some good little jokes and ideas hidden throughout. Literally my only complaint is that I wish the screen was bigger. I ended up tweaking some css and zooming in or something to make it take up more space. It’s such a great game it deserves to be able to take up the full screen!

Thats the only real fault I could find other than the minor item dupe glitch, which didn’t really effect gameplay.

Excellent work!

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Thank you very much, and you’re right, there are many aspects of the game that I didn’t think from the start (such as responsiveness, hints and localStorage), and they were a bit too hard and costly to implement at the end of the jam. I only did localStorage. PS: I fixed all the dup glitches in the post-jam version

comments.author: Samir Hodzic
comments.text: Definitely my favourite entry this year! Puzzles are so well defined, and music adds that flavour of mystery. Very well done.

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Wow, thanks a lot! (And thanks Ryan Malm for the music)

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: So I’ve finished the game before the voting site went up. It’s really clever and different! “How to turn your dragon” is comedy gold.

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Thank you so much! I found that title the day before the release, I spent all the month not knowing how to name that book. ^^

comments.author: Viljami Peltola
comments.text: I played it long! It is on me that I didn’t pass it :D but I did enjoy every moment of it. I have forgotten how much I enjoy a puzzle game. Gameplay was smooth, no error state or confusion. Wonderful full game!

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Thanks a lot for enjoying it! Means a lot to me

comments.author: Adrien Guéret
comments.text: I love the global humor in this game. There’re a lot of hidden details and puzzles are very smart. They aren’t too easy, making the game interesting, while they also aren’t too hard, making me thinking I’m a smart guy :D

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Thanks but YOU’RE a smart guy, your entry is amazing too!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
That was an adventure! Really great castle, puzzles, music and very tricky!

I cracked the one code by brute force messing about which was a bit of luck but I spent ages working the rest out! Took a break half way through and left the game open so my score was almost 2 hours and around 2k clicks!

Two of the gears kept me going for some time I must admit. Really great entry!

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Thank you so much for testing it thoroughly and enjoying it :)

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text:
Yet another interesting and well done puzzle! Took me a while to finish, but it was worth the time. I will concentrate on the weak points only, the good ones would take to much space. So even if this feedback sounds a bit negative, it isn’t meant so.

Theme: Many of your items did not exist in the 13th century, or had a different form at that time. The worst offender is the telescope, invented in the 17th century. Cannons were invented in the 14th century (at least in Europa), though the one depicted looks rather like 15th century. Plate armour covering the whole body also belongs to the 14th century. Regarding picture frames the sources are not clear about when they became common, but sources agree that during the 13th century they were only used for unmovable sacral images like altar pieces.

The code in the library has some annoying glitches. Clicking on a selected book to open it does not always open it (I think it happens when there is another book invisible behind it), the content of the map of the castle sometimes appears twice or even more often.

I think there are too many puzzles of the type “look up instructions in the library, memorise them, apply them”; after I had solved the first ones, the next ones just made the game longer, not more interesting.

comments.author: xem
comments.text: (As discussed by email: ) I wanted to thank you for your feedback. I really appreciate that you took the time to write it. It’s these kind words plus constructive criticism that help me do better games. And you’re totally right, I made a list of medieval objects, without checking if they really existed in the 13th century. The hints are too much concentrated in the library, and some lock combinations are indeed too similar, it’s something I regret too, but didn’t have time to think of a better solution. Glad you had fun! PS: when I saw your entry being submitted I was like NO WAY! a game very similar to mine with an actual character on screen (while mine is kind of first person), same kind of items to collect, nice pixel art, etc… Tough competition (but in a good way)!

comments.author: Grav
comments.text: I really liked this one. I never got “stuck” like I normally do in point and clicks, all the puzzles made sense, things that looked like flavor text became important later. Great work.

comments.author: xem
comments.text: thanks a lot, I tried my best to made it have sense and not leave anything to randomness if we read hints

comments.author: Alexey Kalmakov
comments.text:
First point and click I finished from start to finish. Fantastic entry with crazy attention to detail.

Some small bugs with the mouse duplication are not ruining the gameplay.

PS It’s not as spooky as Xem said. Don’t trust him.

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Haha thank you very much! Indeed, the main event is not meant to be spooky but quite surprising at least :p

comments.author: Leo Kuo
comments.text: Brilliant puzzle game! Everything makes sense! I spent almost 1 hour to escape but I very enjoyed in the game. It’s evident that the author put a lot of effort into designing the levels and the logic behind them. Well done!

comments.author: xem
comments.text: thanks a lot, I tried my best to make it have sense and logic :)

comments.author: Johan Åhlgren
comments.text: One of the best entries I have played this year. It took me a while to finish but I got there in the end. Excellent work!

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Wow, I’m very flattered, thanks a lot

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
This is a really well made game. I can see a lot of thought and effort was put into it. The music and sound effects are very well made, and the graphics + animations are good. The theme is spot on.

I personally would love a mini-map to make it easier to know where to navigate, but after spending a few minutes walking around I was able to memorize each room, so it’s not a big deal.

The puzzles are clever, and the jokes are funny. The game has some fun moments, which I really appreciate. These moments are what keeps me going, trying to solve each puzzle to see what will happen next.

I end up trying all items on everything I can. I happened to break the fragile wall by accident, because I was clicking around like a mad man.

I wasn’t able to find the final cog wheel, but I will give the game a try once more time to see if I’m able to find it.

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Thanks a lot John, indeed, I tried to focus my game design in order to always have “something next to happen” until the final ending :) For the minimap, but also for the hints, extra dialogs, etc, they were part of my ideas for the game but I got limited by the time and the byte budget to include them. I could however include the map in a book of the library !

comments.author: Jeremy Burns
comments.text: Great execution of a game in a classic genre. The puzzles were well balanced and the adventure was well-paced. Very well done!

comments.author: Marcin Walczak
comments.text:
Pretty good adventure game. Well paced and thought puzzles. Good graphics and nice music. LOTS of easter eggs.

There are occasional glitches, like blinking elements, or books opening twice, but nothing game breaking (and as you mentioned fixed in post jam version).

comments.author: Krzysztof Bociurko
comments.text: Good game, had fun playing it, getting stuck and - finally - unstuck. More logical than most point&clicks.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
Really fun point & click with quite a few funny moments and gotchas. I’m amazing how much you were able to pack in the game. The music gave it a really nice ambiance too.

The use of emojis whenever possible was really clever and well executed, at least with my font. There were a couple glitches here and there, like when using the books I sometimes had to click several times to change page, or sometimes another book would open immediately after. I also collected a total of 4 mice 😅 there’s no limit to how many traps I can set.

Finally, the graphics look very different than the thumbnail. Hope you can fix that issue in the next version! :P

comments.author: Tim Sulmone
comments.text: Good use of IPFS and decentralized leaderboard. Enjoyed the puzzles and getting lost a couple times.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: Amazing game. I wasnt planning to finish it but I did!! Clever puzzles and lots of funny jokes all around. Impressed on how you manage to put all this in 13KBs. A huge congrats for your creativity.

api.name: Holy Knight
api.description:
An endless tower defense type game.

You are the holy knight!

Your only task is to prevent the green knights from getting the holy grail. Points are gained by killing green knights. The game ends if a green knight touches the holy grail. Try to beat your previous score!

Keyboard input

  • W: Jump
  • A: Go left
  • D: Go right

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
This is the best theme tie-in so far, good job!

I like the Holy Chalice pixel art.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text: I’m not sure if it’s intentional or not, but I get an alert: “You lose. Points: 1” when I try to run the game. I’ll try again on a different day. It may be because of some server issues.

comments.author: Luke Stapley
comments.text: The character sprites are very well made, and the idea of protecting the grail was fun. But it can be very repetitive. Try to add this to a better theme, like in a large castle. The clipping also was a bit strange, but not a big issue. Sadly no sound to critique. But great job on a good-looking character.

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text: You control a little knight who can run left, right, and jump. You simply have to collide with the green knights that seem to pour down towards you like rain, to prevent them from hitting the holy grail. It gets a little repetitive but the graphics are cute and it’s definitely on-theme. With some more variation, it could evolve into a fun challenge. Also, the game didn’t work at all for me without opening it in its own tab, then it was fine.

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Unfortunately, I could not move: I have “search as you type” enabled in my browser, and you don’t call .preventDefault() on key events, so when I try to move, the page search opens for me.

comments.author: Christian Staudte
comments.text: The sprites are really cute! However, a bit repetitive over time. Maybe the grail could move around? Most of the time I stood next to it and just jumped, which worked quite well.

comments.author: Nathan Long
comments.text: Very simplistic, I think ‘tower defense’ is a bit misleading. Because the falling knights had only one direction and the player can jump forever you can easily skim the top of the screen and never be in danger. It might be more interesting if it were top-down and you had to fend the knights off from multiple directions.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
This is not exactly a “tower defense” game in the usual sense. It’s a very simple game with very little gameplay. I could stay here jumping all day without losing. I only lost because of an odd box collision bug.

Good fit of the theme but would me more fun with more variation.

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: nice simple game! it ran smoothly and worked fine. as I was defending the grail I noticed that if you look at them just right you can imagine the sprites are CYCLOPSES with toothy grins! LOL (instead of a gem emblem on a helmet with little eyes below, as I’m sure was the intention. both ways, I liked the art! good work.

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
This game has a confusing perspective. At first it looks like a top-down because you see the green knights coming in from the northeast but then as you move your character you realised that it’s a side perespective (so it’s raining green knights).

I feel like this game could’ve benefitted from a bit more variety, there’s an area of the screen that doesn’t get used at all and there’s a spot you can position yourself in where all you need to do is jump.

Still, not a bad entry. Good job.

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Well, looks more like some prototype, but there are some gameplay elements here.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I love the sprites in this game, it’s a fun and simple game

It didn’t work in the voting app, so had to open in a different tab

Hovering over or in front of the grail seemed to be a good tactic, but there sure was a lot of knights coming forwards

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
For some reason, I couldn’t start driving unfortunately… and therefore play the game…

Maybe if you tell me how to start the game so that it works - I could try again.

api.name: Musa's Quest
api.description:
A medieval adventure about the search for an exotic gift.

Works on your phone, too. Just keep the finger on the screen to explore a scene.

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text: Graphics are amazing, but have absolutely no idea what to do. I need a rope to get in the opening, but there’s no rope, and nothing I have has any use.

comments.author: Cliff Earl
comments.text: Hey great game! I loved your SVG graphics, very cool. I can’t imagine how much more game you could include if you optimized those ;)

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Awesome graphics, nicely written - I wish there were more clues (and some nice ambient music would be nice to have!).

comments.author: Ben Fox
comments.text:
Very fun game! Took me a while to figure out the rope, a “hint” button that showed up after a little while would be nice.

Then in the cave I was trying to use the Carpet and someone was always watching… then I switched tabs and when I came back to the game I had been moved to the next scene - not sure what happened with that one, but glad it moved me along.

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text:
You take on the role of Musa, son of the King, who must travel to faraway lands in the hunt for silk as a gift for his mother. It’s a point-and-click adventure - everything can be controlled just by clicking (or tapping on mobile). You never need to press a key or drag anything. But you do need to hunt the scene very diligently! Nothing is hidden from you, but you may not think to click certain parts of certain characters to get what you need.

All in all, this is just brilliant :) The theme is perfect, the SVG graphics are beautiful, and the adventure aspects are fun to solve your way through. It was great to get to the end having completed the quest, although I did feel sorry for Bamidele!

comments.author: Johan Åhlgren
comments.text: I loved this game! A pity that a bug prevented me from using the carpet in the cave. But when I switched tabs and back again, I was suddenly in Bagdad. Other than that, a lovely game. Well done!

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: This game is great! I liked the graphics and the story. At first I was lost (not ablet to find the helmet) and I was about to give up. I think the difficulty should be more progressive. But this is a really nice game and at the end I was able to finish it!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: I’m stuck in the cave after talking with the Djin and don’t know how to get out; it says someone is looking but there’s no one there. But then i pressed the Tab key and I got teleported to another completely different scene haha. I feel like I accidentally tripped on secret warp :) Another captivating story with great visual appeal. I wonder if you could fit some ambient music somehow, that would be lovely. Also, some items are a bit too small to click, like the sword. Maybe you could give them a bigger hit box. Edit: Ok, i was able to finish the story, but there was something going on there in the cave.

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Great graphics, great puzzles, great game! (And, unlike the other commenters, I really liked that joke with the fourth wall.)

comments.author: Thiago Romão Barcala
comments.text: I understood your joke with the “Someone is still looking” after a very long time, and by reading the comments here it looks like nobody really understood it. Someone still looking means we are still looking, right? So we had to switch tabs and then come back. Risky joke.

comments.author: Ania Kubów
comments.text: A visually compelling game perfect for those who love a story-based adventure! The colour palette for this game works so well and I love the SVG art. It would be great to incorporate some light movements into the game. Such as an arm wave or a character moving from A to B. A small change like this shouldn’t be too difficult and would definitely elevate it ten-fold. Well done! Can’t wait to see where you take this!

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Interesting quest base, but I didn’t get how to deal with scorpion. Style is great! Good job.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Wonderful vector graphics and well though out narrative.

Some of the puzzles require lateral thinking but all work well.

I would have liked to have been able to navigate freely in a less linear way.

But ultimately it’s hard to fault this great adventure.

api.name: 13th Century Siege
api.description:
13th Century Siege is a Tower Defense type game in which you must build towers along the way to prevent enemy forces from reaching your castle.

To build towers you must click on one of the signs, after which a small menu will open to select the tower to build and its cost. Kill enemies to get money to build them.

comments.author: Konrad Linkowski
comments.text: Fun, but after buying every tower there is nothing to do

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: The towers kept missing if there is a big queue of enemies - instead of shooting some closer enemy they shoot at the farest one that is already getting out of range and missing with 100 percent rate. When I understood that I changed my strategy and had fun.

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: A fun little game. Sometimes I would have liked to have a speedup button, instead of having to wait until the enemies reached some point.

comments.author: Thiago Romão Barcala
comments.text:
Nice art! I didn’t see the option to speed up the game at first, so in my first attempts, when one of the enemies escaped I was restarting the game, because I didn’t have money nor towers that could kill ’em.

I felt like it could have been better balanced. The waves don’t get harder enough, so once you get enough money it gets too easy and you can just sit and wait till you get enough money to buy another tower, without even thinking about where to place it. Maybe some upgrading system could help.

comments.author: Christian Staudte
comments.text:
Great graphics and everything works well. However, after some levels the enemies have no longer a chance when you have a lot of towers. And in the end you can no longer build anything.

Leveling towsers, destroying towsers and stronger enemies over time could improve your game.

However, I loved that there was a speed button! This highly improves the gameplay.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
nice graphics, and gameplay. at leat up to the point, when all towers are palaced, and the player can only release the next wave.

I like the time-speed-option.

The game needs more stages, and more towers, and tower upgrades

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
This game plays really well. The only thing missing is juice, balancing and more content + sound. The graphics are well made, and it all fit together nicely.

Creating the 150 towers is enough to become invincible. I stopped after wave 12, because I was just a few towers away from building all towers.

It would be great with some upgrades, different enemy types and more information about the towers themselves. The large tower could have slower rate of fire, but larger area of effect, while the small tower could have high fire rate and only damage 1 enemy at a time.

I appreciate the speed option, and I wish we had X4, or even X5 as well. I think the game has potential

comments.author: Cameron Early
comments.text: A fun tower defense game! The art was definitely the standout area here, it has a really nice charm to it :)

comments.author: Keith Karnage
comments.text: This is a great game. I love the art, and it plays well. The building sprites don’t come up in the build menu like I feel they should, but I figured it out. It could use some difficulty balance. After a certain point it stopped being difficult. I just bought all the expensive towers and none made it past the third bridge until I got bored, about level 23. If you have any extra space check out zzfx for sound and music.

comments.author: Tom Harris
comments.text: This is a very simple fun tower defense game that I did a few playthroughs on, and decently challenging too. Love the pixel art style. You could add more towers, and options to upgrade existing ones to make this more fully featured. There are many ways the gameplay could be expanded from here. Sounds would also help add more immersion to the gameplay. Overall it’s a great entry for js13k.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
It was wave 26, we were surviving as best we could… actually after a few waves, you almost can’t lose once you build enough towers, so the game needs some extra balance.

I think it would be interesting if the enemy had several ways of entry that would intersect at certain points. Also, I would like to sell the weaker towers I built in the beginning and replace them with stronger ones.

Also, I did not immediately understand that it is possible to change the speed of the gameplay - it would be good to somehow draw the user’s attention to this button.

In general, an interesting game - congratulations!

comments.author: Taufik Sani
comments.text:
The good: Concept of the game is easy for players to understand, yet the game loop requires players to delve into strategy and thought. Level design - sufficiently complex to pose a challenge for the player, yet allow players to overcome AI. Fast-forwarding is a nice touch.

Suggestions: Audio - Aware that it might be tough including audio in a 13kb build, but I think including background music when enemies are attacking would generate excitement for the player. Adjust Balancing - The first few rounds are challenging (perhaps not that challenging if you’re a tower defense pro:

  • 1st wave 5 enemies.
  • 2nd wave 10 enemies.
  • 3rd wave 15 enemies.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
I love tower defense games, and this one has really cute graphics. I couldn’t find a good strategy though. I played several times and it was a bit frustrating. The initial game balance seems a bit off, it’s really hard to earn enough money. The bullets are quite slow, same speed as the enemies, so many bullets are wasted aiming at the enemies running away from the tower. The towers are not upgradable so all you can do is fill-in the designated plots with towers. I also don’t understand what the difference between “100” and “150” towers are.

When the enemies escape your towers, you have to wait so long for them to attack you; a way to skip to the end or speed things up would be nice.

Great potential if you improve the game balance a bit. Also, music and sound effects to match the pixelart!

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text: Nice little tower defence game! The game could be improved by tweaking the balance a bit. I managed to quite quickly build all towers and it felt like I could keep playing wave after wave without losing. It would also be a nice touch if I could upgrade the 100 coin towers to 150 coin towers.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I enjoyed playing the game, and with limited assets choosing the best spot for towers is tricky.

Love the graphics and game play mechanic.

There’s a lot of waiting around. Once you have placed enough towers that you run out of money, you’ve got to wait ages for the invaders to navigate the path until they are either wiped out or complete their quest. It would be nice to have a fast forward button.

api.name: A Khan's Soul
api.description:
In the 13th Century, Mongol Emperor Genghis Khan created the largest contiguous empire in history.

Year 1215 CE,
The Great Khan lies before you, mortally would by an enemy arrow. You, his shaman, must run across the Sacred Bridge to Heaven’s door and destroy all the evil spirits if you want to save his soul.

  • ↑ : Jump
  • ↑ > ↑: Double Jump
  • ←, →: Move
  • ↓: Roll
  • Shift: Magic Bolt
  • Esc: Pause

May The Great Khan assist you!

comments.author: ch!k0
comments.text: i was able to run in between the gaps of the floors heheh it was a very fun game and the characters were very cute! some of the controls didn’t seem to work at times other than that it was very amusing! thank you

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Fun to play platformer. I liked the different enemy types.

Also the double jump was good especially when you could use the second jump when you were about to land rather than straight away. Felt a bit like coyote-time jumps.

The “tutorial” didn’t do anything different for me compared to the other options.

I like that if you jump left, you can die with -7 steps, nice touch.

Perhaps making it autoscroll would add a higher level of skill.

Also after playing with keyboard, to then need to click the mouse to retry was a bit disconnected.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Nice start to a platform game with some good features. I liked how you were rewarded after dying which kept me retrying.

Nice pixels!

Could do with more background decor and some sounds

comments.author: Grav
comments.text: A great start to a platformer. The controls are really solid (not easy to accomplish) and I liked the roll to block feature. The sprite work is quite good, but the game’s action took up such a small percentage of the screen. It would have been great to have the game a bit zoomed in, or if you wanted to keep it at a distance to show more, maybe adding more verticallity to the levels. Easier said than done in only 13kb though, what you have here is a very solid (fun) game, that kept me engaged until I finished (on my last chance haha) very good match on themetoo

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Good runner with bad controls… Would be nice to add WASD for movement too, loved the jump! But it was not possible to shoot… a few clicks on the shift button and Windows notifies about the keys sticking - the space bar would be much better in my opinion, so I just jumped the whole game :).

Overall a good game! I had fun, thanks and congratulations!

comments.author: Ania Kubów
comments.text: Very smooth movements from this platform game! I also like the story behind the game and was pleased that it stuck to the 13th Century theme. I was expecting the tutorial to have some kind of explainers pop up as you navigated around the platform. if that or something similar could be implemented it would differentiate the tutorial state from the game play state quite well.

api.name: Petty kingdoms
api.description:
Take control over one of 4 kingdoms and unite the land under your rule. Recruit, move armies and counquer your neighbours.

Features

  • Up to 4 players local multiplayer (hot seat)
  • Computer controlled opponents
  • Map generation
  • 3 map sizes (10, 16 and 36 regions)
  • Fullscreen mode (if supported)

Controls

Click / touch to interact.

Tips

  • To buy / send troops you have to click region owned by you first.
  • You can move your troops only to directly neighbouring regions.
  • After recruiting, moving or attacking, used troops cannot move or attack till the next turn (but can defend the region).
  • During attack either attacker or defender can receive advantage: 0-50% increase to army power.

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text: The send army button had gone down in mobile screen, it took me some time to figure it out, but yet it is good game to play!!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: Nice risk boardgame. Had fun playing it. I like the presentation as old map. Would be nice, if you add some kind of “miniatures” to representate the strength of the troops. Audio-effects like rolling dice would be cool too.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Nice UI and simple graphics, I had some issues working out how to use the buttons at first, a quick intoduction would be good for the player.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
I liked it, had a lot of fun. A nice, but simpler, version of Risk. I lost the first time because I didn’t go all-in in my initial strategy. The second time I knew I could win if I simply played passively and didn’t attack for 3-4 rounds while I stockpiled units and let the PCs fight among each other. They wouldn’t attack a bulky kingdom with 3x their force. But once my single territory grew for 4 rounds, I became unstoppable. I just had to play slow and steady ad keep all my units on the borders.

I was a bit confused at first about how the recruiting worked. It’s not clear that the recruiting and gold is not linked to a single region, but the moving is. You could improve the UX of that part a little.

The game is visually appealing, but I would make the text a bit larger, and the buttons as well, but better UX. Sound effects would be nice. In the future you could improve the AI a bit to have a variety of strategies (e.g. my stockpiling strategy).

While the game works OKis in mobile, you have to scroll up and down a lot, which isn’t very convenient.

edit after player for another hour: The full sized map is a bit trickier but the same strategy still applies. Being greedy is counterproductive, and it’s always better to stockpile units.

comments.author: Marcin Walczak
comments.text:
Thank you for your feedback.

I’ll have to tweak a liitle bit UX responsiveness.

I’ve planned to give PC’s different behaviors, but I didn’t have enough time. To be honest I’ve lowered PC’s aggressiveness, by reducing their attacks to 1 per turn, so they won’t gobble up each other too fast.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: Nice risk game, very well implemeted! I like the map and the minimalist intuitive controls. Maybe adding music or sound effect would be great.

comments.author: Marcin Walczak
comments.text: Thanks. I’m planning to add some music after voting is over.

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text: Played this the longest of all games sofar! Very polished, looks nice, good AI. Great game!

comments.author: Ben Fox
comments.text: I’m bad at this, but it was really fun, great execution.

comments.author: Aurélio A. Heckert
comments.text: I did play more time than I think I would! I’m bad at this game, but still good. I don’t understand why yellow allways grows troops so much faster than all others. I couldn’t defeat they.

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Not a bad version of Risk, maybe a little boring. UX is not the greatest, it would be better to show selectors only after territory selection (doesn’t work by default) Maybe need some features to add additional strategical layers, for example less units but lessa coins, so game will became more random but more territory related. But in general, good job!

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text:
What I liked:

  • It is a twist on RISK :)
  • It is multiplayer, yeay!
  • The CPU are smart
  • The graphics are simple but nice
  • Maps are randomly generated, sweet!

What I likes less:

  • I played about 20 times and won only once. I feel the CPU are a bit too strong (like, best possible attack each time?)

Overall, it is a good version of RISK, I had a great time playing it.

comments.author: Frederic Kyung-jin Rezeau
comments.text:
Absolutely love this game although it can become quickly challenging due to the AI being particularly strong without many opportunities to correct a wrong move (made the mistake to not finish off a weakened oponent, preferring consolidating, but they grew stronger and I paid the price later).

Great execution overall.

api.name: Sails!
api.description:
You are an aspiring ocean trader, trying to pay back the debt you owe. By buying low and selling high, you’ll soon be free to roam the sea! A bit of luck would help too.

Controls

  • WASD: Steering and menu navigation
  • E: Confirm
  • Q: Close dialogs

comments.author: Rémi Vansteelandt
comments.text:
I really like the graphics, and the controls felt right (thank you for not asking me to use my mouse in the UI when the gameplay only uses the keyboard).

I wasn’t sure what a good strategy was. I guess buy low and sell high?

Random events were a nice touch, I just wish they were somewhat visible on the map.

comments.author: Andrew Higson-Smith
comments.text: Cute game - I played around and explored some and made a bit of money - nice work

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Unfortunately, I could not move properly: I have “search as you type” enabled in my browser, and you don’t call .preventDefault() on the key events, so when I try to steer, the page search opens for me.

comments.author: Thiago Romão Barcala
comments.text:
Very nice graphics. The UI fits the game very well and it feels polished.

I started playing by just travelling around, and buying random stuff without thinking much. When I realized that I should buy low and sell high I tried to keep track of the prices, but then, for my surprise, the islands change every time. From one side, this adds a bit of challenge, but I’m not sure if it makes a lot of sense in a game about navigation.

comments.author: Keith Karnage
comments.text: This is a pretty well made little game. I bumped off the islands in funny ways a couple of times, but otherwise it works perfectly. Too many mean pirates though. Check out zzfx for simple sounds and music.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Graphics in this one are top notch, absolutely wonderful! The whole visual presentation was delicious.

The game is interesting in principle, and just sailing about is quite satisfying, but I have to admit I couldn’t be bothered to get into the trading. I tried sailing to a few islands, and just gave up trying to memorize prices. I’m not sure I could even get back to the same island again, even if I did remember the price.

I’m still surprised at the sheer amount of stuff you managed to get in here though. Opening cutscene! Random events on the high seas!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: I gave the game an honest spin until i got stuck inside an island somehow. The controls are simple to graps, and the animations are smooth. It’s got a lot of potential but it’s a bit confusing and has some critical issues. I love the graphics although everything was a bit blurry. I ran into frequent issues while navigating, namely that the boat would bump in invisible corners of the islands, my position would suddenly jump or islands would pop in and out of existence (this might be caused by large floating point numbers). I found it hard to keep track of the cost of the items I’ve purchased. Maybe the game could keep track of the average cost of my items. Also, i felt pretty lost in the open sea. Some sort of map to be able to find previous locations seems like something a merchant would have. With a bit more time, it could become a really nice game.

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Hey there!

The intro is waayyyyy too long, you’re losing players with it, keep it short so you can jump in the game quickly. Too bad you haven’t thought of players who are not on a qwerty, you could have supported arrow keys too :)

Neat design though, well done!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text: Congratulations! My result is 31255 :).

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Interesting trading game. Gameplay is a bit of random because of islands, if you cant plan and predict your path it’s really hard to understand the level of prices and efficient sales. Game have a good quality, good Job.

comments.author: Frahaan Hussain
comments.text: Different for sure, needs sound effects and an improved keyboard layout.

comments.author: Jeremy Burns
comments.text: The graphics and color palette are really nice, and it feels good to control the ship. I was surprised I wasn’t able to use the mouse to control the dialogs, but otherwise had fun roaming around the sea. Nice work!

title: Journey to the East; Silk Road Adventure
author: Crystal Parker

api.name: Journey to the East; Silk Road Adventure
api.description:
This is an educational strategy game about the Silk Road. It’s somewhat difficult. To win you must make it to the end of the route with at least one member still alive. This game is inspired by “The Oregon Trail”. Read below for some tips.

Decentralized

If you log in with NEAR (mainnet) and you have Mr.Brown NFTs you get Mr.Brown as your first member of the caravan and he’s sturdier than the average member. In addition, you get a score multiplier depending on how many Mr.Browns you have!

Controls

  • Fill in 5 caravan members’ names using input.
  • Use click/tap to press buttons and fire bow.
  • Visit pages:
    • Market: Buy and sell, try to make a profit.
    • Status: Check on your supplies, your members and make decisions about speed, food, and load
    • Hunt: You can only hunt while on the trail, click to fire the arrows.
    • Big Map: See a larger version of the map, touch the dots to see the names of upcoming cities.
    • Logs: Sometimes you’ll miss events when on other pages, you can look back through previous events on this page.

Spoiler tips

  • Start with lots of water skins, while sometimes you stop at oasis to refill, other than that you can only refill at cities.
  • Each member and camel eats according to the diet you select, so you control how fast the food runs out. Also finding an extra camel can be a blessing and a curse.
  • There are many ways to die, but the fastest ways are by running out of important supplies.
  • If you have Mr.Brown NFTs you can have Mr.Brown as your first member and he is impervious to random death/illness events… he is only affected by neglect.
  • It’s easier to go fast, you may lose more members but you’ll get there sooner.
  • My 12-year-old daughter beat the game! So you can too!

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Fun management/trading game.

I liked the marauder/hunting mini-game :D

I didn’t realised clothes were important in my first playthrough so I got a lot of sunburns xD

I noticed that many of boxes (areas or buttons) where text was meant to appear only worked if I zoomed in my browser to 140%, otherwise they were displaced.

comments.author: Chris Glover
comments.text:
I liked this a lot and was impressed with the variety of events that could happen. I also had the issue where the text wasn’t aligned quite right, but it didn’t impact my playthough.

Arrows/prey are too plentiful. My strategy boiled down to hunting and selling the food at the next city for huge profits. I still lost a bunch of people and camels along the way, but I guess that’s just the cost of doing business.

I’m painfully aware of the 13K limit, but party members having traits that help with certain events (e.g. reduces load, heals sick party members, makes camels walk faster, finds camel feed as you travel, expert marksman, reduces prices) would have made losing them feel more meaningful.

comments.author: Leo Kuo
comments.text:
Interesting resource managing game. Well done! If the game provides more illustrations will be super attractive!!

I like the concept of random events. The unpredictable element makes the game more interesting! A little suggestion is that, maybe the logs can directly scroll to the bottom to see the most recently events!

comments.author: Keith Karnage
comments.text: This is a neat little game. My only problem was that it takes a long time, but that’s just the way some games are. A fast forward button might be nice in a game jam.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Traveled 14795 miles in 557 | Score: -2539900

In the first campaign I lost all the camels and I walked for a long time until I realized to buy them again and then I just flew :).

It seemed to me that when I clicked the left mouse button during the campaign and the robbery started in the next frame, the arrow had already been fired.

Also, I didn’t really understand how to trade, so I only bought what I needed, and when I didn’t have enough money, I sold what I bought earlier, probably because of this such a disadvantage :D.

Interesting idea, congratulations!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
The lore seems interesting, and I like this sort of games. I dig the tunes as well.

I’m a bit confused with what to do. Clearly there are some products I must buy at the market before heading off, but this was really not clear, so I starved.
