The game looks adorable. The people burning look very cute. The only thing graphics-wise I don’t like is that the rest of the UI isn’t cohesive (the title, the start button etc).

The gameplay is really simple, but a bit repetitive. It’s basically a sort of wack-a-mole and I was bored after a minute. It also seems like it’s really hard to lose as the houses take a very long time to burn down, and there didn’t seem to be a limit to the population that could die.

I feel like you could use the screen estate a bit better on mobile, the game was too small.

edit: ok, after playing a little longer, the game turns from dead easy to impossible to win.

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Not a lot of instructions here, it too me a bit to understand what to do, expecially because I was clicking a flame and nothing would change. I then tried clicking another and it did die out.

Nice graphics but could do with some sound for extra atmosphere.

comments.author: Frederic Kyung-jin Rezeau
comments.text:
Score: 4110 Round: 42 on my last try.

Great execution on a simple gameplay mechanic, lovely graphics, Love the progression, it gives us a great feeling of urgency (and hopelessness :)

There are many opportunities for increased depth (maps, boosters to help with extinguishing fire, levels for a sense of achievement…).

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Simple clicker about firefighters Not a complicated game, but with dome difficulty and logic. Good job.

api.name: Entitled
api.description: In entitled, you play as royalty trying to keep peasants eating dirt. Your weapons are ‘Empty Promises’, ‘Patriarchy’, ‘Religion’, etc. Your beacons do not hesitate to use these at everyone. You do not have much control. Push the button.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: Interesting graphic-style, once you change the aspect-ratio to portrait (browser window more higher than wide) it looks much better. Sadly the interaction-possibilities are a bit too less to make it fun…

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: I was really intrigued with the game’s premise but I couldn’t understand the gameplay. The game was very distorted until i resized the window as suggested by another player. This allowed me to finally understand I was looking at a castle of sorts. There doesn’t seem to be a limit to how much I can build, but the weapons i build don’t seem very effective. The game seems unfinished.

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: Intriguing graphics! It looked very cool but I also struggled to figure out what I was looking at exactly - it seemed like an abstract representation of an imagined visual, but it was rather interesting in spite of the glitches. I enjoyed I building my little distorted fractal dream castle and defended it from those filthy villagers for as long as I could.

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Doesn’t look like there’s a lot to do. I can’t see any resource and it appears that I could just keep making empty promises without any consequence (though sometimes some peasants came away in a red colour).

Graphically, even after adjusting my screen so that the game would be a square, the perspective still appeared warped.

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text:
Art: even with the limitations in place, I would have tried to make it a bit more lively with the theme and resemble the actual function of the building and/or character.  

Execution of the theme: very original combination a tower defense game with a catchy medieval narrative.

Gameplay: the game play is a little shallow. Allowing the player to select the tower and drag it to their preferred place would make the game more dynamic. Alternatively you could have “build” on top of the idle mechanic, either by strengthening the forces of the building or the attackers.

Solid, fun prototype!

comments.author: Aurélio A. Heckert
comments.text: Very funny political criticism! XD The gameplay and graphics are not good, but we get it.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: It’s more of a political message than a video game I guess.

comments.author: Jedidiah Weller
comments.text:
Hi Almar,

You startled the heck out of me! Haha, I really enjoyed the transition when I said I was “drinking” and all of a sudden it became a shooter. I wasn’t expecting that 😀 Here’s my feedback!

  1. Great work on the controls - I liked the nod with mouse approach a lot
  2. Great work on the FPS and design, really well done!
  3. The collision boxing on the trees could use some work; I found myself inexplicably blocked a couple times
  4. The moon was a really nice touch as a guiding star! Was that intentional? I assume I was going the right way 🙂
  5. Good job on the README and license!
  6. Would love to see some information on the repository about code standards and linting to make it easier for other developers to contribute meaningfully.
  7. I think the music could be a little more ominous before the guns come out

Overall it was a good submission, and I look forward to seeing what you do with it next. Hit me up on twitter @jedihacks for any discussion!

api.name: Tiny Yurts
api.description:
Connect the yurts to the farms!

Left-click and drag to place paths and re-orient your yurts. Right-click to delete path segments.

Heavily inspired by Mini Motorways by Dinosaur Polo Club. And by 13th century Mongolia!

Mobile is a little small and fiddly, but the ‘Fullscreen’ button puts it into landscape mode and is highly recommended.

Sounds are generated on-the-fly, so each guitar twang and path-placing thud is ever so slightly unique.

Graphics are all in SVG, the UI is mostly HTML, and there are a whole load of CSS transitions. There is no canvas!

Special thanks to Kang Seonghoon for RoadRoller (my asset-less source code is 191kB, which I think might be a Js13kGames record lol), Maxime Euzière for the Karplus-Strong algorithm that I sort of nabbed from his MiniSynth for the sound effects, and Steven Lambert for Kontra.js - the game is now using very little of it, but starting off with an engine was super helpful!

Also thank you to everyone who tried out the game over the last week or so, it’s been great seeing your scores, screenshots, and yurt-organization strategies :)

comments.author: Ethan Smith
comments.text: Excellent animations and transitions! Wish there was a time warp option.

comments.author: newman s lanier
comments.text: It took me a minute to figure out what to do… AH! I see you can re-orient your yurts. I love the theme and the innovation with the SVG and beautiful CSS transitions. Well done. Best so far!

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: My favorite game of the year! Please consider making a post-compo version and releasing on Steam.

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text: Excellent game and amazing presentation!

comments.author: Crystal Parker
comments.text: I absolutely love this game. I hope you keep it going after the game jam is over!
games: Journey to the East; Silk Road Adventure

comments.author: XiaoLPeng
comments.text:
I loved the game! The gameplay is nice and the art style is soothing. The sfx are awesome!

feedback:

I hoped there might be a tutorial or something as it took me a lot time to figure what to do.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
Super relaxing, very good looking game!

I would add a speed-up button definitely.

I’m not sure if I’m missing some end-game strategy, but it seems how far you survive is very impacted by RNG - sometimes yurts spawn in places that are too far to reach (even destroying other roads to have more “blocks” wasn’t enough).

Perhaps instead of ending the game when an animal isn’t taken care - that animal would die and the game ends when you have too few animals to feed everybody - that way would make it feel less punishing, and the chaos would start growing until there’s really no way to save the situation - but overall, very cool :)

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text: Practically perfect. I think I might have spent an hour playing this while watching videos. Super chill, looks great, and the quasi random string plucks reminded me of an ambient Koto groove. Absolutely awesome.

comments.author: Brian Hambley
comments.text: Looks lovely. Really slick. Another game I want to get further with

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: I really love the gameplay and the smooth presentation with the zoom/rotate/blend effects. And the minimalistic style. Great!

comments.author: Alexey Kalmakov
comments.text:
Amazing homage to Mini Metro/Motorways. It’s probably the most polished entry of this year! Great job.

It would be very nice to have a speed-up button.

comments.author: Leo Kuo
comments.text: Super polished! Despite the concept comes from Mini Motorways, it’s incredible to make such game within 13kb. Besides, the graphics, animations and transitions are well done. Great job!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Very chilled game play until you see the timers ticking away and you are almost helpless! Really well made, nice UI and interface all really polished.

A post jam version where you have more input into the game would be great, at times I was waiting for new paths and could not input much into the game loop. More game options / upgrades etc would be great.

comments.author: xem
comments.text: exceptional polishing / UX! it’s just not clear how new road parts are earned… glad I could help for the music, it’s very nice too. My fav entry this year

comments.author: Adrien Guéret
comments.text:
Very nice and relaxing game, I could spend hours to try to beat my score!

The only bad point is that, sometimes, it feels a bit unfair (too RNG based?).

Solid entry anyway, definitively on my top this year :)

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
OK, this is going to be a dangerous procrastination game for me, haha

Awesome that no canvas is used. Gameplay is really nice and satisfying! Sound and graphics are great, too!

If I can find anything to gripe about:

  1. The first time playing, I couldn’t figure out how to feed my fish. I noticed the grey dots in my second playthrough, though.

  2. It seems like when I connect roads, settlers don’t always come out of one of the yurts? I can’t immediately figure out the mechanic there.

Overall, really cool game that I will continue to mess around with :)

comments.author: Marcin Walczak
comments.text:
Probably one of the best entries this year. I’ve already played it several times.

Great gameplay, visuals and animations. Also guitar plucks are a nice touch.

I hope you’ll continue work on it.

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: So clean. So well put. I was expecting some other build-utilities (like cobled roads) but I understand the constrains. I will be playing this a lot! Good job!

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Easily one of my top games this year.

I felt the audio was a bit too annoying at times, which is the only minus point.

The graphics are beautiful and the animations as so fluid. Bonus points for making it SVG only in the DOM!

I love that it stays super faithful to the minimetro/minimotorways style (even with the losing screen!).

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
I have no idea what the goal of the game is, but it’s beautiful and I like it a lot. It’s zen. The sound effects are spot on, the UI is beautiful, and overall a great presentation of the game. The controls are great too (I only played on desktop.)

I think it’s impressive that you were able to create this without a canvas. I will be looking at the source code to see how it works. Full score from me.

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: absolutely amazing! the art, the sounds, the smooth inputs, the great curvy line merging! what a perfect version of mini motorways. this might be my favourite game I played this year at js13k, but perhaps because it duplicates a masterwork of a commercial game, perhaps a bit too closely. but in all the great ways that I loved about the original. I like your take on it, with animals and yurts instead of cars and driveways. I really like how new areas slowly spawn, and you get ready for it to finally appear. such a great project. nice work!!!

comments.author: Kushagra Gour
comments.text: totally loved the graphics and the experience. I am not sure I got the gameplay right still, that was the main issue I faced. Controls being not super clear added to the friction

comments.author: Ben Fox
comments.text: Great stuff, only nit is wishing there was a little less RNG to the winning or losing.

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: Amazing game, super smooth graphics and controls, mesmerizing to play. I got a TypeError around t.x on this line: let t = Xt.find((t=>t.width >= 4 && t.height >= 4)), e =13 > t.x ? 1 : 0

comments.author: burntcustard
comments.text:
Thanks for the positive feedback! Aha, yeah that’s a rare bug - the more you restart, the more trees don’t get properly cleared, and then ponds don’t spawn because they can’t spawn on the invisible trees, and then when a fish farm fails to find a pond to spawn in, that error occurs.

You can work around it by refreshing the page after every few restarts, or you can play a fixed (post-competition, still <13KB) version at https://burnt.io/tiny-yurts

comments.author: Gauthier Billot
comments.text: I never played Mini Motorways so I was a bit lost at the beginning. Anyway I still played it a lot, very nice graphic style!

comments.author: Rene Hangstrup Møller
comments.text: Very nice and addictive little game. I like the clean simple and clear art style

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A very zen-like game which can be played for hours

Requires some careful planning, although there is scope to reroute paths after they’ve been built which is great

Graphics and sound were excellent and added to the fun

comments.author: Jeremy Burns
comments.text:
The audio and visuals suit the zen atmosphere perfectly, and the challenge ramps up at a nice pace taking you comfortably from a calm gardening-like experience into a stressful time sensitive puzzle game.

It took me a second to realize what to do since it didn’t seem like the people were doing anything different when they turned around inside a farm vs when they harvested from an animal. It took a minor insight to connect the (!) and the person being there, so if those were more connected in some way, or if the person pulsed a bit at the same time the (!) disappeared, it might be helpful.

Once I figured it out it was fantastic, and it otherwise was an intuitive and interesting game. Great work!

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: the graphical svg is always a plus, the gameplay is vague and I did not understand anything even after reading the description. the controlls are easy and fullscreen is cool to have.

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

  • My first impression and understanding (before playing the game, only reading the description, screenshot and title):
    • Mini motorways!
  • Great work on:
    • Having a completed working game!
    • Really nice graphics and idea. Great execution!
    • The UX of the game. The way I could build paths and everything just snapped into place was very satisfying.
  • I liked that you:
    • Made the animals play a note of different instruments. That made the board to come even more alive and give such a nice feeling when playing.
  • I took some screenshots https://twitter.com/javascripl/status/1709696921453953381

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text:
This is just incredible! Connect your yurts to the animal pens in order for your people to tend to them. Animals get grumpy if not tended to, so you have to connect them quickly, or its game over. You only have a limited amount of paths to use, however, so very careful planning of the routes is required. You gain another 9 path segments in January, and believe me, you’ll find yourself sat there, frantically watching the months tick away, praying for the year to end, just to better connect your cows, goats, and fish!

Graphically, this is stunning. It’s entirely SVG and CSS, which is a feat in its own right. A side-effect of this is that it did tend to slow down once I had loads of villagers and yurts on the go and not much room left on the grid.

The sound effects are random and gently plinky without getting annoying. The presentation is top-notch and I know it’s “just” a js13k version of a very well-tested game (Mini Motorways), but damn, it’s still done so well it deserves full kudos all the same. Exceptional.

api.name: Hex Crusaders
api.description:
A small band of Templars has run into some eastern horse archers, and must battle through several waves to survive.

Touch to select, touch to move, then touch to choose which direction to face or touch another unit to select them. Double tap to stop on the current hex. The green meter is health, the blue is run/charge time. Charging in will do extra damage, as will attacking from behind. If you run out of run/charge meter, that unit will cancel their pathing, and move to the hex they are on. You can rearrange your units before you start. They will attack when fired upon, but you will need to give some commands if you want to win. It is actually pretty hard to beat.

Thank you so much for checking out my game everyone, I can’t wait to check out yours! I didn’t have enough time to spend on this game. My job is very physical and I come home really tired. I worked on this everyday, and am proud of the finished work, as buggy as the pathfinding is. Not enough time testing. It is only 11.7kB.

comments.author: Elliot Nelson
comments.text: Nice hexagonal grid! Selecting and ordering units felt all right, although as you mentioned their pathing was sometimes odd.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
I couldn’t quite work out what I was doing, or how to win all the way to level 3, but this is a fantastic start. There’s definitely a fun game in here somewhere.

Loved the look of it, reminds me of old Sensible Soccer.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Nice little game. I couldn’t beat the level 3 (1 was easy, 2 was a challenge - 3 seems impossible or super hard). My biggest issue with it is that I couldn’t understand what each type of unit did - if I knew which ones were ranged and which weren’t, I could structure my army in a better way.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: nice pixel-graphics and interesting table-top feeling

comments.author: Johan Åhlgren
comments.text: Loved the retro feel of the graphics! A bit hard to understand how I could actually affect the outcome, other than make sure my units didn’t obstruct each other. Very satisfying to see the enemies perish! Nice job!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Fun little game, but I couldn’t figure out how to win. I just don’t have enough soldiers to pass Level 3. The music and SFX were on point and the graphics were really cute. While the paths were a bit wonky, I think it’s a good solid entry. It would look even more polished with a bit more care in the menus, buttons, fonts etc.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Pixel art and animations on the units are nice. The grass made them a little unclear though, a less busy grass design might have helped.

Movement could be improved.

Sometimes I was not 100% sure which were my troups and which were the enemy.

The fights were entertaining and I liked see the arrows flying!

Once my army was dead I could go into battle and instantly lose which was funny :D Adding in a reset would be nice bu I could just F5 to start over.

You have managed to implement some nice features and its tough in 13kb so good going!

comments.author: Magnus Lindh
comments.text:
Fun but hard to find a good strategy. I just attacked forward and it worked on level 1 and 2., but level 3 seems impossible. With some different terrain types such as forests, hills and mountains the game probably would be more fun. The main problem is perhaps that there are too many units to move in a short time. If you need some inspiration there is a great RTS game from 1989 called North & South, see link below:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/North_%26_South_(video_game)

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text: For a game with some clear problems this was GREAT! I loved the graphics (the arrows the gore the paths), hexagons are cool, the whole strategy, sending your troops into battle. Really fun! You know, except for level 3 where you just get slapped around, LOL Not sure if the perma death per level was intentional but even that I liked. All of the feedback in the other comments are spot on, but this game the definite beginnings of something great!

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: I love hexagons! so I was excited to play right when I saw the game! it was really good, I like how fast the units wiggle as they march and I loved the cool pathfinding lines that previewed where a squad would go. excellent work.

comments.author: Victor Debone
comments.text:
Hello! Thanks for your submission to js13k! I’m sure you’ve worked hard on your game, but I’m not aware of how much time you spent on it, so excuse me if things I mention were in your mind, but got left out in the rush of the month or just because you hit the 13kb limit :)

  • My first impression and understanding (before playing the game, only reading the description, screenshot and title):
    • The title implied some boardgame mechanics, and I saw the screenshot told me yes.
    • Chess.
  • Great work on:
    • Having a complete working game!
    • Core loop is working! I got engaged in the gameplay, trying to organize my troops and control their behaviour during battle.
  • I liked that you:
    • Added sound effects and music.
    • Created this persistency between the levels! It’s very unforgiving as you get less troops for when it’s really hard, but apart from that, it makes you think more strategically than not care about what happens in the first battle.
  • You could improve (again, I’m aware you might already know all the below):
    • Maybe there was some designs you tested where the different types of units had incredible success against the arrow-on-horses, but I couldn’t really make it work in anyway form. Maybe it’s a matter of balancing or you had some other ideas during play testing.
    • I think this kind of game lives on the dexterity of issuing dozen of correct commands in time, but the quirks here made it hard to control even one troop. I think the lack of multiple selections and being able to move multiple troops at the same time killed any chances I had on winning :)
  • I took some screenshots here https://twitter.com/javascripl/status/1709329298056691923

comments.author: Jedidiah Weller
comments.text:
Hi keith, goodjob on this submission! I really enjoyed the timing of the strategy game, and look forward to seeing what you do with it next. Here’s my feedback and suggestions:

  1. There was a responsive issue on my desktop monitor where the game only showed on the right 50% of the side, and the left 50% was just for the mute button. Make sure to pay attention to responsiveness as UI is one of the biggest parts to any game!
  2. I really enjoyed the timing mechanic that was coupled with the unit positioning. Bonus points for this mechanic!
  3. The music was fairly appropriate, so good work there
  4. I liked how you added dead bodies to it - I couldn’t make them out because of the pixelation but I assume that’s what they were 🙂
  5. There were times when I couldn’t move characters that seemed like they should be able to move. Either it was a bug or intentional; but if it’s intentional need a UI element to tell the user what that part is
  6. Minus points since the readme wasn’t filled in any no license file on the repository.
  7. The source code needs some work - instead of having everything in one file, make sure to break up the code into multiple files with a coherent folder and file structure and naming convention
  8. Additionally, try to use typescript combined with eslint/prettier to help guide you (and others) for code quality.

Overall it was a good submission, and I look forward to seeing what you do with it next. Hit me up on twitter @jedihacks for any discussion!

api.name: wor
api.description:
In ‘Wor’, step into a realm where strategy meets combat. Command your units, master the terrain, and outmaneuver the CPU’s forces. Can you dominate the battlefield and vanquish your foes to claim victory?

Controls

  • Click on a unit to command your units
  • WASD to move your units

comments.author: Ben Fox
comments.text:
This was a fun little battle sim! I liked the added touch of the gravestones in particular.

It took a bit to get the hang of the game and get used to the controls, an in-game explanation or small tutorial might have helped. Additional units to make for different strategies would be a great extension/addition.

comments.author: Rémi Vansteelandt
comments.text:
Battle sims are always fun!

Some suggestions for improvement:

  • when placing unit, it’d be nice to have an indicator of where the unit is going to be placed. Sometimes I’d place a unit slightly off because I couldn’t figure out the grid
  • being able to move units after they were placed (before the battle)
  • smooth animations when units move
  • make it more obvious that you can move unit during battle

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Alright, interesting. RTS is hard to pull off, specially with AI, double specially within 13kb. So congrats on the game, it looks cool and has lots of potential. I don’t understand what’s going on or how should i better organize my units. Even after playing a whole round and winning, I don’t feel like I did so well, because I had no strategy. I just sent my soldiers and hoped for the best. The graphics are cute, although the overall game UX is not clear enough. Controlling units with WASD is too slow to be fun. Placing units one by one, having to click 30 times, is also a bit tedious.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: A nice idea, pixel art and animations are nice. Controls need to be improved to allow a more fun battle experience.

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
I like the premise but the controls don’t really do this game any favours. Perhaps it’d have worked better as an auto-battler?

On the plus side it was easy to understand what to do.

I like the graphics but the movement would’ve looked a bit better if it had a smooth animation rather than jumping from square to square every second.

comments.author: Aurélio A. Heckert
comments.text:
It is ok. Too simple, maybe, but fun.

The most weak feature is the walking speed. It’s difficult to understand what is happening. “Am I controlling this move?”

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Hey there!

Really fun concept.

The graphics are on point, and it’s quite simple to get into it.

The games are a bit too long in my opinion, making it less replayable. A bit of music would make it better to play along too :)

Nice idea overall!

comments.author: Frahaan Hussain
comments.text: Super cool concept, needs audio and I don’t know why I could move the whole level around by clicking and dragging, seems like a bug.q

api.name: Anachronic
api.description:
Hi, you just finished your portal to the thirteenth century.

Only, as it happens, you don’t have the necessary equipment at home (to live in the Middle Ages).

At the same time, you only have $750, so you have to think carefully about what you buy.

Controls

  • left screen tap: change direction to left,
  • middle screen tap: jump,
  • right screen tap: change direction to right, or you can use WASD

comments.author: Ben Fox
comments.text:
Couldn’t get it to work on mobile, but desktop seemed to work fine.

I like the concept but the variety of graphic styles made it feel a bit chaotic.

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: It worked perfectly on my desktop pc and the framerate was great. The art didn’t all match, but like anachronistic tech, it wasn’t really meant to fit into the 13th century anyways! =) It was a successful 13kb game. Thanks for joining the fun!

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Strange mix of graphic styles. You’ve got some very simplistic stick figure and blockouts while the horses are super detailed and the archers somewhere in-between.

I’d have ranked the graphics higher if they were all stick-figures as that would’ve been a cool style.

The collision detection seems a little too large for the sprites (maybe there’s a lot of transparent padding).

comments.author: Frederic Kyung-jin Rezeau
comments.text:
Fits the name perfectly :) Love the originality of this side scroller.

I had a few minor issues with the gameplay where collision was off sometimes and the HP disappeared at the top.

comments.author: Aurélio A. Heckert
comments.text: Fun background history, but the game itself could get more from your creativity. By the way, there is potential.

comments.author: Krzysztof Bociurko
comments.text: A bit hard to play on mobile, also two horses in a row are not jumpable over.

api.name: Fortifia
api.description:
Fortifia is a game all about defending the crown of your kingdom! Lay bricks Tetris-style (use A, S, and D) to build up defenses around the crown. Extra bricks can be placed by holding SHIFT and background bricks can be placed by holding SPACE. Waves of armies will appear to fend off and crush with your bricks. The crown is in your hands. Save the kingdom!

I found out about the jam a few days before the due date (I forgot to mark my calendar, go figure :D). I didn’t have time at all to polish it (enemies can attack backwards, though a pretty cool super power, wasn’t necessarily what I was going for). I think the concept is great and would really like to release a more complete update to it at some point if possible. It was a fun concept game to put together!

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
I like the tetris-like mechanic of how you build your castle.

Shame it’s unfinished!

comments.author: Santiago Zapata
comments.text: I really liked the sprite graphics. I found that the game took a lot of time to build up and never felt the crown was in danger since the waves of enemies appear too slowly.

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Weird tetris, but speed is too high on my pc (maybe it’s not fixed FPS but just a bad balance) At least it’s too high for me

comments.author: Francesco Bedussi
comments.text: It is too easy to play, so not to much egaging

comments.author: Allen Schmidt
comments.text: Cool concept! Definitely felt a bit slow and I didn’t really see a point in not holding shift because why wouldn’t you want extra bricks all the time haha

comments.author: stxtus
comments.text: The controls arent well outlined. But i love the colors and screens

comments.author: Jupiter Hadley
comments.text: What a fun concept! I like that I can kill the enemies by landing bricks on them - very silly. There should probably be a cooldown to making it lots of bricks and to landing on the enemies heads.

api.name: The Last Tower
api.description:
Mongolian invaders are coming from every direction. You won’t give up and will defend the last tower till the last drop of blood.

Movement: WASD. Attack: left mouse button (point the cursor in the direction of the enemy you want to attack).

Goal is to defend the tower and staying alive as long as you can.

The tower is helping you out by shooting projectiles at the enemies. The worse the health of the tower, the less damage it deals. You can heal the tower by killing the invaders. You can also heal yourself by avoiding being attacked for a few seconds.

comments.author: Rémi Vansteelandt
comments.text:
Now that’s my kind of game! Simple action games are always fun.

I wasn’t sure what the best strategy was. I tried to lure the mongolians away from the tower so it would shoot them, but they kept spawning so it didn’t work for very long, and luring them away caused them to attack me.

Add a few more animations, tweak healing, add enemy types, and you can take the game to the next level :)

comments.author: Armaan Mohammed
comments.text: Love this game ! Simple yet effective tower defence. Feedback: There is no audio which is a category for the overall rating, The background could have been made more intersting, different weapons would make it more immersive. Overall A solid entry for js13k 😀.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I liked the game concept of a tower defence with invading enemies.

At first I assumed because I started in the tower that I was an archer, but soon realised that nothing was hitting the invaders.

The combination of mouse and keyboard play worked well.

Lasted 49s on my first go which seemed good, but is probably average.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Works well, nice pixels, combat is simple but effective.

How I would improve, some simple games you need lots of nice feedback to the player, maybe zoom in a bit, add particle FX, a little screen shake etc to add more of a fun feeling.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Cute, simple game. The controls are not the most practical. It would be more intuitive if the mouse moves the attack directly directly, instead of the direction depending on the position of the mouse cursor. But it’s a fun game.

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Unfortunately, I could not properly move: I have “search as you type” enabled in my browser, and you don’t call .preventDefault() on key events, so when I try to move, the page search opens for me.

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text:
What I liked:

  • The controls are fine
  • Simple tower defense

What I liked less:

  • Only one type of enemy (from what I experienced)
  • No upgrade mechanics
  • No sound/music at all

Overall, it is a super simple tower defense, but it works well.

comments.author: Taufik Sani
comments.text:
I like how the rougelike feel to the game. The player life is sufficiently adequate to allow players to make a stand, but the tower’s life and attacks is overpowered.

If you could add a “marketplace” where you can allow players to upgrade their weapons after 20 kills, or redeem a health boost, that would increase longetivity of the game.

Controls are comfortable, although I was playing with a touchpad so the hack and slash was quite tiring. It’d be interesting to add touch control for the hack and slash.

api.name: Necromancer Colony Sim
api.description: Necromancer Colony Sim is an incremental colony sim. You are a necromancer trying to rule the world. Start from nothing and build a kingdom of Skeletons to conquer the lands.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Works best on Mac as Windows was a bit dodgy with the emojis for stone, wood etc. Once I knew what I needed to do was quite addictive and was determined to complete the game; something nice about stat / clicker type games.

The page layout was good and the ascii art was great!

Critique

  • Tool tips got in the way a little on the wood and stone collecting
  • Took a long time to get all the resources needed to win
  • Battles could have played a bigger part in the game
  • Losing a battle; didnt take much damage or lose much
  • Sounds missing

Overall a solid entry, enjoyed playing thanks.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback. Could I ask what about the tooltips getting in the way on the stone and wood collecting was a problem for you? Was the same thing a problem for all tooltips or only a certain group (like just the Action button tooltips)?

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
I like the minimalistic approach (art-wise) and that the action of the game mostly takes place in ones fantasy, like a pen&paper game.

The tooltips are a bit to big, and on Windows some icons are missing, resulting in guessing whats needed for a build…

But i played it quite some time, and was entertained :-)

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback. Could I ask what exactly about the tooltips made them feel too big and why that was a problem for you? Was it all tooltips or only a certain group (like just the Action button tooltips)?

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A nice simple game. Frantic watching many progress bars deplete and clicking them to move on to the next stage.

I ended up just clicking boxes where the progress bar was back down to 0, not sure what I was meant to be doing or if there was an end goal?

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: The tooltip got on my nerves - it could use 1-2 seconds of delay. There isn’t a significant difference when a button is inactive - a bit more contrast or colors and a mouse pointer could help the new player. Most emoticons didn’t work on my PC - an emoji font can be used as an external resource via @font-face - it’s allowed as long as the game works without it.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback. Could I ask what exactly about the tooltips got on your nerves and why? Was it all tooltips or only a certain group (like just the Action button tooltips)?

comments.author: Grav
comments.text: Cool clicker/idling game! Emojis were fine for me. really cool when all the research-locked items appeared and the game opened up. Also liked how at end it came back down to basic resources, so you had to pull all your skeletons off everything else to produce stone and planks. I was never able to fend off an attack, until I made a special effort at the end, but it didn’t seem to trouble me much. Music might have gotten repetitive, but maybe some sound effect?

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
This game is incredibly complex and rich. The tech tree is quite long and you need to read a lot to know what your plan should be. I had some fun playing for a bit but the GUI and some bugs depleted my enthusiasm.

The GUI is lacking a bit. It’s not fun to have to scroll up and down all the time. This could have been solved with a grid layout or any number of ways.

Some things didn’t seem to work well, or worked in weird ways. I created a militia but that didn’t seem to have any effect. The villagers attacked and pillaged my resources. And when I developed the warehouse, the max amounts didn’t increase. It’s only vaguely related to the theme.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text: Fun clicker! I admit I’m not the biggest clicker fan, but this looked so nice, that I hacked it to go quite a bit faster so I could see everything you’ve built. I appreciate the pretty ASCII art, I like the depth of the tech tree, and I had a laugh at the theme. Great job!

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Resource management? Colony building? This is my kind of game!

The only thing that is a bit annoying is the tooltips covering the options above.

Solid game!

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback. Could I ask what exactly about the tooltips covering the options above was annoying? Was it all tooltips or only a certain group (like just the Action button tooltips)?

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Really cool game. At the beginning it felt like a clicker game, but then as soon as you were able to research a whole world opened up and I realized where you wanted to go with this game. I really like the simplicity, but still you need to think a lot on how you want to use your resources and skeletons. Some sounds would have been really cool. I am not so sure if I would rather make the items which are not yet available at least viewable. It would help a bit to understand to what you are building. On the other hand it is also exciting to explore the different buildings on the way. Very well done. I had fun playing it.

comments.author: Jeremy Burns
comments.text: It’s always amazing how addictive a well-executed clicker game with simple UI can be. This ramps up at a good speed, and the variety keeps it interesting. Well done!

comments.author: Jedidiah Weller
comments.text:
Hi Steven,

Great work on this submission! It was simple yet elegant, and I had a fun time playing!

Here’s my feedback from this submission:

  1. Good work on simple controls; and the timing of the click-downs are good
  2. There were some small subtleties, such as the icons being the same for chopping wood and gathering stone that should be different. You could potentially use a CDN icon library to keep the size down while streaming - although full transparency I’m not sure if that was included in the rules so not going to consider it for grading purposes
  3. It would be nice to have some sort of limitation to how many actions can perform at once outside of Mana. This would give it more of a realism feel since you’re a single necromancer starting out.
  4. Good job on the overlay tooltips! Very useful for when icons were not exactly explanatory
  5. I’m not sure if it’s a bug or UI, but I wasn’t clear on playing the purpose of incrementing the wood cutters and carpenters. I guessed should be to increase productivity, but I didn’t notice productivity being increased at all. Might want to look into this
  6. Good job on folder structure on the repo, but please make sure to add a Readme!
  7. Also suggest adding linting and prettier to make it better for community contributions and personal contributions from a code standard perspective.
  8. Good job on file naming!

Overall, good submission - keep up the good work and hit me up on twitter @jedihacks for any discussion!

api.name: Barbenheim 13
api.description:
A simple tactical RPG created for the JS13K 2023 game jam.

Control your units with the mouse.

As usual I ran out of time, so the story line is weak :-(

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
The theme is spot on, and the controls are good. It’s difficult to create good controls in a tactical RPG game, but the simple one-click action works.

I think the graphic works well, except the pink part, It was a bit difficult to read the text with the current colors.

Some animations, sound effects and music would elevate the game even more.

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text:
I think the graphics were pretty good! The game was simple, but fun to play. Some improvements could be:

  1. A bit more visual feedback for the player to know what was happening: bigger even huge effects are way better than something small that seem closer to reality.
  2. Sound and music
  3. Visuals that fit on the screen (I had to zoom out to 60%)

Congrats!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Well now.. that was a plot twist. The game looks really good and the controls were intuitive. There should be some sort of transition between levels because it was confusing at first when everything changed after killing the last monster. The game would be amazing with some music and sound effects.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: A fun game, the atomic bomb was suprisingly funny! A little easy but a good job!

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Lol, a good reminder to check whether the princess actually wants saving.

Graphics looked great to me, nice pixel art. Shame there’s no audio, but the game played well enough, and you managed to get a solid start/middle/end in there. Good job on getting as far as you did in the time you had!

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
This is grand! I love how dwarfs can mine the rocks (although it didn’t mine out for me, so had to go around after a couple turns).

Controls are nice, I like how it clearly indicates if the unit is walking or firing.

Shouldn’t the bomb have splash damage btw? I think the one from the movie did. :)

There’s a ton of potential for turn-based strategies on js13k!

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: The game was much larger than my screen, so I had to scroll a lot while playing, which did spoil the gameplay quite a lot.

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: That was amazing! The art was incredible, the tactics gameplay was spot on, and the variety of weapons and enemies made it a really great tasty treat of a short, awesome game. The text was hard for me to read, but otherwise everything looked great. Imagine if you added to this wonderful base project in the future? I would love to see MORE! Excellent work.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
In the end, I thought that the princess should be saved, but she asked herself… :).

Nice game, congratulations!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A fun game, I enjoyed sending the warriors out and leveling the foes.

Graphics are good, control system works well - it’s nice to have the breadcrumbs.

Levels are simple but effective.

comments.author: Aurélio A. Heckert
comments.text: Should I kill the princess????? HAhahahahahahahahahaha Unexpected!

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text:
Super nice game, big fan. Really like the simplicity of it but it also feels like a complex enough game and loads of different ways to play. Eventually I just ended my turn instantly until the enemies came into me and just picked them off, super nice. Story is good too, atomic bombs arent the most 13th Century but it works with the rest of the theme!

I had some issues with the game crashing on Firefox and the rocks didnt get mined by the dwarves but otherwise it worked well. Pixel art is really nice here too, just enough detail to make the world feel alive but not too much. Congrats on a really nice little game!

comments.author: Jupiter Hadley
comments.text: I like that you had a story and found the game to be well made! I love the graphics and I really enjoyed killing the goblins!

comments.author: Jedidiah Weller
comments.text:
Hey there Rene,

Great job on the tactical RPG! I know you ran out of time, but you still did a good job! I went through the game and here’s my feedback:

  • The pathfinding was great - good job on that
  • For the UI, I think there could have been improvements - specifically, I didn’t find the size of the map to contribute to the gameplay at all (since the direct path was the easiest path). So instead, what I’d suggest is to decrease the size of the map and increase the size of the assets so that you can focus more on the visuales and players can see more clarity in the characters themselves.
  • The pink and blue was hard to read - I’d suggest using a tool like this https://uxplanet.org/best-three-color-scheme-for-music-player-5da996d7f460?gi=403ce9d9bec0#:~:text=There%20are%20some%20patterns%20what,very%20edges%20of%20human%20perception to determine what colors you’ll use together. As a software developer myself, it took me a long time to start learning the importance of the UI side but tools like that can help us ^
  • Good work using prettier
  • I’d suggest also using eslint and stylelint as well if you want other community members to contribute to your code

Overall, great game and great job! What’s next? Feel free to hit me up on Twitter/X @jedihacks to have a discussion!

api.name: Robin of Thirteensley
api.description:
The year is Nineteen Seventy Thirteen. The personal computer craze is just starting to really take off, and the most popular computers sold are… video games consoles. Inspired minds around the globe are racing to invent the future, and hungry corporations are eager to fund them.

You’re a streetwise 13 year old, browsing casually through the broad aisles of ToysRSus, when you turn the corner into the gaming isle and there it is: Robin of Thirteensley, running on a demo station. You see animated sprites! Platforming! Swashbuckling! The gigantic 13kb cartridge, you learn from the prominently placed sticker, hosts untold adventure; basically the most realistic video game ever made. Your world narrows into focus: You. Must. Play. This game.

This vintage action platformer is meant to be played on a desktop browser, with the arrow and space keys.

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: Loved the swordfight, thought could use more diverse enemies requiring different tactics. Platforming was ok, but nothing new or interesting. Would enjoy game more without it. One thing I did not like was that I had to jump into the unknown several times. Though on the plus side in all those cases had landed safely.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text: Thanks for playing! I appreciate the feedback :)

comments.author: Oliver Smith
comments.text: Absolutely captures the retro aesthetic but with modern sensibilities. The tutorial as part of the game is great, the progression is good and it is fun to play. Really nice job.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text: Thanks! I’m glad you had a good time.

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
Cool sound effect/music combo, and the presentation is cool. I liked the sword fighting, gave me prince of persia vibes.

It fell apart for me when the platforming started. Too much momentum and gravity, and making me try to jump up to a tiny platform with fire on each side, die, then go back and fight the two purple dudes was just too difficult and time consuming to come back to after two tries.

But still a cool game with interesting design, just some level design tweaks and it would be amazing!

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
I’m sorry it ended that way for you, but it was the 80s and games were just mean. They really wanted you to put another coin in, and couldn’t shake the habit even when they made games you paid for once and took home!

I think they toned it down in the sequel though, for what it’s worth. Thanks for playing!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
I didn’t manage to beat the game - got stuck in the swordfight (meaning: I wasn’t able to defeat my oponent), but I liked it. The “faith jumps” were cool!

The post procesing, animation, and specially sounds are top notch.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Thank you for the kind words. Yeah, the swordfighters are jerks. They are super predictable jerks though… if you’re patient you can basically always parry them, and they’re guaranteed to be vulnerable afterwards, fools that they are. The feints are more of a random chance thing if you’re feeling lucky, flashy, or both.

Glad you liked the over the top filter! I’m positive CRTs weren’t actually that bad back then, but they sort of feel like they were, 40 years later, don’t they?

comments.author: Christopher Lis
comments.text: Played this game a few days ago. Sound effects here are incredible. Love the strumming sounds that come with sword swipes and hits—they really stuck with me and have legit thought about them during my day-to-day (lol). Cool graphics and easy-to-get-going controls.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Thank you for playing! Sorry not sorry that the guitar was an earworm* :)

  • now I’m going to hear guitar strums every time I pick up and put something down, aren’t I?

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
I am impressed by the presentation, the crt-effect ads so much to the (really good) pixelart. The animations are great too! I also had fun with the platforming and the swordfights, only that giant pillar with the fire below is a big turndown. motivation-wise. BEcause when you miss the jump, its over without any chance of recovery.

The music/guitar string-thingie fits very well. Also the storytelling below is great.

I*d love to see a version with more (reachable) content .

(And kudos for your game description :-) “Nineteen Seventy Thirteen” hehehe four years before i could lay my hands on my good old Amiga 500, of which this game reminds me of)

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Thanks for enjoying the Nineteen Seventy Thirteen line, the dad joke corps appreciates your service.

Also thanks for playing the game! Yeah, the fire leap was definitely a big difficulty spike. And it was so mean they put it after the big fights! I don’t know how we all survived the 80s, early game design was just so raw and mean. I blame Pitfall and then later Prince of Persia.

comments.author: Luke Stapley
comments.text: Wow, the graphics and sound design are intense. Very good job. The only issue is that fire death automatically takes a heart from your next life. Other than that, it’s great. Really good game design.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text: That is indeed a mean bug, but thank you for chugging through it! Much obliged at the kind words.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I played this game when it was first posted and enjoyed it a lot.

The graphical style looks really good, and has a wholesome retro feel.

The sound effects were nice and worked well.

Some of the battles are quite tricky without knowing the exact way to defend and attack.

Plenty of replay-ability, making me want to come back again and play some more.

Great job on this game.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text: Thank you kindly Jasper, I’m glad you had some wholesome fun here!

comments.author: Grav
comments.text: Brilliant, especially the sound effects. Framing it as a retro demo was clever too. The CRT effect adds a lot.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Nostalgia is a heck of a drug, isn’t it? It’s weird how the guitar stuff is a totally a retro non sequitur*, but because it’s old timey, and the theme is old timey, and pixels on CRTs are also now old timey… it all sort of blurs together?

  • fun fact: this method of guitar synthesis was actually well known back then, but I guess it wasn’t popularly used because the spacey electronic sounding synths were all the rage!

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text:
What a great game! The old-style Atari 2600-like graphics, the lute music generated through your actions in the game, the cool swordplay; it’s all great!

The platforming could be a little hit or miss at times, but that didn’t take away much from the game for me. Really impressive that you managed to pack so much into such a small game, great job!

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Aww, very kind of you to say that, thank you!

I agree, the half horizontal control while you’re in the air is a bit too mean, I don’t think most folks intuit momentum based jumping at all, so while it’s learnable, I’m now sure it’s not pleasurable for everyone, especially in the short period of a gamejam playthrough! A cautionary tale to others (and future me).

comments.author: Gauthier Billot
comments.text: Very impressive, everything is good in this game. Special mention to the dynamic audio/music!

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text: Thank you! It wasn’t the plan, but now that I’ve stuck my head into that rabbit hole, procedural music isn’t something I’m going to be able to avoid doing for a while now. It’s so bizarrely satisfying to see music and picture line up, especially when it’s a percussive moment. Here’s hoping next year’s theme has good synergy with music!

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: A really competent platformer! Sword fights are great, the reuse of controls for jump/parry is a nice touch.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Thanks! I was specifically toying with the idea that some enemies in a platformer could soft lock you into combat, ala Prince of Persia. That seems like a lost line of evolution that maybe would be fun to play with again? The wild boars are sort of the consequential opposite scenario: what do you after learning that combat is a mode switch, when the game then tells you, “no, these aren’t for fighting?”

Maybe I (or someone better at this than me) will revisit these ideas again in the future.

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
This is awesome! Love the presentation (both graphics and sound)

The sword-fighting feels like fencing (I mean, I’ve never fenced before, but I imagine this is what fencing feels like). It seems like there’s some forward momentum when you attack, so you need to reposition backwards a bit afterwards. Nice!

I couldn’t get past the part with the fire, but… even that is fitting for a punishingly difficult 1980s platformer!

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Aww, thank you for the kind comments. Yeah, it is a bit of an 80s brat. I’ve realized since publishing that because I’m framerate compensating, there are actually devices on which the math works out to make the double jump just ever so slightly shorter in height, which may have affected your playthrough, especially around the fire jump. It’s like the old NTSC vs PAL game speed bugs!

tlrd; It wasn’t you, it was the game.

api.name: Tide of the templars
api.description:
Embark on a perilous voyage in the 13th-century world of “Tide of the Templars.” As a valiant Templar knight returning home after the last crusade, your mission is to steer the ship through treacherous waters. Utilize the W and S keys to navigate the ship, as you strive to keep the vessel afloat and the crew motivated.

Test your skills with precise controls, avoiding rocks. Collect fish to replenish energy and keep your crew ready for the challenges ahead. Can you guide the Templar knight safely home on this treacherous journey? Prepare to embark on an unforgettable seafaring experience in “Tide of the Templars.”

Controls: W and S

comments.author: Erifyli Evangelou
comments.text: I loved it!! Very fun to play and I really enjoyed the water effect!

comments.author: Pauline Dupuid
comments.text: Well done, awesome game, it rocks!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
nice game. The up/down movement, could use some ease-in/out effects, so that the change of direction is fine tuneable.

and more diverse obstacles qould be nice. maybe they should also not all be placed on random positions? Some kind of patterns?

comments.author: Veronika Milic
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback, I agree that obstacle diversity would make it less monotonous!

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Unfortunately, I could not properly move: I have “search as you type” enabled in my browser, and you don’t call .preventDefault() on key events, so when I try to navigate, the page search opens for me.

comments.author: Veronika Milic
comments.text: So sorry to hear that, thanks for the feedback!

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • Night and day cycles were a nice surprise
  • Water effect looked nice

Feedback:

  • The water background didn’t load for me (Firefox on Windows 10). Worked in Chrome though
  • Holding W or S resulted in a super fast and uncontrollable speed. Needed a cap on max speed or something
  • It took a few playthroughs before I realized what the fish did (sorry, didn’t fully read the instructions). Since they replenish energy I would make them spawn much less so the game is more challenging. Right now they spawn a lot so getting hit doesn’t really hurt as I can heal it almost immediately
  • Collision box of either the rock or ship is bigger than the sprite so I would hit a rock even though the boat sprite didn’t touch it

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
I liked the idea and the pixel art, but for some reason my game background was white, without the ocean you show in your screenshot. The game window was also small and on the corner of the window; you could have used the available space better.

The controls were way too sensitive for my taste, even though that seems to be by design, as per your description.

comments.author: Veronika Milic
comments.text: We should have set the background to blue, that is for sure a minor improvement that would make the experience better. Also, more cross browser testing next time! Thanks for the feedback!

comments.author: edouard
comments.text: Awesome game! Love it! I especially like the attention to details.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Quick bit of fun avoiding the rocks and picking up the fish.

Hard to control the boat. It feels like it moves too quickly.

The rocks are pretty lethal, it would be nice to have health rather than instant death.

Maybe I just need to practice more!

comments.author: newman s lanier
comments.text: The controls are what make the game fun. A solid effort and cool that you worked together on it. to improve? Add some audio or some character elements. Who are these Templars? Also, the day and night was fun. Thanks for submitting!

comments.author: Ania Kubów
comments.text:
Big fan of the pixel sprites and retro feel that keeps to the game competition theme. I enjoyed playing this and thought the movements of the ship were very in-keeping with that of manouvering an actual ship or treacherous waters.

however at points the ship collisions were a bit off? I am not sure if that would be an easy fix or not?

Well done!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Well, everyone was alive and well for at least 4 days :). I think that a lantern from a ship that would shine at night would add interest to the game. As well as additional weather conditions, such as rains that generate killer waves, fog, etc… And also increase the game area :).

Overall a good game - congratulations!

comments.author: Frederic Kyung-jin Rezeau
comments.text:
Great game! The difficulty ramps up fast. 50 hours my best.

I noticed that restarting after loss without closing the game generates harder worlds with more rocks, is that intended?

Love the art and execution. Well done!

api.name: Guess the year
api.description:
Imagine Wordle, but for Math Geeks 🤓 (but don’t worry if you’re not)

And now try to guess all the years of the 13th century with the provided clues.

You start with a “blind” guess and then will be given clues based on the fun mathematical properties your guess shares with the secret number.

These properties are:

  • even/odd
  • prime factors
  • sum of digits
  • (more to come in future versions 😉)

Each round will be a new random number in that range. And you can collect stars based on your performance.

Don’t know where to start or how to continue? Check out the cheat sheet.

Had enough of the 13th century? Check out the other ranges via the config wheel.

Have fun!

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
Brain too smol, I just can’t compute prime factors of large numbers like that.

But the premise of the game is very unique! I guess some visualization might help. Like, 30 dots is 5 groups of 6 dots each, something like that. To me, looking at that is immediately obvious, and saying “2, 3, 5” just isn’t.

(Okay for small numbers it actually is, but for like 1209 having factors of 3, 13, 31 I can’t imagine that in my head at all, and I also can’t compute 39 by 33 on the fly anyway, because brain too smol.)

comments.author: Almut Kieffer-Jones
comments.text:
Thanks for your detailed feedback!

One thing I originally had, but then kicked out due to space reasons on mobile: was the greatest common divisor (or greatest known divisor). Meaning in your example: 30 = 2 * 3 * 5. (It was kind of duplicate with the factors, so I thought it was fine to remove). But I guess it could help making the next guess. E.g. You guessed 1260 and 30 was the gcd, then it can only be 1290 or 1230 …

But in general for the 13th century numbers, I was also using the cheat sheet a lot, there are just too much prime numbers out there

comments.author: Curtis Robinson
comments.text: Fun game! I like how you can try to get 5 stars on every number. I got over 200 stars while in a work meeting, without looking at the cheat sheet. Would’ve been nice to have a fun fact about the year to be more on theme.

comments.author: Almut Kieffer-Jones
comments.text:
Good idea with the fun facts! 💡

I just was a little bit low on time this year 🙈

comments.author: Ben Fox
comments.text: Really cool concept, definitely goes over my head.

comments.author: Francesco Bedussi
comments.text: Nice little game, fun to play for a while

comments.author: Arthur Brongniart
comments.text:
I found the default mode 1201 - 1300 insanely hard without the cheat sheet!
But I had fun with the easy one :D

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: I am not clever enough for this type of game but I appreciate the effort that went into it!

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text: An interesting take on Wordle gameplay, but with a math twist. Mathle? Good stuff.

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: The parity property is redundant, as it also is covered by the prime factors, so in fact we only get two properties. Nice that you managed to add l10n.

comments.author: Almut Kieffer-Jones
comments.text:
Thanks for the detailed feedback!

True, if the player starts with an even guess, the property becomes redundant. I did consider getting rid off it, but decided against it, because this is the property everyone immediately understands. I was now rather thinking the opposite direction and giving more redundant information, like the greatest common factor, to give more support to non-math-geeks (:

An additional property I did have on some point and wanted to readd if I had more time, was the amount of prime factors. So you would find out faster if it’s a prime number or not. My plan was to allow the player to “buy” this additional property for 1 star. And get matching digits for 2 stars (when you are really desperate for the solution)

The reason I added German L10n was that I let my parents test the game :D

comments.author: Jeremy Burns
comments.text: Fun puzzle game based on an interesting idea! As another commenter suggested, I think some way of visualizing the properties would be a nice way to get people with less of a math background interested in it. Either way, nice work!

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Interesting, but really complicated puzzle. Also need some calculator if you want to test different options and proposals. Guessed several digits on 5 stars, but it depends on digit delimiters mostly. Not the greatest game, but really interesting puzzle, good job!

comments.author: Frederic Kyung-jin Rezeau
comments.text: Very original concept, fitting the theme of the competition. The clue system is well thought as well. Great execution!

api.name: Young Magi, Awaken
api.description:
You’re a powerful mage who discovered the power of the void. Many envy it, many desire it. You must keep the void out of the greedy hands of the Rivington Knight Clan and protect its might at all costs.

Controls

  • Click, drag, and release to move the void (black hole).
  • WASD to move the mage.

!!! Be sure to consume all of the game’s text to see all tutorial steps.

!!! Still some issues with knights being out of position. If it happens to you, just refresh the game to place them correctly.

Wish I could add more, out of time though. Enjoy!

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Huh, that was fascinating. I think you’ve got some very very interesting ideas here. The sprites are adorable, and the whole look was cohesive too.

The actual controls though, didn’t feel very solid. I could easily tell you wanted me to make a path to walk the mage up, but the void’s controls made it too fiddly to execute on the 2 attempts I made, and then the wizard’s movement made it hard to land up on a single crate. Because everything was destructive, you don’t really get a second chance either, so one accidental nudge and your run is over.

There’s definitely something here though, overlaying a golf game and a platformer might be a win!

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text:
“I’m tired and need to go to bed now” :D Nice game and thanks for submitting it!

I really liked the visuals and onboarding experience with consumable tips. Also noticed a small problem: if you hit light in the beginning, you still can get rid of the first knight, however, the rest of the game becomes problematic, since the void sphere became smalled. Would be nice to get some music in future.

comments.author: Crystal Parker
comments.text: I really loved the game, but it would have been nice to have more levels.
games: Journey to the East; Silk Road Adventure

comments.author: Grav
comments.text: This one was awesome. The gameplay concept is absolutely unique, the spritework is amazing and the presentation is so cool. I was stuck on the big guy for a while until I realized your hint of “carving your path”, now I even see there is a hint in the thumbnail 😓. That merger platforming and absorption style gameplay is really fun. It just needs a brooding soundtrack and a few more levels and it would be perfect.

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
Woah, this is sweet! I love the main gameplay mechanic. Graphics are great, too. The void eating all of the tutorial letters was really cool

The void was a little hard to control, so as someone else mentioned, making platforms for yourself was a little hard. Maybe instead of changing the void motion, the wizard could be able to jump a little higher, to make that part a little more forgiving.

In any case, this is really cool! Would love to see it expanded upon

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: Thats a cool combination of gameplays! Good graphics, and the seamless integration of the “how-to-play” into the level is great. This game deserves more levels, please make a post-competition-version! :)

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text: Good looking game with simple controls and a fun, powerful VOID! Fits the theme well and has a charming aesthetic. Nice work!

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Unfortunately, I could not properly move: I have “search as you type” enabled in my browser, and you don’t call .preventDefault() on key events, so when I try to move, the page search opens for me.

comments.author: Taufik Sani
comments.text:
Controls - I used the mouse and took a little while to get used to controlling the void. Looking at the controls, it seems the drag is optimized for touch screen?

Onboarding - I like how the game eases the player into the level with tutorials and increasing difficulty.

Audio - no comments as I wasn’t able to hear any.

Innovation - The dual character control of the void and the mage is awesome. Games that require coordination of two characters at the same time are not plenty, and I felt the game managed to exert good amount of challenge to the player.

Graphics - it’s great for a 2D side scroller. I don’t see how it could’ve been better considering the 13kb rule.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
Nice intro, and it was fun to absorb the text. It was a great aha-moment.

I love the concept. It’s really innovative. The controls are a bit difficult to use at first, but I think it’s a good choice to have the controls as-is, because the game would have become too easy otherwise.

I wish the game had some sounds and music, which would elevate the experience.

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Hey there!

Incredible concept.

I’ll be honest, because I was on a trackpad, it was incredibly hard to play. Also, I don’t have a qwerty keyboard, so I would have enjoyed using the arrow keys.

The tutorial is nice, well thought, though it took me a few minutes to understand I had to swallow the letters from the first screen as I kept dragging lol.

Really cool concept, well done. A nice music would be amazing too!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Great idea - congratulations!

Maybe with the next levels, you can add one more sphere, which would be the opposite of the black hole, namely - repelling instead of attracting - I think you can come up with a lot of interesting things :).

api.name: Kite Kaos
api.description:
By the 13th century, the Taoist monks had made man-carrying kites into a common technology.

You have been strapped to a kite as punishment. The local guards are using you for target practice with their newly invented rockets! Avoid the cross-hairs and dodge the debris. The lower the number of hits, the better you’ve done!

Controls

  • Use WASD or the arrow keys to move.
  • Press the Space bar to soar upwards and escape from harm.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
Nice clear graphics, good music, good controls.My beste score after some attempts was 90… (i guess i’m a bad bullet-hell player)

A restart-function would be nice And maybe the kite movement should ease in/out a tiny bit, to make it smoother.

comments.author: Daniel Jeffery
comments.text:
From the playtesting I did, 90 is a very reasonable score, given the difficulty. Well done!

Thanks for the feedback - I’ll be sure to incorporate it if I continue working on the game post-competition!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: I love the music. The graphics are simple but effective. I feel like it’s basically impossible to avoid hits, and if I think about the backstory, being hit 100+ times without dying doesn’t seem to make much sense.

comments.author: Daniel Jeffery
comments.text:
Totally agree with the story aspect - I think I was trying to make the mechanics fit the theme, rather than the theme be an inspiration for the mechanics.

Thanks for playing, and thank you for the feedback!

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: The music is amazing! This is a perfect casual game: easy to understand, hard to master. When the player reaches the end of the game, I think a new game could start.

comments.author: Daniel Jeffery
comments.text:
Thank you so much! I really glad you enjoyed it!

A restart button is definitely getting added if I work on it post-competition.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done

  • The music was great. Just when I thought it was going to get repetitive it changed and that made all the difference
  • Liked that the explosions are timed to the beat of the music
  • Different sizes and speeds of bullets was a nice touch

Feedback:

  • I didn’t realize that the soar upwards ability had a limit so was really confused when it stopped working. By the time I noticed that “escapes” at the top of the screen it was at 0 and I had no idea what that meant
  • When the soar ability ends it felt like it immediately took damage from everything underneath. Would have been nice to have some sort of indication of when the kite was about to be let down to know not to be over something
  • Would have been nice if there was an indication of which type of bullet would be shot from the reticle
  • The random nature of the spawns and the way the bullets all spread out from the center makes it feel impossible to dodge stuff. A more pattern based spawning with possibilities to dodge would make the game feel more replayable

comments.author: Daniel Jeffery
comments.text:
Thanks Steven! Really appreciate all the detailed feedback.

Also, thank you for Kontra.js - it was my first year using it and once I started to understand the programming patterns, it was a lot of fun to work with.

comments.author: Frahaan Hussain
comments.text: Reminded of Bubble Trouble and a mega brick breaker game, interesting concept but lacked replayability. Maybe different levels and difficulties would help this.

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text: The visual is clear, simple, and lovely, and the music really matches the style very well! The mechanism is fun and effective, and it is enjoyable to play the game! The soar ability is not super clear from the beginning, and it would be nice to have some additional animation to show the effect of the soar ability ( like a glow around the kite). Another thing that could be helpful is to introduce players to the game more gradually, with increasing difficulty levels that start with fewer crosshairs and progress to more challenging scenarios. To motivate players, it could be nice to have some setup goals or a fake leaderboard system to encourage players to keep playing. Having some rewards like additional escape chances could also enhance the gaming experience!

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Bullet hell like game, but you can’t shoot) Interesting concept, game is simple, but interesting. Maybe better option would be to avoid hits for maximum time and increasing dencity of fireworks. Also it looks like your kite have rectangular collizion form, so it’s not fear you can’t avoid som bullets with your invisible body part. Good Job!

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text:
Great music! Timing the explosions with the music was pretty cool!

The game feels good.

I would have liked if one could get more escapes, somehow. For example, with something that one pick up after it is spawned in a random position in the screen.

api.name: CannonBolf
api.description:
Destroy all the castles with a cannonball in the fewest number of shots! Use the mouse to aim and click to shoot. Once you’re moving, hold down the mouse to turn the ball back into a cannon, and release to shoot again.

The game is pretty short, but you can try and go back and birdie every hole if you get to the end and are looking for a more difficult challenge.

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
The physics are amazing, and I really love the concept. I have definitely not played this game before! When you slow down in mid air and nail another shot it feels amazing!

I do feel it’s maybe a little too hard. Maybe have slightly bigger castles to aim for? Or maybe it’s just a level layout balance. I’d also like either a movable camera so I can see the level ahead of me, or the ability to look straight down the cannons, or both. Sometimes lining up a shot, even with the little aim helper can be hard, especially if the castle is somewhere I can’t see.

But still had fun with it. I’d love to learn how you did the physics. If you do a post mortem or any tutorials or articles on it I’d love to see it!

comments.author: Jeremy Burns
comments.text: Thanks! Maybe I’ll make a markdown file in the repo that explains how everything works. Wouldn’t be too much extra effort, and I enjoyed reading yours and others’ post mortems, so I should contribute one.

comments.author: Christopher Lis
comments.text: I had a lot of fun playing this. A+ gameplay and controls, really easy to get the ball going where you want it to and move its trajectory with consecutive shots.

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: The game is fun, but doesn’t really fit the theme, as 13th century artillery wasn’t strong enough to penetrate castle walls.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: Some of the levels are devious! Very enjoyable puzzle game.

comments.author: Marcin Walczak
comments.text: A pleasant and challenging puzzle game. Really well done in terms of gameplay, graphics, controls and audio.

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text:
Very interesting. It is a pretty engaging game. I can see people spending a good amount of time playing. Good controls and great physics and mechanics.

On the other hand, I don’t feel the time is quite right. The hints are a bit unclear, I think (but this could be a preference).

Great job!

comments.author: xem
comments.text: This game is pure mastery, in terms of graphics, ambiance, and challenge. Impressive for this size

comments.author: Francesco Bedussi
comments.text: Simple concept but really well done.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
