comments.text: Very good! The 3D gaphics, physics and controls are impressive. The progressive increase of the difficulty - with a new challenge for each level - is very well done.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Great graphics, the 3d levels worked well.

The game was fun to play, and at first I didn’t realise I could stop the ball mid-motion to switch back to a cannon - which actually really helped to play.

Gave me a monkeyball vibe.

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Looks like a reskinned golf, but interesting game) Pretty impressive implementation with 3d and physics. Gameplay is a little weird, but fun.

comments.author: Aurélio A. Heckert
comments.text: It’s ok, it is fun, but you could have polished it more. I think.

comments.author: Allen Schmidt
comments.text: Awesome game!! Kinda felt like mini golf mixed with Neon White, but with cannons! The simple graphics worked great, and the physics were on point. The castle hitboxes felt tiny, but I still managed to birdie most of the holes. Really well done!

comments.author: Markus Fisch
comments.text: Fun game with really nice graphics! I like that the camera is following the cannon ball so closely! Very nice take for a golf game!

comments.author: Ania Kubów
comments.text: Very impressed with this game! From a coding perspective, but also game play perspective. I am always impressed when the physics of throw are implemented well. This game was a very unique twist on golf that takes you on a visual journey I have not experienced before - in terms of following the ball. I would love to see this game developed for the professional market. Well done!

comments.author: Gauthier Billot
comments.text: I really enjoyed this one, I completed all the levels. The slow-motion trick is nice!

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text: What a crazy idea for a game. But damn fun! It takes a little while before you get good ar reusing the cannon after the first shot, but once you get good at it you can do some pretty cool things. Well done.

api.name: Vagabond King of Cards
api.description:
A young vagabond is adopted/abducted (abdopted?) and enters the patronage of a wealthy and powerful personage; they are introduced and then immersed in the shadowy world of King of Cards - a mysterious and possibly supernatural tournament of 1:1 duels where the only weapons are quick eyes and keen minds.

The goal of each match is to find a set of three cards that are a “true trinity” faster than your opponent.

Each trinity card has four aspects: illustration, inner border, color and count. Each aspect has three possible variations.

True trinity is balance and harmony. Each of the four aspects of a true trinity card must either fully align or stand unique among its partners.

False trinity lacks balance. For two cards an aspect’s form aligns, but for their third it does not. One is a lost soul and true trinity is impossible.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Neat idea! I didn’t get it at first, but once it clicked I enjoyed playing a few rounds. I really like the little cutscene of travelling to the next match, adds nice atmosphere, as does the well cued audio.

Nice one!

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text:
I didn’t get it. The two tutorials did not seem to teach me anything.

Graphics are very nice though. I love the paper look! Music and sounds are very fitting for the game. I really would have liked to understood it.

comments.author: Luke Stapley
comments.text:
The tutorials really should share the reasons why you won and don’t need to be randomized. I also had issues seeing all 12 cards when in the browser. the rest went off the screen.

The music is excellent and works perfectly for this game, so I really enjoyed that. Keep it up. You have the right idea.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
My kids have a very similar card game called “Set”.

Good version of this gameplay, nice matching graphics and sound

comments.author: Johan Åhlgren
comments.text: I am afraid I didn’t understand what to do, using the two tutorials. It’s a pity, because the graphics and sound are really nice and somehow I think that if I could understand it, I would also appreciate it. Nice job!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: I had no idea what was going on until I read the comment saying this was a version of the game “Set”. Good use of the theme to reinvent this classic. There were a couple visual glitches (cards drawn outside bounds, text overlapping) but otherwise I quite like the graphics. If this is a single player game, I don’t see the point in having the big red button. It just confused me at first.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: This game is similar to the “set” boardgame. It is really well done: I like the graphics and the sound. However I spent lot of time to understand how to play (first click on “Strike / Claim”, then click on each individual card of the trinity). I think you could improve the tutorial part.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • Cards looked nice
  • Different difficult levels and tutorials are really nice

Feedback:

  • I still don’t understand how to play. I’ve tried to read the game description and play the tutorials multiple times, but it’s not clicking for me
  • Having to click “strike” before tapping a card wasn’t clear at first
  • Clicking strike as soon as the game starts let you stall a bit while still looking at the cards, thus getting more time to figure out a trinity while your opponent cannot do anything
  • I tried the 12 card game for fun and all the cards wouldn’t fit on my screen. Only 6 cards were visible and no way to see more. Had to resize the screen to a larger size in order to see all 12

comments.author: Santiago Zapata
comments.text:
It took me a long time to find out how to play; I had to refer to the help text, outside of the game, several times, to try to figure it out :P

So, of course, it would have been best for the tutorials to make a better effort to teach the player the game (or maybe even have some narrative, heck, just adding the instructions to a help button you could check at any time, with a couple of examples, would have sufficed)

That’s the end of the feedback to improve because, other than that, I loved this entry and I’m glad I stuck to it until I had learned. There’s a fun and challenging game here!

Graphics and audio are also top notch.

Also, the cards didn’t wrap up properly in the voting site, had to play outside.

My preferred setup is 6 cards, 3 points, for now!

Thank you!

comments.author: Ben Fox
comments.text:
Really nice visuals, overall well executed but would have benefitted from more explicit instruction.

After figuring out how to play I really enjoyed it. I think the words “Strike Claim” were confusing, and at first I thought it was two separate buttons telling me either to “strike” non-trinity cards or “claim” trinity.

I didn’t understand how/if the Duel Choices affected the play or the difficulty. It seems too ‘easy’ that I had as much time as I wanted to inspect the cards before I could click the button to go and immediately selecting the trinity.

comments.author: Marcin Walczak
comments.text:
Nice graphics and gameplay. Cards design is readable. Game on higher difficulty level can be challenging. I appreciate match customization options.

Tutorial levels don’t explain why you win / loose (although instructions in game description explain rules sufficiently).

Sometimes cards don’t wrap properly and end up in line beyond screen (lowering window height helps in these case).

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Great Job! Really interesting puzzle. It’s hard to understand rules, but after some training it’s really interesting game

api.name: 13 Geese Defending Rome
api.description:
Variations of the fox and geese game have been popular in Europe since at least the 13th century.

This variation travels along 13th century trade routes between the major cities of Medieval Europe.

It may not be in the spirit of the JS13k competition, but since JavaScript was not around in the 13th century this game is completely JavaScript free (aside from being generated by Node).

Good luck and I hope you enjoy the game!

Rules

The fox and geese alternate turns moving along the paths. You play as the geese and will move first after clicking Start.

Any one goose can move 1 space in any direction to an unoccupied city as long as there is a path between the cities. The fox can also move to any unoccupied, connected city or it can jump over one goose to remove that goose from the game.

A jump must remain on 1 path so it must end on an unoccupied city on the same color path as it started.

  • The goal for the fox is to reach Rome (or kill enough geese to guarantee victory).
  • The goal for the geese is to trap the fox so that is has no moves.

Controls

To move, click a goose and then click the city to move it to. Once you have clicked on a goose you must move it or leave it where it is! You only have 60 seconds each turn to make your decision. After taking your turn, make the fox’s move by clicking the box in the upper left two times (for a regular move) or three times (for a jump).

Notes

If you like JS13k games because they lightweight, then this game is probably not for you as it ends up inflating to more than a MB of CSS.

This game might work on some mobile devices, but it is likely unplayably slow and/or too small. Safari is probably slow but playable if you have enough CPU. Firefox might have text-shadow issues but seems to be much faster. Chrome on a newish laptop/desktop is recommended for a (hopefully) flawless experience.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
I read the description, and the in-game “How to play,” but I never figured out how to play the game unfortunately. The theme is well interpreted, and it looks like a game with a lot of effort put into it.

The geese never showed up. I guess the black squares I saw were supposed to be the geese emojis, so I had that in mind when I tried playing the game. I was able to move a goose, but I was never able to trap the fox.

If the fox is the enemy, I think it would be better if an AI automatically moved the fox, instead of letting the player click the button to move it.

comments.author: Grav
comments.text: Nice game, I didn’t have any graphical issues over here, and was able to win after all. It took me a few goes, the key to victory was the rule that the fox can only jump and land on the same colored path. Would be great if there was some thinking music playing too. Overall a fun game I think, thanks.

comments.author: xem
comments.text: geese didn’t show up for me either :(

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: The geese are really hard to see, you should use a darker background. Also I didn’t like I had to click so many times to make the computer move the fox. If I understand it correctly, that’s due to the fact you didn’t use any JS on purpose, which is really cool. Otherwise a nice game.

comments.author: Johan Åhlgren
comments.text: A nice game, although I didn’t see any geese. just squares. I felt the controls were a bit clumsy, perhaps the fox could move/jump/kill without any interaction from the player? Nice graphics too. Good job!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
I was really intrigued about a game that was javascript free, but unfortunately I can’t seem to play it. The page seems to be loading for a long time, loading a lot of XML files, but it never stops. The instructions say to click on a goose, but I only see a map with roads.

Edit: ok, it seems to work on Chrome, but doesn’t work on Firefox at all. The game was interesting, but I lost. I’m veeeery curious to learn how you implemented this. I vaguely remember doing some XSL wizarding in college but my brain promptly erased that from my memory. I’ll give you 5 stars in innovation just for that!

comments.author: Tim West
comments.text: “Running on latest Firefox” is one of the js13k rules. Otherwise, I’m very impressed with the extra challenge of using no JavaScript.

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text:
Cute title! Basing a game on animals is always a good time.

I like that the instructions or “how to play” is embedded within the game. Unfortunately I didn’t see any Geese icons. Either there’s a bug (I’m on Chrome / OS X) or I’m not understanding how to make them appear.

Personally I don’t think the time limit is necessary. I also think the game could use some clarity around what state the game is in, and what valid move(s) are currently available to me.

Sounds like this is based on an old European game that I’m not familiar with. Fans of that game might love it!

comments.author: Frederic Kyung-jin Rezeau
comments.text:
Loved this game, fits the theme perfectly and has great depth.

Hack: It is also possible to get a head start by selecting “Start” then clicking “Restart”, you can then play a move before clicking “Start” again ;)

Bug: I was not able to see the goose (playing in Chrome). It shows squares.

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text: Cool game! I’ve never played this before so it’s cool to see it being remade! Pretty bang on for the theme I would say which is really nice! Not a whole lot of notes or pointers given the game is pretty simple! It took me a minute to figure out the game (the geese were invisible for me!) but enjoyed it once I got into it. Nice little game, congrats!

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Really interesting puzzle. It looks like classical “wolf and sheeps” game but with non regular grid as a level. Sometimes it’s really complicated. UX is not the best, sometimes it’s hard to understand there is the issue and what happens with actions. Good Job!

comments.author: Jupiter Hadley
comments.text: I have read how to play, but I still do not understand how to make my interactions work. I do not see any geese in the game. I feel like there is something I am just not getting.

api.name: The Book of Ani
api.description:
The game is based on the 13th century papyrus named The Book of Dead. Ani is your hero and he haves to pass through challenges and puzzles to send all the Amulets to the gods and weight his heart on the scale of the Osiris Court.

  • left, right - walk
  • up - jump
  • down - sit / shaddow
  • enter - enters/ play again

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Intriguing concept, great music! As for the gameplay - I failed to understand what I had to do to leave the first room.

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text: Pretty good music! The game was actually engaging. Although it was kind of difficult to understand how to do things. Either way, I finished the game, and it was cool to play it. Congrats!

comments.author: andreryuji
comments.text: Loved the theme of the game and the concept that links gameplay and the soundtrack.

comments.author: Rémi Vansteelandt
comments.text:
The idea is great, interesting take on the theme and I like the idea of telling a story in this fashion. It was very difficult to figure out what I was supposed to do, and I got stuck in chapter 2 towards the left of the first block I moved (couldn’t jump back on top of it).

I would highly suggest increasing movement speed. Such slow movement tends to get frustrating despite the idea being really good.

comments.author: xem
comments.text: didn’t find the amulet at the beginning but nice ambiance !

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Loved the concept and the graphics of this game. The music was really good.

It was fun to explore the levels.

Some of the movements were a little slow and there could have been more animation when moving, e.g. the character opened their legs when moving rather than open/close legs cycling.

I’ll have to play this game again!

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I finished the game! The concept is very interesting as a mix of platformer / puzzle game / story telling. I loved it. Also the music is amazing. To be perfecct, I think you could add some animation to the main charatcer.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Very well done! Loved the music. Also loved that this had a story, a whole beginning middle and end. The graphics were satisfyingly detailed too.

The gameplay was charmingly odd, from the stilted jump to the lack of explicit direction, it felt a lot like a sort of narrative puzzle game rather than an action one, and I liked that a lot.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • Loved the concept of telling a story through gameplay
  • The graphics were all really nice. Really liked the egyptian pose of Ani when he sat
  • Loved that you used Egypt rather than a European location for your inspiration of the theme
  • Music was great and went well with the story and gameplay

Feedback:

  • It took me a good while to figure out what I needed to do in the first room as it wasn’t obvious that you needed to sit to activate things (an unconventional mechanic)
  • I got stuck in the second room to the left side as I couldn’t jump back up above the movable block
  • The shaddow ability can drop through single floors. It would have been really cool to see a puzzle based on that

comments.author: jadirferreirajunior
comments.text: Muito bom Divertido, simples e bonito. Eu Realmente gostei

comments.author: VilligerM
comments.text:
Great soundtrack

I love that jumps are calculated to be increasingly difficult, and the shadow power gives us access to well kept amulets

It was quite fun to figure out the way foward

comments.author: lucasprp123
comments.text: Excelent game! Outstanding creativity

comments.author: Allen Schmidt
comments.text: Cool little game! I liked the graphics and the music, and following the story was neat! Some of the jumps were a little tricky, and the character movement felt a bit slow, but for a puzzle game it still worked. Well done!

comments.author: Frahaan Hussain
comments.text: Very cool concept, reminded me of when I went Egypt. Fun game overall.

comments.author: Tom Harris
comments.text: It wasn’t immediately clear what to do, but also not too difficult to figure out. Once you figure it out, this is a nice puzzle game that is quite engaging. The Egyptian theme is strong with visuals, sounds, and music coming together nicely.

comments.author: Frederic Kyung-jin Rezeau
comments.text: Very nice little game, completed all 4 chapters and wanted to play more! The challenges are original and well conceived.

api.name: Slowed Dao Run
api.description: The slowest runner ever: an meditative runner with an painterly style set in 13th Century Japan. Use the arrow keys or the buttons on the right to select the next move.

comments.author: newman s lanier
comments.text: I thought this was a cool game. Good attempt on the audio, but it’s tough. The controls are so smooth and slow - slow Dao’d, but the audio pulled me out of the meditative game play. I loved the art work of the character moving and jumping. really nice. thank you!

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
Wow, I love the graphics! Really cool effects, I’d be interested to see how you did it. I thought choosing the moves was pretty fun, especially when like rolling off the top of a hill. My favorite part was climbing a dog and stepping on a child.

The audio is bad. I also wish there was maybe a bit more to do? Like some pickups or powerups? And I wasn’t always sure how to increase my style/speed rating.

Still, I quite liked it (with the volume down), and the artistic style is top notch!

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text: The controls in mobile are smooth!! Good game, I really enjoyed it playing!!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
I liked the idea of picking up your next move, I just wish the movements would chain in a more impressive way (the drawings on the “cards” to the right didn’t look much like the actual movements of my character).

Slowing down time at moments is always interesting, but a complete slow motion game becomes “heavy” after a while. It’s also one of those games to which a toggle for music / sound effects would be super welcome.

The graphics are outstanding!

comments.author: Alexey Kalmakov
comments.text:
Great entry. Amazing style and graphics, not feel like 13k at all. The music was hard to listen. The gameplay is nice, no rush, just see how runner is running, relaxing experience(without sound).

Did not understand how style value works.

comments.author: Marcin Walczak
comments.text: Visually it looks amazing. Imitating ink brush painting was a great idea. The gameplay is quite relaxing. The only caveat would be the audio (that ping can be a little bit distracting).

comments.author: Sami Heikkinen
comments.text: Very beautiful, smooth experience. Might need a possibility to fail?

comments.author: Allen Schmidt
comments.text: Really nice work on the graphics! I like how they flow into view as you progress… the game itself was terribly slow though, and I had no idea if anything I pressed really made a difference. Not something to play during the afternoon slump as I did start falling asleep a little to the repetitive sound through the game haha. Nice work on the animations as well, and fitting everything in!

comments.author: Chris Glover
comments.text: Interesting idea, beautiful graphics

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
And really reassuring, thanks, it was fun!

Although the description of the game states that this is an application for 2024, well … will wait :D.

Congratulations!

comments.author: Ania Kubów
comments.text:
Wow! I was impressed with the visuals form the start - but when the right hand pop up’s came in I was blown away. A beautiful visual adventure.

It is also interesting to see a slow runner in comparison to the many fast runners we see today.

I enjoyed playing this game!

comments.author: Frahaan Hussain
comments.text: Very cool art style but the gameplay is too slow and doesn’t keep you hooked.

comments.author: Jeremy Burns
comments.text: Really fun idea, and the graphics are unique and well-done! Feels really satisfying to chain moves together. I expected once I finished a level that the camera pulling back was about to show my run at full-speed. That might be a nice feature to add! Even though “slowed” is in the title I did find myself wishing it moved a little faster. Very interesting mechanics though, and great work on the execution!

api.name: Snakes and Ladders
api.description:
Originally invented in 13th Century India as a game to teach children about the virtues and vices of life. (There is a great Wikipedia article about it.)

Snakes and Ladders is a two player game. In this version you, the Human, play against a Bot.

The Human goes first.

Click the dice to play.

Colour indicators and text indicate when it’s your turn to play.

comments.author: ch!k0
comments.text: i love the fact you shared about the game-i never knew it was made for such reason. however i wish you could have added your own twist/version of the game to make it more interesting. also the game screen was cut in half, i dont know if it’s supposed to be that way or not. all in all i enjoyed playing this nostalgic game, thank you!

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
The board is cute!

Gameplay-wise, however, I think it’s a miss, pressing the only button and then waiting, pressing and waiting…

Speaking of which, this might be an interesting take on an idle game: imagine that instead of playing yourself, you get to buy players (like smol ancient Indian robots) who each roll and resolve their turns, and you get more money / upgrade the ladders / do whatever, and then there’s the opponent who does the same thing but moves in the opposite direction or something, and upgrades the snakes.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Great research on the theme! I did enjoy the illustrations a lot too. This appears to be an entirely competent implementation of the referenced game.

Unfortunately the game itself isn’t very engaging. I did watch it take the first dozen turns or so, but I was not compelled to continue to the end.

comments.author: Francesco Bedussi
comments.text: Is there something else to do than just throwing the dice and watch the dot moving?

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
G’day!

Oh, won for the 10th time :D.

Good game! In my opinion, it would be even better if you add some sound to the dice play. It would also be an interesting feature to establish the randomness of the positions of snakes and ladders. Also, a game against a person in 2 or even 4 would add emotions :). It would also be a good addition in my opinion - a little animation of snakes and ladders when the character comes across them.

Just some crazy random, I’ve run into snakes so many times…. :D. But it was fun, thanks!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Well made, all works great, fun when you win! I hit the big ladder which was nice, plus avoided the big snake.

Did not know this game was 13th Century.

Good entry.

To improve, would be cool to have some animations, different size levels and maybe some random events you have to react too and maybe even bonus levels etc.

Could go full out and have achivements and upgrades.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A nice simple game.

Graphics worked well and looked good.

Sound effects were fun.

Maybe the dice rolls were a bit slow.

comments.author: Allen Schmidt
comments.text: Nice implementation of snakes and ladders, just doesn’t make for the most interesting single player game. I like the illustrations, and how when the two players are on the same spot the marker splits to show it. Good job!

comments.author: Emre Guneyler
comments.text: Fullscreen issues trying to play the game without scrolling is an issue. Apart from that, audio could use some juice, and a little more innovation would make this game sweeter to play. Oh, And some cookies at the end game.

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: I love how the snakes look! almost like gummi worms with cute googly eyes! great work on the game, it ran smoothly and was easy to play. my little kid would have loved this game a few years ago. I appreciated how when both tokens were on the same tile they are drawn combined. a nicely polished entry! well done.

comments.author: Tom Harris
comments.text: It’s classic snakes and ladders with minimal gameplay interaction through the use of a dice. The visuals are nice and well polished.

comments.author: Jupiter Hadley
comments.text: Everything works and it’s pretty well made. I did not know the history of Snakes and Ladders - so that was interesting to learn. Good entry.

api.name: Hugo the Wizard
api.description:
Mouse only. Drag goblins left or right and see what happens.

Decentralized

Log in with NEAR wallet to get a bonus goblin at the start. Mr. Brown NFT owners get another bonus goblin.

A rougelike goblin-builder game.

Game Setting

Set in Scotland in 1276. You are real person that existed at the time. You travel to castles that existed in the 13th century on real and plausible path that will take you to battle King Alexander III where he lives.

Audio

Atmospheric sounds. Effects that are near one side of the screen are louder on that speaker. Works on phones too if you have headphones. Two 13th century songs. One 18th century song written in Scotland. One song written by me for this game.

Credits

Music

  • Alta Trinita Beata - Laudario di Cortona - 1200s
  • Totus Floreo - Unknown - 1200s
  • Goblin Battle - Curtastic - 2023
  • The Bonnie Banks o’ Loch Lomond - Unknown - 1700s

Development

  • Game Design - Curtastic
  • Code - Curtastic
  • Artwork - Curtastic
  • NEAR integration - Vertfromage
  • Compression - RoadRoller

Special thanks

  • Slay the Spire
  • Liath Wolf
  • Mark Nicol
  • Desertify Urbex
  • The World from Above HD
  • Fortress of Lugh

comments.author: Oliver Smith
comments.text: A bit of a slow burn to get going, I found it confusing at first but ultimately it is very playable and entertaining. The presentation is great, with some different style to it and I really appreciated the music. Good stuff.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
I liked the mechanic of discarding one unit - but I feel it didn’t provide enough incentives (specially when you don’t get much choice in getting new units - I just grabbed everybody all the time; I always discarded the “weakest” of them).

I wish there were better alternatives for protecting yourself - in upper level castles (I made it to level 6), even with a lot of imps on your side to bump up your shield, you still receive massive damage and die.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
What a great time! The music is excellent, the background research is impeccably so far past the average bar, it’s unreal.

The graphics were OK, loved all the animations. Palette was a little garish though, it didn’t really convey the tone your music and research set up.

Enjoyed all the comedy. The priest’s dialog was great :)

The gameplay was alright. Honestly wasn’t sure what I was doing even on the second playthrough, so that was a bit of a letdown. Probably need a mini manual out here in the description to get the most out of it.

comments.author: Curtis Robinson
comments.text: Thanks I’m glad someone noticed the amount of research I did in a seemingly nonsense game! I went through the massive list of castles in Scotland to find the ones that existed that year and mapped out a path to where the king’s castle was at the time. My art on the Goblin Hall is based on real photos. When Alexander III talks I included a quote from a real medieval poem written about Hugo. When you defeat him you take the crown from the Stone of Scone. The priest sometimes says a quote from an actual incantation used to remove demons. The Redcap goblin was a famous one who’s said to have a red cap from soaking it in the blood of his victims so I made his hat get brighter read each time he deals the final blow. The kelpie’s special ability to a pull a goblin to the grave pile is from medieval stories of kelpies pulling people in to drown them. And of course the music. Was a let down to see that my game only got 0.67 more on “theme” a game that has nothing to do with the 13th century, just “castle” in its name.

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text: Fun graphics and music fits it very well :) Laughed a lot :)

comments.author: Miguel Ángel Pérez Martínez
comments.text: Looks and sounds very good. Gameplay was a bit confusing, I wasn’t sure how much I was affecting the results of the game apart from dragging goblins right. Might benefit a lot from some instructions

comments.author: Curtis Robinson
comments.text: Thanks ya I should have added more instructions. Your actions matter a lot because I beat the game nearly every time I try, while nobody else has ever, as far as I know. I’ll put some here because I don’t think I can edit. Drag left for special. When no cannon is coming, drag Grogs left for +1 defense on yourself for the rest of the battle. If a Redcap deals the final blow it gets +2 strength permanently. Get rid of Torchers if you have no fire. Otherwise always get rid of Stabbies. Save your Clones for an upgraded Redcap or for a Kelpie or a Caller.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Works well, cute graphics and playable / humourous.

Was not sure if my deciding on attack or extra defence was having much impact on the battles, felt I was always headed toward defeat.

Well made and some fun.

comments.author: Grav
comments.text: One of the standouts. I’m terrible at deck-builders but really liked this one. Some text instructions in the description would have really helped the learning curve, but once you get it, it is a lot of fun.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Really unique game with goofy graphics and quite creative and fitting songs. I like the speech sound effects. The game seems bit repetitive after a while and I didn’t understand if there’s some sort of strategy I should follow. I just kept swiping my units at the towers because I had so many units, and little control over which units to send to the front. Overall good job.

comments.author: Magnus Lindh
comments.text: The problem with this game is that it is too easy. Alternatively it takes too long time to get hard. It’s a shame since everyting is so polished! Sound,graphics and controls are all fantastic.

comments.author: Tim Sulmone
comments.text: The game had a lot of humor to it, I can tell you had fun making it. Good ol’ reliable stabbie. Expecto Patronum!!

api.name: Feast and courtesans
api.description:
Visit a medieval feast as a courtesan.

To feed yourself, you need to seduce at least 7 boyars on a holiday. But beware of their ladies!

Press the arrows to walk.
For a seductive wink, press the space bar.

comments.author: ElinaAlexeenko17
comments.text: This game very interesting My voice for you

comments.author: Condescendingfox
comments.text: it’s a shame there’s no sound, but I like that.

comments.author: G300rG3
comments.text: Dude, the game is so cool. Graphics, character design, gameplay, setting. I don’t know why there is no music, but I’m sure the game is worthy of a soundtrack from Danny Elfman! My point to you)

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
The first time the ladies discovered I got totally caught by surprise, well done! :)

I like a lot that it’s not the typical castle/forest setting, and the graphics are super nice.

I tried many times but never managed to get past 4 - it’s tricky to get the camera to see everything you need to see in order to not get caught. A nice simple song would have made this much better :)

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: Funny concept and cool graphics. Gameplay is a little too basic. Maybe some alert levels for wives could help, so they don’t attack immediately, but also are getting suspicious even if you just stand to close to their husbands. Also, they could walk around a bit.

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text: The concept is funny and engaging! The graphics are great! Good job!

comments.author: Luke Stapley
comments.text: The art style is fun, and the stealth-like gameplay is fun, but the lack of depth is what hurts the game. I do like the stabbing. Who wouldn’t?

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
Ha, amazing graphics and animations, hilarious concept.

Gameplay is a bit rough. Ladies would kill me even if I didn’t wink at their man. If I was winking in their direction they’d kill me, even if i winked at someone 3 people over. That combined with just sitting there waiting for people to turn around was a bit much.

But if you could improve the gameplay or expand the concept and keep the art style and add music, it would be amazing!

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text:
“All the boyars are seduced”

Perfect JS13k game 10/10 A+ no notes.

comments.author: Grav
comments.text: graphics are amazing, there is so much going on in the bg! Would be great to hear some music in here too. .

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
I like how well the theme is interpreted in this game. The mechanics are easy to understand. I got stabbed pretty early on, but it helped me understand the rules quickly, so I didn’t mind.

The graphics are nice, and I only miss some music and sfx.

comments.author: Federico Jacobi
comments.text: Awesome and simple … and those graphics  😘🤌 just gorgeous

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
It’s so cute until he pulls out a knife from under the skirt and stabs your stomach :D.

I think this game is the most fun this year for me :). All the time, I was waiting for a reason from the last courtesans, that they would somehow accidentally return, or make deceptive turns - I think then it would be even more interesting!

Also some fun courtesan style music would make the game even more thematic in my opinion :D.

I had fun! Thanks and congratulations!

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Ok, that was hilarious. Those wives will cut a b***h! I have 7 Boyars in my pocket though now, so I’m good for the winter.

The graphics in this are gorgeous, I really like how you captured the feel of eastern European embroidery, and the heave of breathing under a corset. Just fabulous stuff. I would have loved to hear appropriate music during this one, the atmosphere really wanted it!

api.name: Idle Merchant Guild
api.description:
Welcome to the exciting world of the 13th century! Get ready to embark on a thrilling journey of trading and resource management in this captivating idle game.

In this game, you’ll dive into the bustling world of the 13th century, where empires and kingdoms thrived on the art of trading. Explore the rich tapestry of resources that were exchanged during this era, from luxurious silk to humble rice.

As you progress, you’ll unlock different buildings that allow you to gather a wide variety of resources. The best part? These resources are automatically gathered in the background, so you can watch your wealth grow even when you’re not actively playing!

But don’t rest on your laurels just yet. To turn your hard-earned resources into cold, hard cash, you’ll need to actively engage in the market. Strategically sell your resources to maximize your profits and become a savvy trader.

But be warned! The path to riches is not without its challenges. Keep a watchful eye for invaders who might attack your cargo while you’re running a trade route. Stay alert and protect your precious resources at all costs!

Get ready to flex your trading muscles as you manage multiple bases and navigate the intricacies of the market. Discover the thrill of making lucrative trade deals and become a master of resource management.

So, what are you waiting for? Dive into this immersive idle game and experience the excitement of trading in the 13th century. Are you ready to build your trading empire and make your mark in history? Let the adventure begin!

Controls

Use your mouse / finger and tap on the button :)

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • I liked the use of emoji for showing which climate the base is built in
  • Max sell button is very helpful
  • The different tabs was an interesting way to compact the information

Feedback:

  • It was difficult to figure out what to do at first (what’s a guild, what’s a base, etc.). It took awhile to figure out how the icons on the different buildings related to things
  • I wish I had a way to purchase resources and not just sell. With how much things start to cost after 3 buildings of the same type I would just have to wait for a long long time before being able to build the next level building
  • It would have been nice if the tab I was on in one base stayed on that same tab when clicking on a different base. That way I could mass sell resources for each base faster (stay on Market tab as I click between bases)
  • Should limit the number of decimal points on the money (or not show decimals at all). Were a lot of cases where JavaScript decimal math resulted in 10+ decimal points
  • I’m not sure what the different climate zones do for a base. I first thought that it would unlock different resources (that would be worth more when selling), but that doesn’t appear to be the case as they had the same 4 resources

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: The UI is sleek, and I like how the saved games work.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
G’day!

Either I didn’t understand how to play or something went wrong… All I could do was create new settlements, each with the same resources regardless of location, as well as the same guild name… maybe something will open up if to completely pump some settlement, but it takes too long…. (I created so many settlements that the scroll appeared in the list :) ).

I liked the UI, I also liked the timing of resources (nostalgic). The description called for trading with someone, but I didn’t get it, and I didn’t see any robbers either, but I guess there wasn’t enough time / size for it, if so, it’s a good start! If there was something more functional, but due to some errors I didn’t get it - I’m sorry…

A list of settlements that can be collapsed/expanded to see their graphical display - nice!

The process of loading the level increase was also pleasing to the eye. As for starting the leveling of various resources - sometimes I had the feeling that it does not start progress from the first click - I think it would be good to look into this.

I was pleasantly surprised by the game save mechanism - cool!

In any case, it was fun to play, although not very functional, but if it does everything it describes, I think it will be a lot more interesting and fun! Thanks!

comments.author: Francesco Bedussi
comments.text: Nice game, but after a while it becames too repetitive. I’d preferred if the different bases produced different output and you could cross use the materials between bases

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
I built the buildings in my new camp, sold some resources but was not sure what to do next. Would have been interesting to see what I was able to unlock so I had something to aim for, am sure there was more to come back I dont know what I dont know.

The layout and use of emojis is good and it all works well.

You have lots of positives here just needed:

  1. tutorial to get the player started
  2. show/ grey out what they cant yet build
  3. If you dont want to give away all the new buildings just the next 3 for examples
  4. music

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
Although I like this sort of game, I found that it lacks a little something to captivate me. I didn’t feel any sense of reward in building new buildings or selling my products, and the only purpose of money is to create new colonies which just mean extra work for me (more bases to manage). This means the game got progressively more worksome without any extra reward. Maybe a little visual reminder that I’m expanding and that my bases are growing, like a mini map, would do the trick.

There are some issues in the number rounding, as my money was “2718.8746499999997”.

I don’t understand the difference between the different climates where I built my bases, and I don’t understand how you “Strategically sell your resources”, as the market doesn’t seem to fluctuate.

There’s an abundance of tabs in the UI that make visualizing your empire too difficult. There’s so much empty space on the screen that could be used more effectively, at least on desktop.

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: what a great simulation! it felt fresh and complex, but relaxing! I really liked the nice progress-bar-background informational boxes with attractive icons for each section. it looked so professional and sleek. very cool project, nicely done.

comments.author: Santiago Zapata
comments.text:
This is a nice foundations for a bigger game for sure, with different types of goods per base, random geopolitical events affecting you, etc.

From what it’s here, I liked the cleanliness of the UI, and it was pretty stable.

Minor UI tweaks like merging all 3 tabs in a single screen would have made it a bit more useable.

It also didn’t seem there was a victory condition, or sinks for the gold other than building new bases. Also every base was super independent so you couldn’t really use your empire’s might to grow faster.

Thank you!

comments.author: Joep van Duinen
comments.text: Cool mechanics! It is very engaging! Well done! It would be nice to have some guide in the beginning because the tabs and options can be a bit overwhelming to players. The UX/UI design has some room for improvement, like shorter currency numbers, and a clear distinction between the maxed-our building and the unfinished ones. The game could also benefit greatly from having various resources in different areas and connecting all areas in the end with the trade or buildings!

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Well, simple time management / idler game. Not a lot of options or targets, so looks more like prototype.

comments.author: Jupiter Hadley
comments.text: Simple idea - well made game! Well done.

comments.author: Frederic Kyung-jin Rezeau
comments.text:
Really like that this game is idle with multitasking.

Would love to see a “buy” option in the market and also nudge buttons with preset amounts.

I will definitely be back for more :) Well done.

api.name: Road to Roam
api.description:
Road to Roam is the story of a young adventurer who sets off from his small village to find Rome, only to find himself lost in a field of hazards.

The goal is to ride as far as possible. Speed & obstacles increase over time!

Gameplay is heavily inspired by the classic Windows game, SkiFree.

Mobile is supported. Browser must be refreshed if the screen is rotated.

Controls

Supports:

  • Mouse (pointer)
  • Arrow keys
  • Touch

Esc = pause

Credits

  • Art by Juli West
  • Kontra.js game engine
  • TypeScript Starter by Rob Louie
  • SoundBox for music
  • Vite, Terser, RoadRoller, ECT for bundle & build

comments.author: Justin Sullard
comments.text: I was unable to play this one. There is an error loading a webp image (relative vs absolute path I think).

comments.author: Jack Le Hamster
comments.text: It’s good this game got fixed now. Since I missed it from my first video series, here’s a video for this game: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DIh_KGYZ4Fg

comments.author: SimonPen3
comments.text: Simple game with decent music. Rewards spawning at places would maybe give the game more content. Overall fun game.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: Simple game but very pleasant to play! I like your version of greensleeves song. I would suggest to keep playing the music when the game restarts (after a game over) instead of having to click again on the audio unmute button.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A fun skiing type game, I enjoyed the speed up and increase in obstacles as things went on.

A couple of times on my runs down the hill, I did better by not pressing anything at all!

Got to 316m after a bit of trial and error.

Perhaps when playing by keyboard, have a keyboard button to restart rather than clicking mouse?

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Simple but fun, loved the music! Decent graphics

All works well, controls could be a bit more responsive.

comments.author: Christian Staudte
comments.text: simple but fun to play. I really liked the music! I am playing on my laptop and to me the arrows were a bit too small.

comments.author: Daniel Whiffing
comments.text: Art is nice, though it would’ve been good to ensure it was rendered pixel perfect. Everything is quite small and blurry when I play. Music is nice and suits the theme. Gameplay is simple and to the point. Good job!

comments.author: ch!k0
comments.text: i died after a few seconds in. i couldn’t determine which objects were obstacles and which were not. which added to the thrill factor of the game. however i had to change direction to manipulate the sound icon-which lead to my death in one of my attempts to play this game-i wanted to win lol! it was fun and i love how you named in road to roam and literally just roam around the entire time. just curious though will the adventurer ever get to roam all the way to rome? ;) great game!

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Jeah a FreeSki clone! Reminds me of my childhood where this was basically the only game we had on the computer :). Fun little game! Regarding the graphics I would have liked if you had a sprite which looks diagonally. Even if you move right and left you always move down. The graphics are very nice, but I would try to somehow turn of anti-aliasing, as the nice sprites get blurry on large screens. I think Pixelart should be always crisp. Congratulations to this nice game!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text: The fact that it is not possible to move vertically is a little confusing, but in general the game and music are interesting - thanks and congratulations!

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: Good difficulty progression and really nice music, some sound effects could improve the experience and maybe rendering the chariot in the same angle of movement.

comments.author: Jeremy Burns
comments.text: Fun take on ski free! Would have loved to have seen an equivalent to the ski jumps in there for something to aim towards rather than avoid like all the other objects. Before the speed ramps up it feels a bit easy and empty, but it’s good once it gets going!

comments.author: Jedidiah Weller
comments.text:
Hi there Tim!

Great job on this submission, very good submission and I enjoyed playing! Here’s my feedback and recommendations:

  1. First off, the music was great. I thought it was fun to listen to throughout the journey and didn’t get too old
  2. One UI recommendation - since the controls are with the mouse direction, you force the player to risk their gameplay by turning on/off the sound. I would recommend having the mute button bound to a hotkey like (m) or so
  3. After playing through, I wasn’t quite sure if the levels were getting more difficult from going east, going down, or the distance. It might be nice to let the player know either in documentation or by having more clarity on that goal
  4. The hearts and obstacles were very intuitive! I also enjoyed the arrows and wheels; although it would be great if they had an origin so that I could “pass” the archer or wagon.
  5. It would have been nice to have some sort of checkpoint or clearer milestone progressions.
  • points for a valid readme
  • points for not having eslint or prettier - always want to have those setup for consistency and community contributions!
  • points for naming convention of files, was easy to follow along!

P.S. you spelled Rome wrong in the title, but right in the description :D

Overall, really great submission and I look forward to seeing what’s next! Feel free to hit me up on Twitter/X @jedihacks to have a discussion!

api.name: Khilji's Folly
api.description:
In this colorful gamification of a real historical event set in the 1300s, you take the role of a sole, supernaturally gifted Tibetian Monk in charge of protecting the famous Chumbi Valley against the incoming hordes of horsemen and charioteers of the warlord Bakhtiyar Khalji (a.k.a Khilji).

Shoot the incoming enemies with the multitude of fiery balls of fire (with fashionable particle effects to boot!) and see the score rise. Be careful, though. Some enemies can soak up more damage than most ammo can make!

Highlights

  • Fireballs! With particle simulation.
  • Dynamically generated backdrops.
  • Full Gamepad support.
  • Endless hordes for the competitive shooter.
  • FPS counter.
  • Clever sprit-work.
  • Automatic performance optimization depending on FPS.

Controls

  • You can play either on the keyboard or a gamepad.
  • Left/Right arrow keys or [DPadLeft] [DPadRight] to run along the hill.
  • Up/Down arrow keys or [DPadDown] [DPadUp] to aim.
  • Press F / [Button B] to cycle through projectiles.
  • Press SPACE / [Button A] to shoot.
  • Press F11 to toggle fullscreen.

Credits

  • Developers: Sayem Shafayet & Shuhan Mirza
  • Music: Mysha Azfar

Acknowledgements

  • Sprites by chierit (https://chierit.itch.io)
  • TypeScript js13k starter kit (https://github.com/mtmckenna/js13k-webpack-typescript-starter-party)

Cut Content

We had to skip our original soundtrack made specifically to the game due to space constraint. You can still enjoy it here cut-content

License

GNU General Public License v3.0

comments.author: Grav
comments.text: What a great game. The sprites are indeed smooth and the fireballs look really cool. It feels snappy to control and fits the theme well. Great work there!

comments.author: Shuhan Mirza
comments.text: We are glad that you loved the game! Thanks for your comment!

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well Done:

  • Sprites were really nice
  • In game reminder of all the controls was very helpful
  • The aiming arc was great and let me hit enemies with precision

Feedback:

  • I felt that the largest fireball that did the most damage was also the fastest to cast and thus the only one I used, especially because it could 1 shot all enemies. The smallest fireball that was quick did so little damage and had such a slow fire rate that I never found it useful to use
  • I mostly never moved from where I started so I’m not sure what use moving along the hill served. Maybe if there were some ranged enemies that forced me to move I would have found it useful

comments.author: Shuhan Mirza
comments.text: Thanks for your valuable feedback! It means a lot to us. After submitting the game, I felt the same about the need for an archer horse-rider too!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
I enjoyed playing this game. I think it gets more and more difficult over time, which helps keeping me engaged. I also like the animation of the characters, and the overall look and feel.

I think the controls could be improved if we also had the option to select our fireball with “1”,“2,”, and “3” as well, because for me, it’s easier to change to a desired weapon with one click instead of scrolling through them. If we had this option, I could probably handle the fast 1HP horses more efficiently. I kept using the largest fireball because aiming wasn’t a problem after getting used to the speed of the fireball.

I wasn’t sure what advantages the monk would get by moving back and forth. If positioning the monk accordingly had some advantages, I think it could be more useful.

The sound of killing a horse, and shooting is very similar. The game would feel better if the “hit/kill” sound was different. Some music could also help elevate the game experience. I know it’s difficult to get everything done in less than 13kb and under 1 month.

That being said, I liked this game, and I think it has potential.

comments.author: Shuhan Mirza
comments.text:
Thanks for your thorough feedback! I liked your suggestion about choosing the fireball type using three keys instead of scrolling using one key. Secondly, If we had an enemy with a ranged weapon players would have to move upwards or downwards to avoid the arrows. That would improve the gameplay.

We made some amazing soundtracks for the game. However, we could not incorporate these because of the size constraints.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Great little arcade game! Clear premise, well executed. I really liked the sprites, they looked super detailed for a 13k game. Also great 13th century reference, it really stands out from the crowd.

I didn’t find much of a need to move the monk though. I’m assuming you had other mechanics planned that didn’t pan out that would have needed that?

comments.author: Shuhan Mirza
comments.text:
Thank you so much for your comment, Chris. We wanted to feature warlords that shaped (for better or worse!) South Asia since we hail from that part of the world :3

I felt the same about the monk’s ability to move on the mountains. I guess, if we added horse-riding archers in the enemy army, it would be more fun to play the game!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text: I think the animation of burned enemies will add effectiveness to the game :). Loved the fiery arrows - it was fun, thanks and congratulations!

comments.author: Shuhan Mirza
comments.text: Thanks for your feedback! As there was a size limitation (the main challenge :V ) we had to trade off the animations depicting the death of the enemies!

comments.author: Frahaan Hussain
comments.text: Super simple and fun to play. It reminded me of a game I used to play as a child but I cannot remember the game, nice throwback.

comments.author: Shuhan Mirza
comments.text: We are glad that the game gave you a ride on the nostalgia train! Thanks for your feedback :D

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Nice game. Gets challenging really quick. There’s quite a lot of options to shoot, but I barely had any time to think about that because the horsies were coming in so fast. Overall really good. The sounds could be softened a little. Nitpick: the 13th century is actually the 1200s ;)

comments.author: Shuhan Mirza
comments.text: Thank you so much for your feedback and thorough review. The event that we depicted happened in 1206. However, we mistakenly put 1300s in the document. Thank you so much for pointing it out :D

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: 13-th century is 1201-1300s) Firebals are also very historical) But all the rest are good, nice Job. Interesting Artillery game, several types of enemies and bullets, some sounds. Also I have some issues with targeting speed, it looks like there are too fast targeting.

comments.author: Shuhan Mirza
comments.text: Thank you so much for your feedback! Fireballs made the game quite dramatic, didn’t it? The event that we depicted happened in 1206. However, we mistakenly put 1300s in the document. Thank you so much for pointing it out :D

comments.author: Taufik Sani
comments.text: Instead of inserting the whole text intro at the start of the game, it might be better to introduce the player to the game and its narrative line by line.

api.name: Noble Rivers
api.description:
Strategic game where you are a baron and have peasants that can build various buildings that produce various resources. And you can expand your territory by buying land.

Why unfinished? My mistake was my workflow and lack of design.

  1. first I made good amount of coding but I was doing this in very bottom-up way, without thinking of concrete gameplay/game goal.
  2. then I put project on hold (had other things to do)
  3. day before deadline I wanted to finish this, and designed clear gameplay for a player using sticky notes
  4. then I started implementing lacking pieces
  5. but was too late to create all game features (not to mention playability tests)

I overally spent 34 hours. But I learned a lot. So it was worth it.

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text: This looks like the kind of game I’d spend hours playing. Shame it’s unfinished!

comments.author: Tim West
comments.text:
Congratulations on completing the game!

It looks like a really fun RTS game in the making. I think the AI peasants would make a really fun core gameplay loop to watch them run around the map.

comments.author: Keith Karnage
comments.text: Seems like a solid concept. It’s a little hard to know how it functions without more instructions, but they were moving around and doing stuff. Best of luck finishing next time!

comments.author: John Swana
comments.text: game i feel it still requires a well outlined goal or/ stratergy, Update: I see its actually unfinished :)

comments.author: Jupiter Hadley
comments.text: Watching the people move around and do their tasks is quite fun! It’s a shame you didn’t finish - if you continue the game, adding in what to use each building for adn the requirements (farms by water) would be really nice.

api.name: Merlin vs Alfonso
api.description:
Alfonso X was King of Castile during the 13th century. Also known as Alfonso the wise, he encouraged learning and had a particular interest in a game called “Ajedrez”, which was a medieval precursor to chess.

In this game you play as Merlin, the fictional wizard who’s popularity peaked while Alfonso X reigned. You are invited to Alfonso’s castle to learn about the game of “Ajedrez”.

As you are a wizard, you have the ability to manipulate objects using telekinesis. Anything that is gold or yellow colored can be moved by clicking on the object and dragging in a direction it is free to move in. Additionally, Merlin can be controlled by clicking on land he can reach, and he will walk the fastest path.

Alfonso X commissioned the “Libro de los juegos”, which included detailed instructions on many games, including “Ajedrez”. The rules for the enemies movements are taken from this text (the peon moves slightly differently than described since there is no “side” to the board).

The music was created using the brilliant ZzFXM: https://github.com/keithclark/ZzFXM , which uses the MIT License: https://github.com/keithclark/ZzFXM/blob/master/LICENSE , which is based on KilledByAPixel’s ZzFX: https://github.com/KilledByAPixel/ZzFX , which also uses the MIT license: https://github.com/KilledByAPixel/ZzFX/blob/master/LICENSE

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text: I love this game! The graphics are so crisp and vibrant, done in a refreshing style, and the gameplay is incredibly engaging. My only wish is that there would be a lot more levels.

comments.author: Ben Fox
comments.text: Awesome style, and great execution. Really well “tutorialed” as far as helping people understand how the pieces work. I had some difficulty on Mobile initially as it wasn’t clear which orientation I should use, and in Portrait mode the click-to-move was super weird, and later the game started slowing down. Worked flawlessly on Desktop.

comments.author: Grav
comments.text: Thank you for your generous reply. I thought alot about the order of challenges, apologies for the experience on mobile but Im happy it worked on desktop

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text: Amazing graphics and very unique gameplay.

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text: I really enjoyed while playing. There should have been a mute button to mute the song. When we make a mistake, for the reply the load time is more.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
The music is nice but becomes a bit repetitive. The graphics are amazing! I missed the same style for the instructions, though.

I liked the environmental manipulation but found the later puzzles confusing and unfair - I understand that the pieces don’t move exactly like modern chess, but I found the instructions and implementation didn’t match - I spent some time checking on which cells I was dying trying to understand why, but didn’t fully got it. Perhaps sticking to regular chess moves would be more clear.

comments.author: Grav
comments.text: thansk for the reply and kind words, and Im sorry for your experience. I think it is partially because the instructions are not isometric like in the game, which could cause some confusion. I did want to make it just normal chess, but chess hadn’t been invented in the 13th century 😉

comments.author: Jack Le Hamster
comments.text: Very good entry. I particularly like the graphic style.

comments.author: xem
comments.text: love the graphics, ideas, level design and music. congrats

comments.author: ch!k0
comments.text: i love the concept of the game and how it was puzzling to move around. it was a very good refresher/brain exercise! the graphics and the sounds were amazing too!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
i really like the style and that the gameplay of “monument valley” (i guess thats the inspiration) is altered with the chess-figures.

very good entry, nothing to complain about.

comments.author: Chris Glover
comments.text: Clever game and, if I’m not mistaken, use of SVG. Feels and looks really nice

comments.author: Grav
comments.text: you are not mistaken, SVGs they are! thank you

comments.author: Johan Åhlgren
comments.text: Amazing game! I just wish there were more levels. Great job!

comments.author: Francesco Bedussi
comments.text: This is the best game in the jam so far. Very nice graphics, engaging game, nice soundtrack. Very good job.

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text:
This is fantastic. Super playable, intuitive controls, clear goals. Even deaths were quick, polished, & informative.

Great game even without the 13k limit! Amazing job.

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text:
Love the concept. It is pretty cool and pretty entertaining. You can definitely play the game for quite a while.

The only feedback that I could tell would be if the graphics were more interesting, probably come animations. The wizard feels kind of static.

I think your game would look so much more interesting with some eye candy.

Congratulations! Great game!

comments.author: Allen Schmidt
comments.text: This was fantastic! The increase in difficulty between levels was excellent, and all the mechanics felt very polished (everything felt very polished, except for the between level text haha). Very impressed you got all this into 13kb! Well done!!

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Absolutely amazing this game! Looks fantastic, music is good, interacting with the objects feels really great, nice puzzles. I usually try to provide some feedback on things to improve, however I found hardly something. The only thing which comes to my mind is the information on how the enemies capture you. It would be nice if you could show it also in the same art style of the game. Congratulations to this game. Definitely one of my favourites!

comments.author: Grav
comments.text: that is great feedback, thank you, I do think the same style of enemy capture instructions would really add alot.

comments.author: Marcin Walczak
comments.text:
One of the best games this year.

Love the graphics and chess related puzzles. Gameplay and art style reminds me of “Monument valley”.

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
I was blown away with the innovation of this game, I’ve never seen something like it before. The art looks cohesive, and the animations are smooth. Some subtle animations on the chess pieces and the wizard would help make the game feel even more alive.

I dig the music, it’s catchy. A few sound effects would help elevate the game to the max.

It’s good that you show the red squares and the hint after we die. It helps me reiterate the rules, and not leave me clueless as to why I died. Really good.

The controls on desktop are tight, but it was difficult to move the wizard, on my iPhone 12 mini. I was able to complete the two first levels on mobile, but had to give up on level 3. I therefore had to give 4 stars on controls since the game is in the mobile category, otherwise it’s 5.

This is one of my favorite games of this year’s game jam. I only wish there were more levels, but this is solid for 1 month and under 13Kb, well done.

comments.author: Grav
comments.text: Thank you so much for your kind words and thoughtful feedback, that is great advice. I’m sorry about your experience on iphone mini, I did test on regular iphone, but not on the mini. Was it in landscape mode? This comment is so good to help me grow as a dev, thank again.

comments.author: Taufik Sani
comments.text:
The good: I wasn’t expecting this level of fidelity for a 13kb game - the graphics, audio, gameplay, and how uncomplicated the controls are for the game.

The game loop is very straight forward - solve the puzzle and get to next stage.

What might be helpful is to have an overlay option to remind players of the rules.

I think you could also implement a timer to encourage players to compete with each other via a speed run - it’ll be popular with the players

Feedback:

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
Lovely music, lovely graphics (in SVG, too!)

I very much enjoy the take on the chess (okay, Shatranj or what’s it called) ruleset as a game mechanic, this has a ton of potential!

comments.author: Magnus Lindh
comments.text: Beautiful game. Hard to say anything negative. Everything just works perfectly. Playable even on a touch device!

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Wow the game is great.

Love the dragging gold and chess piece combination, the mechanics work really well.

You’ve got to plan your routes to the green goal carefully.

Graphics are really good, and I like the isometric level view.

comments.author: Jedidiah Weller
comments.text:
Wow, just wow! Great work on the graphics and gameplay on this puzzle game - truly enjoyed playing and thought you did a fantastic job. Here’s my feedback and suggestions:

  1. Design - You blew it out of the park here! I especially wanted to commend on the first scene where you move the drawbridge - that was well done, and set the stage for the rest.
  2. Design - Great work on the full screen as well! I would suggest adding more cinematic timing in between scenes, such as a 1-2 second delay on a little world/shaking type of effects, but that’s just in addition - as is looks and feels fantastic.
  3. Puzzles - The puzzle piece was really well done
  4. Music - I wasn’t personally a fan of the music - I didn’t feel like it spoke to the castle vibe, so might want to rethink the music choice.
  5. Sound effects - it would be nice to add a sound effect when the platforms are moving.

That’s it for gameplay feedback, you really knocked it out of the park! My last feedback is for the repository itself

  1. Good work on adding license and README; however, they should typically be separate files
  2. Would have been nice to have build instructions at the front
  3. Please make sure to use linting and prettier/stylelint for opensource projects with predefined rules. This makes it easier to maintain standards and accept quality contributions
  4. Folder structure could use a little work; for instance not much point in the vectors folder with just one file.

Overall, good submission - keep up the good work and hit me up on twitter @jedihacks for any discussion!

api.name: Simple Sailing Simulator
api.description:
Simple Sailing Simulator: Brave the north wind and search for York, or simply explore.

Controls

  • +/-: zoom
  • Arrow Keys: Pan Camera
  • W: rudder left
  • D: rudder right

Your sailboat travels fastest going perpendicular to the wind.

comments.author: Luke Stapley
comments.text: A bit too simple, sadly. The game is fun, but I would have liked some predictor of the wind in the game and the zoom seems to be broken. I think this is a great small start and hope the next one builds off what you did. :-)

comments.author: Antti Haavikko
comments.text:
Couldn’t really make much progress as my speed kept halting to standstill no matter what direction I tried to go. Would really help if you could see the wind direction. The camera pan seems kinda pointless and seems like can’t zoom unless you have numpad.

I think you should also be able to rotate the sail separately from the boat for better movement control. And visually representing the sail better would help too.

Moving the camera and zooming (if I could even do it) actually take away from the exploration in my opinion. As you wouldn’t have to actually explore to discover new things.

The low framerate with the jumpy camera movement make it not look very smooth. And the “living pixel edges” don’t really help much either. The pure bright colors make it a bit rough to look at too. Would be better to tone them down a bit and make them more harmonious.

So yeah, ironing out these control issues and maybe adding some actual incentive to keep exploring and it could be fun. I loved just sailing around in Wind Waker finding treasure.

Good job! 👍

comments.author: Vivian Easley
comments.text: I really liked this game. It was relaxing and fun to just sail around for a bit. Not sure if the controls are W/D or actually A/D. I think this game would really benefit by simple things like dots that represent port towns or continent and island names. It would give the exploring a sense of purpose in my opinion. Great work.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Very basic gameplay, I think if you sped it all up a bit could be more fun.

add some more things to see as you travel and make controlling easier.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
I couldn’t get the boat to go very fast. I know it said about the angle to the wind, but I wasn’t sure how to determine what the wind direction was other than turning.

For me A and D seemed to steer the boat, rather than W and D.

I wasn’t sure why would would want to zoom the map so much that your boat was no longer visible?

I like the idea of the game, to explore by boat, but found it a little hard to play.

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text:
I have to admit, I did quite enjoy sailing around for a bit! The camera zoom didn’t work for me, but the controls did, and I liked sailing my ‘arrow’ around the blocky green landscape for a while. I tried to pan the camera, and the game crashed, locking the tab in an infinite loop, so I cut it short at that point.

I assume the game ends if you manage to find York? There’s precious little else to look at or do, but I didn’t mind just sailing for a few minutes. It would definitely need something else to hook me for longer.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
I’m guessing you ran out of time on this one? I see unused routines in the code :)

It’s certainly the start of something great! I love that you’re rasterizing your own image in wasm, the sailing is starting to have that lovely momentum dragging & tacking feel, the map coastlines look interesting, and it’s nice and huge! A few generated labels for things here and there, a HUD showing fluctuating windspeed and conditions, and some thin excuses to sail to various points of interest, and you could easily entertain folks for an hour in here.

PSA: the zoom controls only worked for me when playing the game in Firefox. The code uses the event.keyCode property to determine what key was pressed, and 173 (zoom out), 61 (zoom in) are funny ones. In Chrome, you’ll actually get zoom out on your keyboard’s “mute” button if you have one, and I’m not sure if anything goes to 61. Next time, you probably want the event.key or the event.code instead!

api.name: Feast Night
api.description:
In 1278, Brother Doen is asked to leave the temple during a Feast Night on a mission that might cost him more than his life — his very own soul.

Feast Night is a small first person “narrative” game with a couple twists and multiple endings.

Controls

  • Arrow keys / WASD.
  • Mouse (click, nod or shake for interacting with peasants).

Credits

This game wouldn’t be possible without the help of many — ZzFX, lightgl.js, Roadroller, fellow js13k devs, friends & my family <3

comments.author: Sebastian Dorn
comments.text:
Looks amazing, those colors and the fog! I love the title card drop. Answering by nodding or shaking with the mouse is also a nice mechanic.

The fighting is a bit hard. I tried multiple times and most of them was insta-killed by a demon coming from behind … except for the last attempt when I got to 41 kills somehow.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Thank you! :) 41 kills is not bad by any means. But it is true the game is extremely difficult (and kinda unfair). “Demons” spawn all around you (specially if you get too far from them). This is done on purpose so it’s really hard getting the craziest ending, but indeed might be too much :)

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
The dialogue controls via nodding are really clever! I also like the overall atmosphere of the game, it’s spooky already before the first wrong nod, and gets really intense afterwards.

It’s a bit short, but i like the multiple possible endings.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
Thank you! :)

It was only after everything was in place that I realized how short the main loop was and I decided to add the “metagame” component with the “play, die, play again” mechanic which fits surprisingly well. Text occupies lots of space so it was a compromise between having enough conversations / options and everything else (I originally wanted to be able to enter inside buildings and navigate room by room, but ended the way it is).

I will make a small “extended edition” with a bit more of everything, specially at the end - I want to make the “game over screens” walkable spaces (SPOILER ALERT: in heaven or hell - and you won’t just get a message and repeat the whole thing, but be able to have mini conversations there as well with them).

Oh and I’m super happy you liked the multiple endings! I love games that give you options and make you wonder “could I do this?”. I wish I could add more of those.

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
This game is very much my vibe. Weird, dark mysterious. Seems that cool lore is just baked right in. You don’t know exactly what’s going on, but you know enough to know it isn’t good. I love it.

I think the weakness of the game is that it’s just a little hard to know what to do. It isn’t clear how you save someone or when they’ve been saved. I think this plus like one more mechanic for saving people, like where you have to talk to one person to solve a problem for another, or fetch an item. Something simple but that gives you more task variety, and then ya it’s golden.

Still, a huge fan of the games style, lore, and world. Very good game!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
Oh thank you! :)

At some point I wanted to add a counter for “saved souls” (similar to the counter when “purifying demons” during the second half of the game), or perhaps particle effects (when someone is saved you can see… a white cross fading over their heads or something like that). I ran out of time and bytes to do so, so instead I play a subtle sound when someone is “Saved”. This clearly isn’t nowhere near enough to convey this information.

I will add this to the “extended version” as well as the fix for the “stuck reloading” bug. But in any case - thanks a lot for your feedback! :)

comments.author: Misael Braga
comments.text: Doom like game with nice graphics! It is perfect!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Wow, thank you! I still remember the first time I played doom (like a thousand years ago). That game had it all right. I’m glad it gives you similar vibes! :)

comments.author: Chris Glover
comments.text: I love the atmosphere here. Gives you just enough information without spoiling anything. Well done

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
Thanks! :)

The game changed a lot during its development but when I found the type of atmosphere I wanted, I went for it.

I will probably work on a small “extended edition” after the compo is finished - there are a few things that didn’t make it into the game and I think will make it even more atmospheric :)

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
Super cool style. Love the minimal color palette. You also managed to make a super creepy atmosphere with very simple graphics, which is awesome

After losing, I couldn’t figure out how to get back into the game (stuck on the “number of souls purified” screen) and had to reload. Just want to get back to blastin’ faster

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
Oh, the “stuck reloading” bug :(

I only caught this when the game was already online with no more time for fixes - local builds had no issue. I will submit a fix when the voting is over - the game is meant to work as you describe it, running loops as fast as you can.

I’m super glad you liked the game’s style, thank you! :)

comments.author: Alexey Kalmakov
comments.text:
I love the gloomy dark aesthetic! Was surprised with clever yes/no controls, haven’t seen this type of control before in big games! Can I steal this feature?

The transition to the fight was so cool and atmospheric! It was hard for me to fight tho, but probably it’s my skill issue.

After this one, I decided to go 3D next year. Thanks for the expiriance.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
