I originally saw the yes/no mechanic implemented in “Alba: a wildlife adventure” (although in that game it has no real consequences), so feel free to use it in your own games. And I would definitely recommend going 3D - it could be daunting at first, but the immersion one can achieve is amazing.

And thank you for your kind words! :)

comments.author: Marcin Walczak
comments.text: Visually it looks great and atmospheric. It’s a little bit difficult (especially if demon surprise you from behind) and figuring out what to do can be problematic, but that’s a part of the charm. Answering by nodding is a nice mechanic.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
Thanks! :)

Indeed, the “death by a demon spawning behind your neck” is a big no-no, but it gave me (and others) so many stupid jump scares that it had to stay :D It fit well with the game, but also made more compelling getting to the craziest ending possible. I think I managed once to get 108 demons “purified” but it was madness (if I remember right you need to get 70 kills or so to reach the “definitive ending”. Not that it’s the “good” one though ;) ).

comments.author: Sami Heikkinen
comments.text: Great atmosphere and storytelling!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Thank you and thanks for playing! :)

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Several times I was a bit irritated by the movements: Often I got stuck, though no tree was really near, and couldn’t move forwards even when I turned away. On the other hand, walking backwards always worked, even through trees.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Hey! Perhaps this happened on the edges of the level? While confusing, the level is just one long “corridor of trees” with invisible barriers / colliders on the sides. Walking backwards has “noclip” on purpose to avoid all collisions, it’s a small trick you can use in a few special cases ;) (The invisible barriers on the sides block you walking backwards though).

comments.author: Emre Guneyler
comments.text: Best game I’ve played so far. except I can’t finish it.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
Thank you very much for your kind words and for playing! :D

There are four main endings, some of them block the game forever, some others allow you to continue (re)playing it. Let me know if you want to know more about them :)

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text:
What I liked:

  • The graphics! Woaw.
  • The sound effects and the musics.
  • The controls are great. I like how we interact with NPCs.
  • It turns into madness, great idea.

What I liked less:

  • Not all conversations made sense to me (but maybe I am not a pure soul)

Overall, it is a brilliant little game, I loved it! I didn’t spend enough time on it to finish it, but I am sure I wasn’t too far from the end! Great job.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
Don’t worry about the pureness of your soul: some conversations are difficult on purpose, I am the one to blame for the confusion / evilness happening :P

And thank you very much for playing (and your super clear feedback)!

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text: Feast Night is well made and it builds up a nice eerie mood as you walk through the woods. I really like the way you interact through nods or shakes, a nice touch for sure! And the twist, I was not prepared for that!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Thank you very much for your feedback! :)

api.name: Weird Cannon
api.description:
It’s 13th century and a horde of enemies is charging at you. All you have to defend yourself is a weird cannon that you have bought at discount. It’s pretty powerful and can hit several enemies at once, but it’s damage area has a very weird shapes.

You lose once any enemy reaches the left side of the screen. To make a move, click on the shape in the bottom palette then on the battlefield in the top. You also can rearrange the shapes on the palette, so you have free space to recieve a bigger shapes.

Do you think this mechanics worth expanding on and making a full game out of it? Leave comments here or in discussions on git.

comments.author: Justin Sullard
comments.text:
Reminiscent of Tetris and other bin-packing games, but with a fun twist I haven’t personally seen before. The controls themselves were clean and intuitive to use, and while only a single mechanic was present the puzzle of it was enough to remain engaging.

I felt that aside from the title there was little to tie the game into the theme of the jam, and a bit of additional graphics (perhaps cannon balls and troops) the graphics could have brought the theme to life more. Add in a cannon sound or other “juice” and I’d be playing this game on my phone for sure, and an expansion on the mechanics could potentially find me trying to keep a long running game going for hours.

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text: Pretty interesting mechanics. I would love to keep playing this game!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: It is an interesting puzzle-mechanic With more gfx (graphics AND effects) with a soundtrack and some power-ups (bonus-“bomb”, etc) it would be a game worth expanding.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: I get the idea, it’s interesting to have damage not in a radius, but in a pattern. Can work in an JRPG-style combat situation, where you have a grid of enemies and your attacks have different shapes.

comments.author: Thiago Romão Barcala
comments.text: The mechanics are interesting and seem to be original. It would be great if there were some graphics to represent that these are troops marching towards you, and some explosion animations to indicate that we are shooting at them.

comments.author: Johan Åhlgren
comments.text: Nice innovative strategy game! Good job!

comments.author: Christian Staudte
comments.text: The only think that was missing for me are sound sound effects. With the exception of this the game feels quite polished and I had a lot of fun playing it!

comments.author: Tim West
comments.text:
Neat puzzle game! I thought it was very intuitive after some experimentation.

I wish it had some audio, even just a cannon noise when enemies are destroyed. I also think it could have a more 13th century theme, such as stronger-looking enemies for higher numbers.

comments.author: newman s lanier
comments.text: I definitely agree with others that the mechanics are innovative and worth expanding. It’s a fun and playable game, even without the ‘sauce’ and without audio even. Great creative effort here. Keep it up and I’ll check you out on Github.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text: A very engaging game, I think it’s a great design. With some juice, and maybe one or two more control mechanics to let the game go longer, like a charged nuke, or maybe a special piece that eliminated all cells with the same number, or a clumping bomb that clustered cells to reduce gaps; especially something to reward the best placement, e.g. “a bomb that only charges on turns where you eliminate more than 6 power from the board,” I think this could be a hit.

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text:
Cool! Very innovative idea. I really like the mechanic and I think it is worth exploring it a bit more. Maybe you could add something so that the horde changes and does not simply move to the right. However, it should not be random. Maybe an additional rule, that when a lower unit is two space away from a higher unit it moves two spaces. Not sure if this rule works, but I just want to illustrate what I mean: Add something where the player can influence the horde a bit and has more control of shaping it in a way that they can hit better with their next shot. The main reason why I suggest this, is that it sometimes does not feel very good when you have to waste a good shot just to kill a lonely 1.

Still a really nice game (with a hilarious “story” :D) and worth exploring the mechanic more. Congrats!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Tetris 2.0. :D. 59 turns. I liked it - congratulations!

I think it would be good to add the ability to rotate your shapes, for example a horizontal line on a vertical one, etc., and also to sum them by stacking them on top of each other in your store for example - what do you think?

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Hey there!

I’ve never seen a game like this, really comes out of nowhere, nice imagination.

I will admit, even after reading the description, I was still confused on what to do. I feel that there should be some tutorial in-game on how to place your defences, one by one.

Nice one

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: There is no “defences”. Only cannon:)

comments.author: Frahaan Hussain
comments.text: Nice throwback to games like Minesweeper, in game instructions and audio would enhance the game,

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text: This was a really interesting concept. I haven’t seen anything like this before. I think there’s something here which could be turned into a longer puzzle game of some kind. Well done!

api.name: 13th Barber's Guild
api.description:
You are a barber-surgeon from the 13th century, tasked with curing people to join the prestigious 13th Barber’s Guild, the most renowned group of aspiring doctors.

Click anywhere on the screen to dismiss notifications and drag potions to the cauldron when prompted. Keep an eye on the time limit!

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: I like the glow that comes out of the cauldron when you put an ingredient in it. This game really makes you read carefully! I kept wishing there were icons in the dialogue, or perhaps for symptoms to be colour-coded. I suppose that might make the game too easy even if it might look cool. In any case, wonderful job on the game. It ran great and was unique! Nice work.

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
It’s curious that I started by looking for visual indications of the symptoms, but my character design wasn’t matching with that. I changed the character design in the middle of the process and I now it would work with visual clues.

Sadly, there wasn’t enough time to improve it :)

Thanks for your feedback!

comments.author: Andrew Higson-Smith
comments.text: Had some fun - enjoyed the pretty graphic bottles - thank you

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • Potion graphics looked really good
  • I like that the potion requirements to cure a disease and the location of potions was randomized
  • The little spell effect after putting a potion in the pot was a nice touch
  • The dialog helping me out when I got potion combinations wrong was very helpful, especially when I didn’t know which symptoms matched to which disease

Feedback:

  • It was really difficult to remember what combinations were required to cure which disease. I suppose that’s the point of the game but I really struggled with combining 2 ingredients, 3 was just too much for me
  • Audio got a tad bit annoying / repetitive after awhile

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text: Nice game. Graphics and the mobile controls are very cool. I really liked it!

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text: Thank you Lakshman for your feedback!

comments.author: Johan Åhlgren
comments.text: A bit too difficult to remember the potions, I am afraid. Nice graphics, well done!

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Really cool game. The theme is spot-on and the difficulty is very good. I also like the effect when you add an ingredient to the cauldron. I am not a huge fan of the music, maybe a bit too nervous. Some sound effects, e.g. when you add the ingredient or when a patient dies would improve the experience. Anyway, overall a really nice game and I enjoyed playing it.

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
Music is my Achilles’ heel. I left it to the last day and knew it would be a dragging thing.

Thanks for the feedback!

comments.author: Tim West
comments.text: Good game overall! I wish the mechanics were a little more complex than a simple memory game, but overall still very enjoyable.

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
Thanks for the feedback Tim! My original idea was a little more complex, but the deadline was big a issue :)

My original idea was to assign numbers to the potions and describe the diseases as simple math operations. I gave up thinking it wouldn’t fit the theme very well.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I like the game concept even if, to me, it was very difficult to remember the potions combinations. The graphics and effects are cute, everything is well done.

comments.author: Taufik Sani
comments.text:
Music really blends with the concept and game character - i feel like im a mysterious healer back in the 13th century.

It feels like sort of a cooking game where you mix ingredients, but at the same time you have to remember the combination of which you make these remedies. That was an appealing aspect of the gameplay.

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text: Nice! Thank you for the feedback!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Yay I won! This is like Simon Says on steroids. Was a short game but a fun challenge. Sometimes i got just one disease 5 times in a row, so maybe a bit less randomness would be nice. The game could be expanded to slowly include more diseases, medicines and cures. Th graphics are really cute and the dragging mechanics was interesting too. The music is interesting but a little annoying (I almost didn’t notice there was music if I didn’t read the other comments)

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
The interesting thing is that I hit the same problem. The ingredients to cure a disease were random, but I cared to make them not repeat. I should have done the same with the diseases.

You are also right, music was surely my weakness!

Thanks for the kind feedback!

comments.author: Jedidiah Weller
comments.text:
Hi there Michael,

Great job on this game! It was a fun journey and I liked the humor that you introduced throughout :)

Here’s my feedback on what you built so far:

On the code side, I liked that you introduced eslint - it would be great to also see stylelint as well (I often see devs include one not the other, but it’s very helpful especially if you want other devs to contribute and have a standardized styling method) On the game side, really enjoyed the humor - good work! In the very beginning, I liked the concept and the “barber” piece - I wasn’t sure what the logo represented though or how it tied into the gameplay. Was that just an emoji? It’s a clever memory game - I found myself frustrated (in a good way) when my memory started failing me. It could be a cool idea to introduce some sort of ‘look at the recipes mechanic that can only be used X times or has Y consequences. I really liked the graphics - it almost didn’t feel like only 13kb so good work on that :)

Overall, great game and great job! What’s next? Feel free to hit me up on Twitter/X @jedihacks to have a discussion!

comments.author: Michel Sabchuk
comments.text:
For sure! Stylelint is a good idea! I started being a little more pragmatic about code quality but had to flex during the development to match the deadline :D

I’m afraid the logo you mentioned is really just an emoji. I was surprised as well what I could do with just a little SVG and CSS :)

I did it really for my daughters (9 and 11 years old), to involve and inspire them. That’s one of the reasons the game had a pt-BR translation for instance. My next step is probably turn the game into a math-based game: feels like it will be more enjoyable.

Thanks you for your time and feedback!

api.name: Castle Defender VR
api.description:
In the midst of the 13th century, you find yourself in the role of a steadfast archer named ‘Robin’ who is tasked with defending the majestic stone fortress of Castle Ravenshaw. This castle, perched on a hill, overlooks vast fields and is the last bastion standing against an oncoming horde. The kingdom has been under relentless attack from a power-hungry warlord seeking to seize all territories within his grasp. His army - an intimidating multitude of footmen - steadily advances over the open fields below towards Castle Ravenshaw.

When it seems all hope is lost, Robin takes up arms. Known throughout the kingdom for his exceptional skills as an archer, he had been serving as a simple guard at Castle Ravenshaw but now rises to be its last line of defense. With firm hands and sharp eyes, Robin must utilize his unparalleled precision in archery to stop this invading force from breaching their castle walls.

The future of Castle Ravenshaw and its people hinges upon Robin’s bowstring in these desperate times. Will you step into his boots and draw back that arrow? Remember - they’re counting on you.

Controls

  • Just pull back the string in VR and release to shoot.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
I played this game for so long, that my arms got really tired of shooting arrows! The bow’s movement and the shooting feels perfect.

The only thing I would add (besides a few more sound effects) are other types of arrows that could be acquired somehow - freezing, exploding… etc.

UPDATE: after checking the source code I found there are indeed upgrades and different types of arrows! It might be that I never survived long enough to see them (in this case it would be good perhaps to have some minor upgrade quick enough so the player knows there will be more afterwards).

Despite this minor comments, this is probably one of the smoothest archery VR games I played - ever. It’s simple but very well done and makes you want to keep playing. Kudos!

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text:
Very nice! Solid physics, great how you have to ‘aim ahead’. The bow sound was spot on. Good mix of different enemies, but the waves come on very fast very quickly, making it hard to stay standing long.

Simple but effective graphics, quite liked the castle and the bow. Different enemies were easy to distinguish, and after a while you forget how simple they look which means the game has caught you. Bit more sound from the enemies would have been nice but I appreciate that would have been hard to fit in.

comments.author: Teddy Chen
comments.text: A well-polished experience! The bow interaction is solid, and the environment is nicely designed. In terms of feedback, some more sound effects or music would be nice. After a while, pulling the bow starts to feel repetitive.

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text: Nice models and the graphics are great overall. The fact that this is a WebXR experience built without a framework in 13k is impressive in its own right, but this one also looks and plays great. I enjoyed playing this game, though I didn’t get very far - couldn’t quite get the “aim ahead” adjustment going. I felt like my arrows were lacking some oomph :). It would also be cool to have the ability to switch hands! Congrats to the author on a great execution of the core concept that also fits the theme perfectly.

comments.author: Shauna Lynch
comments.text: Amazing. Great game play, excellent arrow trajectory, I thought I was Katniss. Fantastic game and no library….wow!

comments.author: Sébastien Vandenberghe
comments.text:
Amazing tiny little game fitting in the 13k limit !!!

This is a complete piece really enjoyable to play with. You outdid yourself on the graphics part. The minecraft like effect will speak to most people: simple but efficient and beautiful.

The game play is great as well. Nice tower defense scenario fitting perfectly in the theme.

Well done again on the achievement !!!

comments.author: Fernando Serrano Garcia
comments.text: I love it! The enemy and environment aesthetics are very well executed, and the arrow dynamics feel great too. The controls work smoothly and are intuitive, although I would have liked the option to switch the bow hand. I also appreciate the effort of building all this without using any third party framework as three.js, aframe or babylon. Great job! I see a lot of potential for further development.

api.name: Pied Pipe Revolution
api.description:
Pied Pipe Revolution!

Be the Pied Piper and play your pan pipe to lead the rats from town. Use the Arrow keys to play the notes when they reach the arrows, which will increase your area of influence over the rats. Once rats hear the tune, they will follow you. When the song ends hopefully enough rats will follow you out of town that the townsfolk will rejoice!

As always, this ended up being a mad rush at the end, so I didn’t get to really play test this a ton, so let me know if it feels too hard or too easy. I had planned on adding touch support for mobile.

Also make sure you click on the game before pressing any keys… otherwise the audio will play everything at once when you first click on it or press a key. Forgot to look into that AudioContext piece!

Enjoy!

comments.author: Alex Swan
comments.text:
I enjoyed it. I love the characters and the animations. The sfx were pretty good and the half step off on a missed note is clever.

Since it was my first time playing I had to think hard about what position went to which key. What might work better is four keys that are above each other (like 1QAZ or 963. on the numpad).

I thought it was weird the low notes were high pitched and the high notes were low pitched. It would make sense to reverse that.

comments.author: Allen Schmidt
comments.text: Thanks! Ack, I can’t believe I made that mistake with the low/high notes… I guess I still need way more practice reading sheet music haha!

comments.author: Elliot Nelson
comments.text:
Short and simple rhythm game, felt responsive and the tunes are pretty good too!

My only complaint is that arrow keys feel very odd and made playing the chords more difficult than necessary… supporting other keys, like 4567 or GHJK, would feel more natural to the fingers I think.

comments.author: Allen Schmidt
comments.text: Thanks!! For the arrow keys, I was definitely influenced by other rhythm games (namely, Stepmania) that also use the arrow keys. At least here the chords are only two keys together (though that was more of a choice due to it being a pan pipe, so I kept the chords close together haha).

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text: Cute little game. Made me smile. Nice graphics, and pretty good controls. Wish there were more levels!

comments.author: Allen Schmidt
comments.text: It’s definitely pretty short on levels, but I feel like with how limited the mechanic is I’m not sure I could make it any more difficult. Thanks for the review!

comments.author: Luke Stapley
comments.text: I was hoping for a hidden 5th track after beating the game, but the game shows a lot of promise. Hopefully, better melodies in the future if you keep going. The game is a nice basic introduction to the music games of old.

comments.author: Francesco Bedussi
comments.text: Very original and cute. There is a bug: if you press the arrow keys all toghether you always get the right note.

comments.author: Allen Schmidt
comments.text: Whoops! I only made the penalty be for missed notes… I guess there is still the penalty of not being able to hear the melody :D Thanks!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Nice game, simple but cool graphics, nice songs and all well executed, I noticed a little cheat, hit all the buttons all the time and you win!

Missed notes and incorrect notes could have been punished.

Over all a good entry

comments.author: Allen Schmidt
comments.text: Thank you!! There is a slight penalty for missed notes, in that your influence circle shrinks by a bit, and any currently following rats have a chance of stopping following you. It’s pretty easy to miss though, since most likely you’d be more focused on the notes (something I realized late in development). I should have added a little visual indication of a missed/wrong note!

comments.author: ch!k0
comments.text: what a fun and unique game! the graphics and sounds were simple and very clear. although throughout a level, i was too focused on keeping my eyes on the staff because the illustration of the keys were a little small. i like how each level’s speed changes though after i finish the level, when i return to the menu, the current selected level was 1 and not the level i recently played in. all in all it was awesome! great work!

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: The visuals are fun! Is that just me, or is the beat not synchronized with the music? Anyway, we need moar rhythm games on js13k!

comments.author: Allen Schmidt
comments.text: It was mostly synchronized when I was testing it, but I basically just tweaked the timing until it mostly lined up. I really should have tested it on more computers to make sure but I ran out of time haha!

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
LOVED the illustrations, they look super solid, and in theme. The flute sound worked pretty great, but as I was playing I wished that you’d not assigned a note to each track, and just played out a pleasant melody instead, regardless of which key needed to be struck.

Had a minute and a half of genuine fun with the little entry, thanks!

comments.author: Allen Schmidt
comments.text: Glad you liked the graphics!! That was my favorite part to work on, as it took a bit to get the animations working nicely! That’s a good idea, so the note in time with the rest of the song instead of just whenever you press it. That’d make it a bit more like in Ocarina of Time so there’s a nice melody in the back (though I guess that’s a bit more like after you’ve played your melody haha). Thanks for the review! :)

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: I really like the line art used for the player character. it has excellent design and could be a million dollar logo for a brewery or bakery or something. the whole game exudes stylish minimalism and looks awesome and plays nice and smoothly with great tweens and silky smooth scrolling. the only thing I did not love was the actual music itself, the notes chosen were very dissonant to my ear in terms of scale and interval and I wish they were actually lovely and melodic medieval pan pipe tunes! but that would be a huge ask! =) overall, amazing work, nicely done.

comments.author: Allen Schmidt
comments.text: Thanks for the feedback!! I was pretty happy with the arts, though I wish I had made the rats a little more blocky to match the piper. I had intended from the start to make everything with pixel graphics, so I made the pipe noise a little dissonant to match the pixel graphics, but then I went with line drawing using Canvas instead and didn’t update the pipe sound to match haha! Probably doesn’t help that I went with a bit of a minor scale, since it sounded more medieval to me, though something less melancholy would have contrasted with the outcome of the original story of the Pied Piper (I had intended that to be the final level, but time got away from me!)

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
I liked it - congratulations! The difficulty is well matched to the level, so I had fun playing.

It would be convenient to also have control using the WASD.

Maybe a few houses that would make a map of the city with small paths between them, where the rats could converge - would add interest (their arbitrary arrangement by levels, or a more complex arrangement is also possible…).

If the notes will be made into beautiful and interesting melodies of that time - it will be perfect! (perhaps this is the case even now, in which case it is necessary to choose more accurate melodies that would immediately feel good for non-musicians :)).

Thanks for game!

comments.author: Allen Schmidt
comments.text:
I kind of made the melodies intentionally not beautiful, since that felt a bit more medieval in my mind. I thought it would be more fun to not really look up anything melody wise, and in the end it was just a mad rush to even get the few I had done haha!

Houses were definitely part of the original design, but fell off once I started losing time. Would have made it much cooler though… maybe this would be worthwhile reimplementing in another language and expanding a little bit. I’m not sure if it was a bug in KontraJS (the library I used), but I had the code in there so WASD would work as an alternative. I didn’t test it out extensively (just noticed that it didn’t work and never tried to fix it) so I may have had the code wrong as well.

Thanks for the review!!

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Cool game! I really liked the simplistic graphics and the pretty innovative idea to interprete the theme! Not sure if the arrow keys are the right choice for the notes, as the layout does not really match. Maybe using a vertical key combination, e.g. E, D, C, Space would feel more natural. I think a bit more feedback to when you do an error would have helped, because you can not really watch the rats when you are playing. Maybe having audio feedback (playing wrong notes) would be even better. Anyway congratulations to a very nice game!

comments.author: Allen Schmidt
comments.text: I did notice that early on in development, like it’s really hard to look at what’s happening above to notice the influence decreasing or increasing depending on how you play. There is audio feedback when you miss a note, but nothing for when you play the wrong note (should have made it so any wrong note played would be offkey and give you negative influence). And I was definitely influenced by all the old school rhythm games for the arrow key input (Stepmania, Friday Night Funkin’, though is that even old school?) Thanks for the review!! :)

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
I’m a sucker for rhythm games, and I really enjoyed this one too. I love the animations, the gameplay and the style. I just wish there were more songs to play. I personally think level 4 was too easy as well, and I’d love to see even more difficult levels.

Great entry, and I wish it had more levels.

comments.author: Allen Schmidt
comments.text: Thanks!! I had planned on changing up the song speed to make some songs harder, but I ran out of time. The song implementation is a little wonky anyways, so I think I would have introduced too many bugs late in development. I had thought about adding like three finger combos, but I didn’t think that would be as realistic with a pan pipe (though having never played one, I’m not sure haha).

comments.author: Taufik Sani
comments.text:
When DDR meets rats in the 13th century!

I like the theme, and how you incorporated a rhythm game as part of historical event!

Graphics were cute (rats following the piper), and the captions after you finish a level is just hilarious (have you switched your sound on/off?)

Controls were just like DDR (the combination of directional keys are great to trip up players).

The songs can be longer (understand it was finished in a rush). Rhythm games are always popular with players on whichever platform (mobile, pc.). More levels will be welcomed!

comments.author: Gauthier Billot
comments.text: Nice but I wish there were more levels

comments.author: Marcin Walczak
comments.text:
Despite very simple design, characters are very well animated (that bouncy movement animation is just adorable). Very responsive. Playing off-note on miss was a good idea.

Even though I suck at rhythm games I had a lot of fun with this game which says something.

comments.author: stxtus
comments.text: I love this game good concept, easy to play too

api.name: Cathar
api.description:
1233, Toulouse.

In the current inquisition the catholics try their best to kill all Cathars. In their eyes the Cathars belief in two gods is heresy and must be punished.

You are a Cathar!

Tragically killed by a catholic inquisitor.

Without salvation, you are trapped in an endless cycle of reincarnation in the material realm of the evil god. With the guidance of your gods you embark on a quest to stop the inquisition. Will you be able to stop their Crusade and break the cycle?

Controls

  • WASD: Move
  • Space: Dash
  • Left Click: Attack
  • E: Interact and Take
  • M: Mute Background Music

comments.author: Oliver Smith
comments.text: I like the way the upgrades are chosen for the next round but it would be good to feel more of a sense of progression. The graphics and sounds are minimalistic but that really works as part of the style. The music is good and fits well too. It is a good concept well put together and fun to play.

comments.author: Luke Stapley
comments.text: If you’ve played Legend of Zelda Randomizer, you might never get a sword and fight enemies with a hammer. This is arguably much harder and time-consuming. Which is why I hate those versions. I feel the same as the sword is too small, and swift attacks don’t do damage. I would recommend you add permanent buffs as the game progresses as this is a small game, gives your players a sense of winning, and can then try to beat how many times it took to regenerate to finish. It would add more fun. Also hard to understand what each powerup is exactly.

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Unfortunately, I could not move: I have “search as you type” enabled in my browser, and you don’t call .preventDefault() on key events, so when I try to move, the page search opens for me.

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Very cool game! I really liked the pixel art, the mechanics and the music. It is a bit difficult (or I am just bad) and lacks some explanations to what the different power-ups do when you enter a door. The icons alone are not self explanatory.

comments.author: Nick Watton
comments.text: This looks lovely, and the sound is amazing. I enjoyed the set-up, and the story appears good. Unfortunately the attack control wasn’t working for me so I was unable to battle.

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: An enjoyable roguelike style romp that reminded me of atari adventure in its simplicity. I like choosing upgrades after each battle, but wish they were mega powered. Overall, a very nice game, especially impressive given the 13kb size limit. Great work.

comments.author: xem
comments.text: Nice, but please avoid long unskippable text intros

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
Love the graphics and the game play. Although first time I saw it I didn’t see the sword on the floor so went in to battle and wasn’t able to even hurt any enemies.

Nice music for a fighting game.

Combat was quite tricky having to use both the mouse and keyboard at the same time.

comments.author: Keith Karnage
comments.text: Awesome job! This game was fun, pretty, had great music, and was educational! I wasn’t sure what all the power-ups did. But the easy approach would have been to go with an archer like a typical shooter, but the sword was a great choice. Lots of fun, and a different feel from the shooter I was expecting.

comments.author: Magnus Lindh
comments.text:
Very cute despite the somewhat somber background story. The gameplay is good but I didn’t like the controls. What is the point of mouse control? It is very hard playing on a touch pad, so please consider using only keyboard controls. Also restarting from the beginning is a bit tedious and makes me want to quit playing.

Anyway, great game!

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text:
Good zelda like (Isaak like) roguelite, gameplay is really interesting, there are some strategies and different behavior for different enemies.

Great Job!

comments.author: Jupiter Hadley
comments.text: Cute graphics and sound effects! This feels like a well finished game.

api.name: Rice or die
api.description:
Mongols are invading and you must defend Japan from them! A tower defense type of game.

Did you know rice was sometimes used as a currency in medieval Japan.

Controls

  • Control with mouse.
  • Place towers by picking them from the right side. Towers can be upgraded by clicking on them.

comments.author: Ben Fox
comments.text:
Very entertaining! The controls were hit-or-miss, like sometimes 1 click selected/placed something, sometimes I had to double/triple click before anything happened.

I like the different tower options, both for defense & resources. It seemed a little too easy that the rice farmers produced even when a wave wasn’t happening, since I could just wait a bit and build all the defense I wanted. Also a minimum-build distance would have increased the challenge some more, since eventually I realized I could just make a wall of towers to win.

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Simple TD game Not bad, but the main problem is low interactability - it’s really hard to select towers types and place it (somehow they aren’t select well enough), units are moving with a steps and you can’t see tower attacks. Also you can’t see towers range. With reworked view game can be really interesting.

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: Controls were very clunky to me. Tower placing worked once in n tries. And then I got stuck in some submenu with “pagoda”.

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text: A charming aesthetic that reminds me of the early days of the Internet. Unfortunately I found the interface a bit clunky and had a hard time selecting items or performing tasks. But fits the theme well and runs without error. Nice job!

comments.author: Allen Schmidt
comments.text: Definitely a little clunky, and hard to tell before the first playthrough exactly where the enemies are going to come from. On second playthrough, I just build a few rice producers and put tons of creepers everywhere, which led me to victory! Well done overall!

comments.author: Christer Kaitila
comments.text: Nice work making a TD game in only 13kb! I like all the upgrades and that you can grow rice. I found it hard to control, but overall a great job.

comments.author: Jupiter Hadley
comments.text: A lot of my inputs don’t seem to be working, but it sounds like a solid concept - just needs some tweaking when it comes to the controls.

comments.author: Krzysztof Bociurko
comments.text: Didn’t work on mobile touch, could not place towers

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text: It is a bit hard to understand what is going on, as there is very little visual feedback. I also found it difficult to select and place towers. I had to double click, but sometimes nothing happened.

title: The Horde! The Horde! The Horde!
author: Anthony Cosgrave

api.name: The Horde! The Horde! The Horde!
api.description:
The Horde have destroyed nearby cities and ports. Our city may be next! Help your fellow citizens flee to safety and get our mighty soldiers across the river to battle The Horde!

Alas, a cruel and cunning enemy spy destroyed the existing bridges. Fear not! Some highly confident but, not so skilled, citizens have built fancy extendable makeshift ones!

Don’t leave your fellow citizens waiting too long to cross the river as they may panic and drown attempting to cross the river themselves!

Controls

Left mouse button down to extend the bridges.

Move bridges left or right with your mouse to avoid any burning ships travelling down the river.

comments.author: Vivian Easley
comments.text: Really interesting game. I like the concept and the graphics. The hit box on the ships seems a bit too large. Also, villagers would sometimes not cross the bridge or just run into the water. I’m not sure if that was intentional. Anyways, great work.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Interesting idea, nice graphics and all works well.

Improvements / ideas:

  1. More varied game play
  2. Levels - hold off horde for 10 seconds, 20 seconds etc
  3. Sounds
  4. Ways to hold back the ships

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Unfortunately, I could not play: I have “search as you type” enabled in my browser, and you don’t call .preventDefault() on key events, so when I try to start the game, the page search opens for me instead.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Interesting idea! Sprite work was great, and I like some of the thoughfull touches like civilians running in disarray while soldiers cross in lockstep.

Unfortunately I couldn’t quite work out how to be good at this game. I tried timing it so I pulled the bridges together first so I could start extending them before the ship has passed the dock point, but the collision for the ship was so broad that the bridges started burning long before they touched the ship. In the end, I couldn’t work out if I could do anything better than just extend the bridges, let the ship burn them to give everyone max time to cross, rinse repeat.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • The sprites were great
  • I like the concept of having to get two separate groups across that each serve different purposes. I also liked that each has their own unique time constraint
  • The flaming ships help add a nice conceptual challenge so you can’t just have the bridge extended the whole time and have to strategize when to pull them back

Feedback:

  • I’m not sure what the purpose of being able to move the bridges left/right serves. They can only be used in a certain way (red dock with red bridge) so moving them didn’t seem like it would help. It was especially hurtful if one gets hit or you move to the edge of the screen as the other can move freely and they become out of step with each other and then you can’t line them both up with their respective docks anymore
  • I couldn’t ever get further than like the 3rd flaming ship. No matter what I did I couldn’t find a way to get troops across faster

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: Cute graphics and nice use of monochrome emoji for the people. I don’t undestand what’s going on in the game or what the goal is. The font in my system is also very different that in the screenshot, mine being way way bigger and doesn’t fit in the boxes.

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text:
Nice idea and I like that you came up with an innovative gameloop. However, as it is with new games ideas it takes a bit of tweeking to make it really fun. Here is some feedback to improve the game:

  • I did not really get, why you can move the bridges left and right…
  • The ships are a cool idea to bring in some action and variation. However, you have not much more choice than simply removing the bridges when a ship is coming. Maybe some more decision making would improve the experience.
  • I would definitely add some sounds / music as this adds a lot to a game.
  • If you have a game which is controlled mainly with the mouse I would also make the whole menu controlled by the mouse and not by the keyboard.
  • Somehow I was able to missalign the two bridges so that they do not fit anymore to both places at the same type.

Anyway, still a very nice idea and congratulations to your game!

comments.author: Frahaan Hussain
comments.text: Needs more variety and levels but the art was excellent.

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text: Wow, this was hard. It didn’t feel like my army was able to make much of a dent into the attacking horde, and I quickly lost. It’s an interesting concept though.

api.name: Predecessors
api.description:
Try to find your way out without being caught by your predecessors from the 13th century.

Can you do it, and how many times? They will know you were there. (You can try again.)

Use arrow keys or WASD to move.

Crosses might help you on your way out.

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: I am not sure if blocking diagonal movement was done to increase the feeling of dread, but worked towards the atmosphere. I found it too easy though, without any incentives to keep escaping the same map layout - a randomized room might be better.

comments.author: Sami Heikkinen
comments.text: Thank you for the comment. Did you multiple time as it does be more challenging when you play it multiple times?

comments.author: Christian Staudte
comments.text:
Really cool and unsettling background music that matches the game imo. The idea is a cool take on the theme.

However, it felt a bit short. More levels would have been nice. I think diagonal movement could also improve the gameplay.

comments.author: Sami Heikkinen
comments.text: Thank you for the comment. Yes, time was limited and it would be nicer to have multiple levels. Did you try multiple time as it does be more challenging?

comments.author: Luke Stapley
comments.text: Was afraid of jumpscare. But the music is great. The issue is the number of levels is very low…. just 1. So I’d like to see you move forward in the next game with more variety.

comments.author: Sami Heikkinen
comments.text: Thank you for the comments. Yes, our time was limited and it would be nicer to have multiple levels. Did you try it multiple times as it does get more challenging?

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Nice little game. I really liked the music. I think the floor should have some more texture or more objects should be placed in the room, because sometimes it looked like I was not moving, e.g. when moving along a long wall. I also had sometimes some stutters, not sure if it is my PC.

comments.author: Sami Heikkinen
comments.text: Thank you for the comments. Our time was limited to fix all things and make more graphics unfortunately.

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text:
The aim is simply to get through the maze, avoiding the ghost-like ‘predecessors’, and find the exit. On the way, you sometimes find crosses, which give you a wider range of light and slow down the ghosts. You could argue this isn’t really on-theme, although it is fun for a few rounds.

It’s graphically quite cute, and I really liked the slightly spooky music. The only downside is that the level didn’t seem to change, so once you know where the exit is, you just keep going there level after level. The quantity of ghosts does increase each time, however, so it becomes more challenging in that respect (as one touch and it’s game over) but a variety in levels would have been great.

comments.author: Sami Heikkinen
comments.text: Thank you for the comments. We will try to have more levels and variety in the next time. It was meant to be more in theme but we had trouble finishing our ideas from Finnish history and tell the full story.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Interesting idea. Congratulations!

It seems to me that somewhere from level 5+, when there are more ghosts, the game starts to slow down.

I think the random location of the doors will add interest to the game.

I reached the 7th circle :).

comments.author: Sami Heikkinen
comments.text: Thank you for the comments and playing the game bit longer :) Yes, randomising things would been better or adding more levels, but we did not have time this time.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text: That was spooky! A slightly odd choice on the lead melody, but the vibe in the audio was really effective. The actual game of tag was also very playable and intuitive, nicely done packing all of that and nice visuals into 13k!

comments.author: Sami Heikkinen
comments.text: Thank you for the positive feedback :)

comments.author: Almut Kieffer-Jones
comments.text:
I love the graphics! And the music is also well done, I like how the volume decreases between games.

For quite some time, I didn’t understand that I could walk on the grass, but I guess that’s on purpose to make it more interesting?

comments.author: Sami Heikkinen
comments.text: Thank you for the comment. Yes, the grass (= forest) is where the ghosts shows up but it is not that clear :)

comments.author: Tom Harris
comments.text: This was an enjoyable survival game that managed to create the tension and mood you would expect through the effective use of sounds and visuals. It’s good that the difficulty increases as you progress, but would be nice to see some level variety too.

comments.author: Sami Heikkinen
comments.text: Thank you for the comment. Yes, we should have had time different levels but this way it is bit like old school games that just go harder until you die, right? :)

comments.author: Brian Hambley
comments.text: Reminded me of south park for some reason!

comments.author: Sami Heikkinen
comments.text: You are right, the characters. It was not intentional first but I do noticed it and it was fine for me :)

api.name: Divine Wind
api.description:
The Mongols are invading Japan and only you, the Kamikaze, or Divine Wind, can stop them.

Use the wind channels to blow the Mongols away. You need to unscramble each concentric layer so that there is a path from bottom to top. Once the maze is unscrambled, punch through the maze with your right hand to blow the ships away!

Use your right controller to click and drag the concentric layers.

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text:
This took me a while to figure out (should have read the explanation more carefully).

Felt a bit trial and error. Because of the oncoming hoards you don’t (feel like you) have the time to really look. Missing sound.

The game looks simple, but no doubt the implementation is complexer than it looks.

Lots of mongol ships, good on theme.

comments.author: Nathan Long
comments.text: Couldn’t get past the first screen… so it’s hard to rate it objectively.

comments.author: Shauna Lynch
comments.text: Excellent puzzle game. Right amount of challenge with the time constraints / impending Mongols. Great effort.

comments.author: Sébastien Vandenberghe
comments.text:
Congrats on shipping your game for JS13K !!!

I love the overall idea as I am a fan of puzzle games but I have to admit the overall gameplay/controls are a bit too hard for me to figure.

The theme has been respected overall but I guess some more Audio could have helped with overall immersion. (I know it is easy to say and 13k is definitely tiny :-) )

api.name: Jurassic Nursery
api.description:
Pet and feed adorable triceratops.

If you feed them well they might reproduce. Would you be able to breed a golden one ?

Controls

  • drag and drop fruits and triceratops
  • two fingers to move the camera on mobile
  • left/center button to move the camera on desktop

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: the 3d is cool but would rather have it some other way as fun did not last much

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
I have no idea what is the goal and the relationship with the theme :)

However I love the tech behind this interactive toy: the models, the rendering (shadows, picking) … it’s super cool! I just wish there was something to do other than finding which food likes each animal and making them breed.

comments.author: Luke Stapley
comments.text: Great look at building something in 3D, but sadly not up to the theme that was given. Try harder to work on the theme. Will help you to gain a lot more quality criticism and more interest from the judges and the community.

comments.author: Richard Davey
comments.text:
It plays kind of like a dinosaur Tamagotchi. You view a 3D playfield where a variety of dinosaurs roam around and can drag various types of fruit in for them to eat. Certain colors only like certain fruits, but the happier they get, the more chance there is of them breeding and you getting a new stripy dinosaur.

Technically, very impressive, but it feels wasted in this challenge as it has utterly nothing to do with the theme, sadly.

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
the deisgn, animations and idea are really cool. I read your write up which helped me understand that I had to feed them three times and then breed.

the baby dinos are very cute.

This is clever implementation but not sure dinos were knocking about in the 13th century?!

comments.author: Frahaan Hussain
comments.text:
So you decided to do 3D, very impressive. Nice concept.

A menu system would be nice to have.

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: The technology is cool as all hell, and I enjoyed the postmortem very much. This game is why there’s the “Innovation” category in the voting app :)

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Technically this 3D game is very impressive! However, I do not get the connection to the theme, as I am pretty sure dinosaurs were already extinct in the 13th century :). From a gameplay point of view it gets a bit monotonous after a while. It might be still a good gameplay experience when you are looking for a very chill game. Congratulations to this beatufil looking game!

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Well, don’t looks like 13th century

api.name: The Fall of "The Last Tower"
api.description:
In the 13th century, a kingdom stood as a beacon of prosperity and peace. At its heart a structure said to be blessed by the gods themselves. Many tried to destroy it but in every attempt they failed. Driven by dark ambition, a neighboring realm, launched waves of archers and swordsmen to conquer it. The tower’s guardian, had lost much of his army in previous wars. He knew that facing the enemy directly would be suicide. Thus, using the tower as a strategic point, he rallied the remnants of his troops. Each wave of assault tested not just their strength but their unity, resilience, and cunning. Would the Last Tower stand or will it fall?

Buy units and protect the tower.

  • Swordsman = $100
  • Archers = $60

For a better game experience use a mouse :)!

Controls

  • Slow motion: Z
  • Fast forward: X
  • Buy swordsman: S
  • Buy archer: A
  • Select units: Click (+ drag)
  • Move units: Right click
  • Attack certain unit: Left click
  • Zoom in/out: Mouse wheel / scroll
  • Move camera: cursor over borders
  • Pause: P
  • Full screen: B

Thanks to

  • Catalina Sterenstein
  • Facundo Duarte
  • Ignacio Gurmendez
  • Juan Diego Duarte
  • JV. Gurmendez
  • Pedro Gurmendez

And special thanks to Mark Vasilkov for sharing his pixel art strategy

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
What? Zoomable realtime strategy with timespeed control in 13k? Very nice! I like the graphics, the animations and the handling of troops.

Maybe it could be even better with some more troop types (knigths on horses for flank-attacks, peasants swinging flails to bind units for the archers, etc.)

Unfortunately i am really bad at strategy, could not defend many waves. But it makes fun, so i tried it a couple of times! Good entry!

comments.author: Luis Gurmendez
comments.text:
Thank you very much!! Yeah one of the things I regret most was to not add more type of troops I wanted to add knights, but I didn’t had much space, and for me the art was what took more bytes. The “ai” logic was pretty much the same for archers/swordsman just changing initial values like attack range, I feel like knights would have similar logic but well, that is the idea of this competition!

Thanks for the feedback I’m glad you enjoyed it!

comments.author: Luke Stapley
comments.text:
The idea is there. But I feel you should have added a button for speeds and purchases. If not, at least share the instructions on the bottom of the page.

I think that the idea is great, but man, the waves get pretty intense at the end. Not sure if by choice or by how the code works out.

comments.author: Luis Gurmendez
comments.text:
I totally agree, I should have remove some functionality to gain some bytes and add those buttons. Regarding the waves, I could reach wave 23 after lots of attempts :D it gets pretty insane, specially when your army is all over the map, and the enemies reach your “spawn line” after that there isn’t much you can do :D .

Thanks for the feedback!!

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I loved to play this game! I like the mouse controls which are intuitive to scroll and zoom. At first I was lost because the screen was not centered in the tower, but this game is really well done!

comments.author: Luis Gurmendez
comments.text:
Thank you very much :)) and thanks for the feedback!!

I feel lost too when playgin the game now, I had a similar experience in prev entry, I play it too many times that lost perspective on new people playing it. I’m so angry because it was a really easy fix, camera.position = tower.position. Thanks :)

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • Great looking graphics.
  • Zoom control and camera movement in js13k is impressive
  • Cool to see a controllable army rather than an auto-battler

Feedback:

  • I was pretty confused at first when the game stared and I tried to purchase units and nothing happened. Would have been helpful to start the game viewing the castle and your own army
  • I really, really disliked how the archers would fire then run back as the enemy approached. I wanted them to hold position and shoot while my swordsman were on the front lines but they kept running further and further back (and thus their attacks took longer and longer to hit the enemy). It also made it really hard to attack enemy archers as they would just constantly retreat into the next wave
  • The camera movement was pretty sensitive. By that I mean that it started moving on the edges of the screen a fair distance away from the edge, which made it hard to select groups of units. I can understand that it was probably implemented that way to compensate for non-full screen, but probably could have moved the area it started triggering closer to the edges of the canvas
  • Difficulty ramped up pretty quickly

comments.author: Luis Gurmendez
comments.text:
Thanks for the feedback! I agree on all your points, but the one on camera movement, probably depends on the screen size, I spend a lot of time adjusting that movement threshold to make it work exactly as age of empires I’m sorry you find it hard to work with, will totally try to fix it if I iterate over the game :).

Regarding the archers being so frighten… I feel you, it’s annoying to me too it was too late to fix that when I notice it

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Unfortunately, the controls didn’t correctly work for me: I have “search as you type” enabled in my browser, and you don’t call .preventDefault() on key events, so when I try to buy new units etc., the page search opens for me.

comments.author: Luis Gurmendez
comments.text: :panic: I’m so sorry that happend to you, yeah I should have preventDefault everywhere my bad

comments.author: Javi Agenjo
comments.text: Nice game. The lack of clickable UI to do the most basic actions made it slow. But I liked the presentation and gameplay.

comments.author: Luis Gurmendez
comments.text: Totally agree, I didn’t had any space left unfortunately, but that was the idea. Also I was hoping to add range for archers + strength + armor but didn’t had space for that either :(. For next year I would first build functionality then art :joy:

comments.author: Armaan Mohammed
comments.text: I liked the game, the little sprites were cute and it fits the theme! But the camera movement confused me for a while 🤔and i think it would be more intuitive for the camera to already be pointing on the castle!🙂 Clickable buttons to spawn stuff would be better, simpler and faster! Buttons and Camera Movement aside nice and simple game with cute graphics! 😁

comments.author: Luis Gurmendez
comments.text: Totally! I was struggling the last week to fix the button to spawn functionality, I had to hack the keyboard thing because I wasn’t able to cut down some more bytes :(. I wish I had a spare few to fix that, I think I had like 40 left but in so much hurry I wasn’t able to add it. Also I wanted to increase range + armor + strength but couldn’t reach that either :(. Thanks for the feedback!!

comments.author: Alexey Kalmakov
comments.text:
Going full archers was not the right strategy =) Wave 13 got me.

Zoom and camera control are very impressive. And I like small details a lot. Good entry!

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text:
This is glorious! I was struggling with the micro (on a touchpad, too), but at the same time I feel like with a good micro I could kite the enemy fairly efficiently, so that’s a good skill check right there.

Loved how the map ends up covered in rivers of blood, and the grass and forest are looking great.

Thanks for choosing convenient keys for units, that’s really important in an RTS!

Seeing the assault units charge into battle was cool, seeing the ranged units peace out and scatter was fun and super unexpected :)

One thing I’d change would be, remove the delay when buying, and spawn at the mouse. Like when playing Protoss in SCII.

comments.author: Luis Gurmendez
comments.text:
Thanks for the feedback!! And thanks for sharing your art style :)). That would have been a great idea! Spawn units at mouse position omg why didn’t i thought about that! Damn!! I totally agree the spawning cooldown as useless, I should have remove it I was doubting but I think you are right :)).

Thanks!

comments.author: Misael Braga
comments.text:
Great RTS Game! I have loved to play it!

The zoom option with mouse scroll was a fine choice.

I missed the sound effects.

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text:
Very nice and polished game! Pretty cool what you were able to put in 13 KB. The only thing I was missing were sound effects. Some feedback to further improve the game:

  • Show on the screen the price and keys for buying units, on a desktop PC there should be enough space for that.
  • Make the color difference of enemies and the player units clearer, e.g. by having either a completely different sprite or at least not only differ the color of the helmet tip.
  • As a old C&C fan, I it would be great to have some quality of life improvements most RTS fans know: – Double click on a unit selects all units of the same type – Setting the spawn points of the units
  • Do not forget that there are people with a QWERTZ keyboard in this world :). If you map an action to Z please also map it to Y. These are just some minor things, because the game overall is really good! Congrats. CAST

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
When a lot of units are created, the game hangs, somehow everything froze for me. I prefer archers who shoot in place without moving away. It would also be great if, when selecting troops, I could see how many units of troops I have. As for me, it seemed to me that by the time they reach that tower, the war will be over :D.

Nice game - congratulations!

api.name: XIII Feet Underground
api.description:
In XIII century citizens of small town named Kłodzko (formerly known as Glatz or Cladzco) started expanding their basements. After some time, they managed to create complex network of rooms, halls and tunnels. In this underground city, they had shops, wells, taverns, counting rooms, doctor’s office and… prison.

Criminals captured and sent to the prison were then trailed in court, and often treated with appropriate kindness by the executioners.

You are one of those lucky prisoners! Can you escape from the underground maze? How many ways of escaping can you find? And are you ready to die, like a lot?

XIII Feet Underground is RPG-like game, with roguelike mechanics. Explore Kłodzko’s underground city, interact with it’s citizens, reveal dark secrets of people. There are multiple ways of reaching the end. But watch out! You should not trust everybody!

Controls

Keyboard (WASD for walking)

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
G’day!

Quite an interesting topic (it was interesting to read about your topic on Google :D) and the game plot is no less interesting! I also liked the level design!

The font does not seem very clear, but the voice accompaniment solves this issue. It was quite interesting to play, I was pleased with the versatility of the result!

It was nice to see a friend from prison in the brewery :D, but unfortunately I couldn’t find a way to infect the outside water so that people could buy water from the well merchant in the dungeon. I also did not understand the purpose of the elixir, but I think there should have been a lot of interesting things :)

Beautiful graphics, simple and intuitive.

It seemed to me that the sound continues to play even after the end of the dialogue, it would be good to fix it. It feels like sometimes I can’t start the game because the movement buttons are unresponsive…

Sounds like good potential for story expansion to me… I enjoyed the game!

Thanks for game!

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • The sprites were impressive. I could tell what all the different objects were, even what the items on shelves were
  • Was not expecting text-to-speech, but it worked decently well for the type of game
  • Lots of small variety in the game and multiple solutions that made different playthroughs interesting
  • Overall a fun little game

Feedback:

  • Some of the dialog options that should have ended the playthrough (like dying) did not close the dialog and have you die, but continued to allow you to interact with the person

comments.author: Grav
comments.text: Can’t believe you fit all this in, text and raster art can be so heavy. Funny little story with multiple endings, easy to play, lots of fun. Good stuff.

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: Unfortunately, I could not properly move: I have “search as you type” enabled in my browser, and you don’t call .preventDefault() on key events, so when I try to move, the page search opens for me.

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text: Pleasant oldschool visuals, simple controls, and fits the theme well. The text-to-speech was very impressive! Nice job.

comments.author: xem
comments.text: nice, you should try to not only support WASD but also arrow keys / ZQSD (for foreign keyboard models)

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A really fun and well put together game.

The pixel graphics were good, the rooms and exploring linked together well.

Went into the Innkeeper’s stash and found all the items I needed after picking them up from other places, which made me laugh.

The speech was neat. Only thing I would change is making the WASD keys work several at a time, I seemed to only be able to go in one direction, not diagonally.

Nicely done.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
Unfortunately all I see if a black window and an error in the console :(

Uncaught TypeError: kt is undefined

comments.author: Clément Pasteau
comments.text:
Hey there!

Fun little adventure game here. With a little bit of voice acting as well!

Some humour, nice characters, tbh it’s really well done.

Nice one!

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Oh, I enjoyed this one. Really effective graphics, a good sense of humor, and the surprise text to speech use!

It was like a tiny little Lucas Arts adventure, boiled down into 13k. Great job!

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Interesting game, the whole quest with several characters, stories and items. For me the controls are too fast (i’m flying threw the tunnels, it’s really hard to catch some door), but in general it’s playable. Some endings are unexpected, you can’t predict this result with your action. Maybe it’s ok for such small game. Good job!

comments.author: Benjamin Boyd Austin
comments.text:
This was really fun!

Really funny dialogue and consequences for your actions. The voice only made it funnier.

I was able to escape!

Nice tiny RPG!

comments.author: Jedidiah Weller
comments.text:
Hi there Rafał,

Really interesting game! I liked many aspects of this, and thought you had a great submission. Here’s my feedback:

  1. Found a bug when talking to the banker where if you pickup 2 beers, sell one of them, then the next one you try to sell will ask you to input “10” but you can’t do that on a 1-9 keyboard without accidentally selecting ‘1’ first.
  2. Liked how you added audio too it
  3. From a UI perspective, the WASD controls were self explanatory although I found some of the control options on dialogs to be confusing (switching from letters to characters). I’d suggest keeping it consistent with the WASD being for movement, perhaps e for interaction, but then options always being numeric.
  4. I tried the game a couple of times and it looks like the outcomes are all predictable - it would be great to introduce an element of randomness to small pieces such as the pickpocketing.
  5. I would suggest using typescript and including eslint and stylelint for better code standardization for community contributions
  6. Why include ts-ignore? It shouldn’t be necessary to use ts-ignore - perhaps this is a solution? https://chat.openai.com/share/cdf43203-2caa-461d-a402-c77cd48c40ed

Overall, really great submission and I look forward to seeing what’s next! Feel free to hit me up on Twitter/X @jedihacks to have a discussion!

api.name: 13 Drums
api.description:
A cozy little game set in the 13th century of North America. Visit villagers, gather resources, and gain new abilities as you gather all 13 drums.

(Honestly, I just hope you have as much fun running around making music as I did)

Controls

  • WASD: Move
  • Shift: Run
  • Esc: Pause/resume
  • Click: Interact

comments.author: Rob Louie
comments.text:
Cool graphics, love the day night cycle! Without being able to turn, the game became too frustrating. I also picked up an item when I wasn’t sure what it would do, then figure out i had to give to someone. I had to walk all over until someone who needed it came into my screen. I should be able to swap out items too. Also, you don’t really need a sprint button, just make the character move that fast.

I think with those tweaks I would have really enjoyed it. Still very cool look, feel, and sound!

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: nice graphics but could not figure out how to play after the first 3 villagers and first light

comments.author: Mark Vasilkov
comments.text: Clearly a lot of work went into sound design. Speeding up the walk sound effect when sprinting is a neat find! The schematic people, too, are cool.

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
i like the graphics (especially the 3d-look), and the nice clean sounds. A moon-cycle! Ha, very nice :-)

By the way: you should have added much more of your readme into the game-description. So if any of our fellow devs is giving feedback on this game, have a look here: https://github.com/js13kGames/13-Drums/blob/main/readme.md

It would be very good, if the player could turn around, currently most of the time tha caracter has to walk in a direction in which the player can not see. And my little finger hurts from pressing [shift] all the time. is there any point in using walking-speed?

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: Looks very interesting, but I got lost very early. Could use a guide.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
G’day!

I finally got to the end, it wasn’t easy :)

I liked the sky, both the day, which is iridescent with gradients, and the night, which is covered with stars. Attention was even paid to the phases of the moon, which shows attention to detail - nice! The animation of the day / night change was also amusing.

The game looks quite interesting, nice simple graphics and good music.

Regarding the gameplay - I did not immediately understand that some elements, such as “branches” and “trees” are interactive, I accidentally learned about it when the mouse pointer worked, most of the items were intuitively understandable, but some still did not immediately reflect the accuracy of what is expected. There was also a time when I filled all my free slots with items that are no longer accepted by villagers, in which case it would be appropriate to display a message about the loss, or add a mechanic to free the slot, in any case, I did not see any of this.

When I won, the win text appears at the bottom, but I can still press the “Esc” button and then the info text merges with the win text.

Although the game is interesting and thoughtful, the theme is very generic, lacking any particular resonance between the 13th century…

In my opinion, it would look more fun if the process of collecting livestock was based on hunting, for example shooting with a bow at running cattle :)

In any case, the game interested me enough, although I had to run around a bit :), thanks!

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Nice sounds, the running is cool and I like how it affects the music. The day / night switch over is great and can see a lot of thought went into the graphics.

I cannot work out what to do other than run about, I clicked on people and saw a green circle, not sure why they are running off when I do that?

I think it sounds and looks good, just confused to what I need to do?

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text: Very Cool Game, I really liked the movement of the character!!

comments.author: Michael M.
comments.text: This took very long to load, and I don’t think it really finished loading, at least clicking didn’t do anything, though it said to click to start.

comments.author: Frahaan Hussain
comments.text: Interesting concept and design. Liked the way the character turns around, subtle but cool.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Phew! Lit up all 13 drums. Man, that second to last one was a chore. I had just finished delivering the 3 flowers to the center, and all I had left was a buffalo to 8 o’clock… which meant basically a whole full ’nother circle around. rage intensifies

It was pretty, and I dug the vibe the music grooved, but if you really want to be cozy, I think the map was probably twice as large as it needed to be, and maybe you didn’t need the full circle moat, making the player walk all the way around for everything. Also, I stubbed my toe on practically everything, even the little rocks. I’m not sure you actually needed collision on anything other than the terrain in this one. I also couldn’t successfully get rid of any unwanted items, which was anxiety inducing. I ended the game still dragging an extra deer carcass around like an anchor.

I think if you went back and did a V2 of this, my advice would be let the player set the pace instead of drawing it out with space. It’s cool, I’ll stop and groove with the drums for a bit.

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Nice little puzzle game. I really liked the 3D optics of the game especially that you see the curvature of the world and the night and day cycle. The bisons are also very cute. I think a bit more explanation at the beginning or to make it more straight forward where to go after the first three villagers would have helped. It was not very obvious that the way continues at the back right where you started. Took me a while to find out. Anyway, congratulations to a nice game!

comments.author: newman s lanier
comments.text:
I hate that I have to ding it. But I’m frustrated that this is obviously an awesome game… but I can’t figure out how to play it. I can interact with two villagers and they just move to the fire. without a word - perhaps that’s they culture - cool. But then nothing happens. No music. No drums. Is my browser broken? Am I not going far enough around the map. UGH.

Now the good stuff. I love the moon cycle and the animations. The graphics are simple but good. And, the movement is cool.

To others about to play - definitely check out the readme - https://github.com/js13kGames/13-drums

comments.author: Taufik Sani
comments.text:
Maybe I’m just not good at this game (can’t seem to get past what’s after the first three villagers!)

Gameplay is a little scruffy, as in its easy to get stuck and not know where to go next. I like how clean the graphics are, and the level of smoothness with the moving character.

I think what might be helpful is to add tips and tricks on if players get stuck. The audio gives a sense of mystery to the game, which lends to trying to figure out what the player has to do next.

comments.author: Björn Ritzl
comments.text: Looks like a fun little adventure game, although I did struggle a bit to understand what I was supposed to do. I was able to interact with some villagers, but didn’t quite understand how to interact with/help others. Perhaps some in-game tutorial text, maybe on some signs around the villages, or when clicking on a villager?

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text:
Really impressive game. So many details like sky, day and night, sounds, items, trees

Really good visual style. Game itself are really interesting when you are reseaching all options. After some time it became a little boring, but enviroment are awesome.

Good job!

api.name: The Baltic League
api.description:
Inherit the position of your father and become a merchant in the Baltic Sea!

The 13th century was the beginning of the Hanseatic League. Be excited about new cities being founded as you progress in time.

Buy a cog - THE ship of the Hanseatic League.

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: UI is very confusing. But I like the idea of the reflecting the history through the commodity prices :)

comments.author: Johan Åhlgren
comments.text: I did not really understand what to do here. Really liked the texts though, I felt they gave the game a very nice atmosphere. Good job!

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: I really like the idea of this game and the writing. However, I had some problems to find into the game, despite the tutorial. I think the tutorial should have provided a better starting point, e.g. already doing a few actions, such as buying some stuff and sending it to a location. I also experienced some bugs: The sliders for buying and selling goods was not working properly and when I restarted the game the stats from my last game were still there (goods and money). I really think with a bit of tweaking this is a very good game. Text-heavy games are always a bit difficult for game jams. Many people do not take the time to read it properly and dive into a rather complex game.

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: This game looks really interesting, and you clearly put a lot of effort in the lore and researching the history of the area. But the controls and gameplay are really confusing. The use of HTML sliders for buying and selling are a very poor choice, and are really broken. This looks like it could be a fun game if you work on the UX and I encourage your to continue.

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • Historical map was cool

Feedback:

  • I wasn’t sure what I was doing. The UI was confusing and I mostly just clicked around without knowing what to do
  • Sliders to buy / sell items would have been fine except that they bought / sold immediately on change, which made it neigh impossible to buy / sell the exact amount I wanted. Having the slider there to move but then a separate button to confirm the amount would have greatly helped
  • I died almost immediately the first time I played because I pressed “Next month” over and over

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
I liked the intro, explaining how everything works while sprinkling in a good narrative.

Unfortunatelly I hit a bug where I couldn’t buy anything from the market. Everytime I tried to move the sliders, they would disappear.

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text:
What I liked:

  • The background story and story telling
  • The variety of places, items and all

What I liked less:

  • I was lost and confused most of the time, so I couldn’t do much progress.
  • I didn’t understand how to shop… it is either broken or not very intuitive.
  • I got the game over screen at what felt to be random times.

Overall, I can tell a lot of effort was put in the game. The UI needs some improvements as it is quite hard to figure out what to do.

comments.author: Tom Harris
