comments.text: Nice historical theme and you put effort into the story which you expect for a text-based game like this. I didn’t fully get what I was doing and think the onboarding could have been made easier.

api.name: 1257 Samalas
api.description:
In the wake of the cataclysmic eruption of 1257 in Lombok you find yourself standing amidst the fiery chaos and smouldering ashes. As a time traveler, you face not just the relentless force of nature, but an enigmatic and malevolent AI that awoke in the volcano, seeking to plunge the world into a chilling 700-year ice age. Your only allies in this desperate quest are the mystical Banyan trees, ancient guardians of balance. Harness their sacred power, challenge the wrath of the volcano, and thwart the plans of the AI, Abyssus. The fate of epochs rests in your hands.

Playable on mobile, desktop and especially standalone VR devices.

Instructions are in the game!

Credits

  • Game by Jure Triglav
  • Intensive playtesting: Aleks & Zarja

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text: Update: after checking the video and playing again (on Desktop) now I understand what’s the game about and I like the concept very much (works much better on VR). It is a nice 1 v 1 on a spherical battlefield needs some polishing, but the concept is great!

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text:
Great game. One mechanic but executed very well! Difficult because you keep having to turn around but even that fits well with this game. The swarms are very cool and the little growing trees look very nice. Quick action, don’t give the other side a chance to take the platforms that you already have (and to which your back is turned). Nice music as well.

Very little to complain about except that the controls are a bit rough. You might spend 10 seconds trying to get hold of a platform and point at another. Lack of feedback (pointing ray, whether a platform is selected or not, selection / turning sounds) is frustrating at times, especially in a game where speed of action is of the essence.

Very good!

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text:
Thanks so much for playing! I’ve read your feedback, took it seriously, and tweaked a bunch of things, including targeting and selection visibility, in a post-competition edition of the game, which lives at https://1k257.com.

That release is above 13kb, as I’ve added levels and also significantly improved the graphics & fixed a subtle aspect of the background music, where a fairly funky but super relaxing wave generator is now working (it shipped with the original game, but a paths issue prevented it from being loaded).

Don’t let it influence the rating of this entry, but if you liked this one, I’d be excited to hear what you have to say about the improved version!

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text: Very interesting game! And impressive implementation and animations. The music and sound design makes a lot of sense for the vibe of the game.

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I really like the concept, the game is addicitve, animations are very well done and I enjoyed playing! Maybe you could add some levels with different world size / stucture / shape. Great job, I loved this game!

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text:
Hey! Thanks for playing and your very kind words - it’s so encouraging to hear from players who enjoy it!

I’ve listened to your feedback and added a few levels, as an experiment, to a post-competition version of the game which lives at https://1k257.com. That version is above 13kb, however, as it includes numerous other improvements as well, so don’t let it influence your opinion about this entry. Hope you have fun if you do end up playing it!

In that version you can also move around in VR with your thumbstick, which is quite necessary especially in the large field level. Lots of fun, though :)

comments.author: FoumartGames
comments.text: Very nice execution, working smoothly with a chill music in the background. I’ve seen this concept in a 2D game but was yet to experience it in 3D and it was satisfying! The game is connected with the theme enough through the story explanation - it reads a bit artificial to me, but it is a game about beating an ancient AI and it works pretty well. Good job!

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text:
Thank you for playing and the kind words! The theme aspect is definitely walking on an edge, but to my defence I started out with castles and knights (as evidenced by some early WIP submissions on Slack :)), but then wanted to do something different compared to what I expected the most commonly applied flavour of the 13th century would be.

The volcano eruption, the ice age, it’s all true and historical, I just inserted an AI in there. Not sure if your “artificial” comment was a pun, but I love it. :D At one point I was even emulating an Indonesian gamelan orchestra to produce the music, but it was quite difficult to capture the richness of those sounds (doubly difficult since it’s not using a western scale for notes), so I opted for something simpler.

So yes, I’m pushing the theme’s envelope quite a bit, but hopefully it’s clear that it is intentional, not due to lack of effort and not ill-willed.

Thanks again for your thoughts, much appreciated!

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: loved it, cool game even without having vr gear

comments.author: Nathan Long
comments.text: Very unique! I like the feeling of having to monitor the enemy movement and figuring out how many to send, and then doing the quick calculations in your head of what you can commit. The only trouble I had was occasionally targeting what I wanted to, especially some of the far away platforms.

comments.author: Shauna Lynch
comments.text: Great game. Fantastic intro, love the tree theme and zen music. A very entertaining immersive experience. Addition of a exit/reload button would be handy.

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text:
Well, hard to imagine relation to 13th cent) More like starships battle)

But as a game it’s not bad, classical Eiphoria/nanowars/“galcon” like strategy in 3d. As for me, there are too many islands and it’s really hard to understand situation. Only a little Dummy AI give you option to win. There are some interesting effects and good sound.

Good Job!

comments.author: Sébastien Vandenberghe
comments.text:
Simple but effective !

The game would benefit from a bit more polish at the UX level but I am still hooked and coming back to it with pleasure.

This is the kind of tiny addictive game I could definitely play on mobile :-) but I have to admit it is way more interesting in VR.

Thanks for explaining the relation to the themes in the description as I would have not necessarily get it otherwise.

Well done on your submission.

comments.author: Jure Triglav
comments.text: Thanks for the kind words, Sebastien! Agreed on UX lacking polish - whole thing started as a tech demo for fully async GPU compute, or how many bodies can fit into a N-body simulation where they all interact with each other & it runs great on a standalone Quest 2. I stopped at 4096, since it was sufficient for gameplay, but there’s wiggle room. Next up was a custom procedural music engine, since most things I tried lacked the sort of timbre/depth I wanted. After that was an audio worklet demo, where the envelope used within is passed via a message & evaled in the worklet (due to its size), used to generate the sound of waves, which does not work in this release due to a paths issue. It does work in the updated post-compo version: 1k257.com. Then banyan trees proc gen & animations for it that make sense, then cross-platform, then custom really fast text rendering… After all this it was 8 minutes to deadline & right at the edge of 13kb! Hm, would probably be better to start with UX… :)

api.name: Moai Alley
api.description: Moai Alley is a unique sliding block puzzle game set on the Easter Island. Help the Rapa Nui build Moai statues and find the best way to transport them

comments.author: Oliver Smith
comments.text: Very slickly presented fun puzzle game. The pixel art is great and combines well with the more modern graphic elements.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text: Great game, looks gorgeous. Not sure I fully understood the mechanics, but I would absolutely play 50 levels of this to learn them.

comments.author: Luan Ferreira
comments.text: I love the work done here. Though the controls sucks, I strugled a little to undertand. And Couldnt find realation with the theme easilly. But the inovation and the graphics are the best in ALL the entries I played.

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: nice game I finished all the levels

comments.author: Aleksey Litvinov
comments.text:
Very fun game to play :) Love the graphics, however, got stuck on the level 5 for some reason (only water and Rapa Nui were rendered).

Also noticed a small glitch, on the narrow screen stage number jumps to the next line behind other text.

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text: The graphics are great! Also the sound effects. I was able to play one time, and I enjoyed the game! The puzzles are good, and the difficulty increases with the levels. I tried to play a second time, and the game does not continue to level 2. I was testing with Firefox the second time and Chrome the first time. Congrats!

comments.author: Almar Suarez
comments.text:
It was a bit confusing that the rapa-nui would seemingly follow you (instead of having to push them around), but I liked the idea.

I would hide the mobile controls on desktop (that took a big chunk of the screen) and do a tiny “you finished the game, congrats!” screen after level 5. Despite these comments I enjoyed the game, well done! :)

comments.author: ch!k0
comments.text: im not quite sure how moai related to the theme but it was an interesting game. i was not quite able to grasp the mechanics of the game but i had fun chopping trees and paving roads lol! the graphics and game sounds were on point as well!

comments.author: Steven Lambert
comments.text:
Well done:

  • Graphics looked great. That zoom in from the planet was slick

Feedback:

  • It took me awhile to understand how I was suppose to move the statues. I thought i had to move it like pulling but instead it just moves into the space I occupied, which was not intuitive at first. For example if the statue was to the left of me and I moved up it moves into the space below me, or moving it into the platform was more of a throw and worked differently
  • It took me awhile to figure out that Space did something
  • The game broke at stage 5 for me (Firefox)? It just didn’t load the puzzle and the avatar was just standing in the middle of the blue ocean
  • I’m not sure what the pave mechanic does. Moving the statue seems to automatically pave for me but I couldn’t tell if paving while not holding a statue did anything more for me

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
G’day!

I liked the visual content, it looks lively. Well-chosen sounds for interaction with the user.

On the 5th level there is water all around, unfortunately I was never able to see the end. Also, when getting the next level popup, I have the ability to press the movement buttons (left/up arrows…), which makes it impossible to continue the game, since the progress button switches to another.

It was fun! Thanks for game!

comments.author: Johan Åhlgren
comments.text: Really nice-looking and polished, although I did not really understand the mechanics of it. Great job!

comments.author: Francesco Bedussi
comments.text:
I didn’t understand how to play at first. It took my son to aplain it to me :-)

Nice game, fun to play. Good pixel art style graphics and control

comments.author: Brian Hambley
comments.text: Thought moai statues wher way older but no, 1250 to 1500 so all good ;) Couldnt get past level 5 where it was just a blue screen! (edge browser) but i was enjoying the puzzles up to then.

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text:
Wonderfully polished with a slick and thought-out presentation.

Unfortunately I experienced some bugs, particularly with the game ending but still accepting input/player movement. Still a charming and great looking game!

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Interesting socoban like game with a space puzzle and additional functions. Some levels are really complicated. Good graphics and sounds, great Job.

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text:
What I liked:

  • Quite a cute game
  • Some sound effects (yeay)
  • Easy to understand

What I liked less:

  • Nothing after level 5?
  • I figured out the controls on the keyboard after reaching the last playable level (used the onscreen buttons for most of the time)

Overall, it seems to still be a work in progress, but it is a good beginning!

comments.author: Gauthier Billot
comments.text: Great execution and nice for mobile support

api.name: Crusaders and their Journeys
api.description:
• Move up: W / ↑
  • Move right: D / →
  • Move down: S / ↓
  • Move left: A / ←
  • Fire: Left mouse button (🖰)

comments.author: newman s lanier
comments.text: A really fun and playable game. Sometimes I feel like click around everywhere. I liked the simple nature of the game and the randomness of the of the bullets fired from and bad guys appearing. good game!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
I’m glad you liked it! I also love simple games and their concepts. In fact, during development when I tried to play, the infected had a random person in your army as their destination. Also, their appearance with the robbers was synchronized and each robber could shoot, so it was hardcore… I had to slow down a bit :D.

Still, I’m glad you had fun! Thanks!

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text: Good game! Interesting concept. Pretty simple but fun idea. The only thing that I would recommend, would be to make the mouse more visible. It was difficult to keep track of the mouse. Congrats!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Glad to hear! When I first thought of the idea, I actually had a lot more in mind… but time, size (and a bit of laziness) made adjustments every day and night as the deadline approached :), so you could say I didn’t know how it will all look like in the final proposed version :D. But I’m glad that I was able to show you at least one of the many versions of my little world :).

Regarding the cursor - nice catch, thanks for sharing!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: neat concept ang graphics. The background could use some more details ;-) Sometimes my Crusaders stopped moving (when switching keys fast)

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Thanks for playing my game and sharing your opinion! At the moment, the background is described only by a gradient, it is dynamic and tries to cycle smoothly, I specifically chose the minimum contrast, because with a higher contrast, the background took away too much attention. At the initial stages, a larger world with its own map was conceived, which would include various elements, but we have what we have :).

Regarding the movement, you are right, as currently only one key is processed at a time, with a larger map, I think moving diagonally would have a much better effect, but in this form - so be it :D, thank you!

comments.author: Nick Watton
comments.text: I enjoyed this! Lovely simple game, easy to pick up and play (once I re-read who not to kill!) Maybe some death sounds, with different types for enemies and civilians? The dialogue snippets got a little repetitive.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Glad to know you liked it! Good point about the sounds! I think it will add some interesting interactivity to the game! It was the first time I made a game in this way, where the archive limit is 13KB. What can I say, I learned a lot and was pleasantly surprised how much can be realized in such a small size. But the sounds…. another time :D, instead, as an alternative, I tried to propose the “exclamations” of the crusaders, but I noticed a bug with repetition already after the release… well… :D.

Thanks for your time and feedback!

comments.author: Homer Dilpleu
comments.text: I like the concept of driving a whole army. The game is fun to play, controls are easy to understand, it is perfect for a casual game! I was not able to play the game with Edge (blank screen) but I was able to play with Chrome.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Thanks for playing my game! I’m glad you enjoyed managing your army :).

Regarding Edge - according to the rules of the game, the contest requires that the game works properly in Chrome and Mozilla in the latest versions, so this game is optimized for these two browsers, the others were not my goal.

Thanks for the feedback!

comments.author: Nathan Long
comments.text: I was a bit confused on how to play at first. I thought it was an avoidance game, and only halfway through realized I could fire. The generated voiceovers were a bit jarring when they started rolling out.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Thanks for playing my game!

When we submitted the game to the competition, we were asked to fill in the game control section, which specified all possible actions, namely: movement and the ability to shoot with the left mouse button.

Agree about the sound, it was done at the last minute, so I didn’t give it enough testing.

Thanks for the feedback!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
fun concept and easy to get into. I think it’s cool that we control an army, and not just 1 character. The game also has a feedback loop I like, the smaller the army becomes, the easier it gets (interestingly enough,) because we can avoid enemies more easily.

I would love some more sound effects and animation/particle effects to better understand what happens in the game, like killed an enemy.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Thanks for playing my game! I’m glad to hear you had fun!

Regarding sounds and animations of particles - I think it would improve the gameplay. Thanks for sharing your thoughts - I appreciate it!

comments.author: Frahaan Hussain
comments.text:
Loved it, but I would have preferred smoother movement and the ability to go diagonally. I assume you are using conditional logic for detecting which button is pressed, allowing them simultaneously would be a better experience.

Would have also preferred sound effects.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Thanks for playing my game!

As for smoother diagonal movement - I think it will improve interactivity, as well as sounds - no doubt.

Thanks for sharing the likely implementation of diagonal movement and for the feedback - I appreciate it!

comments.author: Tom Harris
comments.text: Nice game, simple and fun to play. Maybe there could have been some other sound effects to add some immersion, along with a more fluid control system.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Thanks for playing my game!

As for the sounds and the control system, I think it will add interactivity and interest to the game.

Thanks for sharing your thoughts!

comments.author: Taufik Sani
comments.text: Unplayable - everytime I press Enter/Start, system prompts that “You’ve lost! Repeat?” Can’t provide any feedback except to fix the game.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Very strange. Please try in a new tab, Chrome and Mozilla are supported. It also happens that it is buggy to open the game in voting-mode, so it would be great to try to open it in normal mode, that is, here - https://js13kgames.com/entries/crusaders-and-their-journeys

Please let me know if nothing works in your feedback marked “updated:…” - I will be very grateful!

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Nice idea to control a whole army and move them together. Very innovative. However, I think this mechanic needs some tweaking. It felt a bit cumbersome to move all of them and was very difficult to doge bullets and enemies. Maybe making the size of the army a bit smaller would help or just have a bit less enemies. It would have been great if you would have mentioned in your introduction which color are the civilians and which are the enemies. It is not very intuitive that you have to shoot the green guys :). The voice was hilarious :D. The shoutout were really fun and it sounded like the voice which was used for it was not English. Maybe it was German because of my German operating system. Maybe something to investigate further, but it was at least very funny for me :). Anway, this is still a solid entry! Congratulations to this game.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
Thanks for playing my game!

Regarding the army, namely its number, as you rightly noticed, it is very difficult for you to dodge from the beginning, it is almost impossible and vice versa, if your army has already been tortured a little and its number has decreased, then it is much easier to dodge - as one of the players nicely noticed - this is a kind of “feedback loop” (whatever that means but sounds cool :D).

In fact, when the contest first started, I thought about the idea of the game for about a week, but when I started to implement it, everything did not go as planned :), I had to change so many decisions along the way… I literally replaced one mechanic with another, and when something did not work or required much more time, just changed the idea to 361 degrees :D. Well, I didn’t know what would come out of all this chaos :).

Regarding the description of colors - agreement. About the sound - added in the last hour :), glad you had fun!

Thanks for the feedback, I appreciate it!!!!

api.name: Puzzle Tower
api.description:
In the 13th century, a tower under construction began to lean in the Republic of Pisa. Restore the misaligned floors and save the tower from collapsing!

Touch the panels like a slide puzzle, and stack them according to the floor numbers.

You can go around the tower using the buttons on both sides of the screen.

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text: Interesting version of puzzle “15”, you need to switch tiles to target places. But implementation aren’t good, really weird behaviour of tower rotation, so it’s hard to easily get a place to play and find needed tiles.

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text: Good game! Interesting. I liked the graphics and the simple effects. It would nice to be able to rotate the tower by dragging the mouse, in addition to using the left and right buttons. But cool idea!

comments.author: John Edvard
comments.text:
The puzzle is really clever. It took some time for me to understand I could slide a wall down as well.

It would be nice if we could rotate the tower with the mouse in addition to using the buttons.

comments.author: Chris Glover
comments.text: Love the graphics and the puzzle was hard! But fair. Could have done with a bit more time and the ability to rotate individual levels left and right though

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
G’day!

I don’t have time to win in such a time :). Interesting idea, but complicated gameplay. I also liked the scrolling and tilting of the tower, especially its fall :D.

As an alternative to the “left-right” scrolling control buttons, I can recommend the Pointer Lock API, with the help of which scrolling can be implemented using mouse movement, and moving tiles can be implemented using the keyboard, I think it’s worth a try….

Also noticed a negative time if you switch to another tab before finishing.

In any case, I have not seen this before, quite innovative, thanks!

comments.author: ch!k0
comments.text: It was a very interesting game! Though it took me a few tries to get used to play and enjoy it since the rotation was continuous with one click of the left/right arrows. Also there were times i unconsciously dragged the screen to rotate. It was very challenging to complete-the time limit is fair too :)

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text:
Clever 3D and texture mapping, runs well.

Difficult to play, using full screen would have helped, was very small on my screen.

comments.author: Samuel van Egmond
comments.text: Interesting, looked good, strange that it was not full screen. The puzzle was way too hard. Never saw a similar game, nice!

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
That was a fun simple puzzle game! Unfortunately with the speed of the controls the way they are, even though I had worked out what seemed like an optimal sorting algorithm, I still had no hope to solve it in time, so I hacked the timer to turn it off ;) Eventually finished all 3 phases of construction after that.

Loved the graphics, really nicely done 3D render of the tower of Pisa. Great idea!

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text:
What I liked:

  • Visually impressive (3D!)

What I liked less:

  • The controls drove me crazy. There should be another way to rotate than buttons that start/stop the rotation.

Overall, a lot of effort was put in making this game look good, and it does! But hard controls for an already hard game, maybe not the best combination.

comments.author: Tom Harris
comments.text: Very simple game without much to it, and the visual was very small. I enjoyed the puzzle and think it’s a nice idea for a more complete game.

api.name: Only One
api.description:
Defeat enemies alone on the battlefield. It has 5 levels.

  • WASD (or arrow keys): Move.
  • Click on the screen with the mouse: Attack.
  • Spacebar to move to the next level.

comments.author: Oliver Smith
comments.text: Nice minimalistic presentation, simple but fun. Differentiating the ‘level progression’ and ‘you lost’ screens more would have improved the experience for me.

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: a good use of shadows and rotating of sword

comments.author: Christian Staudte
comments.text:
The graphics are really smooth and it felt quite nice to move around.

However, I am not so sure whether the game matches the theme. Also, at some points the seemed like to collision detection didn’t work quite well.

It was a fun game, It just felt a bit empty (more enemies, more sound, etc. would have been nice)

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text: Nice clean presentation, i like the color-palette. Some added eye-candy (particle-effects, screen-shakes,…) and a bit more interaction-possibilities (i.e. dash to a direction with [space] or [right-mouse-button]) would add much to the game.

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: I think it would work better without having to click to do damage(like in https://dev.js13kgames.com/2023/games/knight-dreams) . And with sword hit box being actually aligned with the sprite. So far it seems that enemy is not damaged by the sword top, only by it’s middle/hilt.

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
G’day!

Good balance of levels difficulty. Unfortunately, I did not find in it resonance with the theme of the competition. Also very reminiscent of tutorial games where you learn, good start, but more innovation and uniqueness would have made my experience so much better!

Thanks for game!

comments.author: Allen Schmidt
comments.text: Nice little game! I could have used some indication of where the mouse was, as sometimes it got a bit lost when I was moving around. The hit box for the sword feels kinda small as well (though that might just be me complaining because I couldn’t make it past level 4 haha).

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text:
Minimalist, stylized, and highly playable little arena game! I wish the sword swing was more of an arc around the player instead of a simple waggle / pivot at the handle. I’d also like the archers to give some warning before they shoot.

Overall a simple and fun game. Nice work!

comments.author: Tero Jäntti
comments.text:
What I liked about it:

  • The minimalistic retro style looks good, the shadows and the sword animation add a nice touch to it.
  • I think the challenge progressed well from easy levels to the difficult last level.
  • There was nice variety in enemies with different speeds and archers in the mix.

What could be improved:

  • On Firefox, the mouse cursor doesn’t appear at all. Also, the game froze a couple of times, either for a short time or I had to start all over. After trying with Firefox yesterday it was a bit frustrating, but now with Chrome the experience was much smoother :)
  • It happened to me a few times that you lose all your hearts in one go. Perhaps there could a little time given to the player to escape from a situation before losing more hearts.
  • A dying animation to enemies could add more feel to it. Like some simple “collapsing” or something.
  • A simple storyline, for example with some short introduction text for each level, could add to the atmosphere and theme.

Overall, a fun and complete little game with just a right amount of challenge to play it through!

comments.author: Noël Meudec
comments.text:
What I liked:

  • Intersting controls
  • Sound effects (at least one!)
  • The last level is challenging
  • Cute little sword :)

What I liked less:

  • Completed in 3 minutes
  • Changing the color of the background to indicate the change of difficulty would have been great
  • Hard to play on a trackpad (I don’t have a mouse today)
  • One more enemy type would have been great

Overall, it is a fine game, but very short.

comments.author: Chris Subagio
comments.text:
Nice idea! It’s like melee Asteroids. Looks nice, like the minimalist approach. Could have splashed out on another shape for the archers though. Triangles perhaps?

I think there’s a lot of potential to this concept, but it doesn’t quite feel right yet. The hit detection is a little loose on the one hand, and it’s almost too easy to circle strafe, because the enemy’s can’t deal with it, and they overlap, so they conveniently bunch up for a quick swipe. But it IS one of those things where you can totally imagine what the tighter, juiced up version would be like, and it seems cool!

api.name: Trado Polo explores
api.description:
The game is loosely based on Marco Polo, the explorer and merchant from the 13th century Italy.

Your job is to explore Asia, find the bigger cities and ports along the way and trade for new goods.

In the mini game you have to press the button for the corresponding column when the big dot turns green. Missing it or pressing when a cross is there will count as a fail. You have 5 rounds, but if you fail 3 of them your course will be diverted.

In this version only the keyboard controls work (they are also shown on screen):

  • A: steer your ship/caravan left
  • D: steer your ship/caravan right
  • H-J-K-L: play the mini game on the right side of the screen

This version misses some features and mechanics, I’ll upload an updated and extended after-the-jam version to Itch.

comments.author: Isaac Benitez
comments.text: Congrats for the game! I really liked the graphics and the vibe of the game. Also liked the story! The navigation system is also cool. I quite didn’t understand the HJKL controls. I didn’t know how they work nor what the effect was. But pretty cool!

comments.author: Gabor Héja
comments.text: Thanks so much, I am glad you liked the game! The minigame is rough and a bit buggy in this version, it should divert you from your course so you’d take a longer route and be a bit late - this was added to increase the difficulty somewhat, but it would needs more work. Thanks for playing!

comments.author: Christoph Schansky
comments.text:
I really like the cartography-style and the feeling of exploration, when navigating through the montain-ranges.

It would be cool, to also be in charge of the movement-speed, not only the facing direction. And maybe make ist faster? the long distances made it a little challenge to play the game to the end (and not quit inbetween)

After navigating to the first city, like an old sea-bear keeping clear of any cliff, i was quite unsure where to go, or how to reach my next target. To my surprise, the ship can sail over land! :-) Maybe on land the player-sprite should change, to a horse/camel…?

In the end, when the colden coat is delivered, there ist some storytelling missing. the game just stops. Ah, and regarding story-telling: it would be cool t be able to skip the intro-story, or to be able to make it faster. Ifthe player knows the content already, its really hard to wait for the real game to start.

However, i think thats a really good idea for a game, it just needs more stuff to do, and maybe some eyecandy (some effects, other movement on the map, etc…)

comments.author: Gabor Héja
comments.text:
I am happy you liked the game and wow, thank you for delivering the golden coat and completing it, old sea-bear! :) Yes, you’re unfortunately right, the final dialog is missing here… I will update the itch.io version with that.

I planned to add a caravan or camel sprite for the land, maybe limit switching to land/water transport in cities with ports, boost for completing the minigame successfully, artifacts and a few other features but ran out of time.

Thanks for the ideas, I’m taking notes (really) :)

comments.author: Alexander Petrov
comments.text: Long unskippable intro is a no no for jam games. Unless it’s some super cool intro, but not the case here.

comments.author: Gabor Héja
comments.text: I agree, I should have added a skip intro button. Thanks for the feedback!

comments.author: Roman Miniv
comments.text:
G’day!

I liked the navigation system (compass), as well as the minimalism of the map (reminiscent of the past), as well as the mechanics of opening a new territory (the closer you are, the more the “fog” dissipates). Simple and quite attractive vector graphics and an interesting map design (noticed the coincidence with the real map :) ).

I like the description text at the start of the game that sets the context, but I miss the button to skip the starting text (you could implement it through local storage), it’s fun to read the first time, but when you want to play again, it makes you wait for the end of the text.

Unfortunately due to errors I couldn’t finish the game (crashes when I return to Venice with the golden cloak).

I suppose that the buttons [H, J, K, L] are intended to maintain interest during the journey, but unfortunately I did not really understand their exact purpose, there is not enough information about it. Instead of this [H, J, K, L] button mechanic, I would have preferred pirates for example, where you can shoot, ram, etc… while traveling (during long and slow swims), I would have a lot more fun! :) (but time and size limitations… :D)

It was fun and interesting to play, thank you!

comments.author: Mefistofel
comments.text:
Well, really interesting an unusual concept. Also I liked this abstract trade with adding new items in you cargo But main mechanic - this “piano” is really frustrated. First of all, there is no instruction and I spend lot time trying to undestans something. Still not sure am I right. Second, buttons not corresponding with line, so it’s hard what key from hjkl you need to press to hit. Third, you also need to control your ship and look forward. It would be better to show this symbols somethere close to the ship, for example add it to some “wind” element close to the ship.

But in general interesting game. Great Job!

comments.author: Matt Hackett
comments.text:
Definitely wish the introduction was skippable. The text renders underneath the UI, making it a bit hard to read. The next section with the map and boats is much more exciting to me.

I’m not understanding the HJKL gameplay, but it looks nice, runs smooth, and fits the theme nicely. Good job!

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text:
The lore of the game is captivating and I was ready to explore outside the country of Europe (lol). What I wasn’t expecting was that I was sailing a ship that can cross the desert, but for some reason can’t climb mountains. Seems a little picky for a magical ship.

The art, even if a little crude, was really nice, and “old-map-like”. Good job on the fog of war effect.

I didn’t understand the minigame very well. I mashed all keys and it seemed to work.

I only played for a handful of cities because it seemed to be always the same. The next city always trades with whatever items I collected earlier, so there was little challenge. Maybe this changes later but after playing so long I failed to be captivated by the game’s mechanics.

comments.author: Gabor Héja
comments.text:
Thanks for playing it!

I also found the game rather straightforward, that’s why I added the minigame to shake it a bit up, but unfortunately it was still very rough when I submitted it. I was planning to add some time constraint and additional items to encourage the player to play well in the minigame, but ran out of time.

I am glad you liked the style, that idea was what got me started working on the game :)

comments.author: Lakshman
comments.text: There should have been a fast forward button which could have skipped in the intro for the second time players. It would have been good if the control of the boat speed would have been given to user.

comments.author: Stiggstogg
comments.text: Very good looking game! I really liked the ship driving and the style of the map including the mechanic that you explore the areas (revealed areas when you go there). The mini-game was a bit difficult for me as the point were not really good aligned. It was not so obvious which key you had to press. One detail: If you have a game which is mainly controlled by the keyboard, I would avoid that you need to click with the mouse in between (to continue), which is a bit cumbersome. Congratulations to you game and have fun adding more features and mechanics!

comments.author: Grav
comments.text: Thank you for this game. My experience similar to others, really liked the cartography style visuals, the compass really cool. But I also could not understand the hjkl minigame but did understand when I lost the bearings got mixed up which was good. It is a shame because whatever that minigame is - from your description sounds like a rythm game - it would have broken up the gameplay really nicely. Would like to see your updated extended version. Well done, it is a good game.

api.name: Fort@dmin
api.description: A mini text adventure game inspired by the atelier series

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: i know it was not a rule to use canvas but it feel weird to have a game like this

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text: Could do with a bit of styles to organise the page a bit better.

comments.author: John Swana
comments.text: I assume this game doesnt just load correctly? but good idea with the text adventure

comments.author: João Lopes
comments.text: There’s no problem is developing text-based games, but I have no idea what I’m supposed to do here. The amount of information dumped on us is too overwhelming and the controls are tiny. You could have at least used the description of the game to enlighten us a little, because I’ve never heard of the atelier series.

comments.author: Jupiter Hadley
comments.text: I do not have a clear understanding of what to do - there is a lot of text but not a lot of explination.

api.name: Defense of the 13th City
api.description:
I couldn’t fit the game into 13kb unfortunately, it’s 17.6kb (18,028 bytes).

Game description

Defend the city from the attackers! Recruit soldiers, and build various buildings to succeed.

You have two resources: gold and food. You need gold to recruit soldiers, build buildings, and purchase upgrades. You need food to keep your soldiers alive. If you run out of food, your soldiers will start losing health.

Your goal is to defend the castle, which is the main building of the city. If the enemy destroys it, you lose.

The soldiers start at the castle, and walk towards the enemy. The enemy soldiers are coming from outside the city. When the friendly and enemy soldiers are close to each other, they’ll fight.

Buildings

  • Castle: Main building, must be defended. You can recruit soldiers using the castle. Also produces one gold per second.
  • House: Increases the amount of gold you receive.
  • Windmill: Produces food, which is needed by your soldiers.
  • Blacksmith: You can buy upgrades here to increase the damage done by your soldiers, and to reduce their damage taken.
  • Church: Provides abilities that can turn the combat in your favor.

Controls

  • Left mouse click: select buildings, buy upgrades
  • Right mouse drag: rotate camera
  • Mouse wheel drag: pan camera
  • Escape: cancel current building

To build, select a building (bottom left), then click on the ground where you want to build it.

comments.author: Oliver Smith
comments.text: A well presented development on the tower defence genre. Good to see the possibilities despite it being over budget. Nice work.

comments.author: Mohammad Jawad
comments.text: 3d games is always better but at high challenge for fitting in 13kb cool game though

comments.author: Ryan Tyler
comments.text: Impressive work to fit this into less than 18k bytes!

comments.author: Alexey Kalmakov
comments.text: Whoa, this is fantastic result for 18kb! I surprised that there are animations and so many different models and textures.

comments.author: Tiago Correia
comments.text:
Such a shame that you went over the limit. I feel like you could’ve gotten it down by cutting some content.

I only needed houses and archer towers to clear all the levels. I spam houses in the beginning and then spam a bunch of towers.

Otherwise it’s a fun game.

comments.author: Jasper Renow-Clarke
comments.text:
A fun game to play, the graphics and sounds were both really good.

Would be good to see some activity, like people or something.

api.name: Last Viking
api.description:
The year is 1262. Iceland should be brought under Norwegian rule. For peace, they say. To replace the Gods by the Christian one, Odin says! Get rid of the missionaries and hail to Valhalla!

Controls

  • Use A and D to move sideways.
  • Use Q and E to rotate.
  • Use D to move down faster.
  • Use Space to drop directly.

WIP

Starting as a platformer I later decided to make it a somewhat “Dr. Mario” clone. Unfortunately, summer came back for a few more days and motivation dropped.

What’s missing:

  • Checks + obstacle removing
  • A score
  • New Levels / max cap
  • Start / Winning / Game Over screens
  • Some sound
  • Polishing

Nevertheless I’ll keep developing this project and will release it on itch eventually. Follow me on Twitter / X or on GitHub to stay updated :)

See you next year, maybe with a final project. Learned a lot from this challenge!
